File.Name: g402e_g402-015.jpg
Notes: 26106072782 Here are new berries forming on a shrub of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae). If you look close, you can see a few "folded leaves" on the far-left and below the center that I'm pretty sure are galls - but I don't know whose. I have a closer photo of a galled leaf here, as well as photos of flowers and ripe berries. Yep, I like these shrubs! (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)

File.Name: g402f_g402-005.jpg
Notes: 26105962652 These are the first aphid-galled leaves of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The tumor-like galls are caused the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Specialized aphids live inside those pockets on the leaves. They are strange and quite beautiful when fresh like these. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: g403a_g403-027.jpg
Notes:
25950129970 This handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) found me this morning! It bumped into me as I was sitting in my chair by the creek. I had it on my arm, but it took off again and landed on our dusty road. It struggled some, and I finally picked it up and moved it to a nearby oak leaf. Maybe it's genus Platypedia? This one is pretty tattered, as well as dusty. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that we've been hearing up in the oaks. We do have different cicadas later in summer that do sing. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)

It was breezy again this morning, but I managed to get my photos. The real issue is pollen - and tissue for my hay fever! Just stepping outside brings it on, and of course I was outside all day. It's not just me. Julie hired a young man (with some restoration background) to do some weeding along our road. He worked for an hour and gave up, feeling all stuffed up!
Locality: with some restoration background) to do some weeding along our road.  He worked for an hour and gave up

File.Name: g403b_g403-005.jpg
Notes: 26196856196 This beautiful green Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is Diaea livens, perched for a moment on a leaf of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. I reckon its a male with those enlarged palps. I got this photo in the breeze this morning, and it came out better than I expected. The shadow is pretty cool also! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g403c_g403-016.jpg
Notes:
26222322675 Here are two shots of an unfortunate caterpillar-like larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that has become caught up in the tangled web of a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae). It was thrashing about, hard to photograph. These larvae often hang from the oak trees on silk webs, and I guess this one wasn't careful enough about where it landed.

This is NOT a proper caterpillar (Lepidoptera)! First, it has too many "prolegs", those little stumps in the back that are separate from its three pairs of true legs in front. I count eight pairs, but caterpillars have no more than 5 pairs. Second, it has single prominent eyes instead of the usual obscure circle of eyespots that caterpillars have. Finally, those "forked hairs" seem pretty distinctive. I don't know of any caterpillar that has hairs like that. See this page for a nice review of the differences. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)

File.Name: g403d_g403-001.jpg
Notes: 25617225114 Here is the lovely late-instar nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It's tiny, about 840 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm long, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That's not counting its tail - which makes it look jet propelled! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: g403e_g403-011.jpg
Notes: 26195933866 This is another tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon today. I guess this is a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). There are lots of them in the woods right now on many different plants. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)
CommonNames: Katydid; Coast Live Oak; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: g403f_g403-018.jpg
Notes: 26129239522 This is a furry little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on my sweat pants! It has pretty spotted wings. Hah, it wouldn't pose for me on the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) leaf where I first found it, but it did seem to like my pants. That's not as bad as having the butterfly you're trying to get perch on your camera lens! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2016)

File.Name: g404a_g404-022.jpg
Notes:
25639675494 Here are lots of new red flowers of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. Yep, there was a hummingbird working the flowers, but I couldn't get a photo. Very beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)

I think of all the flowers in my photos today as "second wave" flowers. The early flowers like Shooting Stars are still going, but their time is running out. The summer flowers haven't started yet. It was windy today, which limited my photos to large beautiful flowers like these. I need to go back to Paradise when it's not so breezy.

File.Name: g404b_g404-032.jpg
Notes: 25639501654 I believe these are the flower spikes of uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are taller and more robust than the Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in my [Previous] photo. I'll have to go back for a closer shot when it's not so windy. These flower spikes are actually on the small side at 2-3 feet. They are growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the White Fire in May 2013. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)

File.Name: g404c_g404-018.jpg
Notes: 25638962554 I believe these are pretty new flowers of native Larkspur (Delphinium) in the Ranunculaceae plant family growing in the woodland along Paradise Road. This is a shy woodland plant that just happened to be leaning over for a photo. I was thinking Delphinium parryi, but RandomTruth has me thinking that it's Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum instead, see his comments below. The uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (D. umbraculorum) is more robust, and seems to like sunnier places, see my [Next] photo, and see this photo for a comparison. All our delphiniums are very beautiful, and I might be wrong about all of them. Delphiniums are a tricky group! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)
ScientificName: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; D. umbraculorum
associatedTaxa: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; D. umbraculorum

File.Name: g404d_g404-030.jpg
Notes: 26243344165 I believe this is a large clump of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua or Nasella cernua, Poacea) growing in a mass of non-native weedy grass - mostly Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus). The grasses are all blowing in the wind today. It you look close, you can see what I believe is the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus, Fabaceae) hiding behind the grass clump. I really like this photo. It shows that the native grasses are hanging on despite all the non-native annuals. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)

File.Name: g404e_g404-015.jpg
Notes: 26243186595 This is an uncommon native Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans aka Eremothera boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's growing on a crumbly slope of Monterey Shale on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. I think of it as a desert plant, but here it is happy on a south-facing slope by the river. It's growing in a place that was burned by the White Fire in May 2013, so I reckon it counts as a fire-follower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)
ScientificName: Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae

File.Name: g404f_g404-005.jpg
Notes: 25970183180 I believe this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy flower heads which look a bit like "woolly bear" caterpillars. The long "hispid" hairs are a irritating to the skin. Phacelias are a tricky plant group in our area because we have so many different kinds, but these are distinctive. There were lots of them along Paradise Road today growing as fire-followers after the White Fire in May 2013. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)

File.Name: g405a_g405-008.jpg
Notes:
26171418532 Here are two shots of the same tiny Lace Bug (Tingidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. Look, you can see the tiny bug under the remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but also looks like bubble-wrap! This is the first one I've seen this year, and it's remarkable because it's on a mint leaf instead of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) where I usually find them. It's also remarkable because it's so tattered. Do these insects actually over-winter as adults?? (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2016)

I wasn't really concerned about new photos today because I already had a few nice ones "in the can" from yesterday. It was another beautiful day, little wind and quite warm - in the 80s on our upstairs porch. Tomorrow is expected to be another warm one, but then we have an even chance of rain on Thursday night and Friday! We'll see what happens.
ScientificName: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: g405b_g404-004.jpg
Notes: 25990721890 This is a striking Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae of the order Hymenoptera hanging onto a leaf in the garden. Actually it was thrashing about, with a leg maybe caught in an invisible spider web? I took a bunch of photos in the low light and got this keeper. It's not quite sharp because the wasp was in motion. (But who cares?) It did finally get away. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2016)

File.Name: g405c_g404-001.jpg
Notes: 25658548804 I found this neat little spider on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) growing just off our upstairs porch. I believe it's a Running Crab Spider in the family Philodromidae. One distinguishing trait of this family is that leg II is longer than leg I which you can clearly see in my photo. This one might be genus Philodromus. These running crab spiders are hunters, not at all like the usual Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) that wait in flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Running Crab Spider; Crab Spiders
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Philodromidae; Philodromus; running; Thomisidae

File.Name: g405d_g404-002.jpg
Notes:
25990048150 This is a spring bisexual gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's a "bisexual gall" because it will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls, see this photo which only contain female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2016)

I guess I understand galls, but I don't understand why they are so darn beautiful!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g405e_g405-021.jpg
Notes:
26236539256 These are new flowers of the San Diego Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family. I hesitate just a bit because the flowers in the center that are fairly sharp look perfect with stamens and pistil, and this species is said to be dioecious with separate male and female plants. I think Jepson is wrong. This plant will end up with small white berries, see this photo from last year. These plants climb over other shrubs, California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in this case. The leaves often turn red as the season goes on, and the plants look quite dead by the end of the year. But they come back. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2016)

Plants in the genus Galium are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny 4-merous flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites - even if I get confused by them!
ScientificName: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; look; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Galium
associatedTaxa: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; look; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Galium

File.Name: g405f_g404-026.jpg
Notes: 25657526724 Here are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing on a dry bluff above the dry flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. The aromatic leaves of this fine shrub make healing tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its common name. The flowers seem early this year, but I found them on April 14 last year, which is close enough. I'm too cynical to be any kind of herbalist, but I really like this plant! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 April 2016)

File.Name: g406a_g406-015.jpg
Notes:
25678775634 This is the first Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've managed to photograph this year - over-exposed and not quite sharp, but it's a record photo! This one is perched for a moment on its preferred host plant, Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae). The first butterflies are always a bit skittish, but I'm sure I be able to get better photos as the season goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

I'm running late tonight because TCM was showing the 1947 movie New Orleans tonight. Billie Holiday as a maid was an indignity, but the songs with Lady Day and Satchmo together are sublime. I had to get up often to watch and listen!

There were pluses and minuses with the weather today. On the plus side, it was warm again, though cooler than yesterday. On the minus side, it was breezy and hazy with high clouds that never quite stopped making shadows. On the plus side again, we have a decent chance of rain over the next few days! It sound like Saturday will be the best chance, though there is a 20% chance of rain tomorrow.

Notes: 25678477954 This is a small spider about to grab its aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) prey under the natural "light tent" of a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but maybe it's a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) with the outer eyes joined? Whatever kind of spider it is, this one is very male with those enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

Notes: 26190679552 I found this bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on the same tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that was experiencing an infestation of Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae. See my [Previous] photos. This one seemed drawn to that tree. These flies are known for laying their eggs on other critters, and I hope this one is doing its job. Maybe it's genus Peleteria? (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

File.Name: g406d_g406-037.jpg
Notes:
26190080922 This is the caterpillar-like larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) doing real damage to a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It you count legs, it has three three pair up front and eight pair of "prolegs" behind. Last year these grubs half-defoliated the large oak tree just off our upstairs porch, see this photo and this one. This is a different oak tree, but it has the same kind of leaf damage. Timing might be important with these infestations. Our oak at home leafed out before the critters were out, and there is minimal damage. This small tree leafed out late and is paying for it. These little grubs have forked-horns on their back. I think of them as "devil horns" for the little devils they are! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

We have nesting House Wrens on the upstairs porch right now. I reckon they'll take care of these little devils!

File.Name: g406e_g406-022.jpg
Notes:
26009281030 Here are two photos comparing a caterpillar with something that is NOT a caterpillar. On the left is the caterpillar-like larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). On the right is a real caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). The difference that you can see in these photos is that the wasp larvae has too many "prolegs" - those little stumps in the back that are separate from its three pairs of true legs in front. True caterpillars have no more than five pairs, and this only only seems to have four. Sawflys - at least the ones in our oak trees - seem to have eight pairs. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

This difference is important. The sawfly larvae can become terrible pests of our oak trees, see my [Next] photo.

(I think Alice Abelia managed to track the caterpillar down - Aseptis perfumosa, a Noctuidae moth, see BugGuide.)
Locality: I think Alice Abelia managed to track the caterpillar down - Aseptis perfumosa
CommonNames: Sawfly Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Chamise
ScientificName: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Aseptis perfumosa; Noctuidae
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Aseptis perfumosa; Noctuidae

File.Name: g406f_g406-009.jpg
Notes: 26008990930 Here are tiny ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending even tinier aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a new flower cluster of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). If you look close, you can spot a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) below the abdomen of the ant in the center. I believe the ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae with articulated abdomens and thoracic spines - visible on the top ant if you look close. (One of the few ants I can recognize!) I have no idea what the wasp is, but I reckon this is why the aphids need tending. This is not a great photo - it was cloudy and breezy today. But it is a great scene! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Hymenoptera; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: g407a_g407-035.jpg
Notes:
25697454073 This is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched for a moment along West Camino Cielo road today. I just posted a photo of this butterfly yesterday and commented "I'm sure I be able to get better photos as the season goes on" - I never thought that would be today! In time, these will probably become common and I'll get tired of photographing them. But not yet! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

We did get a bit of rain today! Only 1/100 inch so far, but it's a good sign for the period of active weather coming up, see this new article on the California Weather Blog. It sounds like the active weather will continue for a week, with the best chance of real rain on Saturday. This will do the late-season wildflowers some good.

File.Name: g407b_g407-025.jpg
Notes:
25694884574 This is an uncommon native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. At least most of it - this specimen has already lost 2 of its 4 petals. It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns or (less commonly) after clearings. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn, and sometimes that's enough to trigger these plants. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). This was the only one I found in this place, and I was surprised to find it. There are several plants growing in a different place that was only cleared last year, but I haven't found flowers yet. I'll keep checking. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

Note that the Wind Poppy (Stylomecon heterophylla aka Papaver heterophyllum) looks similar but has a conspicuous style on the fruit and purplish marks at the base of the petals.
CommonNames: Fire Poppy; Oriental Poppy; Wind Poppy
ScientificName: Papaver californicum; Papaveraceae; Papaver somniferum; Stylomecon heterophylla; Papaver heterophyllum
associatedTaxa: Papaver californicum; Papaveraceae; Papaver somniferum; Stylomecon heterophylla; Papaver heterophyllum

File.Name: g407c_g407-014.jpg
Notes:
26026683880 This small plant is the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. You have to hunt for it in the crowd photo on the left - look in the center. (It's growing with Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri, Portulacaceae) and Fremont's Star Lily (Toxicoscordion fremontii, Melanthiaceae.) It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. This one is growing in a place that was cleared but not burned. The seed-pods get long and bend down to give the stems a shaggy look. It's not a common plant in our mountains, even after wildfires. These plants were large and robust when I first found them a few years ago, but they seem to get smaller every year that I find them here. One of these years, they'll disappear until the next clearing. That's how it is with fire-followers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

Hah - in past years, this plant has also been known as Thelypodium lasiophyllum and Guillenia lasiophylla. Those darned botanists!
CommonNames: California Mustard; Brewer's Red Maids; Fremont's Star Lily
ScientificName: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Calandrinia breweri; Portulacaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla

File.Name: g407d_g407-022.jpg
Notes: 25696576293 Here are beautiful flower buds of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants look kind of weedy when you first notice them, like non-native Sow Thistle (Sonchus). But if you look closer, you see how elegant they are. They are a fire-follower that lingers in disturbed places, though this place was cleared but never burned. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

File.Name: g407e_g406-017.jpg
Notes: 26232950151 This is a beautiful bristly bug butt shot of a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's genus Peleteria? I found this fly on a tree that was experiencing an infestation of Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae. See this photo from yesterday. HBBBBT? (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

File.Name: g407f_g405-006.jpg
Notes: 25696218183 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider perched on its web in the mint patch of the garden. These spiders have that odd bump on their abdomen. You can see a bit of the spider's stabilimentum decorated with the dead bodies of its prey, pirate fashion. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2016)

File.Name: g408a_g407-033.jpg
Notes:
26223567722 This is the fantastic dandelion-like seedhead of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The flowers aren't much to look at, but the seed heads are wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

We had a day of Portland weather today! There was light rain or drizzle all day. I only see 0.35 inches in our rain gauge, but the NWS San Marcos Pass weather station a quarter mile away recorded 0.48 inches. (Now how did that happen?) Anyway, it was lovely weather of a sort that is actually uncommon up here. Usually it rains like hell for an hour or a day, then the wind blows all the clouds away. This protracted drizzle is different, a sign that this is a different sort of storm coming from the southwest rather than the north. We have a better chance of real rain on Saturday. This will do the late-season wildflowers some good.
Locality: Now how did that happen?)  Anyway

File.Name: g408b_g407-030.jpg
Notes: 26223461302 These are the first flowers I've found this year of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family. The pretty flowers look fertile, but the shrubs mostly spread by runners and rarely if ever make seeds. The stems grow into gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwhack through a thicket of them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

File.Name: g408c_g407-008.jpg
Notes:
26249290531 This is native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. This is the common form in our mountains with white-hairy stems and petals. Compare the var. hoffmannii with nearly glabrous stems in this photo from near Lake Cachuma. These are the kind of small differences that keep our local botany interesting year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

Did you notice that tiny Beetle on the open flower?

File.Name: g408d_g407-012.jpg
Notes:
26249226061 Here are more new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in Jepson, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. Very beautiful. I have to fight the urge to photograph every one I find! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2016)

They are a fire-follower, though these are growing in a place that was cleared - not burned - a few years ago. Sometimes that's enough.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: g408e_g406-033.jpg
Notes: 26289275916 Here's another photo of caterpillar-like larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) doing real damage to a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There's a green one above and a light-colored one poking its head out from below. I assume they are different kinds, but I'm not sure. Both have forked-bristles on their backs which I think of as "devil horns" for the little devils they are! I don't like these critters, because they almost defoliated my favorite oak tree just off our upstairs porch last year. The tree has recovered, but I'm still happy to see parasitic flies attracted to them! (See my [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)

File.Name: g408f_g406-027.jpg
Notes: 26315082535 Here is one more shot of a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) in a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's genus Peleteria? This one has a white fringe around its head, like an Elizabethan lace ruff! I found this fly on a tree that was experiencing an infestation of Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae, and I'm hoping it will do it's part to control them. See my [Next] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2016)
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Coast Live Oak; Sawfly Wasp
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peleteria; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peleteria; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g409a_g409-013.jpg
Notes:
26270697541 This is a new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the rain today, with green raindrops reflecting the woods. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)

It was another day of Portland weather! There was light rain or drizzle for much of the day, though we did get a break in the afternoon, and even a few brief moments of sunshine. Now we're up to 8/10 inch and it's still coming down, hopefully for the night. This will do the late-season wildflowers some good.

Unfortunately, this was the weekend of the Lucidity Festival at Live Oak Campground near Lake Cachuma. I haven't heard that they've cancelled, but I don't hear any music from our porch either. (The sound usually carries.) This festival isn't my cup of tea, but I sympathize with their situation.
Locality: The sound usually carries.)  This festival isn't my cup of tea

File.Name: g409b_g409-030.jpg
Notes: 25733783643 These are tiny pistillate flowers on a new branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The staminate catkins are easy to notice, but these lady flowers are shy and hidden. They look like little acorns, or at least they will when they get a bit bigger. What's so odd is that the female flowers appear a few weeks after the male flowers are done shedding their pollen. But I can vouch with my hay fever that the air is still full of oak pollen! Hopefully our rain this weekend will finally settle it all. I got this photo from our upstairs porch during a rare moment of sunshine this afternoon. I thought the rain was all done, but it's raining again right now. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)

File.Name: g409c_g409-022.jpg
Notes: 25733364953 These are tiny wasp galls on a fallen staminate catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, see this photo. If you look close, you can see that there's an exit hole on the upper-left gall where the wasp chewed its way out. The exit hole is about 180 pixels high in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.7 mm, about 1/32 inch - and that's for the adult wasp! (See here for how I figured.) I believe these are the spring galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, which will make different summer galls after they mature. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: g409d_g409-019.jpg
Notes: 26269762191 Here are two photos of a beautiful new flower stalk of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family in the rain today. There are no flowers yet, but the buds are forming. At first there are only the long strap-like basal leaves which look like they belong to a different plant. I've only seen a few of the flower stalks this year, but lots of the basal leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)

File.Name: g409e_g409-028.jpg
Notes: 25730826474 This is a bank of Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing on the shady hillside by our mailbox. If you look close, you can see an abundance of strap-like basal leaves of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family, especially near the bottom of the photo. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae

File.Name: g409f_g409-017.jpg
Notes:
26335523175 Green on green...

This is a thicket of mostly Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae) and Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) enjoying the rain today. The bracken is a summer fern in our summer-dry mountains, so a rainy day must be a treat. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2016)
ScientificName: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g410a_g410-040.jpg
Notes:
25757426523 I found this caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a shrub of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family today. At first I thought it might be the caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly. It's the right host plant, one of them at least, but the shape of the body is wrong and it's doesn't have white eye-spots on its head. So unless it's a early non-typical instar, I'm not sure whose larva it is. On the other hand, I'm not sure what else it could be. It is beautiful and fairly large, an inch or two long. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)

Today turned out to be a good day for caterpillars, though I can't identify any of them. It was another day of Portland weather - a few moments of sunshine, but mostly gloomy with some drizzle. At about 4:30, we had a bout of real rain that pushed us over the one inch mark for the storm. This bring us up past the 20 inch mark for the rain year, but that's still only about 65% normal.

I've been hearing the faint sounds of drumming coming up the canyon, which means the Lucidity Festival at Live Oak Campground near Lake Cachuma is indeed going on. I appreciate that they're soldiering on through this weekend of funky weather.
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: g410b_g410-005.jpg
Notes: 26293569991 I found this little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on the upstairs porch railing this morning. No doubt it dropped down from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that overhangs the porch just a bit. At first it was coiled up in a little ball, but then it opened up and tried to get away. I got the photo, then placed it back in the tree. It wasn't there when I can back home, but I'm not sure if it stuck or fell away. It has lots of eye-like spots on its head, but if you look close you can see the typical half-circle of real eyes in the lower-left. I have no idea what kind of caterpillar it is. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)

File.Name: g410c_g410-046.jpg
Notes: 26333711586 This is a little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) with its head stuffed down the throat of a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - where a butterfly should be feeding! I have no idea what kind of caterpillar it is. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)

File.Name: g410d_g410-020.jpg
Notes: 26359430025 Here is the first flower I've seen of native Cardinal Catchfly aka Mexican Pink or Indian Pink (Silene laciniata) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family, growing in the canyon today. The flowers look like it's been torn apart, but this is as I found it. It seems early for this flower, as I've usually found it in May. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)
CommonNames: Cardinal Catchfly; Mexican Pink; Indian Pink

File.Name: g410e_g410-028.jpg
Notes: 26358902635 This is another specimen of the "mystery lupine" in our canyon, growing in a crack in the sandstone bedrock. See this photo. I have the notion that it might be the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the Fabaceae plant family? But that's just a guess, I'm not at all sure of the ID. The leaflets are short and fat and quite hairy, and the flowers are not at all whorled. The Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus) comes to mind, but this one doesn't seem quite hairy enough, the flowers are different, and the leaves are narrower - and this is not the desert. (At least not yet, but if the dought continues...) This web page may be relevant. If you have an idea about this, please let me know. So many lupines! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)
ScientificName: Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus

File.Name: g410f_g410-044.jpg
Notes:
26358371755 This is a little slice of life in our canyon, featuring lots of strap-like basal leaves of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. Yep, they look just like the basal leaves of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus), and the only way I know to tell them apart is to know ahead of time what they will become. See this photo for a sample of what they will look like next July - when the basal leaves will be long-since faded. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2016)

Other native plants in the photo are Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae), Coyote Mint (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) and our dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) at the bottom. There's also a shrub of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) on the right edge getting its first flower buds. This is a busy place!
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Fairy Lantern; Coast Live Oak; Chaparral Yucca; Coyote Mint; Roundfruit Sedge; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Calochortus weedii; Liliaceae; Calochortus albus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

Families: Liliaceae; Fagaceae; Agavaceae; Lamiaceae; Cyperaceae; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g411a_g411-027.jpg
Notes:
26289382532 This is a large caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata, Lepidoptera) working spent flowers of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica) in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. It's 3+ inches long with head on the right and horned tail on the left. I usually find these caterpillars on plants in the Onagraceae plant family, but I found several on these plants today. It's curious that (I think) I found this caterpillar in Death Valley this spring, see this photo. They get around! I haven't seen any adults in the air yet, but they're coming! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)

We were in and out of the clouds today, which made getting new photos tricky. The rain seems to be all gone. We ended up with 1.08 inches over the weekend, which bring our season total up to 20.37 inches. That's still just 65% of normal. We're still in a drought, but the woods look decent right now. This will definitely do the late-season wildflowers some good.
CommonNames: White-Lined Sphinx Moth; California Four O'Clock; Wishbone Bush
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Onagraceae

File.Name: g411b_g411-023.jpg
Notes: 26315356351 I'm pretty sure this is the adult "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that comes from the nymph in my [Previous] photo - Catorhintha apicalis in the family Coreidae, Pentatomomorpha. You can see that it has its proboscis in the glandular flower calyx of a native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica) in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. See BugGuide. I found many nymphs today, but only a few adults. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Catorhintha apicalis; Coreidae; Pentatomomorpha; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Catorhintha apicalis; Coreidae; Pentatomomorpha; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae

File.Name: g411c_g411-013.jpg
Notes: 25778372753 This is the handsome nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the glandular calyx of a native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica) in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. I'm sure it's the nymph of the adult in my [Next] photo - Catorhintha apicalis in the family Coreidae, Pentatomomorpha. Compare this photo at BugGuide. I found many of them when I looked close at the plants. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)

File.Name: g411d_g411-034.jpg
Notes: 26288537682 This is a beautiful new flower of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica) in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. It's still open after noon on the mostly cloudy day. Usually the flowers open in the afternoon around 4:00 pm - really! - and close before 10:00 am in the morning, but sometimes they get confused on cloudy days. Yeah, it's a bit over-exposed. I reckon that's because the sun was in and out of the clouds today. I sometimes kick up the exposure a bit in the clouds, and then forget to dial it down when the sun emerges. It's curious that I found the first flowers very early this season on 30 December 2015, see this photo. There are lots of them now. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)

File.Name: g411e_g411-049.jpg
Notes: 25775814604 These are the first new elegant flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family that I've found this year, growing along the road to Upper Oso. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. Very beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)

File.Name: g411f_g411-039.jpg
Notes: 26314292961 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's kind of a hybrid plant - part stand-alone shrub, but it sometimes has vining tendencies which you can see on that branch on the left. This is a personal favorite. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)

File.Name: g412a_g412-013.jpg
Notes:
25796875043 I found this mating pair of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) in the mint patch of the garden this morning. They are beautiful, like they are cast in bronze. It's interesting that the larger female (below) has her antennae out, but the smaller male (above) has his antennae tucked in along his body. Sure, my photo could be sharper, but it shows what it should - and no more! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

Today was kind of a funky weather day. It started sunny, but it seemed to turn gloomy the moment I stepped outside, with gray stratus clouds moving in. Then after an hour it cleared up for another hour, before turning cloudy again. I ended up staying at home in the garden today, and got some interesting photos.

File.Name: g412b_g412-018.jpg
Notes:
26306997572 This is a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) perched on a flower head of native (but weedy) Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine, Rubiaceae) that has become part of our flora. I got this photo in the mint patch of the garden today. Remarkably, both insect and tiny flowers are in reasonably sharp focus! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

I first had this plant down as non-native. That's what I've always assumed, but I checked today and it it is considered to be native. Well, we have native weeds too! A forest service report has this to say about Stickywilly - another common name for Galium aparine:

"The nativity of stickywilly is debated. While most accept this species as native, some consider it nonnative [163,171]. Still others suggest that stickywilly is a native, but that subsequent introductions have occurred as well [90]. In a literature review, it is suggested stickywilly arrived in the fur of animals crossing the Bering Strait into North America [28]. While debate regarding the nativity of stickywilly continues, it is in all likelihood native and is considered native in most literature [25,44,58,143]. For more discussion on the nativity of stickywilly see [62,90].

File.Name: g412c_g412-016.jpg
Notes: 26332941241 I found this small Bumblebee (Bombus, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) huddled on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden today. It's not much larger than a honey bee. The sun was still out, but clouds were moving in and it felt chilly. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

File.Name: g412d_g412-026.jpg
Notes: 26372922506 This sure looks at first like the slug-like caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. But I'm pretty sure it's the larva ("maggot") of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). Compare this photo at BugGuide. It looks backwards, but that's really its head at the bottom of my photo. I found it curled up in the mint patch today and actually turned it over to see if it had legs. No legs, though it sure seems to have prolegs - at least little stubs to walk with. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

File.Name: g412e_g412-009.jpg
Notes: 26332363981 This is a looking up at a fabulous glandular flower head of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flower heads seem unusually fat and full of flowers this year. I got this under the clouds today. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

File.Name: g412f_g412-002.jpg
Notes: 26306025402 Here are "almost flowers" on a large vine of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There are open flowers up higher, so I'll go ahead and count these as "first flowers". You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaves that the flowers are growing from. I was shooting up to capture these flowers that are growing from vines that cover a Scrub Oak near our house. Yep, this is the same vine I show in this photo. Today started sunny, but it seemed to turn gloomy the moment I stepped outside, with gray stratus clouds moving in. I got this under the clouds. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

File.Name: g413a_g413-003.jpg
Notes:
25813428634 This is the fabulous foliage of a vine of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There are no flowers yet, but that appears to be the grown-together "perfoliate" leaves that the flowers come from of top. Beautiful and elegant. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2016)

This will have to stand in for the rest of my photos today. Our internet connection has been flaky tonight - working just fine for a moment, then just disappearing. I'm tired of messing with it. I went to an 11:15 appointment in the Santa Ynez Valley today... and it turned out to be on FRIDAY not today. D'oh. By the time I got home, the wind was already kicking up. We're due for a proper wind event over the next two days, which makes my kind of photos impossible. I may be glad my uploads were cut short today!

File.Name: g413b_g410-010.jpg
Notes: 26392080076 On the right are typical flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Lamiaceae plant family, photo from a month ago near Lake Cachuma. It's a pretty little non-native weed that sometimes ventures into the woods. On the left is the same plant with little white cleistogamous flowers that never open. They do produce seeds. If you do a web search on "Lamium amplexicaule cleistogamy" you'll get lots of hits. This plant is well known for cleistogamy, producing small non-opening self-pollinating flowers. It's curious that these cleistogamous plants grow in the shade above the retaining wall in our garden. Maybe shade has something to do with it?
ScientificName: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; same plant; cleistogamous
associatedTaxa: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; same plant; cleistogamous

File.Name: g413c_g411-006.jpg
Notes:
25814444773 These are minimalist male pollen-producing staminate flowers of native Meadow Rue (Thalictrum fendleri var. polycarpum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The flowers lack petals, but not beauty. They make up for it with their wonderful foliage. This is a plant that is doing well in our garden, but they also grow nearby in Kinevan Canyon. They are tough plants to photograph, the slightest breeze gets the flowers all a'twitter. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2016)

Our internet connection has been flaky tonight. I'm not sure if I'll be able to post the rest of my photos tonight or not.

File.Name: g414a_g414-010.jpg
Notes:
26409145946 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp path in the garden today. It still looks fresh - you can still see some blue on its forewings, which wears away before too long. I dig those soccer ball eyes! This is actually the first one I've been able to capture with my camera this year. The new ones seem more photo-shy than the later ones. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2016)

It's been very windy today with 30 mph gusts, but the space near the ground isn't so bad. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning tonight and tomorrow morning with gusts of 60-75 mph. Curiously, the winds seem to be easing off a bit right now, but that could change at any moment. I have an 11:15 appointment in the Santa Ynez Valley tomorrow, which will cut into my time for new photos.

File.Name: g414b_g413-013.jpg
Notes: 26342649102 This is another late-instar nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It's tiny, and its tail makes it look jet propelled! Compare this photo. This is one of the photos I meant to post last night, but our DSL internet connection was too flaky. It seems back to normal tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: g414c_g414-014.jpg
Notes: 26408681396 Here are two photos of a tiny spider in the mint patch of the garden this morning. The photo on the left shows it on new bristly nutlets of native (but weedy) Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine, Rubiaceae). The photo on the right shows the same spider hanging on a strand of webbing. I reckon it's a male because of those enlarged palps. I never did get a look at it's upper side so I'll keep my guesses about what it is to myself. Those white eye-spots on its abdomen are striking. It's also a pretty good "butt shot" so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2016)

File.Name: g414d_g412-027.jpg
Notes: 26342046982 This is a mating pair of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) disappearing over the edge of a leaf in the mint patch of the garden - getting away from the intruding photographer! It's an intimate shot - maybe too intimate. I have another less revealing photo of the same mating beetles here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2016)

File.Name: g414e_g411-011.jpg
Notes: 25831443633 This is a "butt shot" of a tiny parasitic wasp (Hymenoptera) showing not a stinger but its ovipositor. This wasp is only about 1/8 inch long. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2016)

File.Name: g414f_g411-017.jpg
Notes: 26161312690 This is the horned butt of a large caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata, Lepidoptera) working spent flowers of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica) in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. I have another photo of the whole caterpillar here. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2016)
CommonNames: White-Lined Sphinx Moth; California Four O'Clock; Wishbone Bush
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae

File.Name: g415a_g415-010.jpg
Notes:
25847343564 I found this brilliant caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) on our upstairs deck railing this afternoon. No doubt it dropped down from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that overhangs the porch just a bit. It has clown colors, like a circus train coming to town! In case you're wondering, its head is on the left. It was upset and moving very fast. I had to take *lots* of photos to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2016)

I went to my 11:15 meeting today, and all is well. It was windy when I left home and still windy when I got home, but I managed to get a few photos. I have another midday duty tomorrow, but then I'll be clear for a few days. Wind, wind, wind - but it seems to be finally dying down. The 60-70 mph winds never did develop last night, but it's still been gusting over 20 mph today. I'll be glad when this wind event is over.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g415b_g415-009.jpg
Notes: 26451844915 This is a neat little adult Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. Maybe this is the same kind as the nymphs I've been finding recently - photos here and here. This little hopper has narrow wings, maybe genus Danepteryx? The narrow wings can't be much good for flying, but maybe they help it steer when they hop. This hopper is small - about 1100 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx

File.Name: g415c_g415-006.jpg
Notes: 25846728924 I found this small mushroom coming up in newly tilled soil in the garden today. I didn't collect it, but I reckon from the shape that it's genus Coprinus. On the other hand, I can't see any sign of black stained gills. It's interesting that despite recent rains, there have been (almost) no mushrooms. I guess their time is done. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2016)

File.Name: g415d_g414-005.jpg
Notes: 26425518446 This is a neat little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. It's rather dull colored for a jumper - maybe genus Habronatus? I don't know anything about it, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2016)

File.Name: g415e_g413-016.jpg
Notes: 26384969461 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) grooming itself on a new leaf in the mint patch of the garden. I guess it's genus Eupeodes - see the images at BugGuide. These flies spend a lot of time flying close over leaves, I guess looking for aphids to lay their eggs by? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2016)

File.Name: g415f_g414-002.jpg
Notes: 26425122526 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched for a moment in the mint patch of the garden. I don't know what kind it is, but it's a bristly little beast! It's really remarkable how many different kinds of flies are "just a fly". HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2016)

File.Name: g416a_g416-012.jpg
Notes:
26405544641 Here is a view of the brilliant upper wings of a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly that's "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil in the garden this morning. Yeah, it's a bit overexposed. The butterfly was perched with wings closed when I got the [Previous] photo. Then it suddenly opened its wings, and I got this photo with no time to adjust exposure. The butterfly looks fresh, you can still see some iridescence in its far wing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2016)

I had a midday duty in town today, but I had some time for photos in the morning. The big winds have died down, but there was still a breeze today. It seems pretty calm tonght, and maybe tomorrow will be a perfect photo day!

File.Name: g416b_g416-008.jpg
Notes: 26445614566 This is looking under the closed wings of a new Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly that's "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil in the garden this morning. The cryptic underwings are remarkable, like the marbled endpapers of fine old books. The butterflies are almost invisible with wings closed unless you happen to see one land. See my [Next] photo for the upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2016)

File.Name: g416c_g416-020.jpg
Notes: 26378881042 This threadbare butterfly is a veteran and deserves some respect. Judging from the remaining marks on its wings, I'm pretty sure it's an Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) aka Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. It's perched for just a moment on a mint leaf in the garden. I've been finding these butterflies since late January, see this photo, and this one may well date back to then. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2016)

File.Name: g416d_g416-014.jpg
Notes: 26404932511 I guess this is a male Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) under a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) - male because of those enlarged pedipalps. Yeah, it's not the best photo. The big winds have died down today, but it was still breezy. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2016)

File.Name: g416e_g416-002.jpg
Notes: 26378519602 This is a large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) sprawled across a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). We have giant ones and small ones, but this one is just large. I tried to get a closer photo of its remarkable face, but the breeze wouldn't cooperate. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2016)

File.Name: g416f_g415-002.jpg
Notes: 26197735050 Here is a little Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) exploring a leaf in the mint patch of the garden, looking for aphids I guess. They can have any number of spots, or no spots at all, but they always seem to have those two white lines on the upper-side of their thorax. *Sigh* These lady beetles used to converge by the thousands in our canyon, but I haven't seen masses of them here since the drought began in early 2012. (I did find a small gathering in a different canyon back in November 2012.) If I ever see the ladybugs again, I'll expect rain! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2016)

File.Name: g417a_g417-017.jpg
Notes:
26494008645 This is another "almost mating" pair of unusual "Timema" insects - no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical walkingstick insects that are quite "sexually dimorphic". That's the green female on the bottom and the much smaller male on top, looking exhausted. I can see that they're no longer engaged, but still in intimate contact. I found them on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), one of their preferred host plants. They were hanging vertically, but I tilted my camera almost 90 degrees to get more room on the screen. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are. They are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot. I rarely see them except during mating season (now), when they seem to become bolder. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

It was warm to day, in the low 80s on our upstairs deck. It was also pretty breezy. I reckon that's the earth breathing, and I hope that one of these days it will hold it breath! I have to do an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley tomorrow, but that will give me a chance to see what's happening near Lake Cachuma. There's always *something* to photograph!
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g417b_g417-021.jpg
Notes: 25890790813 This is a handsome caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I believe Alice Abelia managed to track this caterpillar down - Aseptis perfumosa, a Noctuidae moth, see BugGuide. See Alice's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Aseptis perfumosa; Noctuidae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Aseptis perfumosa; Noctuidae

File.Name: g417c_g417-042.jpg
Notes: 26401001032 I believe this is a California Orange-winged Grasshopper (Arphia ramona), the first I've seen this year. It's a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae of the Acrididae, Orthoptera. This one has blue tibia and brilliant orange wings when it took flight a moment later. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

File.Name: g417d_g417-023.jpg
Notes: 26466945566 This is an aphid-galled leaf of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) showing actual aphids on the gall and the husks of their last molt on the leaf above. I believe the tumor-like gall is caused by the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The specialized aphids mostly live inside the red pocket on the leaf, but they seem to come out to molt. Housekeeping? You can also see a tiny fly or wasp in the upper-left of the leaf, waiting for *something*. Part of why I love photographing galls is that my photos often show such ancillary activity. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: g417e_g417-038.jpg
Notes:
25889709633 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The shrub is distinctive for its narrow leaves which are quite unlike the more typical Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) which has broader leaves and is more common away from the coast, see this photo. It's endemic on the south slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains, mostly west of San Marcos Pass. It's not easy to get a photo that shows both flowers and narrow leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

The great botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen with narrow leaves from the coast in 1831. The inland variety with wide leaves is more common throughout the area, but the first named collection remains official. They really do look different - leaves, flowers, and berries. I'm not a botanist, but maybe this one deserves to be its own species?
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata

File.Name: g417f_g417-027.jpg
Notes:
26400056802 Here is another photo of the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills, but a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. This one is growing in a place that was cleared but not burned. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

Here is another photo of the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills, but a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. This one is growing in a place that was cleared but not burned. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

Hah - in past years, this plant has also been known as Thelypodium lasiophyllum and Guillenia lasiophylla. Those darned botanists!
ScientificName: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla
associatedTaxa: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla

File.Name: g418a_g418-021.jpg
Notes:
26514746955 These are pretty blue flowers of native Tuberous Skullcap (Scutellaria tuberosa) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I found this little mint in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. It's said to be a fire-follower, and this place did burn about 15 years ago. They are small plants, just 2 or 3 inches high, but very pretty. They look like an escaped garden ground cover plant, but they are native. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 April 2016)

I did both of my duties today - went to the Santa Ynez Valley and stopped by Lake Cachuma on the way to see what's happening in the woods. It was another warm day and also another breezy day. The breeze did seem to die down after I got home, so maybe tomorrow will be the fine day I've been waiting for?

File.Name: g418b_g418-011.jpg
Notes: 26422023212 Here are new flowers and lots of buds on a shrub of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's a stand-alone shrub, with vining tendencies. Compare this photo of the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) with narrow leaves that grows on the coastal side of the mountains. These small differences help keep local botany interesting. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 April 2016)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata

File.Name: g418c_g418-024.jpg
Notes: 26241187280 Here are two different plants of native Soap Plant aka Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae or Agavaceae plant family. Both plants are starting to get their tall asparagus-like flower stalks. No flowers yet, but they're getting close. The wavy leaves help distinguish it from Fremont's Star Lily (Zigadenus fremontii) when they are young, but by this time in their development, there's no question about what's what. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 April 2016)
CommonNames: Soap Plant; Amole; Fremont's Star Lily
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Zigadenus fremontii
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Zigadenus fremontii

File.Name: g418d_g418-029.jpg
Notes: 26421534302 This is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on a daisy flower in the garden today. Maybe it's just my imagination, but this one doesn't look so good - with dull eyes instead of the usual bright soccer balls. Maybe it's just the light, or maybe it's got a "bug" like I seem to have right now. It did let me get close without taking wing. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2016)

File.Name: g418e_g418-009.jpg
Notes:
26447483441 Dreams of freedom...

I found this fairly large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) tangled up (and blue) in a spider web this morning. It was pumping its wings furiously, to no avail. Sure, I broke the web. It was attached to my truck anyway, and would be broken as soon as I drove away. I have no idea if the fly got away. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2016)

File.Name: g418f_g418-001.jpg
Notes: 26447377211 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Is it ovipositing - laying eggs? It's not the best photo, but it's an interesting moment. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2016)

File.Name: g419a_g419-009.jpg
Notes:
25930596963 This beautiful greenish Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is guarding a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I guess the spider is Diaea livens, though it's not as green as they usually are, and it's not associated with Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's something else? I don't know what those dark spots are. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

I drove up East Camino Cielo road into the higher mountains today to see what I could find, and I found lots of new flowers! It was another warm day, though a notch cooler than yesterday. It was also quite windy, which is always frustrating. But it's interesting that the wind was from the south, the ocean side of our mountains, and there were increasing high clouds. This may be an early sign of our next maybe-storm, with a chance of rain on Friday.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Woolly Blue Curls; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Diaea livens; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Diaea livens; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g419b_g419-011.jpg
Notes:
25930425223 These are the first new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains. They look like one of those "Mexican Sages" in the garden, but these are native Californians. I've always wondered what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, and maybe I found it in this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

I'll call these "first flowers" since they are the first ones I've seen this year, but they've been going for a while. I just haven't gone up East Camino Cielo road into the mountains for some time.

File.Name: g419c_g419-040.jpg
Notes: 26533042565 Here are two photos of the locally uncommonWide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipes) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. I only know it from this one place in our mountains, but I seem to find it every year. This one is growing on a steep shaly bank, but the plant was down low where I could get to it. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

File.Name: g419d_g419-032.jpg
Notes:
26260038980 This is a tiny plant of native Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The whole plant isn't much more than an inch high! *Sigh* What five years of drought does to our natives, but this one is still soldiering on! Very beautiful - if you get down on your knees to see it. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

The seeds of this plant are nutritious and have a reputation for being an energy source. "Chia Seeds" are available commercially, but I'm sure they are not from this plant. See this page at Wikipedia.

File.Name: g419e_g419-037.jpg
Notes: 25927664854 Here is one of the first new flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family that I've found this year. You can see just one of its four real stamens above, and the non-fertile "staminode" at the bottom - which hangs out of the flower like a Rolling Stones tongue. That's the mysterious "fifth stamen" - which gives this "pen-stamen" family it's name. The flowers are surprising and beautiful, though they do an odd color shift when you try to photograph them, and end up looking more magenta than they do to the eye. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

File.Name: g419f_g419-017.jpg
Notes: 26506509566 These are new dreadlock-like staminate catkins and pistillate flowers of a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The erect catkins distinguishes it from true oaks in the genus Quercus. Already! But last year I found the first catkins on April 15 and the year before on May 5, so maybe they are right on schedule? I still think of these as June flowers, at least in our shady corner of the woods. It was windy again today - from the south, which is interesting. I took a lot of photos and selected this keeper. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

File.Name: g420a_g420-004.jpg
Notes:
25946576724 Here are two different early instars of caterpillars of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera). The one on the left is tiny, maybe 3/16 inch long, on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The one on the right is larger, but still less than 1/2 inch long on a spent flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). In time they will develop circus colors like this one, but it takes a few molts to get there. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2016)

*Sigh* There was a steady breeze again this morning that kept everything in motion. Neither of these photos was good enough to stand alone, but maybe they work together as a pair. At least they show that I tried today! The wind has been curious lately - breezy in the morning and calm in the evening, just the opposite of the usual pattern. It was another couple of degrees cooler today. Our chance of rain on Friday seems to have evaporated.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Wild Blackberry; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: g420b_g419-004.jpg
Notes: 26485049021 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), which have a more petioled abdomen than genus Polistes. It's a paper wasp, and these wasps build their nests under the eaves of our porch and roof. Yeah, they look threatening, but I've never been stung. This one is perched on a spent flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It seems to have an injury to its right front leg. Good luck little wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2016)
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Polistes; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

Notes: 25947895403 This is another shot of a mating pair of unusual "Timema" insects - no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical walkingstick insects that are quite "sexually dimorphic". That's the green female on the left and the much smaller male on the right. I found them on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), one of their preferred host plants. I also tilted my camera 90∞ to get this photo, but this shot is as I found them, hanging near vertical. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are. They are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot. I rarely see them except during mating season (now), when they seem to become bolder. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2016)

File.Name: g420d_g420-016.jpg
Notes: 26524647786 Here is the first new flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. (No relation to gillyweed in Harry Potter 4!) It's a summer plant that's just starting to flower and will hopefully continue until September or later. Last year, I found the first flowers on April 11, so it seems to be right on schedule. It's a minor flower, but I love these summer plants that keep the insects going all through our summer-dry season. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2016)

File.Name: g420e_g419-031.jpg
Notes: 26550354155 This is a new plant of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family, growing in front of a rock that's mostly covered with crustose lichens. It's a beautiful plant with fabulous foliage even when not in flower, but even better with flowers - see this photo from last summer. It already seems to be forming flower stalks, but I don't expect to see flowers until June. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

File.Name: g420f_g419-025.jpg
Notes: 25947279983 These are new flower buds and pinnate leaves of the locally uncommon native False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. The shrub is deciduous, and just starting out for the new year. The new leaves look droopy instead of sharp, but I guess that's how they are. Sure, this photo is more "interesting" than "beautiful", but it's the best shot I could get along ridge top in the steady breeze yesterday. This shrub is not common in our mountains, and it really looks more like it belongs in the desert. It occurs near the Santa Ynez River and on the mountain passes - Refugio and Arroyo Burro, but for some reason it skips San Marcos Pass. I find this shrub to be very interesting! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 April 2016)

File.Name: g421a_g421-036.jpg
Notes:
25963379804 Here is another shot of a striking little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a native shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The striped legs are remarkable. See my [Previous] photo. I couldn't decide between them, so I posted them both - but I put this one first. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2016)

Today was a very nice day - calm in the morning and breezy in the afternoon, as it should be. It was cooler, only up to about 70∞ today, but still quite pleasant. Maybe ma nature is just taking a breather, as the forecast is for it to be quite windy later weekend.

Notes: 26295121320 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a wasp perched on a spent flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I believe it's a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), which have a more petioled abdomen than genus Polistes. See this photo that I posted yesterday. HBBBT!
