File.Name: g421e_g421-026.jpg
Notes: 26567714155 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants look kind of weedy when you first notice them, like non-native Sow Thistle (Sonchus). But if you look closer, you see how elegant they are. I believe the flowers form a proper cyme inflorescence where the top flower opens first, followed by the lower flowers. They are a fire-follower that lingers in disturbed places, though this place was cleared but never burned. The flowers and buds are very similar to the Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana). Did you notice that tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying into the flower on the right? (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2016)
CommonNames: California Chicory; Sow Thistle; cyme; Desert Chicory; Wild Bee
ScientificName: Rafinesquia californica; Asteraceae; Sonchus; Rafinesquia neomexicana; Anthophila; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Rafinesquia californica; Asteraceae; Sonchus; Rafinesquia neomexicana; Anthophila; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g421f_g421-030.jpg
Notes: 26567565565 This is a stem gall on a new plant of native Leafy Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. This is the typical species plant with broad leaves, but we also have what has been called var. stenophyllus with narrow filiform leaves, see this photo. The variety gets the same galls. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2016)

File.Name: g422a_g422-049.jpg
Notes:
25983641453 This is a stunning Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing along the Santa Ynez River. I've been seeing them for a while, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. I also saw an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) and several Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterflies working the same flowers, but I couldn't get a photo. *I'll be back* (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)

It was a bit breezy today, but not too bad. It clouded up this afternoon, and we even had a few sprinkles. Then the wind kicked up and blew the clouds away. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it might be windy all weekend. I made a point of taking lots of photos today, to have a few extra just in case.
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Bicolored Yerba Santa; Anise Swallowtail; Gabb's Checkerspot
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Chlosyne gabbii
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Chlosyne gabbii

File.Name: g422b_g422-013.jpg
Notes: 26493853372 Hah! This is looking at the underwings of a lovely female Sara Orangetip aka Pacific Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly that is perched on the far side of this plant of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I'm sorry its head isn't visible, but it is a nice photo of the underside. I found the butterflies back in February, then they were gone for a while, so I guess this is their second brood. They don't like posing, and I mostly see them flying by. This photo is missing something, but it's still a lucky shot! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2016)
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Pacific Orangetip; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: g422c_g422-029.jpg
Notes: 25980913944 I noticed this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a flower head of native Chick Lupine (Lupinus microcarpus var. densiflorus) in the Fabaceae plant family, see my [Previous] photo. It's a bit over-exposed, but I like how you can see its leg attachment from underneath. I never did get a look at the back of the spider, so I have no idea what kind it is. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)

File.Name: g422d_g422-027.jpg
Notes:
26312660940 Beauty and the weeds...

This is a group of native Chick Lupine (Lupinus microcarpus var. densiflorus) in the Fabaceae plant family growing with non-native weedy grasses along Paradise Road, mostly Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus, Poacea). The crowded flowers of the lupine are stacked in whorls on the stem, varying from yellow to white. I used to think these lupines were all introduced, but I believe they just prefer roadsides. The grass is horrible stuff that lives up to its common name. Most of the summer "foxtails" that get stuck in your shoes and socks are really this plant, at least in our area. The seeds haven't started to get loose yet, but I have "weed gaiters" for when they do. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)
ScientificName: Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus; Fabaceae; Bromus diandrus; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus; Fabaceae; Bromus diandrus; Poacea

File.Name: g422e_g422-005.jpg
Notes: 26585527815 Here are brilliant new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, captured with the sun behind. You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that the flowers are growing from. I think this is a magical photo! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2016)

File.Name: g422f_g422-038.jpg
Notes: 26492757142 I found these new flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family growing by the river today, past the second river crossing in Paradise. The flowers are surprising and beautiful, though they do an odd color shift when you try to photograph them, and end up looking more magenta than they do to the eye. I found the first flowers a few days ago near Arroyo Burro Saddle in the mountains, but these flowers by the river seem to be on the same schedule. I only know them from a few places. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)

File.Name: g423a_g423-023.jpg
Notes:
26539519791 This is another cute little adult Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It has narrow wings, maybe genus Danepteryx? Those narrow wings can't be much good for flying, but maybe they help it steer when it hops. This hopper is small - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2016)

I was expecting big winds today, so it was a pleasant surprise when we just had normal breeze this morning. The winds did pick up in the afternoon. I hope the winds die down again tomorrow morning!
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx

File.Name: g423b_g423-021.jpg
Notes:
26001669293 This is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on a stem of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family today in the canyon. These butterflies really are variable. Compare this photo and look at the amount of orange trim. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2016)

Hah - it's kind of a naturalist-photographer's secret that these butterflies are no doubt the easiest of our butterflies to get close to and photograph. Pretty though! I also saw an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) and a single Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) in the canyon today. These are much harder to capture.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Wild Rose; Anise Swallowtail; Gabb's Checkerspot
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Chlosyne gabbii
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Chlosyne gabbii

File.Name: g423c_g423-015.jpg
Notes: 26001419393 This is another lovely female Sara Orangetip aka Pacific Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the canyon today. Yeah, it's too tight of a crop and a bit overexposed, but these sweet butterflies are hard to photograph. I found the butterflies back in February, then they were gone for a while, so I guess this is their second brood. They don't like posing, and I mostly see them flying by. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2016)
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Pacific Orangetip; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: g423d_g422-016.jpg
Notes: 25999015784 This is a neat little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) bent over backwards while munching a new leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). If you look close, you can see a good view of the semi-circle of eyes that true caterpillars have - as opposed to the simple eye-spots of sawfly larvae, compare this photo. I have no idea what kind of adult this one will become. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2016)

File.Name: g423e_g422-033.jpg
Notes: 26000895563 This is a large caterpillar of a White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans aka Eremothera boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for the plant. It's head is on the right, away from its typically horned butt on the left. These caterpillars seem quite variable. Compare this photo of the same kind of caterpillar on a different plant. I haven't seen an adult moth yet, but I'm sure they're coming. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)
CommonNames: White-Lined Sphinx Moth; Shredding Evening Primrose
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera; Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae

File.Name: g423f_g422-030.jpg
Notes: 26000766783 Here are new flowers of native Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans aka Eremothera boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. They are growing on a crumbly slope of Monterey Shale on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I think of it as a desert plant, but here they are happy on a south-facing slope by the river, growing in a place that was burned by the White Fire in May 2013. This is another host plant for the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata, Lepidoptera), see my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)
CommonNames: Shredding Evening Primrose; White-Lined Sphinx Moth
ScientificName: Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae; Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae; Hyles lineata; Lepidoptera

File.Name: g424a_g424-014.jpg
Notes:
26024404443 This is a handsome Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working a flower of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family in a weedy place on the edge of our parking area. (Sure - Miner's lettuce is native, but it's also weedy.) I was down on my hands and knees snapping photos without a chance to check exposure, and the flowers are a bit over-exposed. It's still a nice shot. See my [Previous] photo. Yep, this one is missing a good-sized chunk of its right wing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)

It was cloudy and breezy this morning with an annoying steady breeze that never stopped. By noon, it became sunny and breezy. Now in the evening, it's downright windy. *Sigh* that's a lot of wind for a wimpy dry storm front! We're under a NWS High Wind Advisory until Tuesday.
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae

File.Name: g424b_g424-013.jpg
Notes: 26354376560 This is a handsome Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working a flower of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family in a weedy place on the edge of our parking area. (Sure - Miner's lettuce is native, but it's also weedy.) I was down on my hands and knees snapping photos without a chance to check exposure, and the flowers are a bit over-exposed. It's still a nice shot. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae

File.Name: g424c_g424-005.jpg
Notes:
26627164925 I found this small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It can't be much more than 1/4 inch long. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. (See Alice's comment below.) I got the photo on the left this morning, when it was overcast and breezy. I took *many* photos to get this keeper, but it was worth it. I got the photo on the right later in the day, when it was sunny and breezy. (The breeze never stops!) In some ways, I prefer the photo on the left. It actually shows the body of the spider better. I didn't see any sign of a web. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)

It does kind of have a "cat face" like the much larger real Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) in this photo. But that one is much larger.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Wild Sweetpea; Cat-Faced Spider
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus gemmoides

File.Name: g424d_g424-004.jpg
Notes: 26561370811 This is a fairly large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) hanging from leaves of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) this morning. I didn't realize that something was wrong until I got the photo up on my computer screen. Look how it's abdomen is hanging by a thread. Mating stress? I guess this one is a female with that pointed abdomen. See this web page for some info on sexing crane flies. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)

File.Name: g424e_g423-003.jpg
Notes: 26020913584 Here are male pollen-producing staminate flowers of native Meadow Rue (Thalictrum fendleri var. polycarpum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The minimalist flowers lack petals but not beauty! This plant is doing well in our garden, but they also grow wild in nearby Kinevan Canyon. The flowers remind me of a soft spring rain. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2016)

File.Name: g424f_g423-001.jpg
Notes: 26561077751 These are new florets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing as a welcome volunteer in our garden. This native grass is a perennial with short awns, which distinguishes it from weedy annual bromes like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it, but it's surprising common in the woodland when you look for it. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2016)
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: g425a_g425-001.jpg
Notes:
26045639703 I found this handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a dead leaf in the mint patch of the garden, down low where the wind wasn't so bad. One good thing about chilly mornings is that the insects aren't inclined to move, so you can get close. This one stayed on its perch for some time, allowing me to get a photo. I guess it is genus Eupeodes - see the images at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2016)

What an odd weather day we had today! It started unseasonably cold, just 39∞ on the upstairs porch when I got up this morning. And its been very windy all night and day - which made it feel even colder. It was windy enough to knock out the power for a few hours last night, over 35 mph by the NWS San Marcos Pass weather station. And then in the gale this afternoon, it clouded up and started to rain a bit! We actually got about 0.02 inch, enough to measure and get the porch wet. There was nothing about this in the forecast. Now we're under a NWS High Wind Warning until early tomorrow morning, which means possible gusts up to 60 mph. Wow, this is a lot of air going somewhere in a hurry!

File.Name: g425b_g425-003.jpg
Notes: 26583383131 I found this little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) hunkered down in a new flower bud of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. Hah - the cacti were one of the few plants that wasn't rock 'n rolling in the wind this morning! I have no idea what kind of caterpillar it is, but I did my duty trying to get new photos on a very windy day. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2016)

File.Name: g425c_g425-008.jpg
Notes: 26042924584 Here is a new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family, the first one I've found in the mountains this year. This one was growing with the weeds by our mailbox, but I also noticed two more as I drove down our road. I guess their time has come! They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily, see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2016)

File.Name: g425d_g422-026.jpg
Notes: 26042674234 This is looking into the heart of a beautiful Mariposa Lily (Calochortus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I assumed at first that it's a Catalina Mariposa Lily (C. catalinae) since those are the ones I usually find first, but I'm pretty sure this is actually the similar Butterfly Mariposa Lily (C. venustus). D'oh, I didn't pay attention to the shape of the nectaries, but I think those extra maroon spots above the nectary glands give it away. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2016)
CommonNames: Mariposa Lily; Catalina Mariposa Lily; Butterfly Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus; Liliaceae; C. catalinae; C. venustus
associatedTaxa: Calochortus; Liliaceae; C. catalinae; C. venustus

File.Name: g425e_g422-053.jpg
Notes:
26044457033 Farewell to spring... say it isn't so!

Here are two different Farewell-to-Spring flowers in the Onagraceae plant family that I've found in the woods lately. On the left is native Winecup Clarkia (Clarkia purpurea ssp. quadrivulnera) growing in a place on San Marcos Pass. I also found it flowering by our mailbox today, but it was too windy for a decent photo. The flower on the right is native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides), a more shy flower growing on a shady bank along the river in Paradise. Both flowers are harbingers of the end of spring. It feels that way once the weedy grasses start to release their nasty seeds that get stuck in your socks and shoes. I haven't experienced that yet this year, but I reckon I'm about to. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2016)
ScientificName: Onagraceae; Clarkia purpurea; quadrivulnera; Clarkia epilobioides
associatedTaxa: Onagraceae; Clarkia purpurea; quadrivulnera; Clarkia epilobioides

File.Name: g425f_g421-013.jpg
Notes:
26621148196 This is an odd one that still has me confused. It looks like a perennial dandelion and grows like a weed along West Camino Cielo Road, though I also found it on the Zaca Fire burn a few years ago. I've had the names Malacothrix phaeocarpa or Malacothrix clevelandii in the Asteraceae suggested to me, but it doesn't look like any photos of those on the web. It still looks like a weedy dandelion to me, and I doubt that it's native. Here it is in its typical habitat with weedy rip-gut and foxtail grasses. If you know about this one, please leave a comment! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2016)

Update - Jeffrey Caldwell may have nailed this one - the Weedy Hawksbeard (Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia), introduced from Europe. It's interesting that almost all the observations at CalFlora are from the Bay area, and none south of Monterey county, so it's interesting to find it in the Santa Barbara mountains. At least I don't see it listed as a horribly invasive weed.
ScientificName: Malacothrix phaeocarpa; Malacothrix clevelandii; Asteraceae; Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix phaeocarpa; Malacothrix clevelandii; Asteraceae; Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia

File.Name: g426a_g426-045.jpg
Notes:
26064068773 I found this neat little "Timema" insect on my shirt sleeve after my walk in the woods today. I placed it on the beat-up side of my truck for a photo. I'm sure it's Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical chubby walkingstick insects, which I've mostly been finding in shrubs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), see this photo. I guess I was unintentionally "beating" the shrubs as I walked by. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are. They are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot in the wild. It helps when they hitchhike on your shirt! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)

Today was actually a very nice day - after the high winds and even rain yesterday, we had a calm morning. We used to have a chance of rain tomorrow, but that forecast seems to have turned into more wind, wind, wind. *Sigh* We'll see what happens. I got extra photos today just in case.
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g426b_g426-016.jpg
Notes: 26062204484 This photo shows a little Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a still-new flower of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The flowers do an interesting dance as they mature. Look at that central pedestal of stamens and pistils. The flowers start with stamens low and pistils up high, like this photo. Then as the stamens mature, they rise up to the center and the pistils become low - like my [Previous] photo. I reckon this is to give insects a chance to pick up pollen and then deposit it. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetle; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: g426c_g426-029.jpg
Notes: 26574426572 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) on no-longer-new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The flowers do an interesting dance as they mature. Look at that central pedestal of stamens and pistils. The flowers start with stamens low and pistils up high, like my [Next] photo. Then as the stamens mature, they rise up to the center and the pistils become low - like these flowers. I reckon this is to give insects a chance to pick up pollen and then deposit it. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: g426d_g426-008.jpg
Notes: 26640673386 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) with full pollen baskets on a new flower of the "mystery plant" I show in this photo. Thanks to Jeffrey Caldwell, I now believe the plant is the Weedy Hawksbeard (Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia), introduced from Europe. It's interesting that almost all the observations at CalFlora are from the Bay area, with one outlier in Humboldt county, and none south of Monterey county, so it's interesting to find it here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I don't see it listed as a horribly invasive weed, but I am concerned as it seems to be spreading. I've been seeing it along West Camino Cielo road, and today I also noticed it along East Camino Cielo road. I figure it's a weed to watch. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Crepis vesicaria ssp. taraxacifolia

File.Name: g426e_g426-034.jpg
Notes: 26393626350 Here are cheerful new flowers of the Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It commonly grows as a fire-follower, but this plant is growing along West Camino Cielo road. This place did burn in the Gap Fire back in 2008, but I reckon these plants are just lingering in a disturbed place. The flowers are large and quite beautiful, though individual flowers only last a day or two. The "scorpioid inflorescence" of these flowers - coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a violin - is striking. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, fading to seed pods behind. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)

File.Name: g426f_g426-035.jpg
Notes: 26062785603 This is a whole flowering plant of native Phlox-leaved Bedstraw (Galium andrewsii) in the Rubiacae plant family. The 4-merous flowers are really tiny, a mm across or less. It's a real "suffrutescent perennial" that forms low mounds in the woods. It's one of my personal favorite native plants, as is the whole genus. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)

File.Name: g427a_g427-028.jpg
Notes:
26686595415 This is a brilliant Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly hanging on to a Mallow (Malvaceae) flower in the garden in the wind today. I only had time for a few quick shots before it took off, and this was the best I got. It's not the best photo, but the wind was whipping the flowers back and forth, and this is a record shot anyway. I've seen a few this year, but this is the first one I've been able to get a photo of. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on its forewings runs along the wing rather than across it, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

*Sigh* It's been another very windy day, with gusts up to 40 mph as measured by the NWS San Marcos Pass weather station. Hah - just try to get a sharp photo when holding a flower out of a car window at 40 mph! I think I did pretty good today, considering. Darn, our NWS Wind Advisory goes on until tomorrow night. It's been a windy month.

File.Name: g427b_g427-031.jpg
Notes: 26080424934 This is an already tattered Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp garden path today. This is one of only two duskywings in our area with a white trailing edge to its hindwings, though it's almost worn away in this photo. Not the best photo, but this is a record shot - the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g427c_g427-018.jpg
Notes: 26593373132 This is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp garden path today. It still looks fresh - you can still see some blue on its forewings, which wears away before too long. This is an easy butterfly to photograph on the ground, even on a windy day - but it sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g427d_g427-002.jpg
Notes: 26412678530 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning, before the wind kicked up too much. Maybe it's a female Thiodina hespera with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes? It's almost translucent. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g427e_g427-010.jpg
Notes: 26685689205 This is a brilliant little deep red Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae, Hymenoptera) - "Ichneumonid" at least! Maybe it's subfamily Cryptinae? I found it this morning on foliage of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). This is a "parasitic wasp" that uses it's long ovipositor to inject its eggs into other critters where they will mature. This was a lucky shot on a breezy morning. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

associatedTaxa: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Cryptinae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g427f_g426-027.jpg
Notes: 26081518603 Here are the first open flowers of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family that I've found this year. These plants along West Camino Cielo road only grow a foot or two tall, so I guess they are the the real S. mollis. Our plants at home are more like S. albus at 2-4 feet tall. There's also a mating pair of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) visible on the right, and another one in the flower above it. Pretty obscure, but HIHD anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus; Coleoptera

File.Name: g428a_g428-001.jpg
Notes:
26677034126 Here is another Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on our retaining wall this morning. It has blue mating colors - and Ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida)! I guess the "sweet spot" (for ticks) is the lizard's ear tympanum, down from it's eyes. But there's another "sweet spot" down lower that I don't understand. Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that usually get me in the summer, though I've already been "ticked" a few times this year. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2016)

Today was another windy day, though not quite as bad as it's been - the gusts were only about 30 mph, not the 40+ mph winds we had yesterday! I'm tired of the wind. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory until 3:00 am tonight, but it does seem to be settling down right now. In addition to the wind, it's been unseasonably chilly, only about 40∞ when I get up in the morning. Maybe tomorrow will be better, but I have an early afternoon errand in town that will interfere with new photos. I'll do what I can.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Ticks

File.Name: g428b_g427-005.jpg
Notes: 26676928286 Here are two more shots of the tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden yesterday. I believe it's a female Thiodina hespera with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes. It's almost translucent. Isn't it interesting how its front eyes seem to change color as the angle of the light changes. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g428c_g428-007.jpg
Notes: 26099309363 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on leaf in the mint patch of the garden today. I suspect it's more of an ovipositor shot. This little fly looks a bit like this photo, but it's smaller and holds its wing together instead of out like a jet plane. We seem to have many kinds of syrphid flies. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2016)

File.Name: g428d_g426-014.jpg
Notes: 26676339196 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of an uncommon "Timema" insect - no doubt a female Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical chubby walkingstick insects. See this photo for the whole insect. I found this one on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), which seems to be their preferred host plant in our mountains. Maybe she's advertising for a mate, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2016)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g428e_g424-001.jpg
Notes: 26636844571 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a mint leaf in the garden a few days ago. I guess the beetle was trying to hide head-down on the leaf, but the sneaky photographer found it anyway. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)

File.Name: g428f_g424-027.jpg
Notes: 26096914304 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on leaf in the mint patch of the garden a few days ago. I suspect it's a lady who's ovipositing. We seem to have many kinds of the flies, with different patterns on their abdomens. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2016)

File.Name: g429a_g429-006.jpg
Notes:
26627623362 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. Those emerald eyes... The eyes actually change color from green to red to black depending on the angle of the light. This spider really is tiny. Its head is only about 400 pixels across in the 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1.6 mm - about 1/16 inch! (See here for how I figured.) Despite being so tiny, it's perfectly formed. The hairs under the mint leaf are pretty cool too. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2016)

I had an early afternoon errand in town today that interfered with new photos. I got home late, so just four photos today. Our big winds died down this morning, though there was still a steady breeze to make getting new photos a challenge. Then the winds kicked up again in the afternoon, and we're under another NWS Wind Advisory until early tomorrow morning. It's been a windy month.

File.Name: g429b_g429-001.jpg
Notes:
26114383704 The caterpillar and the ant...

Actually, there's not much of a story. The ant took one look (or taste) of the caterpillar and scooted off the other way. The caterpillar is an early instar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It's small, only about 450 pixels across in this 1:1 photo, a bit over 1/4 inch long. I don't know what the ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) is, but it didn't much like the caterpillar! It was a lucky shot to get both in almost decent focus. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2016)

File.Name: g429c_g428-005.jpg
Notes: 26720355335 I'm pretty sure this is a Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera, probably genus Rhagio. I found it perched for a moment in the mint patch of our garden yesterday morning. My second guess is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, but this one is less hairy and has more filiform antennae. (I have a history of confusing these genera.) It seems to be missing a piece of its left rear leg. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2016)

File.Name: g429d_g427-023.jpg
Notes: 26116667753 I found this dusty Fly (Diptera) on a garden path. I'm never sure, but I suspect it's a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). Along with all the bristles, it has a very hairy head. It was gone a moment later, and I don't know anything else about it. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g430a_g430-005.jpg
Notes:
26646809922 My son found this large Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the dry woods today! I guess it should more properly called Cantharellus californicus now. It's a large mushroom, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. This one felt light and was very pale, so we decided against collecting it for the table, but they are one of our best edible mushrooms. The real question is what is it doing out now?? I've found them early and late - but never *this late* after all other mushrooms are done. I haven't found them in our woods since March of 1993. They don't take well to drought. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

Our internet connection has been terrible tonight. It's frustrating because I don't know if it's our ISP (Frontier/Verizon) or my router at home. I'm giving up trying to upload my last two photos for today. I'll try again tomorrow.

File.Name: g430b_g430-002.jpg
Notes: 26673245691 I found this ornate moth (Lepidoptera) perched on last year's fruit in the cactus patch of the garden today. I guess it's in the family Noctuidae, maybe genus Syngrapha which have the right sort of bumps on their backs. This photo should have been better, but the focus plane is off. Well, it's pretty awkward setting up my tripod in the spiky cactus patch! You can still see the moth. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

File.Name: g430c_g430-008.jpg
Notes: 26466638820 I found this neat little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) walking across an old orange tarp covering a woodpile in the woods today. It's actually not-so-little, maybe an inch long or a bit more. It has a pretty "blue dun" color, like a trout fly. I have no idea what kind of moth (probably) or butterfly it will become. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

File.Name: g430d_g430-016.jpg
Notes: 26133255424 Here are elegant new flower buds of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family at home. There are no flowers here yet, but they're getting close. See this photo for an open flower near our mailbox in a sunnier place. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

File.Name: g501a_g501-012.jpg
Notes:
26158773303 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on a non-native plant in the garden today. She might be ovipositing - laying eggs - see my [Previous] photo of the same butterfly on the same plant. She has just two large eyespots below on her hindwings, but four or five smaller ones above. The butterflies almost always have a small white dot in the first orange cell on the rear of her forewing. The first colored cell on the front edge of her forewing is typically white, but more orange in this one. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2016)

These butterflies typically use Everlasting (Gnaphalium) Asteraceae plant family as their host plant. I believe this garden plant plant does has gnaphalium connections. It's only flowered once in all the years we've had it, but I remember the everlasting-type flowers that it had. The plant may be the Silver-Bush Everlasting Flower (Helichrysum petiolare), which is considered to be an invasive plant by Cal-IPC. Julie has had to hack it back every few years, so it does have a tendency to spread. It hasn't flowered in a decade, so I'm not worried about seeds. Anyway, it's interesting that IF this is the plant, THEN the butterfly is a pretty good botanist to find it as a substitute for gnaphalium!

Just four photos again today, as our internet connection is still being cranky. I had to reset the router twice just to get these photos up! The weather was cranky too. There was no wind in the forecast, but it turned out to be quite breezy all day. It felt like the "fog wind" that forms at the top of the marine layer fog - but there was no fog. Maybe there was still a temperature inversion boundary?
CommonNames: American Lady; Everlasting; Silver-Bush Everlasting Flower
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Asteraceae; Helichrysum petiolare
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Asteraceae; Helichrysum petiolare

26158052553 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a non-native plant in the garden today. The first colored cell on the front edge of her forewing is more interesting. That cell is typically white, but more orange in this one. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2016)

File.Name: g501c_g430-013.jpg
Notes: 26761246815 This is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp path in the garden yesterday. This is the first of these beautiful butterflies I've managed to get a photo of this year. It still has some blue on the leading edge of it's forewings, which means it's fairly new. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

File.Name: g501d_g430-014.jpg
Notes: 26667702382 Here are two photos of the same Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp path in the garden. The difference between their upper and lower wings is remarkable. They almost disappear among the fallen oak leaves when their wings are closed. This is the first of these butterflies I've managed to get a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2016)

File.Name: g502a_g502-039.jpg
Notes:
26510345840 In the center is the first open succulent flower I've seen this year of Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. The flowers have a wonderful waxy look unlike any others, very beautiful. These flowers are coming up next to a yucca plant, visible on the right. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 May 2016)

It was breezy again this morning, so I went up East Camino Cielo road into the higher mountains to see what was happening. Sure enough, there was a marine layer fog bank coming up to about 1800 feet in the mountains, with a layer of turbulent air above it. I guess these were the same winds we had yesterday. Once I got above the turbulence, it was pretty calm. I guess the time of "June gloom" weather is coming.

File.Name: g502b_g502-043.jpg
Notes: 26510055630 I was surprised to find this flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today, but here it is. Usually these are the last of our native thistles to appear, but this year they are the first. Hah, I just checked my records, and discovered that I found them last year on April 23. How soon we forget! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 May 2016)
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle

File.Name: g502c_g502-029.jpg
Notes: 26509899180 These are new flowers of the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family - now known as Camissoniopsis hirtella . I think of it as another fire-follower, but this one is growing with the weedy grasses in a clearing. The flowers open from the end and turn to seeds lower down the stem. They will keep coming until the plant runs out of steam. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 May 2016)

File.Name: g502d_g502-031.jpg
Notes: 26178553313 This is a new flower stalk of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family, with fabulous foliage and a top-knot of flower buds - already showing a hint of scarlet color. I don't expect to see the brilliant flowers until next month, but they seem early this year. I have a photo of last year's June flowers here. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 May 2016)

File.Name: g502e_g502-011.jpg
Notes: 26178216233 I believe these are new florets of native Purple Needle Grass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family. Hah, they're growing against a background of native Soap Plant aka Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae or Agavaceae plant family, it's an interesting place! At least I assume this is Stipa pulchra, but it might also be native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua) which has "twisted awns". You need a hand lens to separate the two, and I didn't have mine with me. It's a beautiful native grass. The summer hills would look different with this grass instead of the annuals - more of a soft green than the gold we are used to, and no *@%$ foxtails to stick in your shoes! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2016)
CommonNames: Purple Needle Grass; Soap Plant; Amole; Nodding Needle Grass
ScientificName: Stipa pulchra; Nasella pulchra; Poaceae; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Stipa pulchra; Stipa cernua

File.Name: g502f_g502-002.jpg
Notes: 26509208880 These are new florets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing at home. This native grass is a perennial with short awns, which distinguishes it from all the weedy annual bromes like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it, but it's surprising common in the woodland when you look for it. It seems to be strictly a woodland grass, growing under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2016)
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: g503a_g503-003.jpg
Notes:
26735822671 We had a hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning. They were emerging from the far edge of our parking space, 20 feet or so away from the house near the base of a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, so I'm not too worried. Maybe they are the Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae)? There were several places where the termites were emerging. The birds and lizards were enjoying all the termites they could find, but I don't think they ever found the mother lode like I did! It's curious... I usually expect a termite hatch after a rain, but it's been dry lately. There is a chance of rain for a few days staring tomorrow night. Are they jumping the gun? (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

Today was a pretty nice day, though high clouds started to build up. I had to keep changing my white-balance from sunny to cloudy. There was some breeze.
CommonNames: Termites; Coast Live Oak; Subterranean Termite

File.Name: g503b_g503-022.jpg
Notes: 26709330432 Here is a beautiful female Blue (Polyommatinae, Lycaenidae) butterfly perched for just a moment on one of the last flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the canyon today. At least it's one of the last flowers on this plant, other plants are still going. I did get a glimpse of the underwings of this butterfly, and I suspect that it's an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon), the first one I've managed to photograph this year. It's a tight crop, but still very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g503c_g503-031.jpg
Notes: 26530103160 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched for just a moment on a long-awned floret of weedy annual Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus or kin, Poaceae) in the woods today. This is the first one that I've managed to photograph this year. Compare this photo of the very different short-awned California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g503d_g503-025.jpg
Notes: 26801815455 Here is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that I can't identify in the deep throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. It seems to be doing *something* with it's mouthparts, which I don't really understand. If you look close, you can see that the stigma of the flower is closed, no doubt because it's been bumped. Compare this photo showing the open stigma. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g503e_g503-020.jpg
Notes: 26734933961 This is a mass of Parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) pupal cases on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I believe this is the work of Braconid Wasps in the family Braconidae. You can see that some of the pupal cases have opened. I'm willing to wager there's the husk of an unfortunate caterpillar under there. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g503f_g503-010.jpg
Notes: 26529619150 This is a stem gall on a new plant of native Leafy Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. This is what has been called var. stenophyllus with narrow filiform leaves. Compare this photo of the same gall on the the typical species plant with broad leaves. I guess the gall-inducing insects know their plants, whatever the shape of the leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g504a_g504-019.jpg
Notes:
26790024876 It's already the month of May, but I believe this is a March Fly (Bibionidae, Diptera) sprawled across a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - with drizzle drops. It's a fairly large (1/2 inch?) fly which suggests the species Bibio albipennis, though this one doesn't seem to have white hairs on its body. If I'm right on the ID, this is the first March Fly I've seen since the California drought began. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

We were in a cloud today - chilly, drizzly, and breezy. Despite being a crummy day for new photos, I managed to get a few - thanks to taking many shots at each opportunity, and then picking out the few keepers. One good thing about these gloomy mornings is that most insects are grounded, so you can photograph them IF you can find them. We have a few days of "unsettled weather" coming up, and we might even get some late-season rain!
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: g504b_g504-008.jpg
Notes: 26218479024 I'm pretty sure this is another Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera, probably genus Rhagio - with drizzle drops. I found it perched among the glochids (small spines) on a small "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. My second guess would be a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, but this one is less hairy and has more filiform antennae. (I have a history of confusing these genera.) It's a handsome little fly that's grounded by the chilly and drizzly weather this morning. That's a tip to look for insects on these funky mornings before they get loose! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

Notes: 26729808452 This is a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) with remarkably long antennae perched on a half-eaten leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I wonder if the bug did the damage? It seems to have four-segmented antennae - which is a clue. Chris Mallory suggest genus Phytocoris in the family Miridae in his comment below, and that sure looks right. Follow his link to Alice's fine photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

File.Name: g504d_g504-016.jpg
Notes: 26218775603 This is looking *up* at a little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched head-down on a native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) plant growing above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden - with drizzle drops. You can see how its beak folds into a groove on the bottom of its thorax. I tried to bend down the plant stem for a photo, but the bug fell away into the void. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

File.Name: g504e_g504-018.jpg
Notes: 26729235622 Here are two shots of (I believe) small Orb-Weaver Spiders (Araneus, Araneidae). The one on the right is under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) about twenty feet up, taken from our upstairs porch. I believe it is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata). I don't know what the one on the left is, but it's definitely a male, on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It was drizzly and breezy this morning. I did my best for new photos. Neither photo seemed quite ready for primetime, but maybe they are OK together like this. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

File.Name: g504f_g504-007.jpg
Notes: 26550326560 This is a new glandular flower of native Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The pretty composite flowers usually close early in the morning before I get out, so I rarely see them - but they stay open on gloomy days like this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

File.Name: g505a_g505-001.jpg
Notes:
26748917772 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a mint leaf in the garden this morning. I reckon those tubes on its butt are spinnerets. These spiders don't make proper webs, but I've seen that they do cast a safety line sometimes when they jump. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2016)

It was a pretty crummy day for new photos today, dark and chilly and breezy all day. I did get a few photos, but I really had to work at it. We have a few days of "unsettled weather" coming up. The porch was wet when I got up this morning, and we had a few raindrops this afternoon, but nothing measurable. We do have a 50% chance of showers tomorrow.

File.Name: g505b_g505-004.jpg
Notes: 26238110683 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of the small nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the garden this morning. The nymph seems to have turn signals on its rear end. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2016)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: g505c_g427-013.jpg
Notes: 26842276685 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) grooming its abdomen on leaf in the mint patch of the garden. I believe this is the same fly that this photo shows. The fly is small and holds its wing together at rest instead of out like a jet plane. We seem to have many kinds of syrphid flies. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2016)

File.Name: g505d_g505-003.jpg
Notes:
26748278762 This is a tiny aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - but it's brown and swollen beyond its normal size. I believe this one has been parasitized by a "parasitic wasp" - maybe a Braconid Wasp in the family Braconidae of the Hymenoptera. You can see its destiny in this photo. This one is close to home, so I'll keep an eye on it and try to get an "after" photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2016)

It's curious... I've found a few of these parasitized aphids in plain view over the years. I wonder if the parasite causes a change in behavior that makes the aphid walk out onto the leaf to die?
ScientificName: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Braconidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g505e_g503-019.jpg
Notes: 26236489494 I found this handsome nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) perched on a dead stem in the woods. Maybe it's a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes)? (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g505f_g503-023.jpg
Notes: 26807576186 This is the first new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family that I've seen this year, in our canyon at home. The flowers of this shrub are a butterfly favorite later in the season, see this photo. These plants have taken a real hit during our drought years. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2016)

File.Name: g506a_g506-018.jpg
Notes:
26587889010 This is another shot of a mushroom-like spring bisexual gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - in the light rain today. Compare this photo of the same gall taken a month ago, and look how it has changed color from reddish to greenish. I took *many* photos this afternoon to get this one keeper. It still isn't the best photo, but I worked for it and here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2016)

This is a "bisexual gall" because it will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls, see this photo, which only contain female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that!

Today was another crummy day for new photos, dark and chilly and breezy all day, with a bit of rain in the afternoon. I did get a few photos, but I really had to work at it. We've had 1/10 inch of rain so far, but I feel cheated. The Trout Club is just a few miles down the highway towards town, and they've had 1/2 inch. Figueroa Mountain across the valley from us has has 1-1/4 inch! It seems that we're stuck in the middle again! Maybe tonight or tomorrow...
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g506b_g506-001.jpg
Notes: 26255062504 Here is a handsome caterpillar (Lepidoptera) coiled up like the "worm ouroboros" - the serpent eating it's own tail that encompasses the world in myth. This one isn't quite eating its tail, but it's coiled up tight on these leaflets of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I first found this caterpillar on a different leaf, but it dropped away when I tried to nudge it in to a better position for a photo. I actually found it on the ground, and moved it to this different leaf, still coiled up in a ball. It did uncoil and move away, but the motion was too much to capture in the low light today. This one is fairly large, maybe 1-1/2 inches long. If I had to guess, I'd say it was family Noctuidae. We have nesting House Wrens on the upstairs porch right now that are feeding their babies. I can watch them coming in with their food, every so often one arrives with one of these fat green caterpillars! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2016)

File.Name: g506c_g506-013.jpg
Notes: 26587267060 I believe this is another March Fly (Bibionidae, Diptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's a fairly large (1/2 inch?) fly which suggests the species Bibio albipennis, though this one doesn't seem to have white hairs on its body. Yeah, it's not a very good photo, but it's the best I could do on this funky weather day that was dark, overcast, and breezy. I have a slightly better photo of the same kind of fly here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2016)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: g506d_g504-004.jpg
Notes: 26860005565 This is a "face shot" of a fairly large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) hanging on a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I couldn't get a shot of the whole fly because other cacti were in the way, and I've learned the hard way not to mess with them! Their faces always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite, perhaps as drawn by Moebius or Jean-Claude MÈziËres. These flies come in different sizes like t-shirts - small, medium, large, and extra-large. I reckon this one is a large size. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2016)

File.Name: g506e_g506-009.jpg
Notes: 26254295244 Here are the first new flowers of native Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family growing along the road in our canyon. The whorled flowers look like lupines at first, but they are really quite different. The flowers are highly variable - the color varies from deep violet (like these) to pure white. Some plants are quite pale, almost another species. They get their name from the way whorls stack up, but you can't see that in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2016)

File.Name: g506f_g506-006.jpg
Notes: 26792014521 This is the first new flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains this year, though I have seen a few along the river in Paradise. No, I don't understand the common name. Last year I found the first flowers on May 8, so I guess they are right on schedule. This isn't the best photo - none of my photos today is with the funky weather we've been having - but it documents the occasion. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2016)

File.Name: g507a_g507-036.jpg
Notes:
26812765481 This is a perfectly-posed West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly trying to warm up on a path in our garden this afternoon. This is the smallest of our Lady butterflies, and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristic is the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing, though the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies in our mountains have a nearly orange bar in that place, compare this photo. There are other differences as well, but I think size usually is enough. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

Today was another pretty crummy day for new photos, dark and chilly and breezy most of day, though there were moments of sunshine. There was no more rain, though there's still a 30% chance of a shower before midnight. I've been grounded this week with my truck in the shop, but it hardly matters. Our garden is the place I'd most like to be during funky weather. I know it well, and can recognize anything unexpected!

File.Name: g507b_g507-027.jpg
Notes: 26607245250 Here is a handsome little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly - surely an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's perched for a moment on a fading leaf in the mint patch of the garden with its proboscis out. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

File.Name: g507c_g507-005.jpg
Notes: 26786413302 This is a tiny wasp gall on a fallen staminate catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, see this photo for the fresh galls. There's an exit hole on the left side of the gall where the wasp chewed its way out. The exit hole is about 220 pixels high in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.86 mm, about 1/32 inch - and that's for the adult wasp! (See here for how I figured.) I believe these are the spring galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, which are making different summer galls right now. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: g507d_g507-007.jpg
Notes: 26879633935 Here are two shots of the first flower I've seen this year on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. The flowers seem simple at first, with just two stamens and a single blue pistil held off to the side. But they are actually complex, and one of my favorites because they do something. The flower has a sharp bend at the base to keep most insects away from the nectar inside. Only large heavy bees like Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) can bring down the fulcrum of the flower, see this photo and this one. Once the large bees "unlock" the flower, other bees can get in. I look forward to many more flowers are they continue opening. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g507e_g507-019.jpg
Notes: 26606246530 This is a small insect, but a rather large ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera). It's working a new flower of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The ant may well be a Carpenter Ant in the genus Camponotus. They seem to like these flowers, I've seen them here before. At least the flowers get them away from the foundation of our house! (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Camponotus

File.Name: g507f_g507-021.jpg
Notes: 26273552414 Here are the first new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, too high for me to get close to with my tripod, but see my [Next] photo. This shrub is on the edge of our garden, but I suspect other shrubs in the canyon are starting to flower as well. These aren't the sort of flowers you would give your mom for Mother's Day, but they are important flowers for many insects. I got this photo during a rare moment of sunshine today! The sun was in and out of the clouds, which made white balance something to think about. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

File.Name: g508a_g508-012.jpg
Notes:
26298646013 I believe this is a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly perched on a flower head of weedy brome grass. It's smaller and darker than the similar Mournful Duskywing (E. tristis) butterfly that is more brown than black, compare this photo. If I'm right, this is the first one I've seen this year. This one looks grumpy! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2016)

Today started sunny and calm, but clouds soon blew in and lasted all day, with breeze. I assumed they were the usual marine layer fog, but when I went to town I realized that they were just a cloud cap on the mountains. Whatever the cause, it was another pretty crummy day for new photos, dark and chilly and breezy. I hope I get my truck back from the shop tomorrow. It will be nice to be more mobile!

File.Name: g508b_g508-013.jpg
Notes: 26298134923 This is an interesting butterfly-like moth (Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) this morning on the edge of our garden. It almost looks like a Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly, but it's too small and the shape of the wings is wrong. It sure looks like genus Pyrausta - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae), but its face was hidden. I have to apologize for this photo which is not as sharp as it should be. When I went back to get it right, the little moth was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2016)
CommonNames: moth; California Honeysuckle; Fatal Metalmark; Crambid Snout Moth
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Calephelis nemesis californica; Pyrausta; Crambidae

File.Name: g508c_g507-030.jpg
Notes: 26297607773 Here are two more shots of the West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly I found on a garden path yesterday, see this photo. This pair of photos shows how the butterflies almost completely disappear against the background with their wings folded up. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

File.Name: g508d_g507-025.jpg
Notes:
26901393855 These are new bristly nutlets of native (but weedy) Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine, Rubiaceae) catching a rare moment of sunshine yesterday. The retorse-bristly stems and seeds are annoying when they get stuck in your socks and shoes. I've always thought of this plant as non-native, but it is now considered to be native. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

Well, we have native weeds too! A forest service report has this to say about Stickywilly - another common name for Galium aparine:

"The nativity of stickywilly is debated. While most accept this species as native, some consider it nonnative [163,171]. Still others suggest that stickywilly is a native, but that subsequent introductions have occurred as well [90]. In a literature review, it is suggested stickywilly arrived in the fur of animals crossing the Bering Strait into North America [28]. While debate regarding the nativity of stickywilly continues, it is in all likelihood native and is considered native in most literature [25,44,58,143]. For more discussion on the nativity of stickywilly see [62,90].

File.Name: g508e_g508-005.jpg
Notes: 26297037563 These are the first - and maybe only - flowers of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family that I've found this year. I learned it years ago as Psoralea physodes. The plants have few flowers in this fifth drought year. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there, mostly in our canyon that I know very well. The plant has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak but smoother, see my [Previous] photo for a better view. It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! I've never tried making tea from the leaves like the common name suggests, but I should. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2016)
ScientificName: Rupertia physodes; Fabaceae; Psoralea physodes; Astragalus
associatedTaxa: Rupertia physodes; Fabaceae; Psoralea physodes; Astragalus

File.Name: g508f_g508-006.jpg
Notes: 26628178800 This is a whole plant of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. I learned it years ago as Psoralea physodes. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there, mostly in our canyon that I know very well. The plants have few flowers in this fifth drought year, but I managed to find one today, see my [Next photo]. The plant has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak but smoother. It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! I've never tried making tea from the leaves like the common name suggests, but I should. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2016)

File.Name: g509a_g509-010.jpg
Notes:
26829270622 I believe this is another March Fly (Bibionidae, Diptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden today. It's a fairly large (1/2 inch or a bit less) fly which suggests the species Bibio albipennis, though this one doesn't seem to have white hairs on its body. I've found a few of these lately, see here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2016)

We were in a cloud all day today, with a bit of drizzle this morning. It was another crummy day for new photos, and I was also feeling a bit crummy, so just three photos today. I hope we see some sun tomorrow - and that I'm feeling better.

File.Name: g509b_g509-014.jpg
Notes:
26828813592 Caterpillar or Sawfly nymph?

I think this caterpillar-like creature is really the larva of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae, Hymenoptera) in the genus Abia. Compare this photo at BugGuide, and see this photo for the bee-like adult. The big difference between this critter and a proper caterpillar (Lepidoptera) is that it has too many prolegs. Caterpillars have no more than 5 pairs, and this larva has 7 or 8. It's easy to make mistakes. I got the same kind of larva wrong in this recent photo. (One of these days I'll get around to changing the caption of that one.) This photo is from the mint patch in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2016)

File.Name: g509c_g507-006.jpg
Notes:
26888844986 Don't Jump! (At least not until I get the photo!)

This is a cute little "Click Beetle" (family Elateridae in the order Coleoptera) on the tip of a Rosemary leaf on the edge of our garden. These beetles have their thorax drawn out to points that can *click* against the wing covers - though I'm not sure if these small ones do that. The big ones do, dramatically. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

File.Name: g510a_g510-025.jpg
Notes: 26670347700 I found this interesting Fly (Diptera) perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. At first I thought it was a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, but this one is less hairy and has more filiform antennae. Now I'm pretty sure it's another Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera). (I have a history of confusing these genera - again and again!) It's a handsome little fly with interesting mouthparts. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2016) None of my photos today is the best. It was another crummy day in a cloud, but the sun finally did poke through the clouds after 4:00 pm. I had to bump up my ISO into the danger zone (above 400) and take *many* photos to get any kind of keeper. The forecast says that "we will see more of a typical burn off Wednesday" so I have some hope for tomorrow. I did finally get my truck back, I have wheels again! Just three photos again today in a cloud.
Locality: above 400) and take *many* photos to get any kind of keeper.  The forecast says that "we will see more of a typical burn off Wednesday" so I have some hope for tomorrow.  I did finally get my truck back

File.Name: g510b_g510-016.jpg
Notes:
26669864840 I believe this is a tiny Psyllid (superfamily Psylloidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insect on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It's a tiny little critter, just 172 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.67 mm, about 1/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This one really looked like a bit of birdseed on the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2016)

At first I thought it might be Neophyllura arbuti which Chris Mallory identified in this old photo. (See the photo to read Chris's interesting comments.) Maybe they are the same. Both plants are growing about 10 feet apart under a small native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree. But I suspect this is something else with longer antennae and different wings.

Psyllids are an insect group that I know nothing about, so I has happy to find this one. It's not the best photo, but it was a pretty crummy day. I had to bump up my ISO into the danger zone (above 400) and take *many* photos to get any kind of keeper. It's not so bad... considering!
ScientificName: Psyllid; Psylloidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Neophyllura arbuti; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: g510c_g510-007.jpg
Notes: 26943231605 This is another cute little adult Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It has narrow wings, maybe genus Danepteryx? When Julie saw this photo, she said "It looks like a cartoon insect" - it does! Those narrow wings can't be much good for flying, but maybe they help it steer when it hops. This hopper is tiny - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Danepteryx

File.Name: g511a_g511-023.jpg
Notes:
26867544862 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip aka Pacific Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working flowers of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family in our canyon today. These butterflies are said to use native mustards as their host plant, but I'm not sure if that includes these non-natives. This is a pretty tight crop. These butterflies are quite camera shy, and I only had one chance at this photo before it flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)

Today was a much nicer day that we've been having lately, and I'm back to six photos! It started sunny and calm, then the fog rolled in over the mountains and the "fog winds" kicked up, then it cleared up and was very nice for a while before the real wind kicked in. We got into the 70s today instead of the low 50s like it's been.
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Pacific Orangetip; Black Mustard

File.Name: g511b_g511-012.jpg
Notes: 26688157770 This is a close shot of mating Flies (Diptera) on the leaf of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree this morning. They look bristly enough to be Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), but I don't really know. My photo isn't quite sharp - but sharp enough for such an intimate shot! HIHD! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)
CommonNames: Flies; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Flies

File.Name: g511c_g511-024.jpg
Notes: 26356921053 I believe this is the nymph of an Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) grasshopper on the bristly leaves of a a native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. Nymph or adult, they never get much larger wings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)

File.Name: g511d_g511-016.jpg
Notes: 26927605696 I found this fairly large "true bug" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. My first thought was a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, but it looks different from the usual Largus californicus we get in the summer - compare this photo. Maybe it's a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata, Rhopalidae?) Thanks Lynn! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)
