File.Name: g511e_g511-005.jpg
Notes: 26866694082 This is all of a fairly large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) sprawled across a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods this morning. This one is male, as evidenced by the knob at the end of its abdomen. See this web page for some info on sexing crane flies. These flies come in different sizes like t-shirts - small, medium, large, and extra-large. I reckon this one is a large size. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)

File.Name: g511f_g510-017.jpg
Notes: 26356252353 This is a little Beetle (Coleoptera) on the edge of a leaf in the mint patch of the garden yesterday. I suspect it is a Soldier Beetle, genus Cantharidae. It does seem to have a hint of points on the edge of its pronotum, and indeed the cantherids are included within the superfamily Elateroidea which includes the Click Beetles. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2016)

File.Name: g512a_g512-002.jpg
Notes:
26375414974 Look in the shadows...

This Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) has a Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) for breakfast! See this photo for the probable identity of the cicada. I got this photo on the retaining wall above our garden, with some leaves of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) providing the lizard with some shelter. A lucky shot for sure. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2016)

Today was a lovely day with warm temps and mostly calm winds. It got up to almost 80∞ today on our upstairs porch. That's 25 degrees warmer than just 2 days ago!
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Cicada; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Sceloporus occidentalis; Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g512b_g512-006.jpg
Notes: 26376450683 I found this tiny nymph of (I believe) an Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) grasshopper in the mint patch of the garden this morning. This nymph is just 1290 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 5 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's tiny, but perfectly formed. How did I notice it? Well, I happened to see another one a couple of days ago in almost the same place, but I flubbed that photo. So I was looking for it this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2016)

File.Name: g512c_g512-010.jpg
Notes: 26980393475 This is the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing on a hillside in the mountains. It has a squarish nectary below a reddish spot, with (usually) a second paler spot above. There was a breeze when I got this photo today, so I need to get back to this spot when the winds are calm. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2016)

File.Name: g512d_g512-027.jpg
Notes: 26946601716 I believe this is the real native Malacothrix clevelandii in the Asteraceae plant family growing as a "fire-follower" in a place that was cleared but not burned a few years ago. It's a small plant, only about six inches high, but it has a definite "malacothrix look". Malacothrix phaeocarpa is said to be similar in stature, with fleshier basal leaves, which I didn't see. I've seen this one before, but this is the first time I've known what I was looking at. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2016)

File.Name: g512e_g511-013.jpg
Notes: 26979727535 This is another "beautiful bug butt" shot of the small nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. It's antennae seem to go one forever. I'm not sure why these nymphs lend themselves so well to butt shots, but they do. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2016)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: g512f_g507-029.jpg
Notes: 26945920706 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a small spider hanging supine in its web in the mint patch of the garden. It had a sheet-web so maybe it's a Sheetweb Spider (family Linyphiidae), but I can't rule out a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) either. I'm pretty sure this one is male, though the focus point is just a hair too distant to be sure. I got this photo a few days ago when we were in a cloud, with some breeze. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2016)

File.Name: g528a_g524-002.jpg
Notes:
26708081573 Here is the bouquet of flowers that my kids and grandkids sent during my recent hospital stay. I got the photo after I got home last week. The kids sent a nice note along with them:

Dear Grandpa Treebeard,
We all wish you a swift recovery.
Hopefully these captive sacrificial
flowers will brighten up your room.
Sorry, no crab spiders!

I apologize for disappearing so completely from Flickr a couple of weeks ago. I woke up in the middle of the night on Friday, May 13 with a full bladder, but I couldn't pee. By morning, it was becoming painful, so Julie drove me down to the ER at the valley hospital. They drew a blood sample, and discovered that I'd had one (or more) recent myocardial infarctions - a heart attack. They transferred me by ambulance to Cottage Hospital in Santa Barbara. Three days later I had quadruple cardiac bypass surgery. A week after that I came home. Welcome to the zipper club!

I feel like I'm recovering well. I don't feel any particular pain and I'm doing my walks and exercises every day. I'm starting to use a walking stick instead of a walker as I do my walks back and forth along our upstairs deck. Unfortunately the garden is downstairs. I still feel quite weak, and I suspect it will take another week before I can get out into the garden and try to get some photos again. Still, that's my main motivation right now. I'll get back to Flickr as soon as I can.
Locality:

File.Name: g608a_g608-005.jpg
Notes:
27548680065 Here is a large caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly munching a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. The plant is an invasive weed in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the caterpillars and try to remember to trim the flowers before they make seeds. The caterpillars do some real damage to the plant - especially when there are as many caterpillars as this year. There must be at least 100 caterpillars on these few plants, ranging in size from tiny (like little bird droppings) to large, more than I've ever seen before. This is a native caterpillar on a non-native plant, so what was its host plant before Fennel was introduced? (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2016)

Today was actually the first time I've gotten outdoors with my camera since my heart attack a few weeks ago. (See here for more info.) I have regular exercises, but my main work has been taking short walks back and forth along our upstairs deck. I'm finally up to 1/2 hour walks which makes it worth going up and down our stairs. It was wonderful being outside again! I'm making good progress, but I'm still exhausted by the end of the day. Full recovery will take a while.
Locality: See here for more info.) I have regular exercises

File.Name: g608b_g603-006.jpg
Notes: 27271742290 These are mating Crane Flies (Tipulidae, Diptera) on the screen door of our upstairs porch. I got this photo is the shade with sunlight beyond, so white balance is what it is. These flies come in different sizes like t-shirts - small, medium, large, and extra-large. I reckon this one is medium size. HIHD! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2016)

File.Name: g609a_g529-004.jpg
Notes:
27293491630 This is a butt shot of a Woolly Bear caterpillar - the larva of a Tiger Moth (family Arctiini) in the order Lepidoptera, the first one I've seen this year. It's trying to get away from me in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Those two little fringed stumps at the end are the last pair of its prolegs. Julie actually found this critter on the inside carpet downstairs near the door. I guess that it belongs in an oak, so I placed in this tree that overhangs our upstairs porch. It seemed right at home and soon disappeared in the foliage. I suspect that it will become an Edward's Glassy-wing (Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii) moth, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2016)

This was actually the first nature photo I got after coming home from the hospital last week, see here. I'm tired, so just this one photo today. I met with my visiting physical therapist today, and she ordered me to take it easy!
CommonNames: Woolly Bear; Tiger Moth; Coast Live Oak; Edward's Glassy-wing
ScientificName: Arctiini; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii
associatedTaxa: Arctiini; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii

File.Name: g610a_g610-006.jpg
Notes:
27517064901 I found this very hairy Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. Looking at BugGuide, the species Poliaenus obscurus looks like a good match. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2016)

It was pretty gloomy today with an annoying breeze, but I got a few decent photos despite the weather. I was outside along the edge of our garden. That's a real pleasure when you haven't had the opportunity for a while!
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Poliaenus obscurus
associatedTaxa: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Poliaenus obscurus

File.Name: g610b_g607-001.jpg
Notes: 27312809690 This is a neat little Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera perched for just a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's genus Machimus? Nice whiskers! The focus isn't quite right, but it sure is an interesting fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2016)
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Machimus
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Machimus

File.Name: g611a_g531-003.jpg
Notes:
27000686803 Here are dorsal (left) and ventral (right) views of a little brown Bat ( order Chiroptera) - which may indeed be a Little Brown Bat (Myotis lucifugus). A good friend left it surreptitiously on our front porch as a gift after I got home from the hospital a couple of weeks ago. Flowers are more traditional, but I get dead bats! Bats are rarely seen up close, but complicated none the less. See this poster of local California bats. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2016)

We actually got almost 1/10 inch of drizzle/light rain today! The June gloom weather never did clear up and I never got outside. For some reason that lack of activity made me more tired than ever. Funny how that works.

File.Name: g612a_g612-021.jpg
Notes:
27600532896 I found this little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly near the ground today on an empty seed head of non-native Smooth Cat's Ear (Hypochaeris glabra, Asteraceae). It's grounded by the gloomy weather today, but it is holding its wings perpendicular to the brightest part of the dull sky trying to get a bit of extra warmth. This isn't the best photo as I was getting tired and don't bend down so well yet, but it will do. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2016)

I got both photos today down by the dry creek in our canyon. I'm not strong enough to walk there yet, so Julie acted as my driver. It was another gloomy day with thick marine layer stratus/fog, but at least it wasn't drizzling like yesterday. The NWS forecast is that the marine layer will shrink some tomorrow. We might even see some sun!

File.Name: g612b_g612-015.jpg
Notes: 27534430112 I found this impressive caterpillar in a small tree of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) plant family by the creek today. It's fairly large, about the size and shape of my index finger. I'm sure it's the caterpillar of a Sphinx moth (Sphingidae), but I'm not sure what kind. It appears to be preparing to pupate, but I'm pretty sure it's just caught in an awkward pose while feeding on the leaves. It appears to be praying. If you look close at the left photo, you can see that it has retractable prolegs. I've never noticed that before in a caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2016)

File.Name: g613a_g613-001.jpg
Notes:
27044897124 This is a small mama Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) spider guarding by her brand new egg mass under a leaf of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that grows close to our upstairs porch. I don't believe the woolly egg mass was there yesterday morning, but it was in the afternoon. (I might be wrong about the morning?) They are especially handsome little spiders. Note that they usually have six spots on their abdomens, but this one seems to have eight spots. Did you notice that odd shrimp-like critter on the right opposite the spider's head? (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2016)

It was a tough day for new photos. It started sunny, but stratus/fog soon spilled over the mountains from the ocean side bringing "fog winds" with it. We stayed on the edge of the fog almost all day, drifting between clear and cloudy, mostly windy. Tomorrow will be another difficult day for new photos as I have appointments morning, midday, and afternoon. At least I'm getting them all done on the same day.
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g613b_g612-018.jpg
Notes: 27377824650 Here is another close photo showing the head and tiny true legs of the caterpillar I show in this photo yesterday. I'm sure it's the caterpillar of a Sphinx moth (Sphingidae), but I'm not sure what kind. I found it on a small tree of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. How strange it looks up close, like a something seen through a microscope. One structure I can identify is the caterpillar's eye - that broken ring of small dots just below the yellow stripe on the right of its head. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2016)

File.Name: g616a_g616-032.jpg
Notes:
27105199684 Here is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Sure this photo isn't as sharp as it should be, but it's hard to capture these fast-moving moths that never stand still. I'll have lots of time to get it right this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2016)

I apologize for disappearing for two days. I had a full day of doctor visits and procedures on Tuesday, and my son and grandkids arrived yesterday for the Live Oak Music Festival. *Sigh* This is the first festival I've missed in 25 years, but I'm not up to it yet. I'll try to make it out there on Sunday afternoon.
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: g616b_g616-021.jpg
Notes: 27683295046 This is the fantastic early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera), more ornate than our usual green katydids in this photo. They remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. This one is perched on a leaf of a Mallow (Malvaceae) subshrub in the garden. So far this is the only one I've seen this year. These insects seem to be suffering more than most from our on-going drought, now in its fifth year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2016)

File.Name: g617a_g617-018.jpg
Notes:
27663200071 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. This is the first one I've seen since December, but they'll become common as the year moves on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2016)

It was breezy today, which is not good news for crews working the Sherpa Fire near Refugio beach to the west along the front face of the Santa Ynez Mountains. That's still about 20 miles away, though there is some smoke in the air. It stands at 5,866 acres so this is a serious wildfire.

File.Name: g617b_g617-008.jpg
Notes: 27125214464 I guess this bristly fly is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera), though I'm not sure. It's larger than the average fly. I found it this morning on a leaf of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. This phacelia has flowers right now, but I'm still trying to get a decent photo of the tiny bees which love them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2016)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: g618a_g618-020.jpg
Notes:
27146244264 This is a small nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a not-quite-flowering stalk of Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the Onagraceae plant family. (My Mom gave us a cultivar many years ago so it might be a hybrid.) These gorgeous nymphs remind me of the Bush Katydid nymphs in this photo, but they are completely different if you look close enough. You can see a bit of this one's spike-like proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2016)

It was warmer today and mostly pretty calm. The forecast is for growing sundowner winds this evening. We don't get proper Santa Ana winds in the Santa Barbara mountains because there is no direct path to the desert, but sometimes the winds blow over the mountain passes and build in intensity and heat as they compress and blow through the canyons towards the coast. That would really pump up the action at the Sherpa Fire near Refugio beach to the west of us. In addition, it's expected to be 10 degrees hotter tomorrow.

I hope to get out to the Live Oak Music Festival tomorrow afternoon. This is the first festival I've missed in 25 years, and I want to at least make an appearance and greet some old friends. Yipes, it's forecast to be 105∞ out there tomorrow!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Evening Primrose; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae

File.Name: g618b_g618-014.jpg
Notes: 27479561310 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting blue pollen from a flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. I'm sure that this one is in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter or Mason bees because its pollen collecting hairs are on its abdomen instead of its rear legs. We also have different wild bees that are black and look just like these - except that they do collect pollen on their legs. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2016)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Branching Phacelia; Leaf-cutter; Mason
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: g619a_g619-002.jpg
Notes: 27729792441 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flowers of a Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) shrub in our garden. It's the first one I've seen this year. I also saw a male chasing a lady into a nearby oak, so I'll be on the lookout for them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2016)

File.Name: g619b_g619-006.jpg
Notes: 27804889455 This is a nymph of some other kind of Long-horned Grasshopper (Ensifera, (Orthoptera) than our usual green katydids. It sure does look like a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) though, and there are other possibilities - for example this photo. I found this one on spent flowers of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2016)
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: g620a_g618-028.jpg
Notes:
27771014936 The colors of summer...

This is a two-fer of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies working new flowers of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. The Summer Solstice happened at 3:34 PST this afternoon. It's the longest day of the year. Happy Solstice everyone! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2016)

I did make it out to the Live Oak Music Festival yesterday afternoon. This is the first festival I've missed in 25 years, and I needed to at least make an appearance and greet some old friends. Duty done.

Good grief, it was 105 degrees on San Marcos Pass today. That's as hot as I can recall it ever getting at home. It was too warm for most insects and definitely too warm to be out in the sun trying to photograph them. It's still about 90∞ as I write this at 6:00 pm. Fortunately, it expected to cool down a bit tomorrow.

File.Name: g620b_g620-005.jpg
Notes: 27730044421 This is a lovely little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly reeling in its proboscis on new flowers of a Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) shrub in our garden. I'm pretty sure the butterfly is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides), though sometimes these butterflies have blocky markings below. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2016)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: g621a_g621-009.jpg
Notes:
27215061773 I'm pretty sure this sleek and sexy-looking "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). We have quite a few of these in the still-flowering shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. That's the blue-tipped style of a flower going off to the right. The bugs seem to prefer this plant, though I also find them on (or in!) Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus), see this photo. These bugs come from remarkable ant-mimic nymphs, see this photo. All stages of this insect seem to be in the white sage right now. See my [Previous] photo for a side-view. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2016)

It was warm again today, but the heat was mercifully less fierce than yesterday when we hit 105∞. Today it stayed in the mid-90s. It helped that it was breezy in the morning, with winds from the southwest, the ocean side of the mountains. Tomorrow is expected to stay in the 80s starting a few days of cooling - until it gets hot again next weekend.
CommonNames: White Sage; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Diplacus longiflorus
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: g621b_g621-011.jpg
Notes: 27826564455 I'm pretty sure this sleek and sexy-looking "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). We have quite a few of these in the still-flowering shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. (We transplanted it to the garden many years ago after it was washed out by a small slide in the rain.) The bugs seem to prefer this plant, though I also find them on (or in!) Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus), see this photo. These bugs come from remarkable ant-mimic nymphs, see this photo. All stages of this insect seem to be in the white sage right now. In this side-view, you can see a bit of the bug's proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2016)
CommonNames: White Sage; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: g622a_g622-003.jpg
Notes:
27248251003 Here is another small nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). Compare the nymph in this photo, an earlier stage of the same kind of critter. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2016)

I tried to post this photo last night, but I could get past the timeout errors - despite multiple tries and resetting the modem twice. I guess it's our ISP which is Frontier, formerly Verizon, the only choice up here in the mountains. It's not just us. A google search on "frontier verizon internet service sucks" returns about 127,000 results.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g622b_g622-011.jpg
Notes: 27234707873 Here is one more shot of the "true bug" Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). Usually I find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae), but this one happens to be on Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) - less than a foot away from a low-hanging flower stalk of White Sage. This one appears to be a gravid female, full of eggs. You can clearly see it's proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2016)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Salvia spathacea
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Salvia spathacea

File.Name: g623a_g623-008.jpg
Notes:
27866196465 This is a perfect Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of some kind of Sage (Salvia, Lamiaceae) in the garden. How chubby it looks - maybe it's a female with eggs? (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2016)

I've been sticking to the garden since my surgery by doctor's orders - to avoid driving for 4-6 weeks. I had a doctor appointment today and got the word that it's OK to drive again. Wahoo, wheels again! I've got a few places and plants to check up on...

File.Name: g623b_g622-008.jpg
Notes: 27254365964 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) perched on a flower bunch of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). One distinguishing feature of these butterflies is a pair of hyaline (translucent) spots on its forewings. The spots are only visible when the sun is behind (like this), but you can see where the spots are, near the wing tips on the left. This is a nice photo of both butterfly and flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2016)

File.Name: g624a_g624-001.jpg
Notes:
27850741816 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of Showy Milkweed (Asclepias speciosa) in the Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae) plant family in our garden. We got the plant from my Mom's garden 10+ years ago, and this is only the second time its ever flowered because the deer usually eat the buds. Now our dog Brodie keeps the deer at bay. It's a lovely California native from further north with large nickle-sized flowers that smell like jasmine. The butterfly ain't bad either! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2016)

I had every intention of driving somewhere else today, but the visiting nurse decided to come by on her final visit. It was already windy by the time she left, so I spent another day in the garden. In was only in the mid-80s today, with wind. Tomorrow is expected to be near 100∞, with wind. We're under both a NWS Red Flag Warning and a Wind Advisory tonight. It's a bad combination during fire weather.
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias speciosa; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae

File.Name: g624b_g624-009.jpg

File.Name: g625a_g625-011.jpg
Notes:
27628768180 This is a shot of the upper wings of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. It's just leaving a flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Photos of the upper wings of these butterflies are uncommon. The butterflies are common enough, but they always fold their wings up when they perch. The only way to catch their upper side is to get them in the air. I got this shot by starting to press the shutter release button just as the butterfly was leaving the flower. That's how fast they are! It's our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2016)

I took a drive today - my first under my control in the nearly 6 weeks since my operation! It was hot today. in the mid-90s, but not quite as hot as forecast. The flowers were full of butterflies and I got lots of photos. It's time to go up to at least three photos a day. In time, I'll get back to my usual six.
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: g625b_g625-025.jpg
Notes: 27805496232 This is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These are one of my favorite wildflowers, that pick the hot and dry part of the year to flower. I saw many butterflies on these flowers today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2016)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g625c_g625-073.jpg
Notes: 27294298814 These are the fine flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing in Kinevan Canyon. There were half a dozen plants growing right along West Camino Cielo road with buds a month ago. *Sigh* Those plants are vulnerable to "drive-by pickers" - but this year a county work crew cleared the roadside and took out all of them. These crews should have someone aboard who knows what plants are worth saving. Anyway, this plant is elsewhere in Kinevan Canyon. It's a large plant, 9 or 10 feet tall with a dozen open flowers and more coming. What's missing is butterflies, but you can't have everything. You have to pinch yourself and remember that it is native. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2016)

File.Name: g626a_g626-025.jpg
Notes:
27313808994 This is a brilliant Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on its forewings runs along the wing rather than across it. Beautiful. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 June 2016)

It was warm today, up to 99∞ according to the NWS/Mesowest weather station on San Marcos Pass, less than a mile away. I went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road to see how things are going after my absence. They're going well!
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: g626b_g626-005.jpg
Notes: 27891311206 This is the first summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. These fantastic summer flowers attract lots of life - for example the yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae), and a tiny brown spider across from it on the other side, and a wild bee inside. I'm sure I'll get many more photos as the season continues. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 June 2016)

File.Name: g626c_g626-044.jpg
Notes: 27848393961 Here are brilliant flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The plants are just about done for the year, but there are still a few flowers if you look close in the higher mountains. It's another plant where you have to pinch yourself and remember that it is native. It's especially striking at this time of year. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 June 2016)

File.Name: g627a_g627-004.jpg
Notes:
27336178784 Graduation day...

The nymph has become an adult! This is now an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's perched on the same leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) as the nymph in this photo taken just a few days ago. I'm not sure, but I suspect this one is the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I'll keep trying to get a photo that shows the wings better, but this is nice for a different angle. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2016)

It was warm today, up to 100∞ according to the NWS/Mesowest weather station on San Marcos Pass, less than a mile away. I went for a drive up West Camino Cielo road to see how things are going after my absence. They're going well!

File.Name: g627b_g627-021.jpg
Notes: 27335084033 I believe these are both colonies of new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls are made of individual capsules that usually grow together to make a brilliant mass (right), but sometimes they don't grow the interconnecting hairs leaving just the naked flask-like capsules (left). Those same capsules are inside the mass on the right. It beats me why a gall insect which wants to be left alone would make such obvious galls as this! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2016)

I find the moths on a number of different flowers, but these are one of their favorites. They are also one of my favorites, that pick the hot and dry part of the year to flower. File.Name: g628a_g628-019.jpg
Notes:
27969905175 This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a flower of a (I guess) an Aloe plant in the garden. Maybe it's a female Steniolia scolopacea, see this photo and description at BugGuide. I spent some time sitting in the hot sun watching these wasps today. We also have larger and darker wasps that may be the males. They seem to have a circuit through the garden. They visit these flowers and then disappear for about 10 minutes before reappearing - again and again. These wasps have a very long proboscis that works well with these deep-throated flowers - see my [Previous] photo. They are tough ones to photograph. They move very quickly from flower to flower, and they seem aware of me as a photographer. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2016)

It was another warm day, up to the mid 90s. It was interesting that the temperature peaked this morning; then cooled down for midday; then peaked again in the late afternoon. This let me get a short nap this afternoon!

Notes:
27378188844 "If you got emí, flaunt emí!"

This is a three-fer of butterflies sharing one of the last flowers of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae growing in our canyon. The two large ones are Swallowtails (Papilio) butterflies. The smaller one is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly - which just happens to be showing its upper wings! (See this photo.) Yep, there were loads of butterflies in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2016)

I got home late today after my first introductory session of cardiac rehab, so these are quick comments. I'll be starting regular sessions on Tuesday and Thursday mornings. They are pretty early in the morning, so I hope I'll have some time for a few photos when I get home. That is if I'm not too tired. The room is like a gym with exercise machines. We'll see how it goes.

File.Name: g629b_g629-055.jpg
Notes: 27991438915 This is a two-fer of a Swallowtail (Papilio) butterfly and a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly sharing a flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae. It's one of the last flowers in our canyon, so I'm not surprised they're sharing. The large butterfly may well be a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus), but I'm not sure. Our Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies sometimes get cream colored, and I get confused. Is there another way to tell them apart besides color? (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2016)

This one has more black on its thorax and no sign of that "third eye" that the female shows two photos back. Maybe it's a female Steniolia scolopacea, see this photo and description at BugGuide. This one is working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae. It's a terrific butterfly plant. File.Name: g630a_g629-032.jpg
Notes:
27729077460 This is a pair of Swallowtail (Papilio) butterflies sharing a flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon. I guess the butterfly on the left is a a Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) because of its uniform rich yellow color. The one on the right looks yellow on top, but that bit of hindwing looks more black and white to me, and it may be a Pale Swallowtail (P. eurymedon). These butterflies sometimes get yellowish in their summer broods, and I get confused. Is there a better way to tell them apart besides color? (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2016)

I had multiple doctor appointments both in Santa Barbara and Santa Ynez today, so no time for new photos. No problem! Every day I take more good photos than I can post, so it's good to catch up now and then. It was cooler today - briefly up to 93∞ but mostly in the 80s, very pleasant.

I believe the bee is genus Melissodes (tribe Eucerini, family Apidae) because of those large bushy pollen baskets on her hind legs. Hah, they remind me of the Batwing Style Woolly Chaps that some cowboys used to wear. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2016)

File.Name: g701a_g701-012.jpg
Notes:
27992658106 I believe this is the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) carrying its prey on a stem of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). The hunter gets its name from the row of four pointed spurs on the back of its thorax. The hunted is a small beetle (Coleoptera) - maybe a chubby Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae) the way its abdomen sticks out beyond it's wing covers. Now just how does the bug hold its prey on that long beak?? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2016)

The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains from the coast this morning, bringing fog winds with it. This was the first morning fog I've seen for a few weeks. June has been mostly hot instead of gloomy this year, at least the second half of it. I went down to the river where I did find the sun, though it was still quite breezy. I hope the national forest - and us at home - survive the holiday weekend.

File.Name: g701b_g701-008.jpg
Notes: 27413144004 I found this little Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly trying to work a not-quite-open summer flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the dry river today. This plant is the staff of life along the river in the summer, but the flowers are not open for business yet. Last year I found the first flowers on June 11, so they are late this year. For some reason, there are no flowers yet on the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) that grows nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2016)

File.Name: g701c_g701-002.jpg
Notes: 27924697682 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a new summer flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's a bit over-exposed and a tight crop, but it's the best I could do on a breezy day. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2016)

File.Name: g702a_g702-022.jpg
Notes:
27945546312 This is looking into a new summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family, with a bud off to the side. If you look at the narrow sepals between the petals, you can see furry yellow gland patches, which I've never noticed before. If you want to see these stunning flowers, better hurry up. I'm not seeing nearly as many plants as last year, and they may not last long. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2016)

It was another foggy morning at home, so I went into the higher mountains to find some sun. I found the sun, but fog winds too. This cooler weather is a relief after a couple weeks of serious heat, but it's not so good for insects. It was another foggy morning at home, so I went into the higher mountains to find some sun. I found the sun, but fog winds too. This cooler weather is a relief after a couple weeks of serious heat, but it's not so good for insects. Summer isn't the prime time for wildflowers in our mountains, but there are plenty out there if you look for them!
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: g702b_g702-031.jpg
Notes: 27434009414 Here are two photos of flowers of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. The top view on the right is remarkable - like a sea anemone or a bucket of worms! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2016)

File.Name: g702c_g702-054.jpg
Notes: 27970167921 This is native Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Plantaginaceae plant family, formerly in the in the Scrophulariaceae. It really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. It also has distinctive "ternate" leaves in whorls of three, visible on the far left of the main stem. It has classic "hummingbird flowers". These are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It's the only place I find them. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2016)

File.Name: g703a_g703-031.jpg
Notes:
27456400863 About the only thing that's right about this photo is the subject!

This is a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. It's flying into a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family, one of the last open flowers on the south-facing side of our canyon. These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture. Look between the fly's head and the flower and you can see the long proboscis that these flies have - longer than their body! (See this photo.) The fly has to approach the flower with its proboscis out front to fit inside! They seem made for these flowers - just the right size to get in, and a long enough proboscis to reach the sweet stuff inside. It must be tough for the insect when its favorite flower starts to fade. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2016)

The morning fog cleared out early today, but it stayed windy for most of the day. I almost stayed home, and I'm glad I didn't!

File.Name: g703b_g703-010.jpg
Notes:
27789774300 The darnedest thing...

I found this diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) sitting on a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). It appeared comatose, though I could see its proboscis slowing moving. I suspected it was spider prey. After I got a few photos, I tried to pick it up by a wing - and it immediately came to life and flew off with an electric buzz! It went right back to more flowers and got back to work. I've never seen this behavior before. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2016)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g703c_g703-016.jpg
Notes: 27992847271 This is part of a large colony of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with summer flowers. These are growing in our canyon where a neighbor did some weed whacking last year. (I always cringe when I hear the weed whacker, but sometimes it brings good results!) All the photos I've showed recently that involve this plant are from this patch. I believe this fine summer flower is a fire-follower that thrives on disturbed ground, but we have a few more patches of it in the canyon growing in shady places that aren't flowering yet. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2016)

File.Name: g704a_g704-041.jpg
Notes:
27992649472 Here are red, white, and blue native flowers for Independence Day! Here are:

Red - Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family, still hanging on;

White - Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei) in the Agavaceae plant family aka Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae;

Blue - Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)

I always dread the Fourth of July because of people and fireworks and wildfires, but the weather is cooperating today. It's been mostly cool, in the 70s, with morning fog. I did go out today, up into the higher mountains to look for red, white, and blue flowers. Today has been quiet so far, and I hope it stays that way.

27814697830 Here are red, white, and blue native flowers for Independence Day! Here are:

Red - California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family, the first open flowers I've seen this year;

Notes:
27814330930 Here are red, white, and not-quite blue native thistles for Independence Day! It curious that all three have the same binomial scientific name, and all can be called by the same common name - Cobwebby Thistle. Here are:

Red - Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family;

White - White Thistle aka California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family; and

Blue - Lavender Thistle aka California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)

File.Name: g705a_g704-011.jpg
Notes:
27501692424 This is a pair of Swallowtail (Papilio) butterflies working flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). The butterfly on top is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) and the one below is a Pale Swallowtail (P. eurymedon). The latter butterflies sometimes get yellowish in their summer broods, and I get confused. They sure look similar, except that the tiger is larger. The pale does seem to have pointier wings. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)

I had no time for new photos today. I had cardiac rehab this morning followed by another doctor appointment in the afternoon. But tomorrow is clear...

File.Name: g705b_g704-064.jpg
Notes: 28013368422 I found this Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) standing up among the new flowers of a native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. I didn't notice that it has a partner until I got home and had the image on my computer screen! They look different, but I'm sure they are both Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica). They are color-shifters. These are the first I've noticed this year, though I haven't been paying close attention to the buckwheat yet. The only reason I saw them is that the flowers are white. (See my [Previous] three photos.) (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat; Pacific Ambush Bugs

File.Name: g705c_g704-022.jpg
Notes: 27501033654 This lovely flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family is being occupied by a number of "Calochortus Bees" (my name). I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera. Maybe they are Perdita californica? There are at least two mating pairs in this photo, one on the lower-right sepal and the other below on the petal below the central pedestal. I have a closer photo of them for "hump day" tomorrow. I don't see them anywhere else than these flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Calochortus Bees

File.Name: g706a_g706-024.jpg
Notes:
27524628103 This is a fairly large Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) sprawled across leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It's about an inch long, maybe a bit more. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing and don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. But maybe I found a trick. I first found this fly and spooked it away when I tried to move in with my tripod. I went off for other photos and came back the same way after about 15 minutes - and there it was on the same perch! That won't help you find one in the first place, but come back in a while if you lose one. Dragonflies are the same way. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2016)

Our weather has been much the same this week - the day starts clear, then fog rolls in bringing fog winds with it, then it clears up again. I have cardiac rehab again tomorrow, but I'll probably get home just about when it clears up.

File.Name: g706b_g706-027.jpg
Notes: 27524285183 This is a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae) insect in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera caught up in a spider web. I nudged it free, and it promptly disappeared. I reckon it's a Buffalo Treehopper, genus Ceresa. Compare this photo. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2016)

File.Name: g706c_g704-023.jpg
Notes: 27523961744 Here is a mating pair of "Calochortus Bees" (my name) inside a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera. Maybe they are Perdita californica? I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. Maybe that's why they always seem to be "doing it"? They're in a hurry because every moment is precious. HIHD! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2016)
CommonNames: Calochortus Bees; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Calochortus weedii; Liliaceae; Pygoperdita; Perdita; Andrenidae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Perdita californica

File.Name: g707a_g703-013.jpg
Notes:
28083138871 This is a action shot of a diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2016)

I had cardiac rehab again today and it was already quite breezy when I got home before noon. The rehab room is really just a small gym with exercise machines. I go through the exercises and the nurse monitors my blood pressure and EKG and heart rate. I've never much liked the idea of exercise machines, but I have to admit I was tired when I got home! I'll get back to new photos tomorrow.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: g707b_g703-026.jpg
Notes: 27880149200 This is a "butt shot" of a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. It's snuggled into a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family, one of the last open flowers on the south-facing side of our canyon. See this photo for another shot of the same fly approaching the flower from the air and note its long proboscis - longer than its body. The flies seem made for these flowers - just the right size to get in, and a long enough proboscis to reach the sweet stuff inside. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2016)
CommonNames: Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: g707c_g701-014.jpg
Notes: 27545938143 This is a surprising "butt shot" of a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) moving away from me. It has a heart on its butt! This one is walking over a just starting to flower shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). See this photo for another shot of the same insect. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2016)
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: g708a_g708-025.jpg
Notes:
28105218501 I'm pretty sure this is a Feather-legged Fly (genus Trichopoda) in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - though its feathery rear legs are hidden under it. (See this photo.) It's working a new flower head of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). This is definitely an atypical Tachinid, not at all bristly. The thick orange calyptra are remarkable. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2016)

There was no morning fog today, but it was quite breezy. I went to the river to try to get away from it, but it was windy there too. I still got a few nice photos.
ScientificName: Trichopoda; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; calyptra

File.Name: g708b_g708-010.jpg
Notes: 27567246474 These are little mating Datura Beetles (my name) in the insect order Coleoptera on an unwinding flower bud of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure they are genus Lema in the family Chrysomelidae, maybe the Three-lined Potato Beetle (Lema daturaphila). They are in a mating position, but there is no actual contact between them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2016)

File.Name: g708c_g708-024.jpg
Notes: 28078841142 I found this handsome late-instar nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) perched on a stem of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). Maybe it's a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes)? The mostly monochrome colors - except for the blue tibia of its hind legs - are striking. I don't see any red on its femur. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2016)
CommonNames: Short-horned Grasshopper; Scale Broom; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper

File.Name: g709a_g709-042.jpg
Notes:
28101644592 "You shall not pass!"

I'm pretty sure this is a Thread-waisted Digger Wasp (genus Ammophila, Sphecidae, Apoidea, Hymenoptera). It's guarding the entrance to its underground nest, which it provisions with one (or more) caterpillars before closing it up and starting again. This wasp was digging dirt to close its nest as I was taking photos, but I'm not sure if that was part of the plan. It's an impressive wasp. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2016)

We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and a Red Flag Warning tomorrow. I was expecting big winds today, but it wasn't any worse than it's been. Maybe the winds will kick up tonight?

File.Name: g709b_g709-005.jpg
Notes: 28101342272 A small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) has brought down this much larger male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly - which just happens to be showing a bit of its upper wing. This drama happened on a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). The killer bug is just left of the butterfly's eye. I'll make a note of it. (Thank you Flickr, photo notes work again!) I guess the ambush bugs use their crab-like front legs for grabbing and their hind legs for holding on. It's an effective strategy. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2016)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Colias eurydice; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Colias eurydice; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g709c_g709-040.jpg
Notes: 27589466733 I found this Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) sitting on top of a soon-to-open flower bud of Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae, Asteraceae). These predators are color shifters. This one is white to match the just starting to open Baccharis flowers. You can clearly see the bug's crab-like front claws. See my [Next] photo for how it puts them to use. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2016)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae

File.Name: g710a_g710-002.jpg
Notes:
28125682222 This is a middle-instar caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly that's just crawled out of its early-instar larval skin. But there might be a problem. The shed skin is covered with parasitic wasp eggs - the larvae of which burrow into the host and turn it into more wasps. (See this photo.) Now the question is can this new instar caterpillar walk away from that - or is it doomed? The colors do seem a little off, compare this photo of the same stage caterpillar. Is it host to a hoard of new wasps? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2016)

This seems to be the second brood of caterpillars on the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plants in the canyon. It's an invasive weed, but we always keep a few for the caterpillars - and try to remember to trim the flowers before they set seed. This is the first time I've ever seen two batches of the caterpillars. It's curious because I've seen few adults this summer.

The wind never did kick up as forecast last night, but it's blowing pretty good tonight. We're under both a NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning tonight. Wind and even moderate heat is a dangerous combination.

File.Name: g710b_g710-032.jpg
Notes: 27613887354 This is an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly perched on a flower of a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) in the garden. They favor composites. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name, but there's nothing toxic about it. The butterfly is picking up so glow from the flower, like a natural light tent. You can see the sparkly "metal marks" on its wings, like silver threads woven into the scales. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2016)

File.Name: g710c_g710-029.jpg
Notes: 28229240845 This is a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border, and the underwings are different. (Compare this photo.) These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait - to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2016)
CommonNames: Lorquin's Admiral; Arroyo Willow; California Sister
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica

File.Name: g711a_g711-010.jpg
Notes:
27637747233 I believe this is a Baja California Treefrog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but it may be some other species. It's small and cute. It could just about sit on my thumbnail, and it's sitting like a kitty cat with its legs folded under it. I found this one today on a mint leaf near our in our "water garden" today. This garden is nothing fancy - just an old fiberglass bathtub filled with water and plants, but these tubs are important for the frogs and other critters. Five years of drought has taken its toll. This is the first frog I've seen all year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2016)

It was windy last night, and the wind carried over into today. It was too windy to go anywhere, but I did get a few photos in the garden. I have cardiac rehab again tomorrow - every Tuesday and Thursday. I was tired after the last session. We'll see if I want to take some photos or take a nap when I get home tomorrow!

File.Name: g711b_g711-006.jpg
Notes: 28253140865 Here is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a moment on a mint leaf in the garden. We seem to have a huge variety of these tiny flies. Every one seems a little different! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2016)

File.Name: g711c_g710-009.jpg
Notes:
28218538336 Here is another shot of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar with fly or wasp eggs attached to it. See my [Previous] photo. This shot also shows what I guess is the pupal case of one of the parasitoid critters. It looks like a little loofah sponge or maybe its packed in excelsior! I found several pairings of caterpillar and pupa, so I don't think it's a coincidence that they're together. I found this on a non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2016)

I'm not sure of many things about this photo. I'm not sure if it's an early or middle stage caterpillar, though either way the colors seem a little off. I'm sure the caterpillar is alive because I poked it, but I'm not sure if it's healthy or not. And I'm not sure if this is the work of a fly or a wasp. The eggs do look like the work of Tachinid flies, see this page at BugGuide. But the pupa does not look like the work of a typical Tachinid.

Well, what do I know? I'm just the photographer! I'll leave it to others to figure out what I've photographed.

File.Name: g712a_g712-007.jpg
Notes:
28239903076 This is a Buprestid beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) on a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. I guess it's a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There are lots of species, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2016)

I did go to cardiac rehab this morning, and came home tired after a workout. The question was take some photos or take a nap. I did both, in that order. The breeze was already kicking up when I got home, so I stayed in the garden.

File.Name: g712b_g712-009.jpg
Notes: 28170065132 This is a furry little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) - maybe Villa lateralis? It's perched on a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. Zinnias with single flowers are excellent garden plants for attracting butterflies and many other insects, but avoid plants with doubled flowers. Insects can't get into them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2016)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa lateralis; Zinnia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa lateralis; Zinnia; Asteraceae

File.Name: g712c_g712-004.jpg
Notes: 28239493286 This is a different treefrog than my [Previous] photo, on a different leaf of the same mint plant near our "water garden". (They were both there.) I believe it's a Baja California Treefrog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but it may be some other species. It looks cast in bronze! It's small enough to sit on my thumbnail. One frog yesterday, two frogs today - maybe three frogs tomorrow? Five years of drought has taken its toll, so it's good to see these new frogs. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2016)

File.Name: g713a_g713-014.jpg
Notes:
28216370251 I found this little Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on new flowers of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. These are just about the first open flowers I've seen in the mountains this year - odd, because last year they started flowering in May. The hairstreak butterflies have been scarce too. It's been a good year for large butterflies like swallowtails, but small butterflies like blues and hairstreaks have been scarce. I don't know why. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2016)

It was a nice enough day, but I didn't find too many photo ops. I suspect I'll have more days like this as summer moves on. I have cardiac rehab again tomorrow morning, and I'm not sure if I'll want to take photos or a nap when I'm done!

File.Name: g713b_g713-007.jpg
Notes:
28216129201 I don't know what this photo shows...

This is a small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) hiding in a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). But look, some other insect seems to be cleaning its tongue/proboscis, and the ambush bug seems to be obliging. Reminds me of the "cleaner fish" that I saw many years ago at Catalina Island cleaning parasites off larger fish. I can't tell what the other insect is. It did a perfect job of hiding behind a flower stamen. If not cleaning, something else interesting is going on! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2016)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g713c_g713-022.jpg
Notes: 28190490162 This is a nasty biting fly in our mountains, on my hand. Photo and specimen are not in great shape! I swatted the fly, but it stuck in my hand. They hurt! My first thought was deerfly, but despite being not much larger than a house fly, I believe it is a Horse Fly in the family Tabanidae of the order Diptera - possibly genus Apatolestes. Usually the Rocky Mountain Bite Flies (Symphoromyia, Rhagionidae, Diptera) - see this photo - are the worst biters in our mountains, but they've been scarce this year. The small gray horse flies get the crown. I guess there's always something! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2016)
CommonNames: Horse Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Flies

File.Name: g714a_g713-004.jpg
Notes:
28210591972 I'm not sure, but I think this may be a fasciated stem of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). I can't see enough of the stem to see if it is distinctively flattened, but the bend in the stem and the abundance of flowers suggest it. Many plants get fasciated, some often and others (like this one) rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. I'll have to get back to the plant when the flowers are done to get a better look. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2016)

I was tired when I got home from cardiac rehab today, as I should be after a workout. The breeze was starting to pick up, so I took it easy for the rest of the day. There's plenty of time for new photos tomorrow!
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: g714b_g709-048.jpg
Notes: 28314583695 Here are two photos of a remarkable Thread-waisted Digger Wasp (genus Ammophila, Sphecidae, Apoidea, Hymenoptera). It's actually filling in the hole of its nest, barely visible behind the stalk of the abdomen in the photo on the left. See this photo for another shot of the same wasp. I'm not sure if it was done with that nest, or whether the photographer upset it. It's also an interesting butt shot, so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2016)

File.Name: g714c_g708-013.jpg
Notes: 28032769440 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) inside a globular flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) by the river. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2016)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: g715a_g715-011.jpg
Notes:
28333895415 This pair of Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterflies is thinking about mating - at least the male butterfly on the left is! These butterflies have strong sexual dimorphism. The males (left) are mostly white above, with dark chevrons along the wing margins. The females (right) has more extensive dark markings above. I found this pair on a flowering branch of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) by the river today. That happens to be their primary host plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2016)

Today was a nice enough day, but the breeze never let up. Frustrating. There really aren't many summer flowers by the river yet, but there are a few. If you can find flowers in this fifth drought year, you will likely find interesting insects.

File.Name: g715b_g715-025.jpg
Notes: 28333747085 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies have a way of looking invisible and suddenly snapping to life when they open their wings! That's probably a way to scare off predators. I got these two photos a few seconds apart of new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) on the dry river bed today. The butterflies mostly keep their wings closed. The trick is to catch them the moment they open them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2016)

File.Name: g715c_g715-019.jpg
Notes: 28229974222 This isn't a great photo, but it's a remarkable fly! Look close - despite its petiolate abdomen, it has two wings, halteres, and fly-like eyes and antennae. This is a wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (Conopidae, Diptera) on a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). The fly is surely genus Physocephala, probably Physocephala burgessi, but cp. P. texana. The fly took wing a moment after I got this photo, and flew with the wind. Uh, it has impressive genitalia. This was a lucky find for Fly Day Friday, HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2016)
ScientificName: Conopidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Physocephala; Physocephala burgessi; P. texana
associatedTaxa: Conopidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Physocephala; Physocephala burgessi; P. texana

File.Name: g716a_g716-020.jpg
Notes:
28275519611 Here are two different kinds of galls on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Both are the work of different tiny Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). On the left is a striking "saucer gall" which may actually be the work of several different wasps: the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas), the Disk Gall Wasp (Andricus parmula), or the disk form of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed). I'm not sure how to tell them apart. On the right is an unusually pale colony of "naked galls" of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus). A few of the capsules do have hairs, but not long enough to grow together like the galls in my [Previous] photo. This photo has an interesting contrast of textures. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2016)

It was quite breezy today, so I mostly got photos on the scrub oak - with stiff stems that don't sway too much in the wind. I suspect the breeze was just "fog wind" though the marine fog layer stayed along the coast. I could see a flat layer of hazy air above the fog that came right up into the mountains. I suspect that's the turbulent air that occurs along the temperature inversion above the fog. Anyway, it was breezy today!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasps; Saucer Gall Wasp; Disk Gall Wasp; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Crystalline Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus gigas; Andricus parmula; Andricus crystallinus
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus gigas; Andricus parmula; Andricus crystallinus

File.Name: g716b_g716-027.jpg
Notes: 27737924864 This Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) is pretty much covered with galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). The galls are actually colonies made of individual capsules that usually grow together to make a brilliant mass, but sometimes they don't grow the interconnecting hairs leaving just the naked flask-like capsules in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g716c_g716-031.jpg
Notes: 27738313283 This is a colony of "naked galls" of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls are made of individual capsules that usually grow together to make a brilliant mass (see my [Next] photo), but sometimes they don't grow the interconnecting hairs leaving just the naked flask-like capsules in this photo. For some reason, I've been finding more of these "naked galls" than usual this year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2016)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
