File.Name: g717a_g717-030.jpg
Notes:
27761963223 This is a lovely female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly visiting a violet flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae. I was already in close trying to photograph the flower, when the butterfly flew into my photo. Think quick! The main flowers of these plants are done, but this small axillary flower still draws a crowd. (See my [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2016)

It was breezy again this morning. I stayed in our canyon and got some nice photos despite the breeze/

File.Name: g717b_g717-029.jpg
Notes: 27761603733 This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a flower of the violet form of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae. The main flowers of these plants are done, but this small axillary flower still draws a crowd. (See my [Next] photo.) What a mighty proboscis the wasp has! I don't know about those other thorn-like appendages under its abdomen. These critters are hard to photograph. They can see you coming with those large eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2016)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: g717c_g717-023.jpg
Notes: 28377162785 Here are two different flowers of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family growing on two different plants, just to show how variable they are. I want to photograph every one! They're just about done in the higher mountains where I first found them, but they're just starting in our canyon at home where I got this photo. The lowest ones I've found are near the Paradise Store. That's a done shrub of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2016)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Calochortus weedii; Liliaceae; Mimulus longiflorus
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Calochortus weedii; Liliaceae; Mimulus longiflorus

File.Name: g718a_g718-014.jpg
Notes:
28401541655 This is a hoard of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) on a brand new flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains. This is one of the first flowers I've seen this year, and the beetles seem to be loving it to death. You wouldn't think it driving on the highway over San Marcos Pass, but we have a succession of new flowers all summer long. This is remarkable given our usual summer drought, in the fifth year of a state-wide drought. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2016)

Today was a bit warmer and less breezy than it's been, very nice.
CommonNames: Beetles; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: g718b_g718-024.jpg
Notes: 28118300460 Here are two photos of the same leaf of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family - showing fuzzy galls both above and below the leaf. You can match up the galls in both photos. The same kind of gall seems to occur on the old male catkins. I don't know whose galls these are, and they don't seem to be in my book. See this photo for another shot of the same kind of gall. If you look at the very top of the shot on the left, you can see open pistils of a female flower. This is very peculiar since I found proper pistillate flowers two months ago, see this photo. I don't understand. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2016)

File.Name: g718c_g717-014.jpg
Notes: 27784077294 This is another diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). The flowers in this patch are just about done after a good month, though there are still a few new ones like this poking up. I suspect that when the flowers go, the moths will follow. Fortunately, there is another smaller patch in the shade along our creek that has not even begun flowering yet. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2016)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g719a_g719-017.jpg
Notes:
28388844876 I found this little Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working flowers of non-native Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the edge of the garden. The butterfly was totally involved with the flowers and let me get close - though it never did turn completely sideways for me, usually presenting its face or hind parts to me. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2016)

I had cardiac rehab this morning, and I got a proper workout! I was ready for a nap when I got home, but I did my duty and spent some time in the garden with my camera first. It was quite warm today, in the low 90s.

File.Name: g719b_g719-004.jpg
Notes: 27804721654 This is an almost mating pair of Green Bottle Flies (family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera) on a spent flower head of a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. I think of these as trash flies. Maybe they are species Lucilia sericata, maybe not. They're not actually mating, but they sure are thinking about it! Now is this legal for the Insect Hump Day group? (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2016)
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; Lucilia sericata
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; Lucilia sericata

File.Name: g719c_g715-021.jpg
Notes: 28342428741 Here is another photo of a remarkable fly from last Friday, see this photo. Look close - despite its petiolate abdomen, it has two wings, halteres, and fly-like eyes and antennae. This is a wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (Conopidae, Diptera) on a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). The fly is surely genus Physocephala, probably Physocephala burgessi, but cp. P. texana. The fly took wing a moment after I got this photo, and flew with the wind. Uh, what is that red goo near the tips of its antennae? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2016)
ScientificName: Conopidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Physocephala; Physocephala burgessi; P. texana

File.Name: g720a_g720-014.jpg
Notes:
28337384422 This is a handsome little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I hope that it's a sign that there will soon be more small butterflies - they've been scarce this summer. Sure the flowers are overexposed, but the wings on this shady side of the butterfly look just right. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2016)

It was hot today, into the mid-90s. Along with the heat, there was some breeze today. Hot and windy is a bad combination - fire weather for sure.

File.Name: g720b_g720-018.jpg
Notes: 27826068863 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a single flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. It looks flecked with gold. Maybe the fly is genus Geron or Thevenetimyia? (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2016)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Geron; Thevenetimyia

File.Name: g720c_g720-008.jpg
Notes: 28363463931 I believe this is a Leaf-footed Bug(Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. There are many different kinds, and I won't try to guess. It looks a bit like an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, but it's preying on flowers instead of other insects. It's medium-sized , 1/2 or 3/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2016)

File.Name: g721a_g720-011.jpg
Notes:
27845669044 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of (I believe) a Leaf-footed Bug(Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. See this photo from yesterday. It really does have an interesting behind - like an open pipe! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2016)

I had cardiac rehab again this morning, and I got a proper workout! It was also quite hot today, in the mid-90s at home, growing breezy as the day went on. The combination of heat and tiredness and breeze made it easy to skip new photos today. I'll get out tomorrow for sure.
ScientificName: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: g721b_g718-002.jpg
Notes: 28357394342 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a little beetle (Coleoptera) on a non-native (but ubiquitous) flower cluster of Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the mountains. I have to guess it's a Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae) the way its abdomen sticks out beyond the wing covers, but I'm not sure. These little beetles seem very common right now, both in the mountains and at the river. I should know what they are. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2016)

File.Name: g721c_g719-014.jpg
Notes: 28461977505 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working flowers of non-native Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the edge of the garden. Its real abdomen is below, but they have this interesting eye-like shapeon their hind wings that they keep in motion as they rub their hind wings together at rest. I assume this is a defense thing, to distract would be predators from the important stuff. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2016)

File.Name: g722a_g722-022.jpg
Notes:
27865130004 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's the first one I've seen this year, on new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). These butterflies should become more common as the summer moves on. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2016)

It was downright hot today, up to 102∞ along the mostly dry river where I was. It's been breezy as well. We're under both NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning tonight, a bad combination. The good news is that it's expected to be 10∞ cooler tomorrow.

File.Name: g722b_g722-025.jpg
Notes: 27865773203 I'm pretty sure this is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera, probably genus Cylindromyia. It's not the best photo, but it shows the fly. This one is working a new flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). This is probably better as a "butt shot" but I got the photo today on Friday, so HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2016)

File.Name: g722c_g720-019.jpg
Notes: 27865643753 This is another shot of a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a single flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. It looks flecked with gold, and I like the iridescent rainbow colors on its wing. I believe the fly is genus Geron or Thevenetimyia. Compare this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2016)

File.Name: g723a_g723-004.jpg
Notes:
27886773683 This is a real Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I found it on the tile sink counter of our bathroom late last night, and put it is a small custard dish to wait for morning. This is one insect I didn't want to find - especially in our house! Apparently, they live and feed in Dusky-footed Woodrat (Neotoma fuscipes) nests, but they can move also into houses. I'm not surprised - the woodrats have moved into our house too in the past, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2016)

These insects are a potential problem because they are known to be a potential host of Chagas disease, caused by the parasite Trypanosoma cruzi. My book (Charles Hogue, The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin, 1974) says that "The Western Cone-Nose Bug often harbors a trypanosome parasite apparently identical to the one found in the disease. It may be a non-virulent strain." There's more info at the CDC. We'll keep them out of our bed!

The NWS forecast was off. It wasn't cooler today, but hotter - 100∞ at home on the upstairs porch and 104∞ down by the river. (It's still 100∞ by the river as I write this at 6:30 pm!) That's really too hot to be out in the sun looking for photos, though I got a few. We're still under a Red Flag Warning tonight, though the winds have subsided. Now the NWS is saying that tomorrow will have a high of 83∞. Do you believe it?
Locality: It's still 100∞ by the river as I write this at 6:30 pm!)  That's really too hot to be out in the sun looking for photos
CommonNames: Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug; Dusky-footed Woodrat; Chagas disease; The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin
ScientificName: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi
associatedTaxa: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi

File.Name: g723b_g722-011.jpg
Notes: 27885703114 This is looking up at a handsome Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in a shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). If you look close, you can see the groove that its spike-like proboscis fits into. I actually wasn't shooting straight up - more to the side. I have another photo of this insect that shows the four spurs of it's name. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2016)
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: g723c_g723-013.jpg
Notes: 28470207646 This is a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. What pretty desert colors it has. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2016)

File.Name: g724a_g724-019.jpg
Notes:
28495177716 I found this small Bumblebee (Bombus, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower - that doesn't look at all like a flower. This is native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. Sometimes the actual white flowers are visible through the stiff green bracts, but sometimes they aren't. Somehow the bees know there's a flower in there! The plants are common throughout the mountains. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2016)

Today started pretty warm - up to 86∞ by 10 am. But then it started to cool down for the rest of the day - until it warmed up again to 91∞ in the evening. I believe the weather is changing...
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: g724b_g724-005.jpg
Notes: 28243357060 This is Dodder - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae or Convolvulaceae plant family - with flowers. Dodder is a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that gets its nutrients from another green plant like the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in this photo. You can see the bumpy "haustoria" - the structures that will penetrate the host's tissue near the top of the visible fennel stem. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2016)
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Convolvulaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Convolvulaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; haustoria

File.Name: g724c_g724-008.jpg
Notes: 28449012081 These are (nearly) the first open flowers I've seen this year of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The shrubs are dioecious, and this plant is male or staminate. This shrub is pretty much endemic in our mountains, with a few outliers. It is rather common for being endemic, growing from the summits to the river below. Did you notice that little wasp flying towards the open flower on the far left? (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2016)

File.Name: g725a_g725-012.jpg
Notes:
28446096622 This is another diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working flowers of non-native (here) Wild Bergamot aka Bee Balm (Monarda fistulosa, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2016)

I had two doctor appointments today in the early and late afternoon - all is well! I got home late, but found time for a few photos.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Wild Bergamot; Bee Balm
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monarda fistulosa; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g725b_g725-017.jpg
Notes: 27935283434 This is a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) hovering over the mint patch of our garden. It stayed pretty much in one place. I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens and no flash, though the ISO is up a notch. Sure it's a tight crop! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2016)

File.Name: g725c_g724-013.jpg
Notes: 27935138114 Here are the first new flowers of native composite Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a beautiful shrub with especially handsome foliage, 7 or 8 feet tall. This one is growing down a steep embankment, so I couldn't get closer to the flowers. This shrub only grows in the higher mountains, and it's just starting to flower. You have to admire these late-season plants that flower in mid-summer, even in a drought year. They keep the insects alive! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2016)

File.Name: g726a_g726-037.jpg
Notes:
28291051320 This is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly on a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden today - with more white zinnias behind. This happens to be the first one I've photographed this year, but I've been seeing them for some time. Apparently these butterflies are not native to North America, but they do have their charms. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)

The wind was already kicking up when I got home from cardiac rehab today, so I stayed in the garden to see what I could find. It did get uncomfortably warm today, up to the mid-90s at home - and 104∞ down by the dry river. The forecast is for more of the same tomorrow.

File.Name: g726b_g726-007.jpg
Notes: 28542536796 This is another lovely female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden today. Zinnias with single flowers are excellent garden plants for attracting butterflies and many other insects, but avoid the usual plants with doubled flowers. Insects can't get into them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)

File.Name: g726c_g726-002.jpg
Notes: 27957828534 This is a little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. The flower is hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden, and I'm pretty sure the butterfly is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola). It's interesting that the butterfly is entering the flower through a hole or slit previously made by (probably) a Carpenter Bee. Most insects seem to know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes agricola
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes agricola

File.Name: g727a_g727-011.jpg
Notes:
27980272434 This is a handsome caterpillar (Lepidoptera) munching a very glandular mid-summer flower bud of native Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The caterpillar may well be the Darker-spotted Straw Moth (Heliothis phloxiphaga, Noctuidae). see Jeffrey Caldwell's comment to this photo. The plant is interesting too, as the flowers open late in the day and close early in the morning. I'll try to get up early enough to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2016)

It was hot and breezy up here in the mountains today - 95∞ according to the NWS San Marcos Pass weather station about half a mile away, and 103∞ down by the river. We have a few more days of this hot weather.
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Madia sativa; Asteraceae; Heliothis phloxiphaga; Noctuidae

File.Name: g727b_g727-002.jpg
Notes: 28597178005 This is looking close at a handsome nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I'm not sure what kind it is or whether it's the same kind I've been finding earlier this year, e.g. this photo. I'll try to get a more revealing photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2016)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g727c_g727-014.jpg
Notes: 27981159283 There are still new flowers on this native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The shrub is distinctive for its narrow leaves which are quite unlike the more typical Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) which has broader leaves and is more common away from the coast, see this photo. There seem to be other differences as well, like the more pubescent stems. It's endemic on the south slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains, mostly west of San Marcos Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2016)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata

File.Name: g728a_g726-025.jpg
Notes:
28586233016 This is a "butt shot" of a bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably genus Eristalis, on a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. You can clearly see the "spurious vein" on its wings if you know what to look for. I'll (probably) post another photo of the front end of the fly tomorrow. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but still pretty warm - 90∞ on the upstairs porch. I got home from cardiac rehab today, and ... just stayed home. It was hazy today, and the air seemed smoky. I guess it's blowing in from Big Sur or Santa Clarita.

File.Name: g728b_g726-040.jpg
Notes: 28001614014 Here are two photos of my attempt to deconstruct the extraordinary growth I found on an oak twig (Quercus, Fagaceae) in my [Previous] photo. I believe these photos prove that it is an insect-induced gall. I brought the gall home and sliced it with a knife. It shattered - not surprising since it's made of hard wood. In the photo on the left, you can see a small exit hole, the sign of a tiny Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). (I'll make a note.) On the right, you can see the remains of the pupae that were nourished inside the gall. This is a gall that I've never seen before. It's about the size of an almond, and just as solid. Ron Russo doesn't mention this one in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), nor does Joyce Gross. I've only found this one. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)

File.Name: g728c_g724-017.jpg
Notes:
28539666421 The damnedest thing...

This is an extraordinary growth on a twig of a small oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) that I first found last October, see this photo. In that photo I called the oak Quercus pacifica, but I now believe it's Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei), it's surely a black oak. But the kind of oak doesn't matter as much as figuring out what this thing is. After getting this photo, I lightly twisted it, and it came off in my hand. I'm sure now that it is a gall, see my [Next] photo. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Quercus parvula; shrevei
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Quercus parvula; shrevei

File.Name: g729a_g729-018.jpg
Notes:
28533807342 Here are two attempts to capture the upper wings of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working the Zinnia (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden today. The butterflies always perch with their wings closed, and they rebound from the flowers so fast that it's hard to capture anything. At least these photos show the iridescence of the upper wings. I'll keep trying! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2016)

Warm weather continues - up to 97∞ on the pass today. The heat is making me feel lazy.

File.Name: g729b_g726-016.jpg
Notes: 28607797206 This is a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. Note that it doesn't have a "spurious vein" on its wings or a border along the trailing edge of the wing. Compare my [Previous] photo that shows the wings of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)

File.Name: g729c_g726-027.jpg
Notes: 28022909424 This is the front end of the same bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably genus Eristalis, that I show in my [Previous] photo. It's working a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. You can clearly see the "spurious vein" on its wings if you know what to look for, also the false margin along the trailing edge of the wing. Compare my [Next] photo that shows the wings of a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2016)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Zinnia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Zinnia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae

File.Name: g730a_g730-003.jpg
Notes:
28662867535 I have no doubt that this is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working the flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. The butterfly looks brand new, so this must be a new brood. The photo is a bit over-exposed, and it should look even more brilliant. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2016)

A group of former students - now close to 40 years old! - came by to visit this afternoon. I got a late start on these photos, so I'll keep the comments simple.

File.Name: g730b_g729-011.jpg
Notes: 28046316343 This is a beautiful female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I had to double-check the ID because this one seems more contrasty than the ones I usually find down by the river, e.g. this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2016)

File.Name: g730c_g729-002.jpg
Notes: 28662634675 This is a Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly taking moisture in the garden. This is an uncommon local butterfly, which is hard to photograph. They are well camouflaged and quite camera shy which makes them hard to get close to. This is the first time I've seen one in the garden, and I was happy to get any photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2016)

File.Name: g731a_g731-008.jpg
Notes:
28402002130 Be careful little bees - some of those flowers have claws!

This is a small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) sitting on a staminate flower of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are predators that can bring down prey much larger than they are, see this photo. Yep, they have crab claws! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 July 2016)

It was hazy and cooler today - and quite breezy. I went into the higher mountains to see what I could find.
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae

File.Name: g731b_g730-025.jpg
Notes: 28653772226 I believe this photo shows two different kinds of Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies working a flower of the violet form of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. These flowers are mostly finished, but they keep putting out small axillary flowers that still draws a crowd. The butterflies are similar but different. The one on the right is larger and darker than the one on the left, though both have a similar pattern of spots below. I guess the one on the right is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) and the one on the left is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola). I could be wrong. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2016)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; California Thistle; Umber Skipper; Rural Skipper

File.Name: g731c_g730-019.jpg
Notes: 28685608185 This tiny humpbacked Fly (Diptera) has fallen into the grasp of an even tinier Crab Spider (Thomisidae). I found this little drama on a stem of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the creek. I'm pretty sure the fly is a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae), probably genus Thevenetimyia, see this photo. I guess the happy spider is genus Mecaphesa. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2016)
CommonNames: Fly; Crab Spider; California Thistle; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Thomisidae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Thevenetimyia; Mecaphesa

File.Name: g801a_g801-004.jpg
Notes:
28677954056 This is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. Maybe this one is Pepsis thisbe with that "dark submarginal band basal to the hyaline edge of the wing" - see BugGuide. This one is smaller than our usual Pepsis mildei, but still substantial. They are quite camera shy. This is the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 August 2016)

It's been cooler today, but still up to 97∞ by the river where I was. It's expected to gradually cool down to "normal" over the next few days. I'm ready for it.
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Pepsis thisbe; Pepsis mildei

File.Name: g801b_g801-035.jpg
Notes: 28425766790 Here is a lovely little Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) perched on a dead stem by the river today. It's probably a female, but I have no idea what kind it is. What else can I say? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 August 2016)

File.Name: g801c_g801-050.jpg
Notes: 28710173985 Here are two photos of a small net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. I'm pretty sure it's an Issid Planthopper in the genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. They have a goofy looking front end like a cardboard box. This is the first one I've been able to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: g802a_g802-003.jpg
Notes:
28732139205 I believe this is the slug-like caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. I'm not sure what kind it is. Most of these caterpillars are green, so this white one - on white flowers - is interesting. It's pretty good camouflage on the white flowers of this native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Alice says it's a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly, see comments below. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2016)

I had cardiac rehab this morning and it was already breezy when I got home. I did get a few photos anyway.
CommonNames: Gossamer-Wing Butterfly; California Buckwheat; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Strymon melinus

File.Name: g802b_g801-002.jpg
Notes: 28654749631 This is the chrysalis of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar hanging by a loop on a stem of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. I wonder what's happening inside? I've found a number of the old chrysalis husks that have a torn hole in the side, which I reckon is the work of Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) parasitoids, see this photo. We cut the stem and collected this chrysalis, so I'll keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2016)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: g802c_g801-019.jpg
Notes: 28447469230 Here are two more shots of mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) wasps working new flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. Maybe these are Pepsis thisbe, see my [Previous] photo. They are quite camera shy, and it was breezy to boot. They hunt for tarantula spiders for their offspring, but they favor the summer flowers for sustenance. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 August 2016)
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Pepsis thisbe

File.Name: g803a_g803-003.jpg
Notes:
28134981704 Synchronized swallowtails...

Here are two different swallowtail butterflies side-by-side on flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa or A. curassavica, Asclepiadaceae) milkweed in the garden this morning. That's a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) on the left and a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) on the right. They are unmistakable when they are together, but it gets tricky when they are alone because the pale swallowtails sometimes get cream-colored. This isn't the best photo, but it shows the butterflies. I believe the main difference is the shape of the forewings which is clearly visible here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2016)

I had an errand in town at midday, but I got this nice photo in the morning before I left. It was pretty breezy when I got home. It did get into the low 80s this afternoon, but it's been pleasantly cool today.
CommonNames: Butterfly Weed; Pale Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtail

File.Name: g803b_g731-009.jpg
Notes: 28136985313 Here is a mating pair of true bugs (order Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - probably Redcoat Seed Bugs (Melanopleurus belfragei) in the family Lygaeidae. They are "doing it" on a staminate flower cluster of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. HIHD! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 July 2016)

File.Name: g803c_g731-018.jpg
Notes:
28647016122 This is a mating pair of small Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a new flower stalk of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. They seem to spend a lot of time doing this. HIHD! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 July 2016)

Uh, there's something odd about this photo. The yellow female on the right seems to have a straw coming out of its thorax, with an open white cap and white froth at the base. I can't explain this.
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Solidago californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: g804a_g804-007.jpg
Notes:
28488542030 Look at those swollen pedipalps - I believe this is a male Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) on a mint leaf in the garden this morning. This spider is a veteran with three missing legs on its right side, but it's making do. This is the first one of these spiders I've managed to get a photo of this year. They'll become more common as we approach arachtober! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2016)

I got a reprieve from cardiac rehab today because the nurse in charge is one vacation for a couple of weeks, and I've been reduced to just once a week. I made up for it by taking a good walk today. It was a beautiful day - warm but not hot, and calm this morning.

File.Name: g804b_g801-044.jpg
Notes: 28772444775 This is the remarkable golden butt of a large Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river a few days ago. I believe the fly is genus Belvosia in the subfamily Exoristinae. It's interesting that BugGuide has no other records from California. I'll post another photo of the rest of the fly tomorrow. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 August 2016)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Belvosia; Exoristinae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Belvosia; Exoristinae

File.Name: g804c_g729-012.jpg
Notes: 28157496893 This is a "butt shot" of a handsome male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. See this photo for the rest of the same butterfly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2016)

File.Name: g805a_g805-030.jpg
Notes:
28761583626 This is a very large Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) wasp inside a flowering shrub of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This wasp is dragonfly size! It looks black in this photo, but you can see a few hints of blue - so blue that it's black. I guess this is a female Pepsis mildei with those curled orange antennae. I figure the flower heads are about 1/2 inch across, so how big is the wasp? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 August 2016)

I don't know why, but the wind always seems to kick up just when I get out of the car by the river! Despite the breeze, it was another lovely day, in the 80s but not too hot.

associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Pepsis mildei

File.Name: g805b_g805-017.jpg
Notes: 28761380906 This is a "butt shot" of a wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (Conopidae, Diptera) on a flower bud of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). The fly is surely genus Physocephala, probably Physocephala burgessi, but cp. P. texana. That wasp-like petiolate abdomen is remarkable for a fly. See this photo for the other end of a similar fly. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 August 2016)

File.Name: g805c_g801-040.jpg
Notes: 28715923641 Here are two shots of a large bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river a few days ago, showing both ends of the fly. It's standout feature is sure its golden butt! See this photo from yesterday for BBBT. I believe the fly is genus Belvosia in the subfamily Exoristinae. It's interesting that BugGuide has no other records from California, but I don''t think I could confuse this fly for any other. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 August 2016)

File.Name: g806a_g806-013.jpg
Notes:
28529450890 This is a handsome Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. I like photographing these flower-loving wasps, though they are always a challenge. They seem to see everything with those large eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2016)

The marine layer fog came close to our 2500 foot level on the pass - but it didn't quite make it over the mountains. Today was warm but not too hot, and surprisingly calm. It was a lovely day.
CommonNames: Sand Wasp; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: g806b_g805-044.jpg
Notes:
28196146594 I found this venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri aka C. oreganus helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel" - along Paradise Road yesterday near the second crossing. It was right out in the road where it wouldn't have a chance if any other car came by, so I stopped and scooted it off the road - getting a couple of photos as it moved away up the embankment. It's a good sized snake, 3 or 4 feet long with 9 rattles on it's tail. I don't see these snakes very often - just one or two a year, or none at all. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 August 2016)

By the way, don't wait to hear or see a rattlesnake's rattle. The real signs of a rattlesnake are the black color and the triangular head - not visible in this photo. I'm not particularly afraid of rattlesnakes, but I do worry about stepping on them. When I inadvertently step of our cat, she hisses - but if you step on a rattlesnake, it might strike! Really, the snakes do everything they can to avoid contact. Respect their wishes - "Don't tread on me!
ScientificName: Crotalus viridis helleri; C. oreganus helleri
associatedTaxa: Crotalus viridis helleri; C. oreganus helleri

File.Name: g806c_g803-019.jpg
Notes: 28736567481 I found this neat little treefrog in the mint patch of the garden a few days ago. I believe it's a Baja California Treefrog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but it may be some other species. It's small and cute. Five years of drought has taken its toll on our amphibians, so it's good to see these new frogs. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2016)

File.Name: g807a_g807-011.jpg
Notes:
28836983655 This is a white-fringed Duskywing (genus Erynnis) butterfly taking nectar in an odd place - a missing acorn on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This may be the work of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This wasp galls the acorn, which starts a large flow of honeydew - see this photo. Another possibility is that this is the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina, which I don't know anything about. There's some info on p.72 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. Either way, the result is real live oak honey coming from the acorn cups. It's sweet, I've tasted it. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2016)

Today was another lovely day, temps in the mid-80s and not much breeze. May there be many more like today!
CommonNames: Duskywing; Coast Live Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy Nut
ScientificName: Erynnis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina
associatedTaxa: Erynnis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina

File.Name: g807b_g807-041.jpg
Notes:
28731980722 I believe this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera). That "double abdomen" is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes? This one is working the first new flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden, with orange zinnias in the background. They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck! (See this photo.) I haven't found any nests yet this year, but the wasps are out there. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2016)

Hah - the wasp is pubescent so it could be a Hairy Potter Wasp!
ScientificName: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Eumenes; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Eumenes; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: g807c_g807-027.jpg
Notes: 28836582045 This is a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider wrapping its prey. The common name comes from the silver/white part on the spider's top side (screen bottom), barely visible in this photo. A better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen. Their color is highly variable. I found this one inside a flowering shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. I'm not sure what the prey is, but I suspect it's some kind of wasp. There's an interesting long structure visible near the right-most leg of the spider. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2016)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: g808a_g808-035.jpg
Notes: 28860706675 This medium-size Black Bear (Ursus americanus) strolled through the garden about half an hour ago! I got this photo as it was going away down the driveway. The bear seems to have a tag on its ear, so I wonder where it comes from? Normally a bear would have to face our dog Brodie. (He's a poodle/terrier mix - I would run too!) But Julie's up north with the grandkids for a couple of weeks, and Brodie is staying at Doggie Day Care for the period. Without Brodie around, I've been seeing deer, so I guess I'm not surprised to see this bear. Bears and mountain lions should be invisible where people live. There will be problems if it makes a habit of this. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2016)

File.Name: g808b_g808-004.jpg
Notes:
28829295446 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) sampling the Live Oak honey forming a froth in an empty acorn cup of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Maybe it's a Cuckoo Bee (genus Sphecodes, Halicticae)? See my [Previous] photo for more info about the honey. It's sweet, I've tasted it. Insects like it. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2016)

*BREAKING NEWS*
We just had a Black Bear (Ursus americanus) stroll through the garden! I'll post a photo if it comes out.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coast Live Oak; Cuckoo Bee; Black Bear
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halicticae; Ursus americanus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halicticae; Ursus americanus

File.Name: g808c_g808-005.jpg
Notes:
28245200923 Here are two different oaks (Fagaceae) producing real oak honey in our mountains. On the left is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree and on the right is a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub. Both are producing flows of real oak honey coming from the acorn cups. It's sweet, I've tasted it. Insects like it. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2016)

I'm not sure what causes this. One possibility (at least on the Coast Live Oak) is that it's the work of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. Another possibility is that it's the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina, which I don't know anything about. There's some info on p.72 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. Either way, the honey flow starts, the acorn turns dark, and it's finally pushed out of its cup by the pressure of the honey. This affliction sometimes accounts for half the acorns on a tree or shrub.

Notes:
28859365165 I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, which gave me a chance to look for galls on native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. This fine tree doesn't grow in the mountains. This is a white oak that shares some galls with Scrub Oak (Q. berberidifolia). All of these galls are instigated by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera).

The gall on the left is the work of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii). I'm not sure about the one in the middle, maybe the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras trimaculosum)? I guess the gall on the right is the work of a Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), though it doesn't look quite right. Galls aren't creatures, though they are the work of creatures. They are remarkably consistent by type, but there is some variability. You have to hatch out the wasps and identify them to be sure of your ID. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 August 2016)

File.Name: g818a_g818-006.jpg
Notes:
28984582332 This is the smoke plume coming from the Rey Fire yesterday afternoon at about 4:00 pm. This is looking north from a place on San Marcos Pass, with Fremont Ridge in the foreground, then Loma Alta on the left and Little Pine on the right, then the San Rafael Mountains in the back. It was curious that the strong winds were coming from the east and south-east - just the opposite of the usual winds from the north and north-west. This was good news for us as it blew the fire into the ranch land and away from us. The fire stands at 600 acres and 20% contained. It was named after Rancho San Fernando Rey where the wildfire is mostly burning. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2016)

It was a little crazy last night with the fire, the highway closed, and Julie arriving at the airport - all at the same time. I tried to post this photo later last night, but our internet connection was unusable. Maybe everyone in the mountains was trying to get online info at the same time?

File.Name: g819a_g819-058.jpg
Notes:
28810961030 I believe this is a large C-130 converted cargo plane putting down a line of fire retardant on the Rey Fire this afternoon at about 4:00 pm. It's a four-engine prop plane - but huge, with a big load. It has military colors rather than the usual red and white CDF colors, so I wonder if it's a military plane? This photo is deceptive because the ridge in the foreground is some distance from the action in the background, with the dry Santa Ynez River in between. This is on the south-facing side of Loma Alta - but most of the wildfire activity is on the backside of this hill, where I can't see it. The fire laid down all morning, but it kicked up again in the afternoon with the wind. The winds were again coming from the east and south-east - just the opposite of the usual winds from the north and north-west. This was good news for us folks living south of the fire, and probably the reason why the fire people are putting a line here - to keep it from spreading over the pass towards the city of Santa Barbara. I got this photo from the Vista Point along Highway 154. There were a couple of dozen people there taking in the scene. The wildfire now stands at 2700 acres. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 August 2016)

Our internet connection is slow again tonight, but not impossible. I'll stick with just one photo. Darn, I had good bug butt and fly photos, but they'll keep.

File.Name: g820a_g820-074.jpg
Notes:
28833135820 This is smoke from the Rey Fire rising over Fremont Ridge - as seen from our upstairs porch! The smoke looks close but it's actually down the mountains, across the dry river bed, and up a canyon. This is the east end of the fire, burning up and over Little Pine Mountain. The smoke is forming a firehead or Pyrocumulus cloud. There is also a west end of the fire that is flaring up, burning behind Loma Alta towards Santa Cruz Creek and Peachtree Canyon. The word is now that the fire has burned 10,732 acres and is 10% contained. The containment is reduced from 20% yesterday. I guess they're counting the dry river bed as one leg of the containment, so the percent drops as the perimeter increases. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2016)

Yep, wildfires can make their own pyrocumulus rainclouds! Fire and water seem like opposites, but they have interesting connections that produce these clouds. It's an old middle school science teacher's trick to hold a dry glass over a candle flame. In a moment, water vapor will condense inside the glass. The chemical "fire equation" is something like this: CnHm + O2 --> CO2 + H20. That H2O is of course water. Fire really does produce water vapor as a product of combustion, and no doubt it evaporates more water from the ground and burning plants. Hot smoke and water vapor rise by convection into an area of lower atmospheric pressure. In time the smoke cools and gets pushed out of the way by more hot air rising from below. That creates the mushroom cloud shape. (Nuclear bombs work the same way.) As water vapor cools in lower pressure, it condenses into droplets, and the smoke provides plenty of particles to act as nucleation sites - like cloud seeding! The result is that wildfires can make their own rainclouds! See this photo for another Pyrocumulus cloud seen from the air.

Our internet connection is being flakey again, so I'll stick with this one photo. Maybe everyone in the mountains was trying to get online info at the same time? I'm a little distracted anyway with the wildfire so close.

Notes:
29066318661 This is "Tanker 910" - a converted DC-10 wide-body jet air tanker that can drop 12,000 gallons (45,600 liters) of fire retardant in 8 seconds. It's a mighty aircraft dropping a mighty load of fire retardant on the Rey Fire this afternoon at about 2:00 pm. There an info page at Wikipedia. This is the upper part of the creek that feeds the pool above the fourth crossing of Paradise. The fire is active on the east end - burning on the back side of Camuesa Peak and heading towards Madulce Peak in the Dick Smith Wilderness. Some of this land burned by the Rey Fire has burned before - in the Zaca Fire (2007) and the White Fire (2013). That has to help. As of this morning, the fire stands at 18,839 acres - no doubt more by now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 August 2016)

It got a bit smoky at home last night - the first smoke we've had from this nearby wildfire. There was a real airshow of air tanker planes out fighting the wildfire today. I got lots of photos. I'm tired tonight after being out in the sun all day, but I'll post more tomorrow.

File.Name: g822a_g821-082.jpg
Notes:
29061479552 Here are some of the larger air tankers I saw working the Rey Fire yesterday. I was up on East Camino Cielo road on the crest of the Santa Ynez Mountains, and the planes came pretty close as they circled around for their runs. I got these photos with my usual 105 mm macro lens - good for airplanes as well as butterflies. From top-left clockwise here are:

* "Tanker 910" is a converted DC-10 wide-body jet air tanker;
* Coulson Aviation 131 is a modified Lockheed C-130Q;
* Aero Fliteís Tanker 160 is a modified Avro RJ-85;
* C-130 Hercules (number 6) is military with "USAF" and "California Air Guard" on it.

I sometimes wish insects came with ID numbers - but what would be the challenge? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 August 2016)

The Rey Fire is still going, though its gotten hard to see. The wind has shifted a bit, blowing the smoke towards us, along with an ever-so-slight "snow flurry" of white ashes. Also East Camino Cielo is now closed beyond Painted Cave. It wasn't very closed this morning - just a "Road Closed" sign off to the side, so I went up to my usual place anyway. The fire was rather quiet. I didn't want to go back up the closed road again in the afternoon, but I don't see the usual large pyrocumulus clouds. As of this morning, the fire stands 23,546 acres and is back up to 20% contained.

(Soundtrack - Grateful Dead, "Fire on the Mountain", here)

File.Name: g822b_g821-148.jpg
Notes: 28545319904 This is a military C-130 converted cargo plane dropping a line of fire retardant on the Rey Fire on a steep hillside. If you look in the lower left corner near the bottom you can see a pair of bulldozers hurrying out of the way! They didn't get caught, nor those two people (I think?) on the road below the plane. There was a real airshow of air tanker planes out fighting the wildfire yesterday, see my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 August 2016)

File.Name: g822c_g821-074.jpg
Notes: 29166743655 This is another air tanker laying down a line of fire retardant on the Rey Fire yesterday afternoon. The plane is number 131 - a Lockheed C-130Q owned by Coulson Aviation. There's some info on the plane here. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 August 2016)

File.Name: g823a_g823-018.jpg
Notes:
29190468005 I found this perfect fragment of an ash leaf in the garden this morning. It must have been carried here from the Rey Fire, some miles away. It wonder if it was caught up in one of those mighty pyrocumulus clouds, and carried by the wind - intact! - all the way to our garden. Maybe it's a Coast Live Oak leaf? (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2016)

The Rey Fire is still going strong, though its gotten hard to see. The wind has shifted a bit, blowing the smoke towards us, along with ashes like this. It just looks like haze. Also East Camino Cielo road is now closed to all but residents at the highway and to everyone at Painted Cave Road. As of this morning, the fire stands 29,664 acres and is up to 30% contained. It's burning in Indian Creek and maybe Mono Creek, heading into the Dick Smith Wilderness away from everything - except for this unfortunate oak tree.

File.Name: g823b_g820-023.jpg
Notes:
28568088754 This handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly joined me in the garden a few days, taking water from the wet path. It sat there with wings closed for quite some time, only opening them this much. Finally I tried to nudge it, and it flew away. I don't see these butterflies very often up here in the mountains, sometimes in mid-winter like this photo. I don't really understand them. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2016)

You can't sit and watch a wildfire all day. I also got a few other photos in the last few days!

File.Name: g823c_g820-027.jpg
Notes:
29111797121 This female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is taking a break after nectaring from the succulent plant nearby. Hah - I was sitting right there facing the other way when this hummer showed up. I turned around and got the photo, contorting my tripod as I did so. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2016)

You can't sit and watch a wildfire all day. I also got a few other photos in the last few days!

File.Name: g824a_g824-017.jpg
Notes:
28589298204 This is a gorgeous Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae) in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera on mint flowers in the garden. Maybe it's Strophiona tigrina with those dark antennae? This beetle isn't large, but it sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2016)

The near-constant rumble of trucks and helicopters tells me that the Rey Fire is still active, but it's out of sight now, hidden behind hazy smoke and Road Closed signs. As of this morning, the wildfire stood at 31,255 acres with 34% containment. The fire has come to Mono Creek, where they are trying to contain it.

Darn, there are reports of *another fire* burning in Refugio Canyon near the coast!

File.Name: g824b_g824-009.jpg
Notes: 28589194834 I'm pretty sure this is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly on new mint flowers in the garden. It's making neat loops with its proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2016)

File.Name: g824c_g824-015.jpg
Notes: 28589072204 This unfortunate Honey Bee was impaled on a prickly pear cactus spine in the garden. I have no idea how this happened, though I can just imagine a tiny voice going D'oh! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2016)

File.Name: g825a_g821-013.jpg
Notes:
29232517235 This is a butt shot of a Green Bottle Fly (genus Lucilia, Calliphoridae, Diptera) on mint flowers in the garden. I think of these as trash flies, but they visit flowers as well. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2016)

Lots of butt shots today! That's because I had the first three shots already prepared for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group when I noticed the smoke from the Rey Fire nearby. The wildfire photo took precedence last Thursday, see this photo. The wildfire stands at 31,505 acres with 39% containment. Paradise Road is now open for residents only, a good sign that the fire has burned a long ways off. The small Bar Fire in Refugio Canyon was contained at 18 acres. The news is better.

File.Name: g825b_g813-011.jpg
Notes: 29198578896 This is a butt shot of a handsome Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on mint flowers in the garden. I'm pretty sure this is the same kind of fly as this photo, maybe genus Dipalta. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2016)

File.Name: g825c_g815-012.jpg
Notes: 28944421160 This is a cute butt shot of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, probably a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). This one is crawling away from me on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The nymphs look remarkably like the nymphs of Bush Katydids (Scudderia, Orthoptera), see my [Previous] photo. We happen to have both on the prowl right now, and I frequently have to do a double take to tell which one I'm looking at. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2016)

File.Name: g826a_g821-011.jpg
Notes:
28633004193 Here is another large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a leaf in garden, probably taking moisture. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes and antennae like an old-fashioned TV set. These are our "compost flies" - see this photo. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia. I'm really surprised I don't see more of these flies since there are dozens (at least!) of the large maggots in the compost bin. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2016)

I seem to have lots of fly photo for the Fly Day Friday group right now, more than I can post. That's because the Rey Fire interrupted me last week. The fire is still going strong, but it's a long ways off now instead of just down at the bottom of our canyon like it was last Friday. The estimated date of containment isn't for another week. We were in a marine layer cloud this morning, and this cooler weather has to be helping the fire effort. I was busy doing the SYVNHS newsletter today, so I hardly noticed. I'll finish that chore tomorrow.

File.Name: g826b_g819-006.jpg
Notes: 29145521272 This is an interesting little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden. If you look close, you can see that its lapping blue pollen from a stamen. I believe the little fly with the Popeye-like enlarged femurs is genus Syritta or Tropidia in the subfamily Eristalinae. See this photo from last year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2016)

File.Name: g826c_g819-026.jpg
Notes:
29253837285 Thank you Daniel Palmer for identifying this fly as Vanderwulpia atrophopodoides. It is indeed a Tachinid fly (Tachinidae, Diptera). I don't know anything else about it.

(Original comment)
I can't identify this colorful Fly (order diptera) that I found in the garden a few days ago. I spent some time at BugGuide looking for something similar - including doing a search for "diptera" and going through all the thumbnails. No luck. The red abdomen and striped thorax are striking. It's quite bristly, which suggests a tachinid; the striped thorax suggests a flesh fly. I don't know what it is. If you have an idea, please leave a comment. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2016)

File.Name: g827a_g825-014.jpg
Notes:
28654026733 I believe this is the slug-like caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly digging into a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. See this photo and Alice's comment - the spiky hairs on the caterpillar are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2016)

It was another gloomy morning in marine layer fog, but it didn't matter because I was finishing up the SYVNHS newsletter today. I'm done! I emailed it out for comments, and I'll get it to the printer on Monday. I reckon I'll even get out into the woods on Sunday for some new photos!

File.Name: g827b_g820-007.jpg
Notes: 28986577380 I found this odd winged ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) in the mint patch of the garden. It's clearly an ant, but it has pale colors like a termite. It's fairly large and has a single nodal bump. It didn't like the sun. It kept crawling into the shade so I kept nudging it back into the light. I wonder if it could be an alate queen, possibly a Carpenter Ant in the genus Camponotus? (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2016)

File.Name: g827c_g820-015.jpg
Notes: 29274829605 This is a small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for prey in garden mint flowers. You can clearly see its crab-like forelegs, and if you look close you can see it's mighty proboscis tucked under the armor plate of its head. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2016)

File.Name: g828a_g828-020.jpg
Notes:
29268396216 This is a ripe nut on a native shrub of Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I've cracked and tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2016)

After a few days of cool weather, it's become warm again - up to about 87∞ today. Tomorrow is expected to be downright hot with sundowner winds. We're under a Fire Weather Watch from Monday afternoon through Wednesday morning. As the NWS says, "A FIRE WEATHER WATCH MEANS THAT CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS ARE FORECAST TO OCCUR" - in all caps, like they really mean it!

File.Name: g828b_g828-031.jpg
Notes: 29193888462 Like the cherries in my [Previous] photo, there are lots of acorns on this particular shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) while many other shrubs are barren in this drought year. Not every acorn becomes a mighty oak! Some of these acorns have turned prematurely brown due to weevils or infection. Other acorns never developed and remain as tiny caps. It's still a good crop of fat healthy acorns, on this shrub at least. They provide winter food for many local critters. They're important. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2016)

File.Name: g828c_g817-022.jpg
Notes:
29223013681 Here are lots of wild cherries on a shrub of native Holly-leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. Many of the other shrubs have no fruit at all in this drought year, but a few shrubs here and there are loaded like this. The ripe red fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit and pits are actually poisonous. The flesh is thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching and boiling them to remove the bitter and poisonous "cyanogenic glycosides" that they contain. (Store-bought cherry pits and apple seeds also contain them.) When the fruit are dead ripe, the birds and critters usually get them first. I've never tried preparing the cherry pits, but I've always wanted to. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2016)

I got this photo just before the Rey Fire started. I'd like to reshoot it, but East Camino Cielo Road is still closed to support the fire efforts and the fruit are probably all gone into birds by now anyway! I'll get back to it as soon as I can.

File.Name: g829a_g829-038.jpg
Notes:
29327866355 "Big things have small beginnings."

This may be the start of a chain of events that led to the Rey Fire, still burning in the backcountry. An old Coast Live Oak tree came down across the power lines near the White Rock picnic area, and the shock pulled down at least two more power poles off to the right. I'm no expert, but I guess the fire started somewhere along this chain of events. The wildfire now stands at 78% contained and 32,601 acres burned, looking better! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2016)

They opened Paradise Road as far as the first crossing today, and I couldn't resist going for a look. To be honest, there's not much to see close to the road. The Rey Fire has mostly burned across the backcountry. The wildfire is still going, but it's pretty far away by now. (*knock knock*)

It was hot today, in the 90s at home and up to 100∞ by the river. There have been 20 mph sundowner wind gusts, but it could be worse. We're under a Red Flag Warning until Wednesday morning, when it should start cooling down.

They look awesome with that open body - they remind me of dragonflies! The trees in the background have been marked by Phos-Chek fire retardant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2016)

They opened Paradise Road as far as the first crossing today, and I couldn't resist going for a look. To be honest, there's not much to see close to the road. The Rey Fire has mostly burned across the backcountry. The wildfire is still going, but it's pretty far away by now. (*knock knock*)

File.Name: g829c_g829-055.jpg
Notes:
29039450300 This is burned - and unburned - ranch land on Rancho San Fernando Rey across the dry Santa Ynez River. There's an incoming helitanker coming in from the right. It's a Sikorsky CH-54B Sky Crane, Helicopter Transport Services 716, see my [Next] photo. You can see a stripe of red ground running across the photo that is a line of Phos-Chek fire retardant. The white spots on the hillside behind the helicopter are the ashes of burned yucca plants, which seem to burn exceptionally hot - see this photo. This area burned in the first rush of the Rey Fire. It's interesting how many trees didn't burn! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2016)

They opened Paradise Road as far as the first crossing today, and I couldn't resist going for a look. To be honest, there's not much to see close to the road. The Rey Fire has mostly burned across the backcountry.

File.Name: g830a_g830-006.jpg
Notes:
28726304784 Sometimes I get a photo that I can't explain at all. WTF??

I believe this is a once-mating pair of Underwing Moths (genus Catocala, Noctuidae, Lepidoptera). I found the pair sprawled over the spiny pad of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. I don't think either of them is skewered, they just ended up here. It looks like the moth on the left has lost the end of its genitalia, still attached to the moth on the right. If you look close you can see that it has a spine on it. The moth on the right is still alive, moving very slightly. Was it some rough sex? Like I said, I really can't explain this photo at all. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2016)

I had my usual Tuesday/Thursday cardiac rehab workout this morning. I got this photo in the garden when I got home. It was hot today, about 95∞ at home with calm air. It's hard to get too ambitious taking new photos when it's this hot!

File.Name: g830b_g829-011.jpg
Notes: 29315830936 I believe this is another caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly in the mint patch of the garden. If you look close, you can see it's tiny head sticking out next to the plant stem - like a turtle! I don't think I've ever noticed their heads before. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2016)

File.Name: g830c_g821-005.jpg
Notes: 29348877185 Here are a pair of little early-stage nymphs of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) in the mint patch of the garden. We seem to have lots of the beautiful nymphs right now, though I think this means the adults will be quite late this year. I got this photo over a week ago. I fell behind because of the wildfire and the newsletter, but I'm trying to catch up. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2016)

File.Name: g831a_g831-038.jpg
Notes:
29372885795 This is a mating pair of true bugs (order Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on flowers native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I don't know what kind of bugs they are, but their colors give them nice camouflage against the brown older flowers. Yeah, it's not quite in focus - but good enough! HIHD! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2016)

It was hot again today, but only up to 88∞, a bit cooler than yesterday. It's expected to gradually cool down over the next few days, maybe bringing morning fog back to the mountains. I'm not happy to see the fog, but cooler will be appreciated.

File.Name: g831b_g828-015.jpg
Notes: 29264558302 This is a handsome "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug, genus Thyanta. That's what I thought when I got the photo - I didn't notice its partner hiding inside the flower bracts until I got to my computer. This is actually a mating pair, "doing it" in a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. HIHD! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2016)

File.Name: g831c_g831-002.jpg
Notes:
28750796883 This is a close shot of the hardened or "sclerotized" genitalia of the Underwing Moth (genus Catocala, Noctuidae, Lepidoptera) on the left in my [Previous] photo. That word "sclerotized" refers to the hardening of the flexible chitin of the insect skin by the addition of insoluble cross-linked proteins - it becomes hard and shiny, like the "shell" or mouthparts of a beetle. Sclerotized genitalia are useful for insect taxonomy because they are tough and unique to species, but I don't often get a chance to see them. Heh, it gives a whole new meaning to the phrase "hook up"! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2016)

Author Vladimir Nabokov authored Lolita, Pale Fire, etc. and also collected butterfly genitalia:

"Initially, Nabokov considered a butterflyís locus of individuality to be in its wing patterns, which he reproduced in his drawings at a near-microscopic level of detail. Eventually, though, his attention shifted to genitalia, which evolve more slowly, and in a more linear way, than wing patterns. He produced hundreds, perhaps thousands, of illustrations of these intricate, baffling structures. As Blackwell and Johnson write, 'In the genitalia of butterflies, Nabokov discovered an inner space and forms of strange symmetry and asymmetry: beautiful hooks and hoods, forearms, pugilists, and clasps and combs, spurs and brushes and elbows, even hints of Klansmen and tiny caterpillars or elephants!'"
- Elif Batuman, "Vladimir Nabokov, Butterfly Illustrator", New Yorker, 23 March 2016

File.Name: g901a_g831-010.jpg
Notes:
28770325264 This is a "butt shot" - an "abdomen shot" anyway - of a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe the species Spilomyia interrupta or kin. This one is squatting on a mint leaf in the garden wagging its tail from side to side and chasing away other flies, maybe a territorial thing? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2016)

It was cooler today, but still warm - up to 85∞. The news is that East Camino Cielo road was opened this afternoon. I'll take a drive up there tomorrow...

File.Name: g901b_g824-019.jpg
Notes: 29394122785 This is a "butt shot" of the same Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) that I show in this photo. Maybe it's Strophiona tigrina? The beetle is on mint flowers in the garden, and I got this photo as it crawled away from me. There's something about the color of these beetles that makes them go from bright to dull at the slightest change of angle. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2016)

File.Name: g901c_g828-004.jpg
Notes: 28772220183 This is a revealing "butt shot" of a Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on its web in a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's a different spider with a slightly different shape than the one I show in this photo. (It's the top side of the spider that is silver.) You can see it's spinnerets near the screen-bottom of its abdomen. Above them you can see its trachael spiracle which carries oxygen to its lungs. This spider seems to have a pair of them, maybe two pair? Anterior to that, you can see the epigastric furrow and the nose-like epigynum which is the sexual organ of this lady spider. Sure, I had to look this all up, and I hope I got it right. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2016)

File.Name: g902a_g902-010.jpg
Notes:
29127323770 A meeting of small minds...

Here is a Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) face-to-face with an early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera). They are meeting inside a new white Cosmos flower in the garden, probably Cosmos bipinnatus. Of course the meeting was brief. A moment later they were on their separate ways. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2016)

What a nice day! It only got up to about 80∞. It was a little breezy at home because the marine layer fog was almost high enough to pour over the pass. The fog didn't make it, but some of the fog winds did, so I went up into the higher mountains now that the roads are open again. There was no sign of any fire activity.

File.Name: g902c_g831-007.jpg
Notes: 29306794022 This is looking face-to-face with a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe the species Spilomyia interrupta or kin. I show the other end of this same fly in my [Previous] photo, here. This one is sitting on a mint leaf in the garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2016)

File.Name: g903a_g903-026.jpg
Notes:
29402959936 Compare my [Previous] photo. I believe this is our other Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia longipalpis, Oxyopidae) in a flowering shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This one is smaller and thinner. It has long palps (it's a lady), white hairs on its abdomen, and it lacks colors on its abdomen. They sure look different, but is that enough to separate Peucetia longipalpis from Peucetia viridans? (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2016)

We were in a cloud today with some fog drip - note that neat leaf hanging from a web in a fog drop on the right There was fog wind too, of course. It did finally clear up around 2:00 in the afternoon. The next couple of days may well be more of the same. It's hard to get photos, but at least it's cooled down!

File.Name: g903b_g902-048.jpg
Notes: 29357297491 I believe this is a fat Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans, Oxyopidae) in a cluster of staminate flowers of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one has dark hairs and colorful markings on it's abdomen. Compare my [Next] photo for (maybe) the different species Peucetia longipalpis. This one seems to have the flowers wrapped up and I wonder if its spinning a nest. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 September 2016)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis
