File.Name: g903c_g902-014.jpg
Notes: 28815172863 This is a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata, Araneidae) spider on its orb web in a flowering shrub of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. This spider is considered a "garden spider" though I found this one in the woods. It's a large handsome spider that stays on its web - a good thing as you wouldn't want one crawling on you! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 September 2016)
CommonNames: Banded Argiope; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis

File.Name: g904a_g904-001.jpg
Notes:
29461621025 I found this interesting fly (Diptera) on a mint leaf in the garden this morning. It looks a bit like a Drosophilid or a Lauxaniid, but the characteristics aren't quite right and it might just be another Muscidae fly. This one is about the size of a housefly. It appears to have no oral vibrissae, finely aristate antennae, and forward pointing ocellar bristles. So what is it? It is quite pretty, and that's good enough - though if you recognize this fly, please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2016)

We were under a cloud today, but not quite in a cloud - it was cloudy, not foggy. I went back to the higher mountains. I want to go back to the river, but I'll wait until labor day weekend is done.

File.Name: g904b_g904-028.jpg
Notes: 28837369244 This is a mating pair of true bugs (order Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - probably Redcoat Seed Bugs (Melanopleurus belfragei) in the family Lygaeidae. They are "doing it" on new seed heads of a seed-bearing pistillate shrub of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. This shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. See my [Previous] photo for a male shrub and flower. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 September 2016)

File.Name: g904c_g902-056.jpg
Notes: 28839684773 This is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - almost certainly Largus californicus. This one is sucking a new pollen-bearing staminate flower of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (This shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. See my [Next] photo for a lady shrub.) You can see that the bug has its proboscis in the base of the flower, going after nectar. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 September 2016)

File.Name: g905a_g905-033.jpg
Notes:
29378702192 I found this large green Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is just about to start flowering. I believe this is a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) with those leaf-like wings. See Alice's comments on this photo, and this photo for the tiny nymph it comes from. This is only the second one I've found this year - surprising since I found many nymphs last spring. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2016)

Today was a lovely labor day, though a bit cool for the BBQ. It started cloudy but mostly cleared up early, and there was little breeze despite the early morning fog. Temps in the low 70s. Nice!
CommonNames: Katydid; Coyote Bush; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: g905b_g905-039.jpg
Notes:
29486497195 I was looking close at the native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family that is just starting to flower in our canyon, and I found a few new galls. Here they are from left to right. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2016)

* The left photo appears to show a terminal cluster of galled flowers along with a few normal flower buds. Or maybe they are aborted flowers in this drought year? I'll post a larger photo tomorrow that seems to show exit holes. This one is not described in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I've seen seeing quite a few of these this year.

* The center photo shows the succulent gall of a Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera). It also happens to be the first shrub I've found flowering. Coyote Bush is dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this plant is male and staminate. These galls sometimes get large enough to engulf the flowers, see this photo.

* The right photo shows the new swelling of a gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera). It seems the wrong time of year for these galls, but here it is. I have another photo of a late-season gall here.
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; left; center; Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge; right; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; dioecious; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; dioecious; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: g905c_g905-012.jpg
Notes:
29197383480 This is a ripe berry of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) on the left, with a galled flower on the right. I've found these galls before, see this photo which I reckon settles that it's a galled flower and not an aborted fruit. That odd "crook in the neck" is typical. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2016)

Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it may be the ovary of a flower.)

File.Name: g906a_g906-030.jpg
Notes:
28887468243 I found this handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) in a soon-to-flower shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it's another Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae). They seem to favor composites like this shrub. What brilliant patriotic colors it has on its hind legs! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2016)

I had my usual Tuesday/Thursday cardiac rehab workout this morning. I was tired when I got home, but it was too nice not to go exploring - not much wind and temps about 80∞. I'll head for the Santa Ynez River tomorrow. I avoid it on the holidays.
CommonNames: Short-horned Grasshopper; Coyote Bush; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae

File.Name: g906b_g906-022.jpg
Notes: 29475184286 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden today. I've been finding the caterpillars in the same plants, see this photo, so I wonder if this is one of ours? Butterflies are getting scarce in the mountains, though I still find a few. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2016)

File.Name: g906c_g905-003.jpg
Notes: 29400623872 This is a closer photo of what I guess are galled flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Or are they maybe aborted flowers in this drought year? You can see several "exit holes" made by some critter. The holes are about 100 pixels across in another photo taken at 1:1 magnification. That comes to about 0.4 mm, really tiny. See here for how I figured. This one is not described in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I've seen seeing quite a few of them this year. See this photo for another shot along with two more Coyote Bush galls. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2016)

File.Name: g907a_g907-041.jpg
Notes:
28906584574 This is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. The wasp is large enough that it has to straddle two stems for balance, though some are even larger. See this photo and use the flowers as a ruler! Note that this one seems to be missing the lower part of its left hind leg. Life is tough, even for these large wasps. They hunt spiders to provision their young, but they nectar on flowers for themselves. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

It was warmer today - up to 97∞ down by the river when I was there around noon, but it was cooler at home. There was some breeze. I went back to the (dry) river today and found that the road is open beyond the first crossing, hurray! . I was surprised that it's also open to Upper Oso, though the Rey Fire didn't burn into the campground as I feared. The road beyond the camp is closed.
Locality: dry) river today and found that the road is open beyond the first crossing
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: g907b_g907-012.jpg
Notes: 29422067842 This is a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) carrying its tiny prey in a shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). This hunter gets its name from the row of four pointed spurs on the back of its thorax. If you look close, I think you can see an egg of a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on this bug's abdomen. They are more obvious in another unposted photo. Good luck Mr. Bug. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)
CommonNames: Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Scale Broom; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: g907c_g904-025.jpg
Notes: 28906147704 This is a mating party of true bugs (order Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - probably Redcoat Seed Bugs (Melanopleurus belfragei) in the family Lygaeidae. They are "doing it" on new seed heads of a seed-bearing pistillate shrub of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have another photo from the same day of mating bugs here. I won't say that these bugs only think about mating - that one on the left seems to be feeding too - but they spend a lot of time "doing it". HIHD! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 September 2016)

File.Name: g909a_g907-051.jpg
Notes:
28949947163 This is a stunning little Feather-legged Fly (genus Trichopoda) in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera on new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. Maybe this is species Trichopoda subdivisa with that banded abdomen? The feathery legs catch your eye if you are close enough to see them. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

I apologize for not posting yesterday. We had a surprise un-scheduled maintenance power outage on Thursday starting at 8 am and not ending until 7 pm. Hah - we got two separate notifications in the mail about power outages on Sep. 6 and Sep. 27, both from 8:30 - 4:30. It happened yesterday on Sep. 8 instead. This is no way to run a business - or are public utility monopolies exempt from common courtesy? Too bad, I had some nice bug butts lined up for Thursday, but it ended up being too late.

I went back to the river today, but I prefer my photos from Wednesday. It was warm enough today, but there was an annoying breeze that got in the way.

File.Name: g909b_g907-032.jpg
Notes:
29573154945 This is a cute little fuzzy Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. It has sheer fur - so sheer you can see right through it to the skinny fly inside. I believe this might be genus Anastoechus - maybe species Anastoechus hessei with that black abdomen. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

This might also be Lordotus zona. Compare this photo at BugGuide. Nothing new - I'm confused!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Anastoechus; Anastoechus hessei; Lordotus zona

File.Name: g909c_g907-022.jpg
Notes: 28949311073 This is a large Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) visiting new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I first I thought genus Dipalta, see this photo, but the wing venation is different, without the S-shaped cross vein at the apex of the wings. I believe this one is genus Poecilanthrax. It's fairly large - body the size of a honey bee with broader wings. It's also quite wary. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

File.Name: g910a_g910-021.jpg
Notes:
28969930674 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly hanging on to a spent flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly is recognizable by the row of little chevrons along the wing margin - even visible through the wing like this. They are more common along the river where their favorite host plant grows, Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae), see this photo. Maybe this butterfly uses a different mallow? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 September 2016)

I'm posting lots of butterflies today to clear out my backlog. These are really most of the butterflies I'm seeing right now in this fifth consecutive drought year - not a great butterfly year. I need to do the same thing with new flowers which are piling up in my archive. I went into the higher mountains today. It was another one of those days which is breezy when I'm out there and dead calm when I get home!
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Chaparral Mallow

File.Name: g910b_g910-011.jpg
Notes: 28969792094 I believe this is a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly perched on a flower head of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's smaller and blacker than the similar Mournful Duskywing (E. tristis) butterfly that is more brown than black, compare this photo. These are probably the most common butterfly in the mountains right now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 September 2016)
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; E. tristis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis funeralis; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; E. tristis

File.Name: g910c_g907-048.jpg
Notes: 28971803923 Here are two photos of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies in shrubs of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. The photo on the left does not show them mating, but they are surely interested in mating! They are discretely hidden behind a curtain on weedy grasses. The photo also shows how these butterflies can almost disappear when their wings are closed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

File.Name: g910d_g907-028.jpg
Notes: 28969340314 This is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly uncommonly perched in a shrub of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I'm not sure if it is actually working the flowers or just perched. Usually I find these butterflies close to the ground. This is their summer form. They are darker in the spring, compare this photo. I reckon they are another butterfly that doesn't usually feed, like the California Sister in my [Previous] photo. They are multi-brooded and emerge with a full belly and fly until they are empty. I don't recall ever seeing one before on flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)

File.Name: g910e_g904-043.jpg
Notes: 29560551756 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly uncharacteristically perched on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I assume the flowers are just a convenient perch. These pretty butterflies don't seem to eat at all, but they do like a drink - see this photo. I guess they are multi-brooded and emerge with a full belly and fly until they are empty. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2016)

File.Name: g910f_g831-030.jpg
Notes: 29513899211 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's the first and only one I've seen this year in the mountains, on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). These butterflies are more common on the Scale Broom down by the river, but their numbers seem down even there in this fifth consecutive drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2016)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; California Buckwheat; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: g911a_g910-030.jpg
Notes:
29619780375 Here are two shots of a tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on an equally tiny flower of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family, see my [Previous] photo. The flowers are less than 1/4 inch across, and so is the spider. I guess the spider is a diminutive form of either Peucetia longipalpis or Peucetia viridans, but I'm not sure which. It seems odd to find such a small one at this time of year, when the adults are just starting to make egg sacks. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 September 2016)

Today was a nice day, though it started breezy. We're expected to have cooler temperatures and a deepening marine layer coming up, with maybe some drizzle on Tuesday morning. The autumnal equinox is coming up on Sep. 22, so some cooler weather is proper.
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Epilobium brachycarpum; Onagraceae; Peucetia longipalpis; Peucetia viridans
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Epilobium brachycarpum; Onagraceae; Peucetia longipalpis; Peucetia viridans

File.Name: g911b_g911-043.jpg
Notes: 29619481095 This is a tiny but pretty flower and seed heads of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The flowers are tiny, less than 1/4 inch across. The plants themselves vary from one to over six feet tall with stalks that sway in the slightest breeze. Look from left to right - the flower stalk is really an inferior ovary that splits open to release dandelion-like seeds. It's related to common wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry late-summer/autumn soil in our mountains to flower. Most of our autumn flowers are composites, so this one that is something else in interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2016)

File.Name: g911c_g911-014.jpg
Notes: 29508941252 Here are new flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the mountains. Yep, we still have new flowers appearing in September after our usual summer drought and after five consecutive drought years! This one is tricky to photograph because the flowers are widely spaced on bare stems that lack the usual white tomentum that covers the stems. In a good year, these will keep flowering until the new year - but all bets are off for this year. I figure these late season flowering plants are important - they keep the insects alive during the hardest part of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2016)

File.Name: g912a_g912-012.jpg
Notes:
29017678724 This is a pretty gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It's the first one I've seen this year. I got this photo by Lake Cachuma, and I only saw a few of them. I've found none of the galls at home. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad seasons, but the wasps that induce them do. This is not being a good year for these gall wasps in the mountains. On the other hand, the Crystalline Gall Wasps (Andricus crystallinus) are having a great year, see this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 September 2016)

We were in a cloud all day today. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley which was hazy but clear! The cloud at home is just the marine layer fog spilling over the mountains and our house. It's remarkable fast the fog disappears once you get over the mountain. It sound like tomorrow will be more of the same - only more so, with some drizzle or light rain.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Crystalline Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus

File.Name: g912b_g907-063.jpg
Notes: 29532807942 This is a medium-size Noctuid Moth (Noctuidae, Lepidoptera) - either a Wainscot Moth (genus Leucania) or a Darker-spotted Straw Moth (Heliothis phloxiphaga). It's very light colored, which suggests the former - but it also has that dark band along the hind margin of its wings that suggests the latter. Compare this photo by Ron Wolf, and then compare this photo at BugGuide. It's a diurnal (daytime) moth, and I was surprised to find it working the flowers of this native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the river. It seems to have a very muscular thorax. Those pale green eyes are disturbing, like the kids' eyes in the film Village of the Damned (1960). See my [Previous] photo for another shot. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 September 2016)
CommonNames: Noctuid Moth; Wainscot Moth; Darker-spotted Straw Moth; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Noctuidae; Lepidoptera; Leucania; Heliothis phloxiphaga; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Village of the Damned
associatedTaxa: Noctuidae; Lepidoptera; Leucania; Heliothis phloxiphaga; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Village of the Damned

It's a diurnal (daytime) moth, and I was surprised to find it working the flowers of this native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the river. File.Name: g913a_g913-005.jpg
Notes:
29630270146 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a single disk flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for scale, it's less than 1/4 inch long. This is a female with ovipositor evident. It was an overcast day with some breeze, so I was lucky to get this shot! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2016)

We were under a cloud for most of the day, with some drizzle this morning but no rain. Today really felt like autumn, though the autumnal equinox - with the change in seasons - isn't for another 9 days on September 22. I hope you enjoy the real final week of summer!

File.Name: g913b_g913-003.jpg
Notes: 29374487300 This is a flowering stem of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is var. grindelioides with stems sparsely tomentose and recurved phyllaries on the involucres. It's another of our late-season composites with yellow flowers - we have quite a few of them! If you look close at the topmost flower, you can see the tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) that figures in my [Next] photo. For scale, the flowers and involucres are both about 10 mm long, less than 1/2 inch. I'll make a note of the fly. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2016)

File.Name: g913c_g913-011.jpg
Notes: 29583857331 Here are handsome multicolored berries of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, but they usually wait until their time comes. I've been seeing seen Bushtits, Wrentits, and newly arriving Warblers take the berries at home. I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries, as this plant is related to native Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2016)

29596100732 Here are butt shots of two cute little fuzzy Bee Flies (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I have another photo of the second fly here, and I'll post another photo of the first fly tomorrow for FDF. Maybe by then I can figure out what it is. HBBBT! It was nice today, up to about 80∞ with some breeze. It's expected to warm up considerably this weekend, and we're under a Fire Weather Watch. *Sigh* My truck had a flat tire this morning. I thought about changing the tire and ... went for a walk in the canyon instead. I'll deal with it tomorrow.

Oh Lordy, it was hot today. It was 98∞ at home and a whopping 106∞ down by the dry river. At least that's what the NWS/NOAA Los Prietos weather station reported today. Uh, it also reported a relative humidity of 2% - is that even possible without a desiccant?? I do believe it was too hot for photographers and insects both. Even the pesky "Oak Gnats" that fly into your eyes and drive you nuts were absent today. Tomorrow is expected to be a few degrees cooler - but it might be more humid. There's a 20% chance of thunderstorms in the evening thanks to Tropical Storm Paine.

There's a new wildfire burning on Vandenberg Air Force Base, already up to 2,260 acres. This one is called Canyon Fire. It's not near home, but it is in Alice Abelia country.

File.Name: g919a_g919-005.jpg
Notes:
29801496405 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. The male butterflies always perch with their wings closed, but you can see a hint of their brilliant upper wings with the backlighting. I'll keep trying to get a sharp photo of their upper wings, here is my latest attempt. Note that this butterfly is committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. All insects seem to know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2016)

Today started as another hot one - it was 96∞ at 10:30 am. But then clouds started to move in and it cooled down into the 80s - even the 70s for a while. I haven't noticed any rain, but Julie said she felt tiny almost-drops like virga. We still have a chance of "isolated sprinkles" (as the NWS has it) tonight and tomorrow thanks to a remnant of Tropical Storm Paine. Rain would be wonderful - but just the cooling is pretty nice too.

File.Name: g919b_g919-008.jpg
Notes: 29800937355 This is fresh lizard poo - no doubt the scat of a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis). Julie watched it being made and called me over. Comparing this photo of a different lizard scat, I'm struck by two immediate differences. That one is made up of insect parts, but I can't tell what this one is made of. Also this one has that neat little white thing with yellow trim on the end. I guess lizard excrete like birds, in two (or three) parts. The feces is the dark part. The white part is the urates, what would in a mammal be urine is concentrated into a chalky chunk of mostly uric acid. I'm not sure what that yellow part is - more liquidy urine? (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2016)

File.Name: g919c_g917-002.jpg
Notes: 29173178274 This is a neat little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - aka Hoverfly - living up to its nickname and hovering in place. If you look close, you can see that it has Popeye-like enlarged femurs which I believe place it in genus Syritta or Tropidia in the subfamily Eristalinae. See this photo for a better view. Sure this is a tight crop. I got this photo in the mint patch of the garden. Another fly was nectaring on nearby flowers, and this may be mating behavior. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2016)

File.Name: g920a_g920-003.jpg
Notes:
29196886763 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera with its spike-like proboscis in the chest of an unfortunate Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera). I found this scene today in a staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is just starting to flower. These shrubs are magnets for many different insects, and you can be sure I'll be spending more time here. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2016)

It was 10∞ cooler today, but breezier - not a welcome trade. We were awakened last night by the smell of smoke around 3 am. It wasn't local, but smoke from the Canyon Fire on Vandenberg Air Force Base blowing our way, a sign that the weather is changing. The wildfire had burned 10,542 acres as of noon today, no doubt more by now.

File.Name: g920b_g917-028.jpg
Notes:
29195005854 Here are two different galls of Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These "black oaks" don't support the diversity of galls of the "white oaks" like Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia and Valley Oak (Quercus lobata), but there are enough different kinds to confuse me! Both of these galls are made by wasps that produce two different generations of galls - a spring "bisexual gall" that gives rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls which only contain female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2016)

On the left are mushroom-like spring bisexual galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis). These gall wasps normally produce large "oak apple" type unisexual galls in summer, see this photo - but I haven't seen a single one in this drought year. It's interesting that the galls in this photo don't have exit holes. Can they delay hatching in a drought year?

On the right is a late-summer unisexual gall of the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis). These galls are tiny, only about 1 mm across. Maybe these are the summer form of the bisexual galls that form on catkins in the spring, see this photo. I'm not sure about this - the catkin galls may also be the work of the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus). Unlike many galls, this one seems to do some damage to the leaf.

File.Name: g920c_g917-031.jpg
Notes: 29709466412 These are tempting berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, with that distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that the berries are growing from. The berries look tasty, but in my experience they taste bitter, even when dead ripe. Most web sites (e.g. Wikipedia) just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - but isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else. Now why would any plant produce inedible berries? (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2016)

File.Name: g921a_g921-031.jpg
Notes:
29808020056 This is a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. This one has the scars of a veteran. These fine butterflies have been less common than usual this year, as have most of our late-season butterflies. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2016)

Like yesterday, today was cooler and breezier. The autumnal equinox will happen tomorrow morning at 7:21 am PDT! Tomorrow will officially be autumn. It's that time of year when we lose the sun in most of our garden at our north-facing house, but maybe there will soon be mushrooms and banana slugs. *Sigh* I hope we get some rain this winter to end our five-year (so far) drought. Happy equinox!

File.Name: g921b_g921-005.jpg
Notes: 29217220033 This is a handsome Vespid Wasp (Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) by the river today. I believe this one is a Paper Wasp (genus Polistes) - probably Polistes dorsalis californicus, but there are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" like this, all handsome. So much yellow! I mostly see these wasps near the end of summer. I wonder what they do for the rest of the year? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2016)

Notes: 29843277685 This is a mating pair of small Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) down by the river. See my [Next] photo to get a sense of scale, comparing the individual flowers in the composite heads. Yep, male and female are different colors, but I don't think it means anything. These predators are color-shifters. Maybe the white one was on buckwheat before it came here? Happy hump day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2016)

File.Name: g922a_g921-045.jpg
Notes:
29864803585 These are new flower buds of next year's Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are the first new flowers of the next rain year. Yep, they are quite glandular. I usually find the first flowers close to the Autumnal Equinox (Sep. 22 or so), and here they are. Last year they were even earlier, on Sep. 7 - see this photo. These are our first native shrubs to flower, but they don't all flower at the same time like some other natives. New plants will typically keep coming into flower from autumn until spring, September until March. They might be our longest flowering native. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2016)

Today is the Autumnal Equinox, the official first day of autumn. The season started windy and cool, but this is autumn, a time that swings back and forth between hot and cold. We're under two different NWS hazards tonight - Wind Advisory, Red Flag Warning. The winds have been gusting to 40 mph today, and it was quite cool. Tomorrow will be more of the same, maybe a bit warmer. Fortunately the winds seem to be calming down now.

Hooray, I graduated from my cardiac rehab course today! Now it's up to me to keep it up. I like to saunter (as Thoreau said), but I'll need to kick up the pace.

Notes:
29771492682 This is a neat little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a staminate flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is just starting to flower in our canyon. If you look close, you can see it taking pollen grains from a single flower in the composite head. It was windy today, and I have a question. How is it that a fly can hang onto a swaying branch in a 40 mph gust - and then take off the moment I jar the same branch with my arm or tripod? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2016)

It was very windy today with 20-40 mph gusts. It was a challenge to get any photos, but there were quiet moments between the gusts. It was also rather cool, in the 60s and low 70s. We're still under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It sound like the winds will slowly die down tomorrow and it will get warmer. Then it will get *hot* on Sunday/Monday.

File.Name: g924a_g924-014.jpg
Notes:
29823385101 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a new pollen-bearing staminate flower of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants.) I can see blue and magenta colors that I didn't realize were on these butterflies. These are the only Lycaenids I've been seeing lately. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2016)

There was still some residual breeze this morning, but it's become quite calm this evening. It was warmer today and it's expected to be downright hot tomorrow. At least we don't get those nasty Santa Ana winds like Ventura County just to the south of us, where they are under a Red Flag Warning for the next two days. That's because we don't have a direct path to the desert in Santa Barbara County. We do get our share of wind, especially the Sundowner Winds, but not tonight.

File.Name: g924b_g924-033.jpg
Notes: 29906309805 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) occupying a humble flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon today. The composite plants are kind of weedy, mostly growing along roads, photo here - but they are native. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa. I can't explain the white spots - maybe someone was splashing paint nearby or maybe the spider is color-shifting from white? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2016)

File.Name: g924c_g921-054.jpg
Notes: 29611854100 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) perched with flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I wonder if that webbing indicates that she will settle down and make an egg sack here? I poked her and she didn't move much. I've been watching this spider for a while, and she's been getting fatter and yellower over a few weeks. The buckwheat flowers are starting to fade to rusty brown, but there are still fresh flowers in the heads - look for the red-tipped stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2016)

File.Name: g925a_g925-016.jpg
Notes:
29930795165 You have to look close to see that this is an insect and not just something stuck in the spider web! That's pretty good camouflage...

This is not a Walkingstick (Phasmida) insect but a remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This one looks a lot like the species Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. I believe these bugs scavenge prey from spider webs, the web of a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) in this case. *Full disclosure* This bug was almost vertical when I got the photo. I rotated the image 90∞ clockwise to make better use of my screen space. Does it matter? (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2016)

The forecast was right - it was hot today, in the upper 90s at home. Curiously, it might have been even hotter in town, there are reports of temps in the 100s. It was also curious that the winds this morning (when I was out) were mostly from the south-east. That's unusual, maybe an eddy from the Santa Ana winds down south?

Notes: 29847237191 I'm pretty sure this is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly on a fancy flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria or Chicoryleaf Wire-lettuce (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). I was already focused on the flower trying to get those fantastic stamens and styles - when this butterfly decided to get in the picture! In a moment, he realized I was there and took off. The late summer and autumn flowers are one of my favorites, always a pleasant surprise at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2016)

File.Name: g925c_g925-008.jpg
Notes: 29303624223 This is yet another early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) peacefully munching a stamen on one of the last flowers in the mint patch of our garden. Those palps are like little fingers! I know I've been showing quite a few of these, but they are so goshdarned pretty I want to show every one I see, every time I see it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2016)

File.Name: g926a_g926-024.jpg
Notes:
29839716402 This is looking up at a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera trying to suck a seed pod of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii, Liliaceae). There are still seeds in the dry pods. It's a chance to remember the rather complicated parts of the bug's mouthparts. Going from left to right,

* first there is the labrum, a small piece of skin that covers the exposed part of the stylets before they are surrounded by the labium;

* then there are the thin dark stylets which contain food and salivary channels, but need support; and

finally there is the segmented labium which looks like a little leg, and supports the stylets.

Put them all together, and they become the beak of the bug. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2016)

It was another hot day, and curiously it might have been hotter in town again. Time to get to the debate, though I have been listening.

File.Name: g926b_g926-031.jpg
Notes: 29327010073 Here is a hoard of ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending a hoard of small black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm not sure, but the ants might be the same California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) that devil us in the springtime, see this photo. Some of the ants might be harvesting honeydew from the aphids, but I reckon most of them are defending their herd from the tiny flies and wasps that would parasitize them. I'll keep trying to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2016)

File.Name: g926c_g926-010.jpg
Notes:
29659110800 Here are separate male and female flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Coyote Bush is dioecious. It not only has separate male and female flowers, but they occur on separate shrubs. On the right are male staminate pollen-bearing flowers, and on the left are female pistillate seed-bearing flowers. To make seeds, pollen has to get from the male to the female - at least in principle. (In fact, I believe that isolated female plants make seed just fine.) These shrubs are magnets for many insects, especially different kinds of flies. They also pick an important time to flower - now at the end of summer. I'll be showing lots of them in weeks ahead.

This humble shrub is kind of weedy, favoring disturbed places. It is actually a pioneer species, one of the first plants to colonize previously disturbed ecosystems. I first became aware of this at the school where I used to teach. There was one place with Coyote Bush, and I noticed young trees of Valley Oak growing in their shade. Sure, birds would bring acorns to the shrubs, drop a few, which grow into trees, which shade out the shrubs ... and succession happens. Please show this shrub some respect. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2016)
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; pioneer species
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; pioneer species

File.Name: g927a_g926-012.jpg
Notes:
29347831834 Look close! This is a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) tucked into the flower scar at the top of a new fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. This is a narrow-winged female. The males have wider wings and are the songsters of the night, compare this photo and this one. I've been hearing them this year - including in the bathroom at home - but I have yet to see one. They have a clever way of being loud ... and then getting quiet whenever you get close. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2016)

Just this one photo tonight because our power was out all day, again. *Sigh* that's the third time this month. At least we were warned about this one by a letter back in August, which of course I forgot about. It was a notch cooler today, only about 95∞ instead of 100∞ like yesterday! It was still 103∞ at the river today, where I was. Tomorrow should be cooler.

File.Name: g928a_g928-003.jpg
Notes:
29367375944 This is an impressive Yellow and Black Mud Dauber Wasp (Sceliphron caementarium or kin, Sphecidae, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) perched for a moment on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. These wasps are no strangers since they nest under our eaves, see this photo, but I rarely get photos as they are busy hunting spiders for their young and don't visit flowers. The wasps looks ferocious, but they've never bothered me. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2016)

It was cooler today and quite pleasant, temps in the mid-80s. There was an annoying breeze this morning when I was out trying to get photos, from the south-east? I have errands in town tomorrow, but I seem to have lots of photos to share right now.

File.Name: g928b_g927-007.jpg

Don't see any aphids? The larva is why! (There is the husk of one aphid up above the maggot, but that may be an old shed skin.) This larva has an interesting look, like it's inside a diaphanous wrap. Syrphid Flies are good friends to have in the garden. The adults pollinate the flowers (photo) and the larvae are tough on aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2016)

(East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2016)

File.Name: g929a_g928-001.jpg
Notes:
29386287404 This is a butt shot of a large Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) walking away from me on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. These flies seem to have matching red eyes and red butt, though both look more brown in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2016)

I had errands in town this morning, and the breeze picked up by the time I got home. It was pleasant though, in the mid-80s. I hope we get more of the same tomorrow when I have time to enjoy it.

File.Name: g929b_g926-005.jpg
Notes: 29719809850 This is a butt shot of a handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) - maybe genus Villa. It's working the last of our flowers in the mint patch of the garden. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2016)

File.Name: g929c_g927-005.jpg
Notes: 29719682430 Here are lots of little aphid butts lined up on a stem of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri, Onagraceae) on the edge of our garden. They are cute in a photo, but destructive in actuality. It's a good thing there are predators like the critter in this photo! The aphids in this photo look very different. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2016)

File.Name: g930a_g930-029.jpg
Notes:
30033196265 This is a mating pair of Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) along the dry Santa Ynez River. I guess they are Mining Bees in the genus Anthophora. If you look close, you can see that the bee on the bottom is grasping a flower - to hang on! The flowers are just starting to appear - late because they were mowed down early this summer. The county and the forest service have both been overly zealous in trimming along the roads this year. At least these plants are coming back in time to flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 September 2016)

It was another pleasant day, though warmer than I expected - up to 94∞ down by the river where I was. The wind was moderate.

Notes: 29738861780 This is a rather large Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a fading mint leaf in the garden. Neither its common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it's a handsome large fly - that is tough and gnarly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2016)

File.Name: g930c_g929-019.jpg
Notes: 29919073422 I'm not sure what kind of fly (Calyptratae, Diptera) this is, though it is calyptrate. It's rather small. This one is working a staminate flower cluster of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). There are what I believe are galled flowers to the right, see this photo for better focus. Please leave a comment if you recognize this fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2016)

File.Name: ga01a_ga01-017.jpg
Notes:
29759613150 This is an unusual view of a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on a stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This green spider is actually straw-colored - they are highly variable. I had a Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider all lined up in the buckwheat to be my first Arachtober photo, and ... just yesterday the phone company people walked all over it! (It was near the base of a power pole.) I hope the spider is still there, just hiding for a day from the trauma. Oh well, I showed a Green Lynx Spider as my last photo for Arachtober last year, so it's appropriate that it be my first photo this year. It's a treat to be on the prowl for new spiders every day this month! Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2016)

It was another pleasant day with the temps mostly below 80∞. There was some wind, and that's expected to become worse over the next few days.

File.Name: ga01b_g930-001.jpg
Notes: 29759296290 The nymphs are becoming adults! For some time I've been posting photos of Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) nymphs, e.g. here, here, and here. This is the first adult I've seen since June. I believe they are all Leafhopper Assassin Bugs (Zelus renardii), and I guess they do a double brood. They get their name from the typical size of their prey - which seems pretty wimpy for such a fine predator. This one is in the mint patch of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2016)

File.Name: ga01c_g930-040.jpg
Notes: 29426051043 Here are a pair of Grass-Skipper (family Hesperiidae) butterflies on a new flower stalk of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis, Asteraceae) on the dry wash of the river. The butterflies are probably plain Woodland Skippers (Ochlodes sylvanoides). I reckon this shrub isn't widely known, but it has flowers at a time when any flowers are scarce, especially after five drought years. It's another one of those late-summer and autumn flowering yellow-flowered composite shrubs that make their home on the dry river bed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 September 2016)

Notes:
29963740322 This is a a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on it's web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. Hah - the phone company people walked all around this spider at the base of a power pole on Friday, and I couldn't find it yesterday on Saturday. There it was a few feet away today! It almost looks like a cartoon spider with those remarkable "horns" around the abdomen. The common name comes from the silver/white part on the spider's top side. Arachtober 2. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2016)

The trees are dancing tonight! The winds kicked up as forecast - gusts to 40 mph so far, and we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It was cooler today, mostly in the 60s. I saw a flock of Band-Tailed Pigeons fly over the house. It feels like autumn.

I guess it's a white-haired gall of a Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) that has dropped one capsule, compare this photo of the normal reddish galls. File.Name: ga02c_ga01-023.jpg
Notes: 29963270012 This is a still-flowering stem of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is var. grindelioides with stems sparsely tomentose and recurved phyllaries on the involucres. It's another of our late-season composites with yellow flowers - we have quite a few of them, though this one is winding down. If you look close at the topmost flower that's still yellow, you can spot a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae). For sure I have Arachtober on my mind! I tried to move in close for a spider shot, but I hit the stem with my tripod leg and the spider disappeared. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 October 2016)

File.Name: ga03a_ga03-033.jpg
Notes:
29805497850 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The spider is small, but it looks a bit like one of those Crab Spiders that gets large, maybe the Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia). I believe the fly is a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini) or kin. See my [Previous] photo (here) for the view from the other side. Arachtober 3. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2016)

I reckon autumn is the time for predators to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good - "Nature red of tooth and claw" as Tennyson wrote, but it's also making good use of a resource. If I'm right about my spider I.D., it's interesting that this is the first large crab spider (though this specimen is small) that I've seen this year. It was surprisingly calm this morning even though we've been under a NWS Wind Advisory all day today. The wind did pick up to about 25 mph this afternoon, though it's pretty calm again now. I hope it continues through tomorrow.

It's prey is interesting, some kind of free-living hopper (Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) for sure. Maybe it's the late-stage nymph of a Treehopper (Membracidae)? File.Name: ga04a_ga04-001.jpg
Notes:
30007648942 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) tucked into a fuzzy violet flower of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. The spider might be genus Mecaphesa with those hairs (setae) on its body. It's a sultry spider in a plush boudoir! That's four spider photos each taken on the day they're posted. I'll try to keep that up, but I have a few photos in reserve just in case. Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2016)

It was surprisingly calm again this morning, though the breeze started to pick up before noon. We're under a NWS Red Flag Warning tomorrow though I'm not sure why. It sounds like just another day in the 70s with some wind in the afternoon. Yep, that's autumn.

File.Name: ga04b_ga04-039.jpg
Notes: 30086890936 This is a handsome little male Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly taking nectar from a pistillate shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). You can follow the butterfly's proboscis to the flower, and it's not getting pollen from this lady shrub! There were maybe a dozen of these butterflies flying around in this one place, mostly close to the ground but also up in this shrub, and mostly (but not entirely) male. I haven't seen these butterflies since early June, so I reckon this is a new brood. It sure is gorgeous - as befits a newly emerged butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2016)

File.Name: ga04c_ga03-001.jpg
Notes: 30007122462 This is a tight crop of the adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) with its prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I posted yesterday - see the photo. It's too-tight of a crop maybe, but it shows the prey a little better. This creature is a free-living hopper (Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) for sure, maybe the late-stage nymph of a Treehopper (Membracidae)? Its limbs have an odd gelatinous quality. Please leave a comment if you know it is. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2016)

File.Name: ga05a_ga05-024.jpg
Notes:
29513998784 This is a straw-colored Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her new egg sack on a flower head of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). These "green" spiders seem to be highly variable. This might be the same spider I show in this photo, though it's several feet away and has lost some body mass if so. Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2016)

We're under both a NWS Red Flag Warning and a Wind Advisory tonight, though it's not clear why. It was pleasant today, in the mid-70s, and a little breezy, but nothing exceptional. The winds may kick up tonight (40-50 mph gusts?), but maybe we'll be lucky and just skip the whole thing.

File.Name: ga05b_ga05-047.jpg
Notes: 30057816771 This small Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) is gathering pollen from a flower of Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. If the pollen gets onto that V-shaped style deeper in the flower, pollination happens. This is the same kind of fly that fell into the grasp of a Crab Spider in this photo, probably a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini) or kin. The flowers are especially interesting because they are still flowering. This is one native plant that can take summer watering. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2016)

File.Name: ga05c_g930-027.jpg
Notes: 30027643562 Here is a mating pair of Bagrada Bugs (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a dried seed stalk of (probably) non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra, Brassicaceae). They were in a place where native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) grows, and I usually find them on that plant. These bugs are native to northern Africa, but they are a new introduction in California, maybe as recent as 2008. They have become a serious agricultural pest in farm areas like Lompoc. For the past few years, they've been quite common here along the river, but they've been scarce this year. Happy hump day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 September 2016)

File.Name: ga06a_ga06-002.jpg
Notes:
30076661871 This is a butt shot of the large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on it's web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. You can see a bit of the horns that are characteristic of this species like a crown around its abdomen. Many of these large spiders that hang from their webs all day have "faces" on the top of their abdomen, and this spider has a face on the underside as well. You can also see some anatomy in this photo. The spider's spinnerets are near the screen-top of its abdomen. At the screen-bottom of its abdomen, you can see the epigastric furrow and the dingus-like epigynum which is the sexual organ of this lady spider. At the very bottom of its abdomen, you can see her "book lungs" which are a type of respiration organ used for atmospheric gas exchange - thank you Wikipedia. Sure, I had to look this all up, and I hope I got it right. HBBBT and Arachtober 6! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2016)

The NWS Red Flag Warning and a Wind Advisory last night came to naught. It was warmer today and quite calm, almost a perfect day.

Notes:
29551820373 Yesterday I showed "butt shots" of a few spiders - here's the other end of the spider! This is a different Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that is actually green, different from the straw-colored one I've been showing, though its sitting on its egg sack in the same shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's a good view of her chelicerae. Hah - I got this photo a couple of days ago and I liked it better that the one I got today. Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2016)

It was interesting weather today, warm and humid with some tropical high clouds - and no wind! This is not the hot and dry weather I was expecting with Santa Ana winds down south of us in Ventura and LA counties. I'll be happy if there's more of the same tomorrow.

File.Name: ga07b_ga07-001.jpg
Notes: 30180601785 This little fly (Diptera) is cleaning its proboscis, which is puffed out for the occasion. Flies can puff out their proboscis or suck it back into their heads - it's all pneumatics! The fly looks a bit like a Drosophilid or a Lauxaniid - or maybe genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae? (See Thijs de Graaf's comment on this photo.) I found this interesting fly on a mint leaf in the garden this morning, and was lucky to get this shot. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2016)

File.Name: ga07c_ga05-049.jpg
Notes: 29551364773 This is a remarkable Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) taking pollen from a flower of Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. The fly is definitely a female with those widely spaced eyes, maybe the species Scaeva pyrastri in the tribe Syrphini. This fly caught me by surprise when I was trying to get a little bee fly on the same flowers, see this photo. It's fairly large, about the size of a honey bee. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2016)

Notes:
30115996641 This cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) was on a white folding chair in the garden this morning - always look before you sit down! I believe it is genus Phidippus, maybe a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni) or kin. I found a similar spider nearby for Arachtober last year, see this photo. Yes, it looked right at me as these spiders do, but this photo is better. Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2016)

I was looking for something besides another Green Lynx Spider today, and here it was! It was another lovely day - warm and very little breeze to shake things up. I've got too many photos right now. It's a good problem to have.

File.Name: ga08b_ga08-009.jpg
Notes: 30085948272 I found this uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working a flower on a staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in our canyon today. These butterflies mind their own business and quietly work different flowers in the woods. They may be more common than they seem to be, but I don't find them very often. The only other one I've photographed all year was this one. This is a tight crop. The butterfly took off when I tried to get closer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2016)

File.Name: ga08c_ga08-025.jpg
Notes:
30115090901 This is a dusty "Jerusalem Cricket" aka "Potato Bug" (genus Stenopelmatus, family Stenopelmatidae, Tettigoniidea, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on the chipseal road in our canyon. That long string of Latin names is actually interesting because it tells that this critter is more closely related to katydids (Tettigoniidea) than true crickets (Gryllidea). Here's the story... I was sitting by the bridge over our dry creek when I heard a scuffle on the side of the road. A lizard was chasing this creature into the road when a second lizard came running up to grab it! The lizards tangled, and the cricket got away in the confusion. About then, I walked in with my tripod and the lizards took off. The cricket kept running, so this was a lucky shot. Finally it disappeared in the mulch on the other side of the road. That's why the cricket is so dusty. The photo came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2016)

Jerusalem Crickets are an inch or two long. Kids like to torment them, but you should leave them be as the Earth's creatures. They have many names in the southwest, including the Spanish "niÒo de la tierra" and Navajo "wo se cíini" - the "skull insect". Wikipedia has more info and some speculation about the name "Jerusalem Cricket" - interesting because they are neither from Jerusalem nor true crickets.
CommonNames: Jerusalem Cricket; Potato Bug; Jerusalem Cricket

File.Name: ga09a_ga09-041.jpg
Notes:
30189768986 This little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has captured a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. Today was a good day for spider photos. I only got as far as the mailbox and already had photos of this one, another green lynx spider, a crab spider, a labyrinth spider, and a cat-faced spider! I may take tomorrow off. It was a nice-enough day, a little breezy. Interesting - it was warm on the sun, but cool in the shade. It's another sign of autumn.

File.Name: ga10a_ga10-023.jpg
Notes:
29619352093 I believe this is a large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) on its web in the narrow space between our little greenhouse and our big house, with a tall cactus in the background. Near the screen-bottom of the photo, you can see a ball of black goo with a fly head sticking out of it that is all that's left of the spider's recent capture. I've seen spiders turn their prey into balls of goo before, and I suspect it's a kind of external digestion to liquify their prey before sucking it up. I can't explain why the fly's head is the last part to go. If you look very close, you can also see one of the spider's fangs. If you have the stomach for it, see my [Previous] photo (here) for a closer view of the goo. Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2016)

Today was an interesting weather day with some clouds and cooler temps and wind. There's a rumor of rain next week. It's feels like autumn.

This is looking close at (I believe) a large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae), see my [Next] photo. File.Name: ga10c_ga07-068.jpg
Notes: 29617837574 This is an extraordinary little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) on new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. Their outstanding feature is their "cruciform" (cross-like) wings, and what spiky legs it has! It's a diurnal (daytime) moth, but that's all I know about it. I've seen this moth before at this time of year, see this photo. Happy Moth Monday! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 October 2016)

File.Name: ga11a_ga05-020.jpg
Notes:
30235045416 This is a well-camouflaged little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The white and brown colors of the spider almost perfectly match the white and brown colors of the flowers. The spider might be genus Mecaphesa. I reckon there will be crab spiders as long as there are flowers before winter comes. Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2016)

We woke up in a cloud this morning. The fog cleared out, but then the fog winds kicked up, even in the higher mountains. It was really too windy for photos today, but I got a few.

File.Name: ga11b_ga11-016.jpg
Notes:
30269505185 This is a large bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. (I also believe it's the subspecies P. rutilioides nigrescens.) It's quite large for a fly, the size of a small bumblebee. I found this one working new flowers on a pistillate (female) shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. That's definitely the plant they prefer, though I have found them on the male shrubs if the ladies aren't flowering yet. I know this isn't the best photo, too tight of a crop, but it may be the only photo I get this year! These flies are mighty camera shy. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2016)

It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the equally bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae. It's specifically said to prey on the Edwards' Glassy-wing (Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii) moth, see this photo and this photo.

File.Name: ga11c_ga11-009.jpg
Notes:
29972491400 I believe this is a Thalassica Grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica). If I'm right, it's a Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera). This one is on bare ground. A moment later it took wing, but I didn't catch its wing color through my viewfinder. The root of the word "thalassica" means relating to the sea. I'm not sure what the connection is, but it's a nice name. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 October 2016)

I've been avoiding getting down on the ground to photograph grasshoppers this year to save my aching back, but sometimes on a windy day you just gotta do it to get any photos at all!

File.Name: ga12a_ga12-030.jpg
Notes:
30205029641 I'm sure this is a tiny Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera), but I'm not sure what kind. It's on a flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria or Chicoryleaf Wire-lettuce (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). I think the fly might be genus Neacreotrichus in the subfamily Phthiriinae, see photos at BugGuide. What a great proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2016)

D'oh - I inadvertently had my camera set on f/22 from a previous photo, and forgot to set it back. It's not the first time I've done that. The problem isn't too much depth of field, but the lack of detail that diffraction causes. See the links in the comments on this photo. (I don't know why, but I still can't link to that site on Flickr.)

We were in the clouds again this morning, with some breeze when the marine layer fog finally burned off. Darn, it sounds like northern California is going to get rain (again) and we're going to be dry (again) - see the California Weather Blog. We're now going into our 6th year of drought.

File.Name: ga12b_ga12-042.jpg
Notes:
30290048925 This is a remarkable "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) on a small gall on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe the wasp is genus Torymus in the Chalcid Wasps (Chalcidoidea). That long "stinger" is really an ovipositor that the wasp will use to lay eggs on a gall wasp larva inside the gall. The gall itself is small, less than 1/2 across. I'm not sure, but it might be the gall of a Dried Peach Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae. Life is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2016)

Sometimes I rate my photos by how many tags I need. This one rates pretty high.

It's captured a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) in its shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Note that she is still hanging from her egg sack. Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2016)

File.Name: ga13a_ga12-051.jpg
Notes:
30274683716 This is a butt shot of another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). If you use the individual flowers of the buckwheat as your ruler, you can see this this spider is quite a bit larger than this other crab spider I posted a couple of days ago. (The buckwheat flowers are about 1/8 inch across. It's a small world!) The spider might be genus Mecaphesa again. If you look close, you can see that it has a safety line. They seem to be our most common spiders right now. HBBBT and Arachtober 13! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2016)

We didn't have marine layer fog this morning, but I had an appointment in town. By the time I got home, there were clouds and breeze for the rest of the day. We do have a chance of rain over the weekend, up to 40% by Sunday night. I'm not holding by breath, but we do need it. We had a bit of rain in May and June, 1/10 inch or so, but this might be our first significant rain since April. *Sigh* I guess there's a 60% chance it will miss us.

File.Name: ga13b_ga08-021.jpg

It's antennae go on as long again as her body, but this is a nice view of her ovipositor at the other end. Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day - and only the broad-winged males sing. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2016)

File.Name: ga14a_ga14-051.jpg
Notes:
30242638641 Same or different?

These are two shots of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in the same still-flowering shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I got both photos today. They are only about three feet away from each other. Both are guarding their egg sacks. The one on the left has a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) for prey - and the green one on the right had a bee just a few days ago in this photo! There are two different species - Peucetia longipalpis or Peucetia viridans. So are these the same or different? Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2016)

It was clear today, but there was an annoying breeze all day - becoming downright windy in the afternoon, 20+ mph. It wasn't a great photo day, but I got a few. There are clouds moving in. We have a 20% chance of rain starting tomorrow night. Tomorrow might be an interesting day!
