I think the fly might be genus Neacreotrichus in the subfamily Phthiriinae, see photos at BugGuide. What a great proboscis! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2016)

D'oh - I inadvertently had my camera set on f/22 from my [Previous] photo (both in the field and here at Flickr) and forgot to set it back. It's not the first time I've done that. I have another photo of the same fly here.
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Chicoryleaf Wire-lettuce
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Neacreotrichus; Phthiriinae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Neacreotrichus; Phthiriinae

File.Name: ga14d_ga12-018.jpg
Notes:
30242011041 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams. You can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in the light trails. My exposure time was 1/60 second, and I count mostly 3 beats in that time, so they are beating their tiny wings 180 times a second - really fast! Maybe they are tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae? Usually I see them after a rain, but it's still dry this year. I also found them last year on October 6 - after a small rain. Curiously, there might be some rain this weekend. I reckon it's a mating thing. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2016)

The light trails look like little centipedes! My son in Oregon just mailed me a link to bird photos by photographer Xavi Bou which are similar, look here. In some years, we have massive mating flights of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees. See this photo (and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day, 2009. (This photo is on Fotolog - it goes back a ways.)
Locality: and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day

File.Name: ga15a_ga15-022.jpg
Notes:
30349609575 This is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) sticking its legs out of its house - like a classic striptease artist sticking her legs out from behind the curtain! These spiders build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. I tried to coax this spider out of its house with a grass blade with no luck, but sometimes you get lucky - see this photo. Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2016)

We were in and out of the clouds today, and always it was breezy. The clouds seem pretty thick this evening. I'm hoping for rain sometime over the next couple of days. We haven't had a significant rain since last April, and we're ready!

File.Name: ga15b_g321-025.jpg
Notes:
30349228355 This is a tiny "Trashline Orbweaver" (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging on a dead stem of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) last spring. That odd bump on its abdomen is quite distinctive. I believe this one is a male with enlarged palps, see this more-revealing photo (but different!) of the same spider. Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

I'll keep trying to post something old and something new for the Arachtober group, but it's hard going through my old photos looking for good ones I haven't already posted. It's easier to go out and get a new photo so I know for sure I haven't used it.

File.Name: ga15c_ga15-008.jpg
Notes: 30313529786 This is a small net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a dusty leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae in the woods today. I'm pretty sure it's an Issid Planthopper in the genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. They have a goofy looking front end like a cardboard box. They've been uncommonly scarce this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2016)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: ga15d_ga14-002.jpg
Notes: 29717067563 This is a male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) tucked into the flower scar at the top of a new fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day. This is a broad-winged male, the songsters of the night. The females have narrow wings and don't sing, compare this photo which is also in a cactus fruit. Yes, this cactus fruit just is purple, but it will turn red as it ripens. On the other hand, white balance is a bit off. We're losing our sun at home and I need to relearn how to take photos in the shade. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2016)

File.Name: ga16a_ga16-025.jpg
Notes:
30374220275 This is a large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a late-season flower stalk of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) - with raindrops from our first rain of the new water year last night! Because of the rain, the spider is not actually on the flower - no bees so why bother? This might be the same spider I showed in this photo grown larger. Before I thought it might be a Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia), but now it looks more like a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes). There's not much difference between the two. Like many large spiders it seems to have a face on its back. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2016)

We didn't get much rain last night, only about 1/10 inch, but it's our first rain in months and it feels great! It's a start for the new water year. We have a 70% chance of more rain tonight, and then ... a NWS Fire Weather Watch on Tuesday! That's how our autumn weather is - it rocks ... back and forth.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Goldenrod Crab Spider; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Misumena vatia; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Misumena vatia; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: ga16b_ga15-023.jpg
Notes:
30338683166 Greeting you with open arms!

This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The spider might be genus Mecaphesa once again. They are our most common spiders right now, for as long as the flowers last before winter. I know I said I'd keep trying to post something old and something new for the Arachtober group, but it's work going through my old photos looking for good ones I haven't already posted. It's easier to get a new photo so I know for sure I haven't already used it. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2016)

File.Name: ga16c_ga16-011.jpg
Notes: 30076628870 This is a gorgeous late-stage nymph of a Long-horned Grasshopper (Ensifera, (Orthoptera) - with raindrops. Maybe it's the later stage of the Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae) nymphs I've been showing, see this photo. There are other possibilities, but this one shares form and color with the early nymphs. This one is more ornate than I recall, though it's been a few years since I've see the adults. It's hanging out in a native plant of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2016)
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: ga16d_ga16-014.jpg
Notes: 30076293820 We had our first rain of the new water year last night! Not much, only about 1/10 inch, but that was enough to green up these small-leaved Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. I now believe they are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). When they dry out, they form the rolled up black crust at the bottom of my photo. They transition between green and black forms very quickly, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2016)

File.Name: ga17a_ga17-018.jpg
Notes:
30099388280 We had rain last night and termites today! We had a hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this afternoon. Maybe they are the Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae)? They were emerging from 20 feet or so away from the house with large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees nearby, so I'm not too worried. I've seen them before after rains, but I've never seen such a massive swarm of them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2016)

We had another 1/4 inch of rain last night, bringing our storm total to 4/10 inch - not much, but it's the start of the new water year and the first time we've had that much rain since last April! All that the rain brings... so far I've found these termites, snails, and liverworts. Maybe Lichens, mushrooms, slime molds, and banana slugs are next - but more immediately the rain will bring wind. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and a Red Flag Warning tomorrow night. The wind isn't too bad tonight at home, but Julie says it's blowing pretty good in town.
CommonNames: Termites; Subterranean Termite; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ga17b_ga17-005.jpg
Notes: 29765662914 This is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods climbing up the dusty side of our small greenhouse. ("Up"?? *Gulp* I rotated the image 90∞ clockwise to better fit the screen.) I guess it's a Shoulderband Snail (Helminthoglypta), maybe a juvenile - compare this photo by Alice Abelia. Whatever kind of snail it is, it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands. It's definitely a native, brought out by the rain last night. I'm intrigued by these native snails that somehow hang on through our dry summers. I'd like to learn more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2016)
CommonNames: Land Snail; Shoulderband Snail; Garden Snails

File.Name: ga17c_ga17-002.jpg
Notes:
30309613631 This is one cat whose belly you don't want to rub!

This is another large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) - a different one from this photo. This one is resting with its back against a tall cactus. It's off its web, but it's keeping an eye on things - note how it is holding the web with each of its front (screen-bottom) legs, ready to move. That black patch below its spinnerets sure looks ominous, like a demon mask. It's interesting that this one looks different from my other photo in the link above. Arachtober 17. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2016)

File.Name: ga17d_g814-034.jpg
Notes: 30309436051 This is a different large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider in the same patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox that I've been showing. It almost looks like a cartoon spider with those remarkable "horns" around the abdomen. The common name comes from the silver/white part on the spider's top side. It's interesting that this spider was there for a while and then suddenly disappeared after being there for a good month. No problem, we had a second spider which I showed for Arachtober 2 in this photo - and now that spider has suddenly disappeared. Maybe it's hatching eggs somewhere? Maybe a spider wasp got it? Maybe it just got tired of me butting in to get a photo? Whatever the reason, I miss it. Arachtober 17. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2016)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: ga18a_ga18-007.jpg
Notes:
30381639216 Here are two shots of a really tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the same stem of a Mallow (Malvaceae) plant in the garden as the spider in my [Previous] photo. I believe this one is a male as shown by its enlarged palps on the left. The spider might be genus Mecaphesa once again, though I reckon it's different from the ones I've been showing. (There are 16 species in California.) Yeah, the background colors look different. That's what a small change in light and direction can do for our photos. What color is it really?? Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)

I was expected wind today, but it never happened. Well, it hasn't happened yet. We're still under a NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning tonight. Our weather will now swing back to the warm side with temps in the 90s expected by Thursday.

File.Name: ga18b_ga18-003.jpg
Notes: 29785629223 I found this tiny spider hugging the stem of a Mallow (Malvaceae) plant in the garden this morning. This spider can become quite invisible when the sunlight changes. It holds its legs close along the stem . I considered a Long-Jawed Orb Weaver (Tetragnathidae) and a Running Crab Spider (Philodromidae), but Alice believes it's genus Tmarus (Thomisidae) - and now that I look at it again I'm sure that's right . I have another photo of this interesting spider here. I don't see these spiders very often because they are so well camouflaged against the darker background of an oak branch. Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
CommonNames: Mallow; Long-Jawed Orb Weaver; Running Crab Spider

File.Name: ga18c_ga18-030.jpg
Notes: 29786408754 This is a gorgeous nymph of a true bug (order Heteroptera, Hemiptera), I guess the nymph of a Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae. What amazing colors it has! It's resting on a shrub of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae) which is looking kind of sad, even for this time of year at the end of our long dry summer. The leaves are small and unusually congested and they feel dry. I'm not sure if this shrub will make it. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: ga18d_ga18-044.jpg
Notes: 30300374882 This is a another tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) on empty seed husks of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. I believe the wasp is genus Torymus in the Chalcid Wasps (Chalcidoidea), see this recent photo. The wasp in that other photo is on a gall and will no doubt use its long "stinger" - really an ovipositor - to lay eggs on the gall wasp larva inside. I'm not sure what the wasp in this photo is after, maybe critters like weevils living in the seeds? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Torymus; Chalcidoidea
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Torymus; Chalcidoidea

File.Name: ga19a_ga19-025.jpg
Notes:
29806129213 This is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) on prey outside of its house. Compare this photo of the spider in its retreat. You can't see the prey in this photo, but the spider's abdomen is remarkable. I see faces, including a cat face at the bottom. This isn't the real Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) - see this photo - but It will do. It's gorgeous! Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2016)

The wind finally did kick up last night, with gusts up to 56 mph, but it was odd. I was expecting wind today, and we did have some residual gusts early in the morning, but by the time I got outside the wind had died down almost completely. Yep, it was warm today - up to about 88∞ and even warmer expected tomorrow.

File.Name: ga19b_ga19-006.jpg
Notes:
30401332836 "Don't be greedy"

This large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a late-season flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) has captured not one, but two little Bee Flies (Bombyliidae, Diptera) as prey! The spider might be a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) and both flies might well be Sinuous Bee Flies (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini) or kin, maybe male and female. I found this scene in the shade this morning. I got my photo and continued on my walk. When I got back the sun was on them, but the spider had dropped one of the flies. I like this photo better despite the low light. Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2016)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Bee Flies; White-banded Crab Spider; Sinuous Bee Flies
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Misumenoides formosipes; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Misumenoides formosipes; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini

File.Name: ga19c_ga19-016.jpg
Notes: 30437947025 This is an adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) sprawled across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see a bit of its mighty proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2016)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ga19d_ga14-030.jpg
Notes: 30320875932 Here is a mating pair of tiny "parasitic wasps" (Hymenoptera) on a new flower of California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see that the male on the right has to dodge the long ovipositor of the female. I don't know what kind of wasps these are - there are many different kinds. The flower is also interesting because it's a hummingbird flower with a slit on top where some insect did nectar theft - see this photo of a different hummingbird flower with a theft in progress. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2016)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: ga20a_ga20-016.jpg
Notes:
30422618066 Be careful little wasp - some of those flowers have claws!

This is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) along the dry Santa Ynez River - with a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) looming over! The spider left the wasp alone this time, maybe too small? Arachtober 20. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2016)

It was warm today, up to 95∞ down by the river where I was, and almost as warm at home. It was just as warm on the other side of the mountains in town. There was very little breeze today. It will start swinging back to the cool side this weekend.
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: ga20b_ga18-051.jpg
Notes:
30372058621 Same or different?

Here are two photos of different Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus. The one on the right is the same one I been calling a calling (tentatively) a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni), e.g. here and here. It live at home in the cactus patch of the garden. The one on the left is from a few miles away on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Maybe it's another Phidippus johnsoni, but it certainly does look different. These both happen to be pretty good butt shots, so HBBBT and Arachtober 20! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
CommonNames: Jumping Spiders; Johnson Jumper; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phidippus johnsoni
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phidippus johnsoni

File.Name: ga20c_ga20-045.jpg
Notes: 29826096733 This is a butt shot of a Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) along the dry Santa Ynez River. I believe it is a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora. They seem quite common on these particular shrubs right now. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2016)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coastal Goldenbush; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Anthophora

File.Name: ga20d_ga18-048.jpg
Notes: 30421847186 I'm pretty sure this is a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on late-season staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Maybe it's genus Peleteria? This fly is not much larger than a house fly, but it's much more interesting. I've been seeing quite a few of these on all the late-season composite shrubs. This is probably the same fly I showed last week in this photo. I added that one to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, and I'll add this one too. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Peleteria

File.Name: ga21a_ga21-022.jpg
Notes:
29846453133 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) had a blessed event! Brand new spiderlings are emerging from her egg sack, and are already going through their first molt. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse, look how her palps are tenderly toughing some of the babies. I have other photos of the same green lynx here and here. The brown spider is still expecting, see the first link. Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2016)

Today was another very nice day - a few degrees cooler, but also a bit breezier. The winds were from the southwest, unusual for us. Tomorrow is expected to be 10∞ cooler again as we swing back and forth.

File.Name: ga21b_ga19-020.jpg
Notes: 30180006500 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - but what prey will it find on a leaf? The spider is probably genus Mecaphesa once again. These are no doubt our most common spiders right now, but they should be sitting on flowers picking off the last of the pollinators. It must be tough for them as the flowers start to disappear with the onset of autumn. I had some backlight to help with this photo. Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2016)

File.Name: ga21c_ga20-006.jpg
Notes:
30478064045 A difference in scale...

Here is a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) in the upper-left and (I believe) a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) in the lower-right. They are on flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) along the dry Santa Ynez River. Maybe the large fly is genus Peleteria? (See this photo for another view.) Darn, I was hoping the wasp in the lower corner was a tiny fly instead, but it comes to the same thing. Nature covers a lot of ground between tiny and large - and this isn't even a large fly. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2016)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria

File.Name: ga21d_ga18-058.jpg
Notes: 30361026002 This is a a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on flowers of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I believe the fly is genus Thevenetimyia or (more likely) Geron. It's incredibly delicate. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Geron
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Geron

File.Name: ga22a_ga22-009.jpg
Notes:
30413259411 "He has his father's eyes..."

This is a tiny day-old spiderling in the hatch of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that happened yesterday. He or she does indeed have momma's eyes right down to the hairs in-between, compare this photo. It's amazing how fast the baby spiders grow and how perfectly formed they are. Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2016)

It was a few degrees cooler today, a bit more cloudy, and quite a bit breezier than it was yesterday. The winds were mostly from the southwest again, unusual for us. That might be a rain sign . We have a 20% chance of rain Sunday night, and a better chance of rain later in the week. Bring it on!

File.Name: ga22b_ga15-009.jpg
Notes: 30413003191 This is a delicate little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the faded flower head of a Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) shrub in the garden. It's another spider waiting for prey that won't come on faded flowers. The spider is probably genus Mecaphesa once again, but it has surprising green and blue colors. I got this photo a week ago on a cloudy day just before our first rain, perfect light. Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2016)

File.Name: ga22c_ga22-029.jpg
Notes: 30201148360 This is a looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I saw three of these today, and they were all more or less flat - instead of making loops like they typically do. Can you tell which end is which? If you look close you can see three pairs of sharp-pointed slender legs on the right, but only two pairs of stubby prolegs on the left. That's the butt on the left and the head on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2016)
CommonNames: looper; inchworm; Geometrid Moth; California Buckwheat

File.Name: ga22d_ga21-001.jpg
Notes: 30382280142 Here are our first mushrooms of the new rain year! I believe they are the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. We had 4/10 of rain a few days ago, and that was enough to bring these mushrooms up - despite the warm weather since. This mushroom comes up early; under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae); in groups; has white spores; and with copious white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs at the base. (A white rhizomorph is visible at the bottom of the root ball.) Now we just need some more rain to keep the season going, and there happens to be a 20% chance of a bit more rain Sunday night. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2016)
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ga23a_ga23-015.jpg
Notes:
30439044901 Here is a clutch of tiny spiderlings of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that emerged just two days ago, on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It was cool and breezy today, and here they are all huddled together instead of out and exploring. Asses and elbows for sure! Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2016)

I've been getting photos of these spiders every day, though I haven't always posted them. I'm nervous because the power company is replacing a pole near the spiders on Tuesday. I don't want to move the spiderlings, but maybe I should? My first thought is to put up some caution tape with a sign "Spider babies here", but I don't know if that will do any good in the path of heavy equipment. I reckon I'll have to be out there on Tuesday morning and do what's right. "What about the babies?!"

Today was a pretty crummy day for macro stuff - mostly overcast and quite breezy. There's a chance of a shower tonight and in the morning. I thought I felt a drop today - or was that a passing bird? I did get a few photos.

File.Name: ga23b_ga23-025.jpg
Notes: 30227507240 This is surely the tiniest spider I've ever noticed - sitting on a perfect tiny web. It even seems to have a bit of stabilimentum at the top - that thicker white webbing behind the spider's abdomen. I figure it's an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) because of that web, maybe species Hypsosinga pygmaea for its size as much as anything else? It's about the size of a pin head, about 375 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1.5 mm, just 1/16 inch long. (See here for how I figured.) I know, this isn't the best photo. It was cloudy and breezy today, and I had to use flash - but I reckon it's a keeper. Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2016)

File.Name: ga23c_ga23-017.jpg
Notes: 30409148532 This is a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) sprawled across spent staminate flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day - and only the broad-winged males sing. I don't often see them until ... there one is! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2016)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: ga23d_ga20-047.jpg
Notes: 30488642676 This is a pair of Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working around a flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) along the dry Santa Ynez River. I guess they are male and female Mining Bees in the genus Anthophora, possibly the California Anthophora (Anthophora californica). If so, that's the larger female on the left and the smaller male on the right. There are other differences besides size - look at the bushy "saddlebags" on the hind legs of the one on the left. These bees seem quite common on these particular shrubs right now. I have another photo of the same kind of bee here. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2016)
CommonNames: Wild Bees; Coastal Goldenbush; Mining Bees; California Anthophora
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Anthophora; Anthophora californica

File.Name: ga24a_ga24-027.jpg
Notes:
30548530435 This is the large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) in its retreat in a tall cactus in the narrow space between our little greenhouse and our big house. It's looking at me. I have another photo of the same spider here. Come to think of it, Labyrinth Spiders (genus Metepeira) aren't the only orb weavers that make a house separate from its web - they just make a more substantial retreat than this one. Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2016)

We were in a cloud this morning, with wind but no rain. We have a better chance of real rain later this week. Spiders are getting scarce in the woods, but I still a few favorites on call.

File.Name: ga24b_ga24-015.jpg
Notes:
30249822740 Here is today's photo of the clutch of tiny spiderlings of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that emerged just three days ago, on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Mama is never far away. I like how the spiderlings are making a ring around the flowers in this photo. Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2016)

*Sigh* I'm nervous because I believe the power company is replacing a pole near the spiders on Tuesday, but to be honest I'm not sure that it's this pole they are replacing. I don't want to move the spiderlings if I don't have to. I reckon I'll be out there early on Tuesday morning to do what I have to. "What about the babies?!

File.Name: ga24c_ga24-004.jpg
Notes:
29917529584 This is a single flower of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. See this photo for a whole plant. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get down on your hands and knees to really see the small flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring! The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid. The flowers are related to our shrubby Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), see this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 October 2016)

(I have been taking photos of flowers and galls and weeds all along, though posting them is taking second place for spiders as long as we're in the month of Arachtober. That's just one more week, and then I'll catch up.)
Locality: I have been taking photos of flowers and galls and weeds all along

File.Name: ga24d_ga18-047.jpg
Notes: 30460144441 This is non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family showing its tiny seeds in the backlight. The seeds don't stick in your socks, but they are tiny and easy to transport - see this photo of a shoeful! I reckon this has become the worst weed in our mountains, that spreads along trails into the backcountry. See this photo and this one for the whole plant. I've watched this weed get worse over the years. Remove it if you can. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2016)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: ga25a_ga25-036.jpg
Notes:
30271364230 Here's one more photo of tiny spiderlings of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox. They're so cute! That's mom's giant green butt in the background. I've been worried about these spiderlings because the power company was due to replace a pole in our canyon today, but it turns out that it's a different pole. They delivered the new pole and placed it near the spiders today, though the helicopter won't finish the job for a couple of weeks. The spiders are OK, but maybe a little stressed from the activity. I'm glad I waited before relocating the spiderlings, which are now four days old. Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2016)

It was a nice day with a modest breeze from the southwest - which is unusual for us. That's our usual "rain wind" so maybe this is an omen for the predicted rain on Thursday/Friday? There are rumors of an inch or two.

File.Name: ga25b_ga25-002.jpg
Notes: 30570013735 I believe this is a small Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae with its prey. It might well be genus Pityohyphantes, though my book describes those as rather large while this one is rather small. For scale, that's a tiny leafhopper for its prey. The web is in a non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) shrub on the edge of our garden. Yeah, it's not the best photo. I went out early this morning to check on my baby lynx spiders (it's cool, see my [Next] photo) and got this photo when I got back, in deep shade before the sun got here. *Sigh* Sunlight gets scarce at our house as autumn moves into winter. I choose not to use flash, so this is what I got. Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2016)

File.Name: ga25c_ga25-016.jpg
Notes:
30481746361 These are the odd flowers of a native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. What look like skinny petals are really club-shaped style branches. They've been flowering for a few weeks now. It's another of those late-season composite flowers in our woods that are are important for keeping the insects alive! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2016)

I find it so interesting that there's always something flowering in our woods. The late-season composites and others like California Fuchsia overlap the early flowers of Chaparral Currant and Bigberry Manzanita in a way that almost seems like design to keep the pollinators alive. Could this be an adaptation of the ecosystem?

File.Name: ga25d_ga25-021.jpg
Notes: 30270414960 These are pretty fall color leaves of a Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family. Usually this tree stays in the canyons, but sometimes not. The foliage looks a lot like Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae), but the leaves are "cleaner" without the usual sycamore fuzz, and the bark is gray rather than mottled white. Compare this photo. It's curious that most of the trees near home this year aren't showing any fall color at all, the leaves are just turning brown and falling. Maybe that's a response to five drought years? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2016)
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: ga26a_ga22-021.jpg
Notes:
30291051560 Here are male (left) and female (right) Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) showing the difference in their palps - those little leg-like appendages on each side of their face. The male on the left has swollen palps - more than usual, maybe it's been mating? The female on the right has slender palps. See my [Previous] photo to see what the male does with those swollen palps! I don't think I've ever seen a male Green Lynx Spider before, see this photo from August 4. It's missing a few legs. Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2016)

It was quite warm today, up to 87∞ by the river where I was. I got lots of photos, but I'll save them for tomorrow when I have an appointment. Curiously, I found insects but no spiders today. It was calm today, but we're actually under a NWS Flash Flood Watch Thursday night and Friday with maybe a couple of inches of rain. Bring it on!

File.Name: ga26b_g210-010.jpg
Notes: 29956281923 I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, transferring genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigynum. It's a tender moment... and complicated! Mating is difficult for these predators who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. As I understand it, the male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates the spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. He then transfers his sperm to the female's epigynum. If you look very close at my photos, you can see the female's epigynum on the left and the the male palps making contact on the right. I have more photos of spider lover here, and I'm pretty sure I never posted these new ones. Happy hump day and Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; spermaphores; palps; epigynum
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; spermaphores; palps; epigynum

File.Name: ga26c_ga16-021.jpg
Notes: 29956123133 This is the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, with raindrops. I got this photo the morning after the first rain of the new water year, only about 1/10 inch, but good for spider webs. We got more rain the night after, but sometimes a little rain is better for web photos. This is for Web Wednesday, a good idea for a group. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2016)

File.Name: ga26d_ga26-005.jpg
Notes: 30589707575 Here is a hoard of ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending a hoard of small black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm not sure, but the ants might be the same California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) that devil us in the springtime, see this photo. One of the ants in the top-left seem to be harvesting honeydew from the aphids, but I reckon most of them are defending their herd from the tiny flies and wasps that would parasitize them. They're getting excited with me nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2016)

File.Name: ga27a_ga27-015.jpg
Notes:
30609149835 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) for prey on a flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). It's a pretty brutal scene. I believe the unfortunate fly is a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini) or kin. What's bad for the fly is good for the spider! The spiders are doing their job of picking off the last of the pollinators as the last flowers fade. Arachtober 27. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2016)

D'oh, I just noticed that my software settings have automagically changed and I've been saving at a much lower JPEG compression value than normal. No wonder my files are so small! I suppose I should go back and redo everything for the last two days (when it happened). Maybe next Tuesday. Flickr or our internet connection has been so pokey tonight that it's just as well I had smaller files to upload!

I do hope rain is coming. We've had some sprinkles this afternoon, and we have a 90% chance of real rain tonight. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch. The trees will be so happy!

(San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2016)

File.Name: ga28a_ga28-016.jpg
Notes:
30628835825 Here is another photo of a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), with raindrops. I have another photo of the same spider a week ago here. For some reason, the spider came out of its house for our rain last night and today. Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2016)

Yep, we did get some real rain last night, but the totals were disappointing - only about 1/2 inch, less than was forecast. We did have some great thunder and lightning to go with the rain, something you notice at 2:30 in the morning! Curiously, it stayed cloudy and there was no wind today after the storm. There's a 30% chance of a bit more rain Saturday night or Sunday.

File.Name: ga28b_ga28-006.jpg
Notes: 30540360611 This is a tiny Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web, with raindrops from our rain last night. Usually these spiders hang upside down in the center of their webs, but this one has been displaced by a water drop! This one has a clean web, but in time it will develop a "trashline" of the bodies of its prey along the midline, see this photo. I've just started to see these spiders this year - or am I just starting to notice them? Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2016)

File.Name: ga28c_ga26-067.jpg
Notes: 30628316095 This is a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) on a bluff above the dry Santa Ynez River. Maybe it's genus Peleteria? I have a photo of the other end of the same kind of fly here. This fly is not much larger than a house fly, but it's much more interesting. I've been seeing quite a few of these on all the late-season composite shrubs lately. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2016)

File.Name: ga28d_ga26-008.jpg
Notes: 30628143175 Here is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on the lip of a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2016)

File.Name: ga29a_ga29-018.jpg
Notes:
30533862622 "When you got íem, flaunt íem!"

Here is one more photo of the tiny spiderlings of Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox. They've been clumping up more in the cooler weather lately. They appeared on October 21, so I guess they're 9 days old as of today's photo. Arachtober 29. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2016)

There have been increasing clouds today, and the odds have improved to a 50% chance of a bit more rain tomorrow. We could use it. We've officially had 0.88 inch of rain since the water year began on October 1. That's just 61% of normal, so we're already running at a deficit.

File.Name: ga29b_ga27-006.jpg
Notes: 30533589112 Here are two different Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders on their webs. These spiders are tiny, 1/4 inch long or less, though they spin perfect little CD-size orb webs that catch your eye. The spider on the right has a tiny leafhopper for its prey. In time, it will add the remains to its web, developing a "trashline" of the bodies of its past-prey along the midline, like a once-living stabilimentum, that thicker white webbing behind the spider. I've just started to see these spiders this year - or am I just starting to notice them? I got both of these photos two days ago, before the rain. Arachtober 29. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2016)

File.Name: ga29c_ga29-037.jpg
Notes: 30350288100 I found this different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in a native tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I guess this one is genus Sinea, but I can't clearly see if the front legs are spiny. If they aren't, then it's genus Acholla, but that one isn't reported for California. What ever it is, it's a gnarly predator with a spike-like proboscis! This is the first one I've seen this year. It's interesting that I found the first one last year on October 6 in the same oak tree, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2016)

File.Name: ga29d_ga26-047.jpg
Notes: 30613444376 Here is a two-fer of Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) along the dry Santa Ynez River. I believe the one on the left is a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora, possibly the California Anthophora (Anthophora californica). I guess the one on the right is a Cuckoo Bee in the genus Triepeolus of the subfamily Nomadinae. These interesting bees are "kleptoparasites" of other bees, which means that they live off of another bee by stealing its food, rather than feeding on it directly. Wikipedia explains that "they typically enter the nests of other pollen-collecting species, and lay their eggs in cells provisioned by the host bee. When the cuckoo bee larva hatches it consumes the host larva's pollen ball, and, if the female kleptoparasite has not already done so, kills and eats the host larva." I'll post another photo of this interesting bee when I have a chance. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2016)
CommonNames: Wild Bees; Coastal Goldenbush; Mining Bee; California Anthophora; Cuckoo Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Anthophora; Anthophora californica; Triepeolus; Nomadinae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Anthophora; Anthophora californica; Triepeolus; Nomadinae

File.Name: ga30a_ga30-001.jpg
Notes:
30676257325 This is our other mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae), the straw-colored one I showed in this photo back on Arachtober 5. She's *still* sitting on her egg sack, and I hope the eggs hatch - but sometimes the spiderlings fail to emerge. If you compare the earlier photo, you can see a couple of changes. The white flowers of the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) have turned brown as they fade. More peculiar, the egg sack has moved from below the flowers to above them. It's like Igor's hump in the movie Young Frankenstein - you never see it move from side to side, but it does! Over the course of the month, we've gone from lots of spiders to very few. Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2016)

There was gloom, rain, and wind today - sometimes all at the same time! We ended up with a bit over 1/2 inch, more than was expected, and a bit more than we had a few days ago. Every new rain is significant after five years of drought.
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Young Frankenstein

File.Name: ga30b_ga30-010.jpg
Notes: 30044212024 Here is a spider web with raindrops - "catenaries and [tiny] dirigibles" as Joanna Newsom sings. There's no spider to be seen, but maybe this is the work of a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) with that haphazard web? It doesn't really matter, but I believe the shrub is an interesting Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei, Fagaceae) with leaves that are smooth and shiny underneath, see this photo - and ignore what I call it there. There was gloom, drizzle, and wind this morning - all at the same time. I did the best I could for photos. Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2016)

File.Name: ga30c_ga30-015.jpg
Notes: 30675194255 This is a fine autumn flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria or Chicoryleaf Wire-lettuce (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae) in the rain today. Like true Chicory, the flowers usually close in the morning, but they stay open on gloomy days like today. The flowers are just about done for the season, but there are still a few. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2016)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Chicoryleaf Wire-lettuce

File.Name: ga30d_ga30-020.jpg
Notes: 30043752004 Here are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, in the rain today! They are showing color and getting closer to being ripe, and will earn their other common name of Christmas Berry when the holiday season comes. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2016)

File.Name: ga31a_ga19-014.jpg
Notes:
30611710461 This large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is guarding a flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) waiting for prey. Or maybe not. This is the same spider I show in this photo with two flies for prey. I got this photo an hour later as I returned from my hike along the same path. The spider might be a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), but cp. the Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia). (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2016)

I've been saving this photo for a couple of weeks for Halloween - and the last day of Arachtober 2016. It's been great fun hunting for spiders every day as a kind of scavenger hunt. I've found them most days at least. I've learned a lot too, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home! Thanks for your support. Now to get back to all the other creature in the woods.

Our 1/2 inch of rain yesterday brought our total rainfall up to 1.42 inches, 87% of normal. It's a start, but who knows what the rest of the water year will bring? Temperatures have been cool, but it's expected to swing back to the warm side over the next few days. I did find another mushroom today - photo tomorrow!

File.Name: ga31e_ga31-031.jpg
Notes: 30610642821 I suspect this is a tiny Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae. It has a small flat web in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that is not a dome or bowl. The spider is only about 1/4 inch long. Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2016)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: gb01a_gb01-010.jpg
Notes:
30606164442 These are the first real flowers I've seen this year on Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - the first flowers of any native plant in the new water year! It was curious that I found glandular flower buds showing color more than a month ago on the Autumnal Equinox (Sep. 21), see this photo. And then the flower buds ... just dried up. I reckon five years of drought has something to do with that. Anyway, here are real open flowers. May there be many more. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2016)

Today's photo all show some of what the rain brings - flowers, liverworts, lichens, and mushrooms! It was quite windy most of today, but the winds are dying down as I write this, and I have hopes that tomorrow will be a good day for photos.

File.Name: gb01b_ga31-026.jpg
Notes: 30090191634 I believe these are California Liverworts (Asterella californica) coming back to life after our rain a couple of days ago. They are real "resurrection plants" that go from black crust to foliage all at once, visible in my photo from top to bottom. The small-leaved Orobus-seed Liverworts (Targionia hypophylla) are even faster, see this photo. Both liverworts live on sandstone rock outcrops, as the sandstone is porous enough to hold water after the rain. Am I imagining things or do these liverworts already have the bumpy tongue-like structures on the "leaves" that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain? Later in the year the same liverworts might producing umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction, see this photo. Bryophyte reproduction is really complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2016)
ScientificName: Asterella californica; Targionia hypophylla; gemmae; sporophytes
associatedTaxa: Asterella californica; Targionia hypophylla; gemmae; sporophytes

File.Name: gb01c_ga30-004.jpg
Notes: 30421864680 This is a striking colony of Gold-Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) growing with other gray lichens on a mostly-dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - in the rain. The orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. Lichens don't disappear during the dry time of year, but they do shrivel up, so it's a great pleasure when they revive after an early rain. These look like they belong under the sea! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2016)
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia

File.Name: gb01d_ga31-002.jpg
Notes: 30721818295 This is a tiny Mycena mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods yesterday, the first I've seen this year. It actually seems wrong to find just one of these little mushrooms as they usually come in hoards - see this photo from last year. It takes a mind-shift to go from photographing insects and spiders to mushrooms, but that time seems to be coming - at least if the wind lets it. I tried to get another photo today with better color, and found the mushrooms are already starting to dry out because of the drying winds last night and today. QuÈ L·stima. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2016)

File.Name: gb02a_gb02-015.jpg
Notes:
30111253954 This is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - almost certainly Largus californicus. This one is looking for nourishment on a nearly finished staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). This is another bug with five-segmented antennae, but in a different group than the bug in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)

Today was just about a perfect photo day - sunny and dead calm all morning. There weren't many insects, but I had a chance to get my focus right instead of just taking photos in the wind and hoping one comes out. May we have many more days like this!
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: gb02b_ga29-027.jpg
Notes: 30107721673 This is a nickle-size Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a dead-looking shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the canyon. There are many different kinds, so I won't try to guess what it is. This one has five-segment antennae unlike the four-segment antennae of the bugs in my [Previous] photo, hence the family name Penta-tomidae. The shrub is alive but dormant - some plants are starting to show green buds. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2016)

File.Name: gb02c_gb02-058.jpg
Notes: 30626154202 I almost gave up trying to find any mating insects for hump day today, but then I found this mating pair of tiny "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the phyllaries of a spent flower head of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Hah - the whole involucre is only about 1/2 inch long, so the bugs really are tiny. They have four-segmented antennae, but other than that I don't have a clue what kind of bugs they are. Both bugs and flower have interesting textures. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)

File.Name: gb02d_ga28-002.jpg
Notes: 30705966216 This is the web of a Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider, with raindrops from our rain last week. The web is about the size of a CD, and the spider is tiny. You can see it hanging slight off from the center, see this photo for a close shot. This spider has a clean web, but in time it will develop a "trashline" of the bodies of its prey along the midline. The web is in a non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) shrub on the edge of our garden. Arachtober is over, but now there is Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2016)

File.Name: gb03a_gb03-031.jpg
Notes:
30131478654 This is a odd butt shot of an Earwig (order Dermaptera) on a non-native plant of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae) down by the river. It's making a display with its claw-like rear-end forceps. It's curious because I found it a mile from any residence and I think of these as household pests. Maybe it's not the all-too-common European Earwig (Forficula auricularia), but it sure looks like it. I found several of these today, also on the native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) nearby. I'll post a better photo tomorrow, but I like this for butt shot Thursday - HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 November 2016)

It was another almost perfect photo day - sunny and dead calm all morning. I went down to the river again, and I was surprised by how much water there was at the first crossing. I slid a bit getting across in my truck.
CommonNames: Earwig; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed; European Earwig; Coastal Goldenbush

File.Name: gb03b_gb02-021.jpg
Notes: 30726487496 This is a nickle-size Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera in the soft furry leaves of a shrub of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae, Boraginaceae). I'm pretty sure it's the adult that goes with the nymph in this photo. (I got both photos along the same trail.) It might well be a Green Stink Bug (genus Chinavia) - in particular Chinavia hilaris. Thanks for the suggestion Rockwolf! The pattern of overlapping wings and scutellum is beautiful. It also happens to a a photo of the bug's posterior, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)

File.Name: gb03c_gb02-020.jpg
Notes: 30763067525 This is a butt shot of the same Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera that I show two photos back, here. It's almost certainly Largus californicus. This one is on a nearly finished staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Yep, this shot is almost too revealing. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)

File.Name: gb03d_gb02-006.jpg
Notes: 30130751554 This is the bristly butt of a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae), maybe genus Peleteria. I'm pretty sure it's the same kind of fly I showed last week for BBBT, photo here, and see this photo for the other end of the fly. I'll have another photo for FDF tomorrow. These flies are particularly common right now on flowering composite shrubs. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)

File.Name: gb04a_gb04-020.jpg
Notes:
30481597890 Wow, this is a remarkable photo of small galls on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The pea-size galls are the work of Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). First, this photo is remarkable because it shows two different galls on one leaf. I'm pretty sure the white gall on top is the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi), and the dark gall on the bottom is the gall of a Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa). Second, it's remarkable because it shows a real Gall Wasp! The wasp was interested in the white gall. I'm not sure if this is the wasp that induced the gall, or an inquiline whose larvae eat the gall tissue, or a parasite that will attack the gall-inducers larvae, or even a hyperparasite that attacks the larvae of the parasites! The wasp is tiny, less than 1/8 inch long. I love photographing galls, but I don't usually find the wasps associated with them, so this was a treat. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2016)

I need to spend a few days posting photos of galls. I've been finding quite a few of them lately, but they usually get eclipsed by photos of other critters. It was another fine photo day, sunny and calm - though there were some high clouds and a bit of a breeze today. I fear our perfect photo days will come to an end.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp; Woollybear Gall Wasp; Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus fullawayi; Sphaeroteras timaculosum; Atrusca trimaculosa; inquiline; parasite; hyperparasite
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus fullawayi; Sphaeroteras timaculosum; Atrusca trimaculosa; inquiline; parasite; hyperparasite

File.Name: gb04b_gb02-010.jpg
Notes: 30782446305 This is yet another bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae), maybe genus Peleteria. These flies are particularly common right now on flowering composite shrubs like Baccharis and Isocoma down by the river. I have a photo of the other end of the fly here. I find them irresistible to photograph because their bristles are so well defined. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2016)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Peleteria; Baccharis; Isocoma

File.Name: gb04c_gb03-007.jpg
Notes:
30665238002 This is a lovely flower of ill-smelling Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing down by the river. Here it is growing with non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae). After getting the photo, I noticed the tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a stamen of the flower. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 November 2016)

The datura flowers were a favorite of the American artist Georgia O'keeffe - see here. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. The flowers are large and beautiful, but they really do stink.
CommonNames: Toloache; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Datura wrightii; Solanaceae; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: gb04d_gb03-036.jpg
Notes: 30665174122 Here is another photo of the odd Earwig (order Dermaptera) that I found yesterday on a non-native plant of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae) down by the river. It's odd because I found it a mile or more away from any residence and I think of these as household pests. Maybe it's not the all-too-common European Earwig (Forficula auricularia), but it sure looks like it. I found several of these, also on the native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) nearby. It's an impressive insect in its own way. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 November 2016)

File.Name: gb05a_gb05-019.jpg
Notes:
30714827651 Here is a little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) landing on a flower of native Common Rush-rose aka Peak Rush-rose (Crocanthemum scoparium aka Helianthemum scoparium) in the Cistaceae plant family. It's an interesting family that also includes several non-native species in the genus Cistus that become weedy in the mountains. This one is a bushy perennial with the stature of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber) that grows in dry places, often on burns as a fire-follower. The stamens of the flowers have a curious lack of radial symmetry. I usually find it without leaves, as a "broom" plant. I figure than every native plant that is still flowering in November after five years of drought deserves some respect - and a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2016)

It was another fine photo day, sunny and not too breezy. Again, I fear our perfect photo days will come to an end - but not yet!
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Common Rush-rose; Peak Rush-rose; Deerweed

File.Name: gb05b_gb05-014.jpg
Notes: 30171616674 These are ripening berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the woods. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy, and they have an interesting bumpy texture. Not every tree has berries in this drought year, but hopefully there will be enough for the Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata), see my Christmas photo from a few years ago. I've been hearing the pigeons lately in our oak trees. Compare my recent photo of native Toyon berries. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2016)

File.Name: gb05c_gb05-032.jpg
Notes:
30171135444 I found another mushroom in the garden today, though it already feels quite dry after our inch of rain a week ago. I reckon this one is a Lepiota, maybe the Shaggy Parasol Mushroom (Chlorophyllum rhacodes aka Lepiota rachodes aka Macrolepiota rhacodes). The scales on the cap seem a bit smaller than typical, but it has been dry and it's still early in the season. If you look close, you can see that the gills are turning pinkish orange around the cap margin. It is said to be edible and choice if this is what it is, but I don't trust my ID on this one. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2016)

It will take me a while to get up to speed with mushrooms again. Every year I have to relearn all the names, and five years of drought hasn't helped. Hah - in the last five years the whole nomenclature has changed!
ScientificName: Lepiota; Chlorophyllum rhacodes; Lepiota rachodes; Macrolepiota rhacodes
associatedTaxa: Lepiota; Chlorophyllum rhacodes; Lepiota rachodes; Macrolepiota rhacodes

File.Name: gb05d_gb04-014.jpg
Notes: 30170593504 Here are a pair of similar woolly galls on different oaks in the Fagaceae plant family. Both galls are the work of Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). They look similar, though there is a difference in size. The pea-size gall on the left is the same kind of gall I showed in my [Previous] photo - with a gall wasp! I believe it is the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi). It's growing on a shrub of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). The walnut-size gall on the right is growing on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). I believe it is the gall of a Woolly Gall Wasp (Heteroecus dasydactyli). It's remarkable how many different kinds of galls there are, and I enjoy hunting for them. You have to hatch out the wasps and identify them to be sure of your ID. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2016)
CommonNames: Gall Wasps; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Woolly Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus fullawayi; Quercus berberidifolia; Quercus chrysolepis; Heteroecus dasydactyli

File.Name: gb06a_gb06-042.jpg
Notes:
30827322245 Here are the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family that I've seen this year - with tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera). I believe they are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, but predatory on what? This shrub was not flowering a week ago, but I even heard a hummingbird visiting today. See my [Previous] photo for the open urn-like flowers. This is the second native plant I've found flowering this year, after the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) in this photo. These early flowers are important for sustaining many different critters besides thrips. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 November 2016)

It was another decent day for new photos, but with more breeze and high clouds than we've been having. The seasons and the cycles of the year turn, turn, turn...
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; thrips; Predatory Thrips; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: gb06b_gb06-019.jpg
Notes: 30790355126 Here is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working brand new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today. This shrub was not flowering the last time I was up here, on October 25, but it already has lots of flowers. This one particular shrub is early-flowering every year, see this photo from last year. I've found a few early flowers on other shrubs as well, see my [Next] photo. I even heard a hummingbird visiting this shrub today! This manzanita is the second native plant I've found flowering this year, after the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) in this photo. These early flowers are important for sustaining many different critters. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 November 2016)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Bigberry Manzanita; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: gb06c_gb06-007.jpg
Notes: 30525627440 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working one of the last flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). I don't think the flowers will be around much longer, and I'll miss them. The butterflies however are something new, despite the tattered appearance of this one. I haven't photographed one since mid-June, though I've seen a few by the river recently. They are mostly interested in the new manzanita flowers a mile away, see my [Next] photo. You can see the butterfly's four large eyespots below its hindwing which distinguishes it from the American Lady in this photo. The West Coast Lady also has four eyespots, photo here. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 November 2016)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; American Lady; West Coast Lady

File.Name: gb06d_gb06-053.jpg
Notes: 30525471050 Here are deciduous autumn leaves and new seeds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The seeds are pretty to look at, but don't touch if you are sensitive. Sure, you can plant them. It's a good way to keep people out of a place you don't want them to be! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 November 2016)

File.Name: gb07a_gb07-035.jpg
Notes:
30217416694 This is a handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. There are lots of different kinds, maybe this one is genus Chinavia or Thyanta? (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2016)

It was another lovely day with high clouds and not much breeze. The election has been wearing me down, so I'll be glad when its all over - hopefully tomorrow. I wonder if the internet will be compromised as some are suggesting? If I don't post tomorrow, that's why!

File.Name: gb07b_gb07-020.jpg
Notes:
30548309320 "Reach out and touch someone..."

Here is a dramatic shot of a remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the leaves of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. It's small, about the same size as the genus Mecaphesa spiders I find on the autumn flowers, see this photo, but it looks very different. After hitting my books, I believe it is species Coriarachne brunneipes. The almost-black color and the very flat carapace are distinctive. This one is definitely a male with those large palps. I've never noticed these spiders before. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2016)

File.Name: gb07d_gb07-033.jpg
Notes: 30216767024 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on a leaf in the woods, maybe the eggs of true bugs (Heteroptera, Hemiptera). The exit holes are about 110 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 0.43 mm, really tiny. (See here for how I figured.) It looks like they all hatched except maybe that one in the lower left corner. Not that it matters, but the leaf belongs to a shrub of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2016)

File.Name: gb08a_gb08-022.jpg
Notes:
30833670896 Here is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) outside of its house. It's not a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but it definitely has a cat's face on its abdomen! This is the same spider I show in this photo and this photo and this photo. Well, why not? It's beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2016)

It was breezy today. Now it's time to catch up on the election results.

File.Name: gb08b_gb07-003.jpg
Notes: 30234407653 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are lots of these little jumpers that I can't tell apart, and this is one of them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2016)

File.Name: gb08c_gb08-006.jpg
Notes:
30833099056 I find the darned things while looking for galls in the oak trees! This one looks like a raspberry, and it's about that size. I believe it's the start of a gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. In time the galls will become quite large, an inch or two across, maybe the size of a small tangerine or golf ball. This is one of the first new galls I've seen all year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2016)

These galls look superficially similar to those of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus), but they are made by different critters with different lives on different oaks. I have a comparison photo here. Live Oak galls tend to have a more interesting texture. These galls are made by fully sexual wasps, and they go through alternate generations that look completely different - see this photo. The galls of the California Gall Wasp are apparently completely parthenogenetic. Neither males nor an alternate generation is known.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp

File.Name: gb08d_gb08-012.jpg
Notes:
30869361975 Bernhard Jacobi nailed it - this is the nest of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, maybe genus Dianthidium or Anthidiellum. See this image at BugGuide which is almost a copy of my photo. I reckon bees and wasps do have the jaws to lug around small pebbles. Live and learn!

Original comment: br>I found this nest - I guess it's an insect nest - on a dead stem in the woods today, a couple of feet off the ground. It looks like a terrestrial caddisfly made it. Yeah, it might be the case of a moth caterpillar, but how did any insect larva lug all those tiny rocks (really coarse sand grains) up the stem? WTF? I've found these nests before - once in this photo and again in this earlier photo at Fotolog in 2010. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2016)

File.Name: gb09a_gb09-033.jpg
Notes:
30252824953 Blowing in the wind...

This is a seedpod of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae) sending seeds into the world. If you look close inside the pod, you can see a sly couple of Common Milkweed Bugs (Lygaeus kalmii). I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped at my usual place along the highway. Wind and traffic were a problem, but I like this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 November 2016)

It was downright warm today, in the low 90s up here on the pass. That's too hot for November. The election results last night were unexpected and stunning. It was a good day to go for a long walk - in my case, a short walk and a drive.

File.Name: gb09b_gb09-002.jpg
Notes:
30771954382 Here are a pair of tiny galls on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Both galls are the work of Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), and they are about the same size. They are both about about 550 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to a bit over 2 mm long. (See here for how I figured.) The exit hole on the left is just 1 mm across. These are tiny wasps!

I'm pretty sure the gall on the left is the work of the Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus). I'm not so sure about the gall on the right, but maybe it's the work of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus) - that only has one horn? Maybe not. This gall seems to have an association with those fissures that are visible on the leaf veins. I found several of the galls today, and every one was next to a fissure. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Gall Wasps; Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus minusculus; Dryocosmus dubiosus

File.Name: gb09c_gb09-001.jpg
Notes:
30252171883 Here are two photos of different new and old galls of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on small stems of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They are about the size of a blueberry. The green galls usually mature quickly and drop to the ground, but the gall on the right is the first brown gall I've seen, still in the tree. I think it's wedged in between leaves and stem so that it can't drop. Autumn seems to be their time. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2016)

This is a unisexual gall, which contains only female wasps. The lady wasps will emerge next spring and parthenogenetically produce a crop of tiny bisexual galls that contain both male and female wasps. The wasps emerge, mate, and induce another crop of unisexual galls that contain only females, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: gb09d_gb08-017.jpg
Notes: 30252171883 This is the gall of a Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) growing on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The galls start as gradually tapered smooth swellings on the twig, see this photo. In time, the galls develop fissures and the gall capsules somehow rise to the surface, burst through, and drop to the forest floor - kind of like a boil? In this photo, you can see the "shell" of one of the larval capsules, which appears to be woody. Maybe a mouse or bird predator got into this gall before they dropped? Then again, maybe this just is what they look like. Curiously, just I found the same kind of gall on a small tree of (I think) Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei, Fagaceae). I'll post a photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2016)
