File.Name: gb10a_gb10-021.jpg
Notes:
30604944400 Here are two photos of the openings that appear in many of the new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I assume that some critter couldn't wait for the flowers to open, and made its own opening. The photo on the right shows a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the hole rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. I'm sure some other creature made the hole. The flower on the left shows a tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) standing by a hole that you can see into, like those those candy Easter eggs that you look inside of. The thrips run in and out of the flowers through the opening. I would expect the original thief would be after nectar, but aren't those stamens inside where some critter has got the pollen? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 November 2016)

BTW, yes it's one thrips - the final "s" is in both the singular and plural versions of this critter's name, maybe because it's so uncommon to find just one? I believe it is a Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, see this photo.

The NWS forecast was for sunny and calm today, but it was sunny and breezy instead. Not the best day for new photos, but it was so nice that I had to get out!

File.Name: gb10c_gb09-016.jpg
Notes: 30817496731 This Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) is kicking up its heels on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae)! We seem to have both large and small ones, and they both have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. This is the smaller one. Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it's a handsome fly - albeit tough and gnarly. It's also a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2016)

File.Name: gb10d_gb10-005.jpg
Notes: 30789332152 This is looking close at an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn. They that get everywhere, including the stigma on the far left. Is there any way these flowers can fail to be self-pollinated? It does the soul some good to find these flowers at the very end of our season. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year. The plants have been flowering for a while, but this is their main time - right now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 November 2016)

File.Name: gb11a_gb11-016.jpg
Notes:
30809948632 I believe these are both galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but they are growing on different kinds of oak. The gall on the left is on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The gall on the right is on (I believe) a shrub of Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei). That one is an interesting native shrub or small tree that has leaves that are toothed or not, and smooth underneath. Both are "Black Oaks" with thin acorn scales. Could the extraordinary growth in this photo on the same kind of oak be the same gall? These galls are similar but different from the galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2016)

There were high clouds and breeze for most of today. Not the best day for new photos again, but I got a few.

File.Name: gb11c_gb10-003.jpg
Notes:
25290015489 I couldn't resist trying to get a photo of this tiny Vinegar Fly (Drosophilidae, Diptera) on a fallen apple in deep shade from the tree on the edge of our garden. The fly is about 640 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2.5 mm long, about 1/10 of an inch. (See here for how I figured.) Sure, it's not the best photo - but I did get something, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2016)

This fly looks like a tiny version of the fly in this photo in the family Lauxaniidae or Heleomyzidae. I've been confused about these larger flies in the past. I'm sure that this fly is a real Drosophilid.

File.Name: gb12a_gb12-039.jpg
Notes:
25310728859 This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) getting nectar from a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I get a stiff neck just looking at his photo! I've only found two of these shrubs flowering so far this year, and each one has a resident hummingbird that tries to drive away all the other hummers. Of course the other hummers always sneak in on the other side of the shrub! Today it seemed that the hummer was also chasing the Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies from the shrub as well, see this photo. Was I imagining it or were the butterflies also chasing the hummer?? Manzanita is not a classic hummingbird flower with a long red floral tube, but it's one of the first flowers to appear in autumn, and a bridge between summer and spring for the hummers. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 November 2016)

It was cloudy enough today that I had to think about white balance, and cool enough that I was comfortable wearing sweat pants. The weather is changing, but too slowly for my taste.
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Bigberry Manzanita; Painted Lady
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: gb12b_gb12-005.jpg
Notes:
30946455205 These are interesting galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls are about the size of a pencil eraser and look like little muffins. The galls produce real "oak honey" on their upper surface which attracts ants and other insects, but these galls seem to be dry. If you look in the shadows behind the gall on the right, you can see that the galls have attracted a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) - a predator on the wasp larvae inside! I believe the wasp is genus Torymus in the Chalcid Wasps (Chalcidoidea). See this photo of a similar wasp on a different gall. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2016)

There was a breeze this morning, so I took a number of photos of these galls to try to get a sharp one. I didn't notice the wasp at the time, and it only appears in this one of my photos. That's another advantage of always taking *lots* of photos!
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Chalcid Wasps

File.Name: gb12c_gb12-001.jpg
Notes: 30644202440 These are galls of the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on a dying leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Many galls apparently don't harm their host plant, but these pea-size galls do take a toll on the leaves. This has not been a good year for oak galls, no doubt because it hasn't been a good year for the wasps that make them. Five years of drought has made a difference, but I have been finding them. Autumn is their time, and galls nicely fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2016)

File.Name: gb12d_gb11-022.jpg
Notes:
30909459856 Here are a conjoined pair of classic "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They are each about the size of a golf ball. These galls start succulent and red, but these are starting to turn dry and brown. It's curious that at the same time that these are finishing, other galls are just starting. I don't understand the timing of these galls at all. I found another pair of conjoined galls in a different place last year.

These galls are different from the galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in my [Previous] photo, see this photo for a direct comparison. They grow on different oaks and have a different life cycle. The galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp are made by fully sexual wasps, and they go through alternate generations that look completely different. The galls of the California Gall Wasp (this one) are apparently completely parthenogenetic, and neither males nor an alternate generation is known. Galls are complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2016)
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis

File.Name: gb13a_gb13-003.jpg
Notes:
22790782278 This is a "butt shot" of a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) snuggled into an old flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae plant family. She's trying to hide "Ostrich-style" - if she can't see you then you can't see her! Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day - and only the broad-winged males sing. I don't often see them until ... there one is! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2016)

It was sunny and warmer today, into the mid-80s. The wind wasn't too bad. A couple of days ago the NWS Area Forecast Discussion writer said "There will be no ... weather to speak of over the next three days." So it goes...

File.Name: gb13b_gb13-031.jpg
Notes: 30968799565 This isn't the best photo by any means, but it shows a lot happening! Starting from the background, this is a green branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). The branch is covered by a hoard of small black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera). There's also a mysterious orange thing which I guess is a Scale Insect (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera with a shell perforated with odd circular holes. There's an interesting fly (Diptera) on top of the scale insect that is being run off by an ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) which is tending the aphids. The fly did take off, but it only went a few inches. That's a lot of tags for this photo! I reckon the star of this photo is that fly. I'm not sure what kind it is, but the ant sure wanted to chase it away. It doesn't look like a typical aphid parasite, but I reckon it is. I'll post another photo of the fly at some point, but I want to see if I can get a better photo first. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2016)

File.Name: gb13c_gb12-033.jpg
Notes: 30880432831 There are new flowers in the woods like the manzanita in my [Previous] photo that belong to next year's flowers, but there are also the last of this year's flowers. These are new flowers of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Many plants are done flowering, but a few are still going. It's interesting that this shrub has mostly red flowers and just one of the yellow flowers that is typical earlier in the year. I don't know why. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2016)

File.Name: gb13d_gb12-040.jpg
Notes:
30968237765 This is another shot of a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) getting nectar from a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I get an even stiffer neck looking at this photo! See my [Previous] photo from yesterday for additional comments. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 November 2016)

File.Name: gb14a_gb14-003.jpg
Notes:
25356195739 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower cluster of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) - in the mountains rather than down by the river where I usually find it. This one is definitely a male with those (relatively) large palps. Compare the size of the flowers - this little spider could dance on the head of the Jumping Spider of my [Previous] photo. There was a breeze today that was rocking this shrub back and forth, I was lucky to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2016)

Today was another day with "no weather to speak of" - though there was some breeze, just enough to keep everything in motion. We have *another* maintenance power outage tomorrow from 8 am to 7 pm. At least that's what they say, but I've lost confidence in the power company's announcements. We've had warnings with no outages and outages with no warnings lately. Bah. Maybe I can post tomorrow, maybe not.

File.Name: gb14b_gb14-044.jpg
Notes: 30875072082 This is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus on a flower cluster of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) - in the mountains rather than down by the river where I usually find it. I kept waiting for the spider to turn its head towards me, but it refused to model for me. The spider is looking right at me with (I think) its posterior lateral eye! There was a breeze today that was rocking this shrub back and forth. I took a lot of photos to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2016)

File.Name: gb14c_gb03-006.jpg
Notes: 22812543268 Here are two shots of different diurnal (daytime) moths (Lepidoptera) working flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) down by the river. I'm not sure if these are the same or different moths. They certainly look the same, but there are some differences in color and pattern. I'm not sure what kind of moths they are, but my first guess is the Crambid Snout Moths in the Crambidae family. If you look close at the photo on the right, you can see that it does have scales at the base of its proboscis - one of the family traits. These moths have been quite common on the flowers. I got these photos a couple of weeks ago and meant save them for the Moth Monday group. I finally remembered to post them, so HMM! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 November 2016)

File.Name: gb16a_gb16-006.jpg
Notes:
30926279731 I guess these are odd scale-like pupae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Or are they larvae? They are tiny, about 260 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1.0 mm long. (See here for how I figured.) This one fallen leaf was loaded with these critters. If you look very close, you can see white specs that look like eggs on the leaf. (I'll make a note.) (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2016)

It's been exactly 6 months since my bypass surgery on May 16, see this photo from a week after. I'm really feeling good. That's a lot of good natural history photos that might not have been!

We didn't get our power back on until 8:30 last night - too late to even think about posting. I hope the scheduled maintenance did some good. It was cool today, gloomy in the morning and windy in the afternoon. We've had gusts above 30 mph, and we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. Wind, wind, wind - I'm also pretty distracted because we're off to our cabin in Oregon this weekend for a couple of weeks ... and Thanksgiving. I'll be busy for the next few days, but I'll try to get some photos before we leave.

File.Name: gb16b_gb16-014.jpg
Notes: 31040030245 Here are two shots of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) greening up in the canyon. I got the photo on the left a month ago, and the photo on the right today. You can see just a hint of green on the left - just enough to tell that the shrub is not dead. Both plants also have a fine filagree of spider webs, maybe the work of Cobweb Spiders (Theridiidae) with that haphazard web? I reckon it's enough of a web for Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2016)

File.Name: gb16c_gb15-029.jpg
Notes: 31039906435 Here are dry Cattails (family Typhaceae) at what's left of the river. They are starting to release their fluffy seeds. I've never learned cattails, but maybe these are the Narrowleaf Cattail (Typha angustifolia)? This is a place on the Santa Ynez River that had water last year, but has now gone drys after five years of drought. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 November 2016)

File.Name: gb16d_gb15-020.jpg
Notes: 31039502485 *Sigh* This is all that's left of one of the dry swimming holes on the Santa Ynez River after 5 years of drought. It's become a sun-cracked mud flat with an invasion of non-native Saltcedar or Tamarisk, maybe Tamarix ramosissima in the Tamaricaceae plant family, though there are several different species. Tamarisk is a horrible weed along western rivers. It literally takes over the banks of rivers like the Green River, making it impossible to find a place to land on a float trip. These small plants on the Santa Ynez River are hopefully ephemeral - they will disappear the next time the river floods. But there are larger trees upriver that will keep replenishing them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 November 2016)

File.Name: gb17a_gb17-008.jpg
Notes:
31072231375 A blessed event ... and a mystery.

Finally, here are the spiderlings of our other mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox. This is the straw-colored one I showed sitting on her egg sack in this photo on October 30, and again in this photo way back on October 5. The eggs finally just hatched, but mom has disappeared. I figured mom had given up on her nest like I almost had. But here's the mystery. I didn't notice until I got the photo up on my screen, but you can see one of mama spider's legs wrapped around the stem on the right. (I'll make a note.) I wonder what happened? (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2016)

I didn't have much hope for new photos today, but it was less windy than forecast. It's chilly. There are NWS rumors that we'll get our first frost tonight.

File.Name: gb17b_gb17-028.jpg
Notes: 31071583825 This is a shepherding ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) looking over its flock of tiny black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera). Hah, the ant is standing on the mound of a mysterious orange Scale Insect (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. See this photo for the addition of a fly to the scene. (I'll post a photo of the fly tomorrow for FDF.) I'm pretty sure the ant is a California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae). These are the same ants that devil us in the springtime, see this photo. This shot shows the velvety abdomen of the ant, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2016)

Notes: 31071302685 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), which have a more petioled abdomen than genus Polistes. It's a paper wasp which looks threatening enough, but I've never been stung. This one is grooming its hind legs and abdomen on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. It's a good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2016)

File.Name: gb17d_gb17-011.jpg
Notes:
30928346092 What a difference 11 days makes!

I got the photo on the left 11 days ago on November 6, showing newly opened flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I got the photo on the right today, showing the same cluster of flowers, now almost done. How quickly the flowers fade. Fortunately there are lots of other shrubs nearby just starting to flower, so the butterflies and hummingbirds won't go hungry! (See this photo and this one.) (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 November 2016)

Do manzanita flowers always fade this fast or does it have something to do with our ongoing drought? I've also noticed that the new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) are fading very quickly. Darn, I can't remember - but I'm sure I have it in my notes somewhere.

File.Name: gb18a_gb14-030.jpg
Notes:
31100528545 Here are pretty new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - the first flowers of any native plant in the new water year, see this photo and this one from September 21. Now they are joined by the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in my [Previous] photo, but the Ribes will go for much longer. They are coming into their own now. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2016)

It was too windy for photos today, but I've been busy with errands before we head off to Oregon on Sunday. Hah, there's also a good chance of rain on Sunday!

File.Name: gb18b_gb17-013.jpg
Notes:
30293760733 Where have all the flowers gone?

Yesterday I showed a photo of the fading new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Here is where they've gone. The spent flowers have collected in a ditch at the base of the shrub. Work is done, it's time to lie down. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 November 2016)

File.Name: gb18c_gb13-017.jpg
Notes: 30293682283 This is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. We seem to have both large and small ones, and they both have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. This is the larger one. Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it is a handsome fly - albeit tough and gnarly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2016)

File.Name: gb18d_gb15-033.jpg
Notes: 31064036356 I know these aren't the best photos, but they show an interesting predatory fly (Diptera) with small black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I believe the fly is a Silver Fly or Aphid Fly in the family Chamaemyiidae, possibly genus Leucopis. Their larvae are predators of the aphids, see this photo at BugGude. They look a lot like the larvae of Syrphid Flies (Syrphidae). I didn't see any larvae on the plant, but there do seem to be fewer aphids every day that I look at them, so something is happening. The aphids don't seem to pay any attention to the flies, but the ants that tend the aphids really don't like them - see this photo! The flies are tiny, about 600 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots - though it's hard to tell where the actual fly ends. That comes to about 2.4 mm long, a bit less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I can't seem to get a sharper photo of these tiny flies, so this will have to do. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2016)

File.Name: gb19a_gb19-001.jpg
Notes:
30315446763 This is a small fly (Diptera) perched on the beak of a dead House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird above the retaining wall on the wild edge of our garden. The bird makes a good ruler for the fly, which has its proboscis puffed out for work. (Flies can puff their proboscis out or suck it back into their heads - it's all pneumatics!) The fly looks a bit like the Drosophilid Fly in this photo, but it's larger, about the size of a House Fly. Maybe it's family Lauxaniidae or genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae? (See Thijs de Graaf's comment on this photo.) Whatever it is, it looks striking, despite the fact that I got the photo in deep shade this morning. The unfortunate bird got into our house a week ago, and our cat found it before I did. I put it in this place outside to let nature do its work - and keep an eye on the progress. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2016)

I should be packing for our Thanksgiving trip to Oregon, but the forecast is for heavy rain tomorrow (!) so we'll probably wait until Monday to leave. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch! At least that's the forecast. On the other hand, there's not a cloud in the sky, and nothing on the radar map. I hope this isn't another false alarm. We'll probably still be here tomorrow, but we might take off if the storm craps out. We'll be off the grid for a couple of weeks.

File.Name: gb19b_gb19-009.jpg
Notes: 30315259273 This is a handsome Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly resting (for just a moment) in the oak leaf mulch in the canyon. I've been seeing these butterflies for the past week - along with a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly - for the past week, and this is the first one I've managed to photograph. They are quite camera shy! Going through my photo archives to find when I've photographed them before, I find 7 photos from March to July and 17 photos from September to January. This seems to be their time in fall and winter, at least here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2016)

How handsome they are on the white-barked branches. The large palmate leaves look like the leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae) in this photo, but there are differences. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2016)

Notes: 31121525635 Here are dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) caught up in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae. This shrub is sometimes called "Sticky Monkeyflower," but there's not much stickiness left in the leaves at the end of our summer drought season. The Coyote Bush shrubs are just starting to release seeds. They are everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2016)

File.Name: gb20a_gb19-003.jpg
Notes:
31145023305 Here is another shot of the small fly (Diptera) perched on the beak of a dead House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird in my [Previous] photo. The little bird makes a good ruler for the fly. The fly looks a bit like the Drosophilid Fly in this photo, but it's larger, about the size of a House Fly. Maybe it's family Lauxaniidae or (more likely) genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae? (See Thijs de Graaf's comment on this photo.) Whatever it is, it looks striking, despite the fact that I got the photo in deep shade this morning. The unfortunate bird got into our house a week ago, and our cat found it before I did. I put it in this place outside to let nature do its work - and keep an eye on the progress. Just this one photo today, as I spent the day doing the trip stuff I should have done yesterday. That darn NWS weather forecast! We were supposed to have heavy rain today, but the probability went from 100% yesterday, to 80% last night, to 70% at midday... Do you see a pattern here? It was clear all morning, but it finally did start raining about 5:00 pm. It's an odd storm - a skinny band of rain running up the coast that is moving very slowly to the east. The hope is that it might stay for a while since it's moving so slowly. That will improve my estimation of the NWS forecasts to no end!

We're off to Oregon tomorrow for Thanksgiving with our kids and grandkids. Hah, there's rain in the forecast every day next week at our cabin on the North Umpqua River! That will be good for the mushrooms and all the rest that the rain brings. It won't keep me indoors. We'll be off the grid for a couple of weeks.

File.Name: gc05a_gc05-004.jpg
Notes:
31412522666 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a group of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from the stump of a fruit tree in the garden at home. The white powder that looks like a dusting of snow is the natural spore deposit of these white-spored mushrooms. These mushrooms weren't here when we left for Oregon two weeks ago, and they are already past their prime. I'm sure there are fresh ones out in the woods just waiting to be found! It's interesting that for gardeners, these mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "Oak Root Fungus". They seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2016)

We went to Oregon to have Thanksgiving with our kids and grandkids - but also to look for mushrooms! I did find some, but it wasn't the best of times, and we had no fresh fungi for our Thanksgiving dinner. The woods actually felt dry during our first week in Oregon, but we had some rain every day during the second week. We got home last night. I'll be posting trip photos for a few days.

File.Name: gc05b_gc01-019.jpg
Notes: 30641066063 I believe this is the Golden Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe flavescens aka Hygrophorus flavescens) mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. We have the same mushrooms at home under oaks, see this photo from two seasons ago. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2016)

File.Name: gc05c_gb30-018.jpg
Notes: 31411643586 This is a fantastic cantaloupe-size Lion's Mane Mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) growing from a scar on the mossy trunk of an Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana, Fagaceae) in the Oregon woods. We saw several specimens in the woods at Whistler's Bend County Park. We have them at home too on Coast Live Oak, but I haven't found one for many years. (See this old photo from 2006 at Fotolog. That one seems to have much shorter spines.) I got this photo in pretty heavy rain. (Whistler's Bend County Park, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2016)
CommonNames: Lion's Mane Mushroom; Oregon White Oak

File.Name: gc05d_gc01-003.jpg
Notes:
31447755585 These are lovely caps of the Apricot Jelly Mushroom (Guepinia helvelloides aka Phlogiotis helvelloides) in the Oregon woods, and what beautiful little mushrooms they are! These are "jelly fungi" in the family Auriculariaceae. They have the look of an ascomycete like a cup fungus, but they are a proper basidiomycete. They are said to be highly prized by some people, but more for color and texture than its non-existent taste. This is the first time in a few years that I've found them during our annual Thanksgiving visit. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2016)

Every year I forget my mushrooms and have to relearn them. And after four year of drought in California and all the recent changes in mushroom nomenclature, I *really* have to relearn them! Please consider all my mushroom names as guesses at best, and suggest alternatives if you know better.

File.Name: gc06a_gc03-026.jpg
Notes:
31473995455 Here is a mass of mostly Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) in the air at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. There are many thousands of birds, and a docent explained that all the birds haven't arrived yet! I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. We were lucky when a helicopter flew over causing the birds to rise. See my [Previous] two photos, which form a sequence. We have *another* maintenance power outage tomorrow from 8 am to 4 pm. At least that's what they say, but I've lost confidence in the power company's announcements. We've had warnings with no outages and outages with no warnings lately. Bah. I hope they finish their work early so I can post tomorrow, but maybe not.

File.Name: gc06b_gc03-018.jpg
Notes: 30665581003 Here is a mass of ducks and Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) taking wing at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. File.Name: gc06d_gc03-057.jpg
Notes: 30631581954 Here are a pair of odd "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arrhizus) mushrooms - fungi anyway - popping up through a gravel trail at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. Great name! I broke one of them open to show the intricate pattern of the developing spore-bearing "peridioles" inside, which contain dusty brown spores that cover the mushrooms and the ground around as they are slowly released. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone", a reference to the pea-shaped peridioles which are visible in my photo. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. It's a dull looking fungus from the outside, but you can see some hints of color inside. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, Glenn and Colusa Counties, California, 3 December 2016)

File.Name: gc07a_gc07-017.jpg
Notes:
30655356994 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I believe these are two photos of Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus) mushrooms in the woods at home, but they sure look different. The one on the left looks "normal" - with a dry brown cap, yellow gills that quickly turn blue when bruised, and a reddish stem that is tapered at the base. (There are other similar species that it might be.) The one on the right is unrecognizable as it has been engulfed by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that is particularly attracted to these mushrooms. The first stage of an infection is white mold, followed by a yellow crust. There is a final reddish-brown final stage which is visible in this photo. A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we sometimes find in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2016)

Our power came on early today after yet another maintenance power outage. Thank you! The weather was interesting today with clouds moving in from the west and even some thunder! There was a trace of rain over towards Lompoc, but nothing here. It's been an odd rain year so far. Lompoc has 3.41" of rain for 136% normal - the highest percent in the county. San Marcos Pass is usually the wettest place in the county, but we've just got 2.56" for 45% normal, the lowest percent in the county. This might be our sixth successive drought year. This is close to biblical!

File.Name: gc07b_gb29-008.jpg
Notes: 31496344435 This is a group of tiny Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) sporangia forming on a lichen on a mossy fallen log in the Oregon woods. You can see the clear mucous-like plasmodium still streaming up the stalks of the new sporangia, forming a translucent sheath. Yeah, it's suggestive. The sporangia are tiny, about 600 pixels tall in this 1:1 macro shot. That comes to about 2.4 mm, a bit less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) See my [Previous] two photos for more info about these odd creatures that seem to be part fungus and part animal. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2016)

File.Name: gc07c_gb29-012.jpg
Notes: 31125151290 This is a group of tiny Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) sporangia on a fallen log in the Oregon woods. You can see the clear mucous-like plasmodium on the top of the wood, the plasmodium still streaming up the stalks of the new sporangia in the middle, and spores being released from ripe sporangia at the bottom. See my [Previous] photo for more info about these odd creatures that seem to be part fungus and part animal. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2016)

File.Name: gc07d_gb29-025.jpg
Notes:
31380718961 This is the tree-like streaming plasmodium of a Plasmodial Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on a dead conifer log on the ground in the Oregon woods. There's a Douglas Fir needle on the bottom for scale. I got this photo (and the next two) across the new Tioga Bridge on the shady side of the North Umpqua River. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2016)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really more animal than fungus. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate plasmodium without cell membranes like the one in my photo. They end up as tiny mushroom-like sporangia like the ones in my [Next] two photos that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log to another.

File.Name: gc08a_gc08-009.jpg
Notes:
30680631214 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best - especially when they show a beautiful bug butt on a Thursday!

This is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. We seem to have both large and small ones, and they all have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. This is a medium-size one. Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but they are handsome flies - albeit tough and gnarly. This is also a nice butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2016)

Today was a nice day for photos with "cloudy bright" light. There's a 20% chance of rain tonight, and we have had a bit of drizzle to kick it off. *Sigh* This storm is coming in from the west again, and Lompoc is getting more rain than we are.

File.Name: gc08b_gb24-003.jpg
Notes:
30679512104 Here is a triptych of different ascomycete mushrooms from the Oregon woods last week. On the left is a Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom, distinctive with a fluted stem and black cap. The one on the right might be the Hooded False Morel (Gyromitra infula). I'm not sure about the one in the center. It might be the same as the one on the right, but it looks different to me, with a less convoluted cap - maybe Helvella compressa? I wouldn't eat any of them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2016)

Today's mushrooms are all interesting ascomycete rather than more typical basidiomycete mushrooms. They have a different way of producing spores, and a different "look" that makes them recognizable. There's more info on the web, e.g. here.

File.Name: gc08c_gb29-011.jpg
Notes: 31372982272 This is a neat group of tiny cup (or disk) fungi on a fallen conifer log in the Oregon woods. They are growing with a couple of bright orange agarics, maybe genus Xeromphalina? The cup fungi look like little gummi candies! I have no idea what kind they are, but they were a pleasure to find. Different kinds come in all different colors. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2016)

File.Name: gc08d_gc01-004.jpg
Notes: 30678576754 Hah, I actually looked down at the legs of my tripod when I first saw this mushroom, to check whether I'd lost a rubber foot! Nope, it's an odd ascomycete Black Cup Fungus, maybe genus Plectania or Pseudoplectania. It's about an inch across, the size of a Douglas Fir needle. It doesn't seem to have any sort of stalk, so maybe it's P. nigrella? That little white spot on the side is a Springtail (order Collembola) insect. Once I noticed this one mushroom, I found a few more in the woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2016)

File.Name: gc09a_gc09-034.jpg
Notes:
31541010085 This is a hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else?) in the woods today, the first one I've found this year! This was the only one I found today. Wikipedia reports that they are the second-largest species of terrestrial slug in the world, growing up to 10 inches long. The Oregon Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) look quite different, with greenish colors and a pronounced ridge on their back, I'll post a photo or two tomorrow. I really consider it a miracle every time I find my first one after our long dry summer. How do they manage to survive? (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2016)

We did get a bit of rain last night, about 2/10 inch, more than was expected. That was enough to bring out slugs and mushrooms! Hah, Lompoc got more than us again with 3/10! The good news is that we have a 30% chance of more rain tonight and tomorrow day and night. No Oregon photos today, because the rain brings Oregon home.

File.Name: gc09b_gc09-015.jpg
Notes: 31394293432 Here are tiny raindrops on last summer's seed head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. (See flowershere.) Hispid indeed! What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2016)

File.Name: gc09c_gc09-002.jpg
Notes: 31502480206 This is a wonderland of a tiny Mycena mushroom against a forest of toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungi growing from the woodland mulch of mostly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves above our retaining wall at home. (The mushroom is probably Mycena californiensis, see this photo and this one.) What a fantastic little micro-world/myco-world this is, but it's quite ephemeral and will soon disappear without more rain. Happy Fungi Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2016)

File.Name: gc09d_gc09-019.jpg
Notes:
31424670421 Thanks to Rui Andrade's comment below, I now believe this fly is genus Platypalpus of the family Hybotidae, order Diptera - the Hybotid Dance Flies. This is a new family for me, and it is indeed related to the proper Dance Flies in the family Empididae, and it is indeed related to the proper Dance Flies in the family Empididae which I first thought it was. The fly was hopping around out in a pile of fallen oak leaves near our still-dry creek, but fortunately it became quite still when I got my camera on it. The fly is about 750 pixels tall in these 1:1 macro shots. That comes to about 2.9 mm, a bit over 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These flies are predators, and I did see another one with even tinier prey. I like its spherical head! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2016)

Hah - pencil neck? More like a pencil lead neck!
CommonNames: Hybotid Dance Flies; Dance Flies; Dance Flies

File.Name: gc10a_gc10-024.jpg
Notes:
30752888693 I found this brown Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera hiding in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree this morning - with raindrops. Here it is stretching its wings. It looks like it's about to fly away, but it walked away along the branch. It was both cloudy and breezy this morning, so this isn't the best photo even though I boosted my ISO setting. Still it shows the true bug's anatomy. The forewings are thick and hardened in the basal part (the corium) and membranous towards the tip. The hindwings are membranous throughout. When the wings are folded around the triangular scutellum, they form the characteristic "X" shape on the back of the bug. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2016)

(Post to the Insects in Flight group? Well it's thinking about being in flight!)

We didn't get any more rain today despite the 30% chance, but we are in some thick clouds right now so we might get some more. There are rumors of real rain later in the week. I hope it happens. I'm enjoying having some Oregon weather at home with all that the rain brings!

File.Name: gc10b_gc10-019.jpg
Notes: 31524134506 Here are two shots of what I believe is a Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. The young gills look pallid, but they will turn chocolate-brown as the spores mature. These mushrooms have a wonderful sweet almond-like smell. It's said to be edible and delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland agaricus species - "once bitten, twice warned!" Maybe this year I'll finally try them if I keep finding more. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2016)

File.Name: gc10c_gc10-010.jpg
Notes: 31189344280 I believe these are some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushrooms coming up Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae)trees in the woods at home. The gills are quite pallid, though I expect them to have brown spores. (The brown stains on the stems might be spores caught in the fibrillose veil remnants.) These have no particular odor. It's a large genus, many of which are poisonous - but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2016)

File.Name: gc10d_gb26-007.jpg
Notes:
31445398801 Why you should never kiss a banana slug...

This is a large spotted green Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods consuming some kind of animal poo. I'm not sure what kind of animal the scat belongs to, but it's dog size and it sure seems to have apple peels in it. Of course this is out in the woods near our usually empty cabin where there are no dogs or people or apple trees. Anyway, if you compare my [Previous] photo of a California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus), you can see some differences - in color, size, shape, and in having a pronounced ridge on their back. They are both impressive large slugs, despite some questionable habits. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2016)

See this photo for another example of banana slug "coprophagia". Look it up if you don't know the word.
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slug

File.Name: gc11a_gc11-028.jpg

That's enough to keep the oak mulch wet and the mushrooms happy - the saprophytes if not the more interesting mycorrhizal mushrooms. There are still rumors that a strong storm bring rain to us on Thursday and Friday. We stand at just 43% of normal rainfall, so we could use it.

File.Name: gc12a_gc12-006.jpg
Notes:
31573129676 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushrooms in the woods... but I'm not even sure if these are fungi! I reckon they could also be sporangia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). They don't have gills, they have stems that look like they flowed into shape, there are few of them rather than many, and they are quite tough as they stood up to my handling. I don't know what they are, but I'll wager they are fungi. I put my pen in the photo for scale, which has a 0.7 mm ball which is a bit smaller than the size of these whatever-they-are. If you want to be exact, they are about 260 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about comes to about 1.0 mm. (See here for how I figured.) Please let me know if you recognize them. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2016)

There was no more rain today, but it was cloudy enough to keep the mushrooms coming. There are still rumors that a strong storm bring rain to us on Thursday and Friday. Hah, the NWS is giving us a 100% chance of rain on Thursday night - but that's a pretty brave forecast for three days away. We've been burned before. (Be still my heart!)

File.Name: gc12d_gc11-041.jpg
Notes: 31609233265 This is a group of dark-spored Inky Cap mushrooms coming up along our road and already folding up after our rain a few days ago. There are many different kinds of these "coprinoid mushrooms". They all used to be considered genus Coprinus, but now they have been split into Coprinus, Coprinopsis, Coprinellus, and Parasola. Mushrooms in this group "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime! The caps of this one are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. I don't know what they are, but they are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2016)

File.Name: gc13d_gc13-046.jpg
Notes: 31258259920 I believe this is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods today. These mushrooms are highly variable. I found this one in sandy soil under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). When I first found it, I thought it was an Agaricus! Well, it has reddish-brown colors like the Wine-Colored Agaricus in this photo, and they do sometimes have pallid gills. And this mushroom does appear to have a ring on the stem and no volval cup at all on the base. That's just how it is. I also find these mushrooms in springtime. This confirms what David Arora says (in Mushrooms Demystified) that they usually fruit twice - "a small flush after the first fall rains, and a larger crop in the late winter and spring." I didn't notice any reddish-brown stains on the stem from handling, but I replanted it soon after getting my photo, and might have just missed the stains. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2016)
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Mushrooms Demystified

File.Name: gc14a_gc14-022.jpg
Notes:
30811158734 These are the first new flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains this year. The long dangly catkins make for interesting (but odd) "string-of-pearl" flowers hanging like ornaments from the shrub. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows the male, pollen-bearing, staminate plants. It usually takes a while before the female, seed-bearing, pistillate flowers appear on different shrubs. I've been showing lots of mushrooms lately, so it's a pleasure to feature a flowering plant! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 December 2016)

The forecast stands with a 100% chance of heavy rain tomorrow and tomorrow night, but we've been burned before! We're under both a NWS Flash Flood Watch and a High Wind Watch starting tomorrow. It's great fun looking for the mushrooms that favor the rain, so I hope it happens this time.

File.Name: gc14c_gc13-008.jpg
Notes: 31536025331 Here are two shots of what I'm pretty sure is a brilliant Boletus flaviporus aka Aureoboletus flaviporus mushroom in a mossy place under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have viscid reddish-brown caps and a viscid stem in wet weather at least, but their standout feature is surely their intensely yellow pores that do not stain blue. They are said to be edible, but mediocre. They are striking mushrooms that are endemic to the west coast. This is the only specimen I've found so far this year - but the season is young, and more rain is coming! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2016)

Notes: 30810226864 Here are lots of tiny raindrops on a spider web in the cactus patch of our garden. File.Name: gc15a_gc15-004.jpg
Notes:
31299656030 This is a goodbye "butt shot" of a Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea), the kind that cannot roll up into a ball. Maybe it's genus Porcellio? I call these "sow bugs" - as distinct from the "pill bugs" in this photo. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. This one is crawling on across a fallen oak leaf. These are critters that the rain brings out, and this one has raindrops on its back to prove it. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2016)

It's been drizzly all morning, but the real rain is just starting to arrive. We're under both a NWS Flash Flood Watch and a High Wind Watch, and now they've added a Winter Weather Advisory because the snow level might drop to 2,000 feet tomorrow night. File.Name: gc15b_gc15-047.jpg
Notes:
30862041563 I found this forest of tiny sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on the inner bark surface of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. I guess they are genus Didymium, maybe Didymium iridis? I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm ball - a bit larger than the head of these sporangia. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2016)

Today was a good day for slime molds! They are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi. Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another. I think they might be my favorite creatures.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Didymium; Didymium iridis; Mycetozoa
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Didymium; Didymium iridis; Mycetozoa

File.Name: gc16a_gc16-010.jpg
Notes:
31654053076 Here are liverworts, lichens, and mosses growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The liverworts are probably the California Liverworts (Asterella californica); the lichens are genus Cladonia, maybe the Western Pixie-cup (Cladonia asahinae); and I have no idea what the mosses are. It's interesting that some of the liverworts have the bumpy tongue-like structures on their "leaves" that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain, and other liverworts have spherical buds that will become umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction. (I'll make a note.) I've always found both before, but never so close together. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2016)

We ended up with 1-3/4 inches of rain last night - less than expected, but not bad. Now comes the wind, with gusts from 40-50 mph so far, but it's expected to ease up tonight. Now we're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory and a Freeze Warning tonight. It's cold tonight, but I don't think there's any more precipitation in the works, so no snow. I really wasn't expecting to find any new mushrooms today, but I did. I reckon they were already there as buttons, and the rain brought them up.

File.Name: gc16b_gc16-001.jpg
Notes:
31544302402 I found this lovely Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom on the edge of our driveway today, growing in a two inch deep layer of oak mulch over the concrete footing of our retaining wall. This mushroom does not have deep roots! They are edible and tasty, though this was the only one I found today, and I didn't collect it for the table. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan like the view on the left. We also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar. I've confused them before, see this photo. One useful field trait is that real Blewits usually bring up a large ball of mycelium-connected material at the base. You can propagate the mushroom by moving this spawn. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2016)

(Is it Blewit or Blewitt? I've seen it both ways. The former seems to be winning the race.)

File.Name: gc16c_gc16-021.jpg
Notes: 31543960572 I found this very pallid Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today, the first one I've seen this year. It's growing in the deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It clearly has a bulbous base, which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. You can see the remains of an universal veil on the cap, and the cobwebby remains of the partial veil (the cortina) on the stem, stained rusty-brown from falling spores. I believe this is Cortinarius regalis. Cortinarius mushrooms are usually yellow or lavender, at least when fresh, but this one is different. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis

File.Name: gc16d_gc15-028.jpg
Notes:
31653057296 Fly maggots have turned this large cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) into a slime mass. I reckon the maggots are basically aquatic, and turn the mushrooms into a liquid environment. Hah, like people, they consume the caps of these mushrooms first and only go for the stems which I show here when there's no choice. In the past I've thought the maggots were the young of Drosophilid Flies, but now I believe that they are the maggots of Heleomyzidae flies in the genus Suillia, see this photo. I also have a closer shot of the maggots in this photo. When the maggots are done with the mushrooms, they disappear - presumably they burrow into the ground to pupate. One thing I don't understand is why I don't see more of the adult flies, since the maggots are so plentiful. Well they are flies, albeit in a different form than usual, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2016)

(No, it won't hurt my feelings at all if you don't want this in the FDF group. It's a disturbing photo!)

File.Name: gc17a_gc17-018.jpg
Notes:
31595330821 This is looking up at new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a sandstone outcrop. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds. These are creatures of our most shady woods. I found these ferns (and all my photos today) in shady Kinevan Canyon, a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2016)

It was cold last night - not only frosty, but icy, with 1/4 inch of ice in some containers on the upstairs deck. We'll have another cold one tonight, then it's expected to warm up a notch. The wind thankfully died down. I did find a couple of mushrooms today, but I expect it will take them another day or three to come up after our recent good rain.

File.Name: gc17b_gc17-033.jpg
Notes:
31563869212 Here is a pair of young Cortinarius mushrooms growing in the leaf mulch of a mixed woodland of Coast Live Oak, Tanbark Oak, Bay, Madrone, and Maple. The mushrooms have slimy yellow caps, stems with cobwebby cortina hairs stained rusty-brown from falling spores (see my [Previous] photo), and an abrupt basal bulb. All of these features places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. The species Cortinarius fulmineus comes to mind - but look how the mushrooms have surprising blue/violet colors - where the cap is broken on the left, and on the gills at the far right. Because of this, they might be species Cortinarius cedretorum. I should go back tomorrow and try slicing the mushroom in half, but I don't know if I'll make it. I love to photograph Cortinarius mushrooms while they are young and fresh like this, but they all begin to look the same as they age. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2016)

(Hah, I need to start carrying a squirt bottle to clean of my specimens after our recent rain!)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; cortina; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus; Cortinarius cedretorum
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; cortina; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus; Cortinarius cedretorum

File.Name: gc17c_gc17-037.jpg
Notes: 30900283743 This is a close shot of the Cortinarius mushroom in my [Next] photo, showing the "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young. They tear away from the cap as the mushroom matures, and hang from the stem - usually decorated with the rusty-red spores that fall from the gills. See this photo for another close shot of a different mushroom. I love to photograph Cortinarius mushrooms while they are young and fresh like this, but they all begin to look the same as they age. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2016)

File.Name: gc17d_gc17-026.jpg
Notes: 31672482536 This is a cluster of (I believe) Clitocybe mushrooms with funnel-shaped caps and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe the Common Funnel (Clitocybe gibba aka Infundibulicybe gibba). I found these growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees today. They are said to be edible and excellent, but hard to recognize and best avoided. Sounds like good advice. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2016)
ScientificName: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: gc18a_gc18-009.jpg
Notes:
30924302813 Here's something different - my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with this week's theme of Holiday Bokeh. My subject is ripe berries of native Toyon aka Christmas Berry or California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, with lots of lens flare caused by shooting too close to the sun with too large an aperture. The 7-sided lens flare matches the 7 petals on the diaphragm of my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. That lens is getting old, and it's probably time to give it a rest - but it's still almost the only lens I ever use. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2016)

It was chilly again this morning, with frost but no ice. It's warming up and already drying out. We're still only at 69% normal rainfall, and it's looking like year 6 of our drought. That's getting close to biblical with seven lean years...

File.Name: gc18b_gc18-014.jpg
Notes:
31733685395 Here are the first flowers of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've seen this year, but they've obviously already been flowering for a while. Last year I first found them (or spotted them) on January 18, and the year before on November 16. If you look close, you can see the real tubular flowers, not just the brilliant red bracts that first get your attention. This is a small woody sub-shrub, usually just a foot or less high. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2016)

This is the fourth plant I've found this year with flowers. Here are the other three. These dates are when I first noticed the flowers:
Nov. 1 - Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) (here)
Nov. 6 - Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) (here)
Dec. 14 - Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, Garryaceae) (here)
CommonNames: Woolly Paintbrush; Chaparral Currant; Bigberry Manzanita; Veatch Silk-Tassel
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae

File.Name: gc18c_gc17-013.jpg
Notes:
31733332505 This is a beautiful new sprout of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The fine flowers should appear in March, see this photo and this photo. You can see other seedlings as well. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2016)

Is it spring before winter has even started? Nah, this plant is right on schedule, it's the seasons that are a little screwy for us. Another sign of it is that our deciduous trees drop their leaves and go dormant at the same time that the hills (hopefully) turn green with new growth. That's how it works in much of the California Floristic Province. One good side effect is that there's always something to photograph in our woods.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: gc18d_gc18-031.jpg
Notes:
31585915052 These are tiny mushrooms of the Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus aka Gymnopus androsaceus aka Setulipes androsaceus) on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Look how the leaf is sprouting tiny stems that might become more mushrooms if the weather obliges with more rain. They're small, only a few millimeters across, and the stems are tough and wiry. This mushroom can dry out and shrivel to nothing - and then revive after the next rain. It's curious that our last storm brought us more rain, but it seems to be drying out quicker than our small rain a week ago. I reckon that's the work of the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2016)

Maybe these are the mushrooms growing from the oak leaf in this photo. I didn't recognize them with those short stems.
ScientificName: Marasmius androsaceus; Gymnopus androsaceus; Setulipes androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Marasmius androsaceus; Gymnopus androsaceus; Setulipes androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: gc19a_gc19-014.jpg
Notes:
30913653534 I found this tiny Fly (Diptera) on a stem in the woods this morning. That blue background is actually the frosty retaining wall on the shady side of our driveway! Unfortunately, the fly is missing its antenna on the left side. I guess this is a mosquito-like Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae - long antennae, humped thorax, long legs with bristle-like hairs, and tibia with apical spurs. Everything fits. I also just like the family name! Compare this photo of a genus Boletina at BugGuide. The maggots of these flies feed on different fleshy mushrooms and are apparently dependent on particular families of mushrooms. The fly is about 1050 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 4 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has pretty elaborate genitalia, discretely blurry in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2016)

I had errands in the valley today, and another in Santa Barbara as it was getting dark. Two photos will do today. I'll have more time tomorrow.

File.Name: gc19b_gc19-018.jpg
Notes: 31638835711 Here are a pair of Common Puffball mushrooms in the genus Lycoperdon. Maybe they the Pear-shaped Puffball (Lycoperdon pyriforme) - but if so, they are growing on lignen-rich humus instead of wood. Maybe they're something else? Puffballs are easy to recognize, but not so easy to identify. (Coral mushrooms are like that too.) The young caps have small scattered spines on top, which fall off as the cap matures to show a network of cracks. You can see the progression in this photo of probably the same species. These puffballs do have a stem-like base, visible on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2016)

File.Name: gc20a_gc20-019.jpg
Notes:
30933652114 This is a brilliant Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. If you look close, you can see that it has its proboscis out, taking water from the rock. It's puddling. I see these butterflies in the air on sunny days in winter, though I don't always get a photo. It was nice to get both this admiral and the tortoiseshell in my [Previous] photo on the same day. What's next? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2016)

The winter solstice happens tonight at 2:44 a.m. PST. It's the official first day of winter, the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice everyone!

(It's interesting that this solstice night is the shortest night of the year - BUT it's neither the latest sunrise or the earliest sunset. It's a stumper! Look here for my old answer from 1998.)

File.Name: gc20b_gc20-051.jpg
Notes: 31774733655 This is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly getting some sun on a sandstone outcrop. These butterflies are quite camera shy. In a moment, it was gone - this is a very tight crop. It was a nice find for almost winter! If you look close at the rock, you can see a layer on tiny crustose lichens. It's interesting how much of "the color of rocks" is actually the color of lichens! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2016)

File.Name: gc20c_gc20-036.jpg
Notes: 31737240106 I found this dragonfly (order (Odonata) hanging in a half-dead shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) by the creek today. I believe it's a female Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae, but I was confused at first because it looks blue (or "blue dun") rather than red, like I usually find them, compare this photo. Now I believe that's a gender difference. These blue-gray ladies are really well camouflaged despite the colors, and I was lucky to spot this one. These are our usual resident winter dragonflies which come out to hunt when the sun is shining. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2016)
ScientificName: Odonata; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae
associatedTaxa: Odonata; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae

File.Name: gc20d_gc20-032.jpg
Notes: 31774120855 This is a little Wolf Spider (Lycosidae), maybe species Varacosa gosiuta based on it's color pattern? It's missing a leg, but it seems to be getting around just fine. I found this one today on a sandstone bank by the (still dry) creek. These spiders are hunters without webs. I saw several of them today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2016)

File.Name: gc21a_gc21-037.jpg
Notes:
31648535532 I was lucky to find this different (I believe) Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae, Odonata today! Yes, the bluish one that I found yesterday was still in its place on the monkeyflower. This is a different one on a small tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek nearby that's showing some winter colors. This one looks more red that my first one, but I'm not sure if it's male or female. These are our usual resident winter dragonflies which come out to hunt when the sun is shining. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)

It was cloudy and warmer today, and we actually had a bit of rain from a tropical disturbance to the south. We also have a 90% chance of more rain on Friday night! This will be a colder storm.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: gc21b_gc21-048.jpg
Notes: 30954630224 This gorgeous little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) came out of its house. I planned to get a photo of its house in the upper-right for the Web Wednesday group, but the spider quickly ran out, looked around, and quickly ran back in. I was lucky to get a quick photo. These spiders build a proper orb web that's sort of visible here, with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. The spiders themselves are quite beautiful if you can find one out of its house, see this photo of (I think) the same spider. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)

File.Name: gc21c_gc21-024.jpg
Notes: 31647640152 Here are reproductive structures on (I believe) the small-leaved Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla, Marchantiophyta) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I'm not sure if these are the asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain, or real sporophytes for sexual reproduction. They do have the same bumpy texture as the gemmae of the California Liverworts (Asterella californica) in this photo, and I suspect that's what they are. They are quite small, only about about 180 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 0.7 mm. (See here for how I figured.) I didn't think about it at the time, but now I'm curious about those other green leafy plants in the photo. Are they mosses? (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)
ScientificName: Targionia hypophylla; Marchantiophyta; gemmae; sporophytes; Asterella californica
associatedTaxa: Targionia hypophylla; Marchantiophyta; gemmae; sporophytes; Asterella californica

File.Name: gc21d_gc21-004.jpg
Notes: 31794637645 *Sigh* The woods are drying out. I got the photo on the left a week ago, on December 13, and the photo of the right today. This was a forest of toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungi growing from the woodland mulch of mostly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves above our retaining wall at home. (The mushroom is probably Mycena californiensis, see this photo and the one following it.) Now the Fairy Hair is shriveled and the Mycenas have vanished. The good news is that we actually had a trace of rain today, and we have a good chance of more rain on Friday. The rain changes everything in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)
ScientificName: Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis; everything
