File.Name: gc22a_gc21-021.jpg
Notes:
30974262774 Here is one more close shot of what I believe is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. This is the same bluish one that I found two days ago, photo here. I got this photo yesterday in the same place, and moved in close. I believe it's a female. What gives with her eyes?? There's a distinct boundary between the top, where you can see the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes, and the bottom, where you can't. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)

Sorry, no beautiful bug butts today - even though it's Thursday. It's been pretty chilly this week, but also pretty dry. We had enough drizzle last night to get the outside seat cushions wet, but not enough to measure. That might change for the better tomorrow. We have an 80% chance of rain tomorrow and a 100% chance tomorrow night! We'll be under both a NWS Flash Flood Watch and a Winter Storm Watch starting tomorrow morning. There's nothing on the radar yet. I hope it happens, but we've been burned before...

File.Name: gc22b_gc22-019.jpg
Notes: 31698293411 I'm pretty sure these are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms that I found in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I scratched the gills to show the white latex "milk" which doesn't much change to yellow. (If you look close, you can see a few yellowish stains from prior bruises, so I guess it does stain yellow very slowly.) The caps are viscid and slightly zoned, and the caps and stems are "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots, though you can only see the spots on the cap in this photo. I didn't nibble this one, but I believe it has a spicy "acrid" taste that is not quite pleasant. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: gc22c_gc21-010.jpg
Notes: 31814515255 This small cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) appeared in our garden on the stump of a large Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). This particular shrub was already large when we built our house 35+ years ago, and it finally died just a few years ago. I guess they are relatively short-lived. I miss it, because it was always the first shrub to flower. Anyway, it's still supporting new life in its passing. The mushrooms appear dried out from the recent lack of weather, so I don't know if the flies will still be interested. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)

File.Name: gc22d_gc21-038.jpg
Notes: 31004417683 Here are new flower buds and a single ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. I've been waiting for the flowers to open, but not yet. (See this photo.) The trees are related to avocados, and it really shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". The fruit taste very pungent unless they are dead ripe - when they turn black. But the nuts inside are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right - see this video. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but not too much! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2016)

File.Name: gc23a_gc23-002.jpg
Notes:
31796451876 I seem to find these mushrooms every year. I first thought they were pink-spored Pluteus mushrooms. But Jacob Kalichman says they are the atypical Psathyrella uliginicola aka Coprinopsis uliginicola or C. lignicola (name proposed). I still think they look Pluteus-like, and isn't that a hint of a pink spore deposit on the stem?? These are growing from dead wood under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods this morning. I've been finding them here for a few years. Yep, I'm still confused. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2016)

Christmas came early this year with the gift of rain! We've had 1-3/4 inch so far, and it's still coming down. We're currently under multiple hazard alerts from the NWS - a Flood Advisory, a Flash Flood Watch, and a Winter Weather Advisory. The rain started after noon, so I had time for a walk before it came. It's been fairly warm today, but it's expected to drop down to freezing tonight, and colder tomorrow night. Real rain!
ScientificName: Pluteus; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola; C. lignicola; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Pluteus; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola; C. lignicola; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: gc23b_gc23-006.jpg
Notes: 31833274225 Maybe this is a strange Giant Earthstar (Astraeus pteridis) fungus - but it might also be a slightly deformed Hygroscopic Earthstar (A. hygrometricus). This one isn't giant, though it would fill my open hand. I found it in the woods along the road in our canyon, but oaks are never far away. These "mushrooms" are built like an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make the star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. I don't usually find the "puffball" stage, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. This spore case seems to have an apical pore, which suggests A. hygrometricus. The larger A. pteridis is said to rupture more irregularly. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2016)
ScientificName: Astraeus pteridis; A. hygrometricus; A. hygrometricus; A. pteridis
associatedTaxa: Astraeus pteridis; A. hygrometricus; A. hygrometricus; A. pteridis

File.Name: gc23c_gc23-007.jpg
Notes: 31795395746 I found these pretty red Russula mushrooms in the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning, before it started raining. The large one has been here for a couple of days, but the small one is new. When I went to pick it, I inadvertently broke the stem - but that's the "russula test" - if you have any doubts, see if the stem breaks clean like a piece of chalk. It does. This one looks red enough to be in the "Russula emetica" group, but other specimens in the woods have pale blotches. Maybe it's Russula silvicola? It seems too early to be finding this mushroom, but here it is. I nibbled a piece, and it tastes "acrid" - hot and spicy, like slightly "off" wasabi, not quite pleasant. It's probably poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2016)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola

File.Name: gc23d_gc20-027.jpg
Notes: 31459909300 I found this interesting fly (Diptera) trying to warm up on a sandstone outcrop in the sun a few days ago. I first thought it's family Muscidae, maybe a Tiger Fly in the genus Coenosia, but Thijs de Graaf says no. (See comment below.) I now believe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? What hairy eyes it has! HFDF! San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2016)
Locality: Tachinidae)? What hairy eyes it has! HFDF! San Marcos Pass

File.Name: gc24a_gc24-008.jpg
Notes:
31013789324 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. They seem to be having a good year, despite the ongoing drought. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2016)

I drove up into the higher mountains today looking for these berries and other holly-like plants, and I found them. But It was cold and windy today, downright blustery, with 45 mph gusts. We ended up with 2-1/4 inches of rain from our storm yesterday. That's enough to bring our total for the rain year up to 7.1 inches, 83% of normal. That's better than it was a week ago!

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all

File.Name: gc24b_gc24-014.jpg
Notes:
31706858822 Here are new flowers and foliage of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family flowering in the mountains, the first I've seen this year. This same shrub was also flowering very early last year, see this photo from October 25. It's not real holly, but the holly-like leaves are perfect for the holidays. Plants are individuals, and some just are early. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2016)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: gc24c_gc24-017.jpg
Notes:
31706642852 This is the fantastic foliage of an uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family, growing along East Camino Cielo road. I only find this plant in a few shady places where the sun never shines. I've never seen a berry, though I have seen flowers - see this photo. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - though very thin, like paper, not what its looks like. It will do for a Christmas holly photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2016)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: gc24d_gc22-010.jpg
Notes:
31853499065 This is a neat plant of Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a bare sandstone outcrop. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this! It looks like a little conifer, like a Christmas tree, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, a fern ally, because it produces spores rather than seeds. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places like this. I've heard that plants are (were?) collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2016)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: gc25a_gc25-036.jpg
Notes:
31038427994 This is a tiny mushroom growing from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm ball - about half the size of the mushroom cap. If you want to be exact, the mushroom is about 400 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about comes to about 1.6 mm. (See here for how I figured.) This is also my photo for the Macro Mondays group. The theme this week is "Redux 2016" aka "My Favorite Theme of the Year", and I reckon the theme "It's Alive" (Dec. 12) covers most of my photos, including this one. It might also fit the theme "Pencils, Pens, Erasers and/or Paperclips" (Oct. 10). I've only been participating in the group for two weeks, so I picked a theme I've used before. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2016)

(Hah - I just noticed that my photo is pretty suggestive too, but that was not one of the categories!)

Today was a nice enough day to be out with my camera, but it was cold - in the 20's last night, and only up to the low 40s today. It was icy this morning. The radar loop shows a cloud mass moving towards us, but the forecast says it will remain off the coast. Who's right?? It's cloudy right now, which may warm it up a bit - if it doesn't bring more rain!
Locality: Hah - I just noticed that my photo is pretty suggestive too

File.Name: gc25b_gc25-029.jpg
Notes:
31730903052 This is certainly an Agaricus mushroom in the woods under the oaks today, but what kind? I'm not sure, but I believe they are the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola). You can see a "cogwheel" pattern on its unopened partial veil, which protects and covers the young gills, and in time tears free to form a skirt-like ring on the stem. The mushroom caps stain yellow from handling, but the base of the stem does not stain yellow. It has a sweet almond smell. They look delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland species. Agaricus hondensis (here) and A. xanthodermus (here) are the main culprits. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2016)

I found these mushrooms barely showing in a "mushrump" today - the mound of leaves that forms when mushrooms push up through the oak leaves from underneath. I'm sure that's where the new mushrooms are, hiding in the oak mulch. I found a few today, and I'll probably find more tomorrow.
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus hondensis; A. xanthodermus
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus hondensis; A. xanthodermus

File.Name: gc25c_gc25-010.jpg
Notes: 31840518116 I believe these are Vulgar Milk Cap (Lactarius argillaceifolius var. megacarpus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The bleeding white latex when you scratch the gills gives it away as a Lactarius. It's a very solid mushroom, large and hefty. My book says it's "possibly poisonous" - but you probably wouldn't want to eat it anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2016)
CommonNames: Vulgar Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: gc25d_gc25-001.jpg
Notes:
31037151934 When good mushrooms go bad...

This is a group of three moldy Bolete mushrooms, probably the Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus). They are unrecognizable now as they have been engulfed by the parasitic "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that favors these mushrooms. I put them together so I could keep an eye on the infection as it develops. The lump on the right in my photo - it's hardy recognizable as a mushroom - is the same mushroom on the right in this photo from a few weeks ago. The first stage of an infection is a white mold, followed by a yellow crust. Then there is a final reddish-brown final stage which is visible in this photo on the right. Apparently, the white and yellow stages produce asexual spores, and only in the final reddish stage are spores sexually produced (as ascospores). A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we sometimes find in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2016)
CommonNames: Bolete; Oak-loving Bolete; Bolete Eater; Lobster Mushrooms
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus; ascospores; Hypomyces
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus; ascospores; Hypomyces

File.Name: gc26a_gc26-005.jpg
Notes:
31063501934 Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms aren't always blue. The mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan like these. They are edible and tasty, but be careful - we also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar. I've confused them before, see this photo. One useful field trait is that real Blewits usually bring up a large ball of mycelium-connected material at the base. You can propagate the mushrooms by moving this spawn. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2016)

I did get new photos today, but I had chores this afternoon, and didn't get home until well after dark. It's already late, so just two easy photos today. I'll have more tomorrow.

File.Name: gc26b_gc25-007.jpg
Notes: 31903144975 Here is a cluster of Bolete mushrooms growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They have reddish, minutely velvety caps, yellow and red stems, and rather large yellow pores which slowly stain blue. I guess that these are Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus, but there are a number of +/- similar species - B. chyrsenteron, B. subtomentosus, B. truncatus, B. bicolor, and no doubt more. All of these have new scientific names besides "Boletus". All of these are also said to be edible but not choice - but the critters like them! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; B. chyrsenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. truncatus; B. bicolor
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; B. chyrsenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. truncatus; B. bicolor

File.Name: gc27a_gc27-022.jpg
Notes:
31887854766 I was looking for slime molds by turning over fallen branches and bark in the woods today. I found them, see my [Previous] photo. I also found this odd wingless Bristletail insect in the order Microcoryphia aka Archaeognatha aka Thysanura (in part). What an odd beast it is! Like the Chimera of Greek mythology, it seems to be an amalgam of different parts - scales like a moth, antennae like a katydid, and mouthparts like a crane fly. The compound eyes are especially interesting, because they meet in the center of its face. It's not a slime mold, but it is an interesting find to see up close! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2016)

By the way, I never tear rotting logs apart. It's a way to find uncommon salamanders, but it's too destructive. I figure it is OK to pick up branches or pieces of bark, look for critters, and then put them back as you found them. It's important that we don't destroy the habitats we're trying to photograph.

Our last good rain seemed to dry out very fast, at least on the surface. That's what the wind does. The good news is that we have chances of more rain from Thursday through the weekend. As it stands, we're at 7.11 inches of rain since September 1. It's interesting that we on San Marcos Pass have the highest rainfall total in the county, and the lowest percent of normal, just 78%! (See this report, but it will be updated as the weather changes.)

File.Name: gc27b_gc27-009.jpg
Notes: 31551439760 These look like little tiny insect eggs, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). Something different comes from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. They are growing under a well-rotted fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe the photo show sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. In the past I've called them Diderma effusum, but I'm really not sure. I believe these orange ones are a ripe form of the white ones in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2016)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Diderma effusum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Diderma effusum

File.Name: gc27c_gc27-026.jpg
Notes: 31807872231 Here is a group of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak leaves in our canyon today, the first I've seen this year. (This may be a related species because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are?) The white dusting on that cap on the right is a natural spore print from the mushroom above. The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but brittle - like thin glass tubing. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! If you follow the long stem to the right, you can see a copious tuft of white mycelium at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2016)

File.Name: gc27d_gc27-014.jpg
Notes: 31084041574 These are tooth-sized white mushrooms growing on a mossy sandstone rock near home. They are also white-spored - you can see a dusting of white spores on the moss below some of the caps. My first thought when originally found them was genus Marasmius, but Ray Palmer suggests genus Calyptella in a comment to this photo. Both genera are marasmioid, in the Marasmiaceae family of mushrooms. They might be killing the moss. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2016)

File.Name: gc28a_gc28-009.jpg
Notes:
31140030063 This is looking at the cryptic underwings of a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from dry oak leaves in our canyon. There is moisture under the dry surface of the leaves, and you can see the butterfly's proboscis disappearing down between the leaves to find it. I have a recent photo of the brilliant upper wings here. I see these butterflies on sunny days in winter, though I don't always get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

The woods are starting to feel dry, though there is still moisture in the ground. We have an 80% chance of more rain tomorrow night, and there is a chance of another, colder storm this weekend. Bring it on!

File.Name: gc28b_gc28-025.jpg
Notes: 31108688184 I found this neat little wasp (Hymenoptera) sitting on top of the spore sack of the same Earthstar (Astraeus) fungus I show in this photo. My first thought is Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae). Hah, I've been wrong about these wasps before. It might also be a large Braconid Wasp (family Braconidae). Both of these families are in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea - they are all "Ichneumonid" at least. It's a great superposition. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)
CommonNames: wasp; Earthstar; Ichneumon Wasp; Braconid Wasp

File.Name: gc28c_gc28-001.jpg
Notes: 31575056050 This is first new flower I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. Yeah, it's not quite open, but it probably will be tomorrow, and I think I will record it as a "first flower" because it looks fully formed. I found it in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, so the white balance is what it is. Last year, I didn't find the first flowers until February 12, but the year before I found them on January 12. I'm sure that only depends on when we get our first real rains. I figure this is first proper wildflower (not a shrub) that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

File.Name: gc28d_gc27-023.jpg
Notes: 31831585071 I was looking for critters and slime molds under fallen oak branches and bark in the woods yesterday, in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I found them, see my [Previous] two photos, here and

File.Name: gc29a_gc29-001.jpg
Notes:
31130641754 This is looking close at the first new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, related to sassafras and avocado. This tree is growing in a shady place where the sun never shines at this time of year, and I did my best for color and contrast. The flowers are surprisingly intricate. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, then six orange glands at the base, surrounded by an outer-circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each, with a base of (usually) six petals/sepals. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

The woods are starting to dry out, but there's a good chance of rain tonight and again on Saturday - just in time!

File.Name: gc29b_gc29-011.jpg
Notes: 31933511936 Here are red berries on a glandular shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) - with a couple of green critters, a caterpillar (Lepidoptera) and the small nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera). Winter just began, and there are already early signs of spring! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)
ScientificName: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Lepidoptera; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Lepidoptera; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: gc29c_gc28-020.jpg
Notes:
31160743303 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a fallen oak leaf. Maybe it's a Blue Blowfly (Calliphora vicina)? The blue colors on its butt are rather attractive. This isn't really a "butt shot" - it's more a shot of a fly showing its butt, along with the rest of it - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

(Yeah, I've already got more flies for Fly Day Friday!)

File.Name: gc29d_gc27-017.jpg
Notes: 31595956950 This is a small ear-like mushroom, with my pen for scale. It was another find when I was looking under fallen oak branches and bits of bark in the woods a couple of days ago, in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I believe its shape is considered "pleurotoid" - like an Oyster Mushroom, Pleurotus ostreatus, only mush smaller. This one is a bit less than 1/4 inch across. I'm not sure what kind of mushroom it is, but one possibility is the genus Hohenbuehelia. It appears to have abundant cystidia - relatively large non-fertile cells on the gill edges that give them a rough texture. Hah, it also has a couple of tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects. Can you find them? I'm curious about all those tiny white structures like paper crowns that are scattered over the wood. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2016)

File.Name: gc30a_gc30-006.jpg
Notes:
31618712690 This is an odd one! It shows a tiny Cricket (Tettigoniidea, Orthoptera) on the flower scar of a cactus fruit in our garden. Yep, the cricket really is tiny, about 1/4 inch long. I'm not sure why it caught my eye, but it did. (Well, I always check the tops of the cactus fruit, see this photo of a Tree Cricket.) It's a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus. Those little glochid hair-like spines are particularly nasty if you brush against them, much worse than the large cactus spines! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2016)

We did finally get some rain today, but less than expected - about 2/10 inch so far. We have another storm coming tomorrow that is said to be a colder and stronger system. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: gc30b_gc30-003.jpg
Notes: 31955171736 This is a fully-open new flower of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I showed the almost-open buds couple of days ago in this photo. It's a beautiful flower, more elegant than our usual yellow wild mustards (that are non-native). I found it in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, so the white balance is what it is. Last year, I didn't find the first flowers until February 12, but the year before I found them on January 12. I'm sure that only depends on when we get our first real rains. I figure this is first proper wildflower (not a shrub) that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2016)

File.Name: gc30c_gc28-021.jpg
Notes: 31182784823 This fly (Diptera) looks similar to the fly in my [Previous] photo, so it might be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). There are some differences. This fly does not have hairy eyes! It's smaller, with a wider space between its eyes, and a pointier abdomen that is also hairier. This one is also perched for a moment on a narrow leaf of a different native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. Compare the fly in my [Previous] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

File.Name: gc30d_gc28-013.jpg
Notes: 31182538173 This might be the same kind of interesting fly (Diptera) that I posted a week ago with this photo. If it is the same kind, then - thanks to Thijs de Graaf - I believe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), subfamily Exoristinae. It has the same hairy eyes! This one is perched for a moment on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Compare the fly in my [Next] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2016)

File.Name: gc31a_gc31-013.jpg
Notes:
31175214684 This is a new leaf of an uncommon Larkspur in the Ranunculaceae plant family, with raindrops. I believe this is a leaf of Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, growing on a rocky north-facing slope in our mountains. This leaf is full of promise for the new year. It will do for a New Year's Eve photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2016)

We had light rain for much of today - only about 0.35 inch, but a good soaker. Yeah, I had good photos from yesterday, but I did my duty and took a rain walk today with my camera and umbrella. I believe the real new year started more than a week ago on the solstice, and I've always been curious why the new year begins on January 1 in our calendar. Christmas too. Happy New Year!

File.Name: gc31b_gc31-010.jpg
Notes: 31641018890 Raindrops on lupine leaves ... almost a photo clichÈ, but still a pleasure. This leaf belongs to native Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family. I got this photo in light rain today, with some breeze. Both hard rain and wind knock the drops from the leaves, so you have to time your photo just right. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2016)

File.Name: gc31c_gc31-014.jpg
Notes: 31866767952 Here are tiny mushrooms of the Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus aka Gymnopus androsaceus aka Setulipes androsaceus) growing from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, with raindrops. I'm pretty sure I have another shot of the same group of mushrooms in this photo from a couple of weeks ago. Sure, the mushrooms have dried out since then, but these mushroom have the ability to shrivel up to nothing and then revive when it rains again. The genus name "Marasmius" comes from a Greek word marasmos (µa?asµ??) meaning "dry out" or "decay". The related word "marcescent" is used to describe a mushroom which can dry out, but later revive and continue to disperse spores. It's the opposite of "putrescent" - which most mushrooms are. That word has another use to refer to the retention of dead leaves by deciduous trees. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2016)

File.Name: gc31d_gc31-007.jpg
Notes:
31204872103 This is a Bolete mushroom made unrecognizable now as it has been transformed by the parasitic "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that favors these mushrooms. It is probably the Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus), but who can tell? This is the same mushroom on the right in this photo from a week ago, and again in this photo from a few weeks ago. The first stage of an infection is a white mold, followed by a yellow crust. Then there is a final reddish-orange final stage which is visible in this photo. Apparently, the white and yellow stages produce asexual spores, and only in the final reddish stage are spores sexually produced (as ascospores). A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we sometimes find in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2016)

I got this photo in the rain today, using an umbrella to protect my camera. The rain seems to bring out the rich color.
CommonNames: Bolete; Bolete Eater; Oak-loving Bolete; Lobster Mushrooms
ScientificName: Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; ascospores; Hypomyces

File.Name: f101a_f101-008.jpg
Notes:
15550480583 This is a closer shot of the astounding Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. See my [Previous] photo for the whole insect. It looks a lot like Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. I found this one in a hollow in a sandstone outcrop that was full of old spider webs, and BugGuide does say that "some steal prey from spider webs". (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2015)

Its body is about an inch long, but the legs and antennae are remarkably thin. Look how it holds its front legs out front, like an aquatic bug. They look a lot like Walkingstick (Phasmida) insects, but there are differences. See this photo and the ones [Previous] to that.

Today was a few degrees warmer than yesterday, but still quite cold. There is ice and soil in the garden that hasn't thawed for a few days. This isn't helping my cold/flu. *Blah*

It looks a lot like Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. It's body is about an inch long, but the legs and antennae are remarkably thin. Look how it holds its front legs out front. They look a lot like Walkingstick (Phasmida) insects, but there are differences. See this photo and the ones [Previous] to that.

File.Name: f101c_f101-019.jpg
Notes: 15984097177 These are odd Puffball (Lycoperdon?) mushrooms growing from a mossy sandstone outcrop in our canyon. You can see a hint of a sterile base on the mushroom to the right. Now what would nourish a mushroom on sandstone rock? (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2015)

File.Name: f101d_f101-001.jpg
Notes:
15983594659 At the bottom are the first open flowers I've seen this year of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family. This tree is on the edge of our garden, so it gets a bit of summer water. (The white spots on the leaves are the result of our hard well water.) The flowers seem unusually pale, but may that's the white balance. Other trees in our canyon have few buds this year because of our ongoing drought, but there are some trees in the mountains that do have buds. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2015)

This isn't the best photo, but the tree is growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. I had to use a bit of flash, which I try to avoid. Still, it captures the event!

File.Name: f101e_ec31-036.jpg
Notes: 16143750256 This is a little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a flowering shrub of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. I seems an odd place to find a hummer, but maybe she's watching over a manzanita shrub nearby? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)

File.Name: f101f_ec28-003.jpg
Notes: 16167593461 This is a "butt shot" of (I believe) a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) catching some sun on a fallen oak leaf on a cold morning. It has reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt. I have another photo of the same fly here. HBBBT and HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: f102a_f102-010.jpg
Notes:
15560418343 This is lovely new foliage of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. It's growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago as a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2015)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I made my usual stop by Lake Cachuma. It was a few degrees warmer today, but still chilly. I still feel crummy from this darn cold/flu that goes on and on.

File.Name: f102b_f102-014.jpg
Notes: 15992752840 These are new sprouts of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, growing from the dead remains of last year's growth. It's growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago as a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers, but I'd like to see one of those perennial roots too! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2015)

File.Name: f102c_f102-006.jpg
Notes: 16154022126 I don't know what these interesting Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) are growing on the bare ground between the chaparral shrubs near Lake Cachuma. I didn't have my pen, so I put my truck key in the photo for scale. The bumps on the key are about 1/16 inch deep, so this liverwort is very small. It has a pleasant silky appearance. I have no idea what species they are. They are quite different from our "mountain liverworts". I suspect a careless step will destroy the colony. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2015)

File.Name: f102d_f102-002.jpg
Notes: 16178986562 This is delicate Lace Lichen - aka California Spanish Moss and Treebeard Lichen, my namesake! - Ramalina menziesii, hanging from a native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This lichen usually favors deciduous trees like this one, but the tree curiously still has green leaves. Maybe the relatively warm weather last autumn confused it, in which case the cold weather we're having now will do the trick. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2015)
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; California Spanish Moss; Treebeard Lichen; Blue Oak

File.Name: f102e_f102-020.jpg
Notes: 15992148788 This is looking close at a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera growing on the ground in the woods. Maybe it's the Many-fruited Pelt Lichen (Peltigera polydactylon)? It has those odd root-like structures on its lower surface. Maybe they function as a holdfast? I have another photo of the same kind of lichen here. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2015)

File.Name: f102f_f102-021.jpg
Notes:
15557048684 This is another photo of a Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushroom - with a fungus-blackened oak apple gall that I put next next to it. Naturalist humor? Well, if the right person finds them, they will get a kick out of the combination! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2015)

(BTW, I'm not sure if that gall is the work of a California Gall Wasp on scrub oak or a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp on live oak. I found it on the ground, and both oaks grow in this place which is an ecotone or transition area between woodland and chaparral. It's one of those.)
CommonNames: Dark Puffball; oak apple gall; California Gall Wasp; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp

File.Name: f103a_f103-027.jpg
Notes:
16189873012 Signs of a struggle...

Here are brilliant orange wing feathers and a bit of fluff from a Northern Flicker (Colaptes auratus) - which I still think of as a Red-Shafted Flicker. Probably a hawk got it. The feathers are gorgeous. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2015)

Today was a few degrees warmer than yesterday. I still feel crummy from this darn cold/flu that goes on and on. I probably sound worse, but I'm actually feeling better today, hacking up the bad stuff. I have hopes for tomorrow.

File.Name: f103b_f103-030.jpg
Notes:
16164685876 These are Russula mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe they are Russula cremoricolor or Russula albidula, but maybe not. That little bit of green on the right is a stem of California Bedstraw (Galium californicum, Rubiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2015)

The weather has been mostly dry since our December deluge, but there is still moisture under the oak leaves, and that's where the mushrooms are hiding. The slightest bump in the leaves might be a mushroom... or a gopher!

File.Name: f103c_f102-024.jpg
Notes: 16003063480 I found this handsome Russula mushroom in the woods yesterday growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe its Russula cremoricolor or Russula albidula? Whatever it is, this is a voluptuous pose! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2015)

File.Name: f103d_f102-033.jpg
Notes: 16004028239 Here are a pair of different Lepiota mushrooms in our woods that are clearly different. I'm not sure about the one on the left, but I believe the one on the right is the Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota magnispora) - the one that David Arora calls Lepiota clypeolaria var. nabiscodisca. What all Lepiotas have in common is that the cap has a darker center that breaks into scales as it expands. I got the photo on the left today and the one on the right last week, in almost the same place. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014 and 3 January 2015)
ScientificName: Lepiota; Lepiota magnispora; Lepiota clypeolaria; nabiscodisca

File.Name: f103e_f103-005.jpg
Notes:
16188004451 By chance I happened to notice this tiny gall on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. I'm not sure whose gall it is, but I'm sure it's the gall of a Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) because of that perfect exit hole. I just went through my Gall Album at Flickr, and I don't believe I've ever seen this gall before. I got this photo above our 4 foot high retaining wall which makes a perfect eye-level window into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2015)

This gall is tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The gall is about 570 pixels long. That makes it about 2.3 mm long, less than 1/10 inch. See this photo for how I figured.

File.Name: f103f_f103-022.jpg
Notes: 16002477520 Here are a pair of fallen galls of Live Oak Apple Gall Wasps (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on the ground. They look like fruit, and in a way they are - made by the oaks, but containing the fruit of the wasps. You can see small exit holes in these, and a larger hole on the right where some critter tried to get in. The galls are often green when they fall, then turn yellow, then brown. It doesn't seem to hurt them being on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2015)

File.Name: f104a_f104-034.jpg
Notes:
16013934118 Here is a small ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) working the deep blue nectar disk of a new flower of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for a wider shot of the flowers. I'm not sure what kind of ant this is, but it sure has neat antennae. Maybe it's Formica moki? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

Today was another few degrees warmer than yesterday. I still feel crummy from this darn cold/flu that goes on and on. I probably sound worse, but I'm actually feeling better today, still hacking up the bad stuff. At least I got out for a real walk today!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Formica moki
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Formica moki

File.Name: f104b_f104-031.jpg
Notes: 16013991860 This is looking closer at a new flower cluster of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Note the ant in the upper-left and see my [Next] photo. I first noticed these new flowers a few days ago, on December 31. The individual flowers are remarkably intricate, with a deep blue nectar disk in addition to the usual floral parts. In a good year they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

File.Name: f104c_f104-016.jpg
Notes: 15578723324 Here are new female pistillate flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae. The male shrubs are already going and the females lag behind, but look how they are already demurely extending their pistils! In time, the flowers will open into proper catkins. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. Last year, the male plants never opened and the females did just fine ripening seeds without them. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

File.Name: f104d_f104-024.jpg
Notes:
16175195186 These are new male staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family in full flower. The "string of pearls" flower catkins are strange and beautiful, though they get tangled up in the wind. You can clearly see the densely hairy lower surface of the leaves. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, see my [Next] photo for the other half. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

The flower catkins might be wind-pollinated, but they are so striking that one wonders. I did spot a couple of Syrphid Flower Flies (Syrphidae, Diptera) on the flowers today. I got a photo, but it's not the best. Maybe I'll post it tomorrow.
ScientificName: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; dioecious; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; dioecious; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: f104e_f104-022.jpg
Notes:
15581044673 This is a whole flowering shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's growing on the ridge above Paradise Canyon, with Loma Alta in the background looking green. These are our first native shrubs to flower, in September or October, see this photo, but they don't all flower at the same time like some other natives. New plants keep coming into flower from autumn until spring, September until March. That's good for the pollinators and photographers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

See this photo for a closer shot of the pretty flowers.

File.Name: f104f_f104-017.jpg
Notes: 16014951287 This is new growth on a native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, aka Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea in the Adoxaceae. This shrub is deciduous and just starting to get spring leaves - now that winter is really here! It seems too early. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2015)

File.Name: f105a_f103-024.jpg
Notes:
15588703364 Here are two photos of Crust Mushrooms (Stereaceae or kin) on a fallen branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I got the photo on the left on December 16, after our big rains. The photo on the right is the same oak branch on January 3. Damn, the woods are drying out. I hope this won't be a repeat of 2012 which started wet, but then the rains stopped in December and never started again. I'm having drought concerns again. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2015)

It was quite warm today, into the upper 60s even on our winter-shady porch. Lovely, but not right.

File.Name: f105b_f105-006.jpg
Notes:
16023447198 These are gray lichens on the trunk of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon, maybe Platismatia glauca. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2015)

I got email from a neighbor this morning asking (innocently enough): "would you happen to know the name of the predominant lichen species on the live oak trees on our property?" Their son is doing a science fair project that involves these lichens. I've always avoided lichens because there are so many different kinds and I don't really understand their taxonomy, but that email got me started looking at lichens today and doing some research. If I have the name wrong, please let me know!
Locality: innocently enough): "would you happen to know the name of the predominant lichen species on the live oak trees on our property?"  Their son is doing a science fair project that involves these lichens.  I've always avoided lichens because there are so many different kinds and I don't really understand their taxonomy

File.Name: f105c_f105-001.jpg
Notes:
15588371504 Here are a group of lichens growing on a fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I moved the twig to above our retaining wall to make it easier to photograph and protect my aching back. It's actually several different lichens! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2015)

I got email from a neighbor this morning asking (innocently enough): "would you happen to know the name of the predominant lichen species on the live oak trees on our property?" Hah, I've always avoided lichens because there are so many different kinds and I don't really understand their taxonomy. That email got me started looking at lichens today.

I guess that the feathery ones on top of the twig are some kind of genus Usnea, the foliose one on the twig is Platismatia glauca, and the one hanging down is Evernia prunastri. That's not to mention the tiny yellow and gray ones! I'm just guessing, so please correct me if I'm wrong.
Locality: innocently enough): "would you happen to know the name of the predominant lichen species on the live oak trees on our property?"  Hah
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Usnea; Platismatia glauca; Evernia prunastri
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Usnea; Platismatia glauca; Evernia prunastri

File.Name: f105d_f105-019.jpg
Notes:
16208758791 Even the rocks are alive with lichens! This appears to be a small orange foliose lichen on a large sandstone rock in the canyon. It's surrounded by tiny crustose lichens with apothecia (spore-bearing structures) about the same size as the sand grains in the rock. And those light colored "water stains" on the rock are actually more gray foliose lichens. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2015)

Lichens are pretty amazing, and they last longer than mushrooms! This may be the year that I finally try to understand them.

File.Name: f105e_f105-021.jpg
Notes:
15590565043 This is a little white crustose lichen on a sandstone rock, surrounded by small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants. This white lichen always reminds me of a bird turd against that green background. I believe the dark cups on the lichen are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2015)

The liverwort is interesting, as it is much smaller and more weather sensitive than our more common California Liverwort (Asterella californica). I'm not sure what it is, but I've had Targiona hypophylla suggested for a name. They dry out very quickly, see this photo.
Locality: Asterella californica). I'm not sure what it is
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; apothecia; Asterella californica; Targiona hypophylla
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; apothecia; Asterella californica; Targiona hypophylla

File.Name: f105f_f103-002.jpg
Notes: 16209374562 This is a different little Cladonia lichen in the woods growing with mosses. It has unbranched and pointed stalks. A colony of these is growing on a bit of upturned soil. It's a "fruticose" lichen but it comes from a "foliose" base, which you can see close to the ground. Maybe it's Cladonia ochrochlora? (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2015)

File.Name: f106a_f106-018.jpg
Notes:
16219123835 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched on a sandstone rock in the canyon. I'm pretty sure it's Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. It has brilliant red "underwings" that makes them easy to spot in the air, but they almost disappear on the ground with wings folded. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. This moth is "puddling" - taking moisture - from the still moist sandstone. Water from the rock! This isn't the best photo, but it's not bad for my first encounter with these moths this year! See this photo for a clearer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

Winter has hardly started, and it already feels like spring! I found insects and spiders today. I think I hear frogs in the canyon, but I'm not sure. In between posting photos tonight, I've been sitting outside on the porch watching bats pick off the moths on the sliding glass doors. But we seriously need rain to keep it all going!

File.Name: f106b_f106-035.jpg
Notes: 16219032015 The white specks are tiny Midge flies, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera. They are dancing in the sunbeams high in the oak trees in a mating swarm. They are nearly invisible unless the sun is at just the right angle - close but not too close. The flies form strange columns that slowly move up and down. I may have a photo of an individual midge in this photo. I hope they come back tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

File.Name: f106c_f106-030.jpg
Notes: 16033109217 This is another photo of the tiny spider that I found today on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon. You can use the spine on the leaf for scale, about 1/16 inch. The spider looks superficially like a Lynx Spider (Oxyopidae), but it lacks a carapace and the eyes are wrong for that family. (See the useful page on Spider Eye Arrangements at BugGuide.) I believe this is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe Diaea livens? I actually first noticed it on the back of my hand. I transfered it to the oak leaf, and it seemed happy there. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Lynx Spider; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Oxyopidae; Thomisidae; Diaea livens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Oxyopidae; Thomisidae; Diaea livens

File.Name: f106d_f106-026.jpg
Notes:
16032962717 This is a tiny spider that I found on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon today. You can use the spine on the leaf for scale, about 1/16 inch. The spider looks superficially like a Lynx Spider (Oxyopidae), but it lacks a carapace and the eyes are wrong. I believe it's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe Diaea livens? See my [Next] photo. What an interesting abdomen it has, with a mask! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

I first noticed it on the back of my hand. I transfered it to the oak leaf, and it seemed happy there.

File.Name: f106e_f106-008.jpg
Notes: 16032884867 This is a small back Sawfly wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) on a sandstone rock in the canyon. It doesn't have a wasp-waist like other typical wasps. This one has multi-segmented antennae that arise between the eyes. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

File.Name: f106f_f106-014.jpg
Notes: 16218658025 This is a rather large Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) catching some sun on a sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon this morning. The red eyes are typical, as is a reddish spot on its butt that is not visible here, but see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

File.Name: f107a_f107-012.jpg
Notes:
16226479752 This is a brilliant though tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly getting moisture from the oak leaves in the canyon today. They are quite camera shy. In a moment, it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

I believe this butterfly is a migrant that overwinters here. I don't know that it actually breeds here, but we have lots of their favorite Ceanothus host plants in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They are one of a trio of butterflies that I mostly find in mid-winter, also including the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) and the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa).

It's been quite warm, a record-setting 82 degrees for Santa Barbara yesterday. This is bringing out the butterflies and other insects, but it's not right for early January. It's forecast to begin cooling down tomorrow.
CommonNames: California Tortoiseshell; Red Admiral; Mourning Cloak
ScientificName: Nymphalis californica; Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae; Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis californica; Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae; Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa

File.Name: f107b_f107-005.jpg
Notes:
16040992559 This is a remarkable little furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched oh so delicately on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. I saw them for the first time yesterday, and got this photo today on the ground. I first noticed these flies last year on Christmas Day. I think of them as winter flies. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

This one looks poised for take-off, and a moment later it was gone. I see these larger ones and also smaller ones that I guess are female and male respectively. I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their meeting place.

File.Name: f107c_f107-022.jpg
Notes:
16040845049 I found this fairly large caterpillar-like grub on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. My first thought was a caterpillar of course, then my second thought was that it might be the larvae of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) - maybe this one.

According to Alice, I should have stuck with my first thought! Alice thinks it may be the caterpillar of a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly. That makes sense, as they are common butterflies in our woods. See this photo of the adult. I suppose it could also be a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly, photo here. Both are said to feed on the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cimbicid Sawfly; Mournful Duskywing; Propertius Duskywing
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cimbicidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Erynnis tristis; Erynnis propertius
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cimbicidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Erynnis tristis; Erynnis propertius

File.Name: f107d_f107-017.jpg
Notes: 16040751049 This is a "butt shot" of a small fat spider snuggled into new leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I can't see its front end, but I guess from its legs that it's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

File.Name: f107e_f107-008.jpg
Notes:
16040669329 We've had lizard weather lately!
*Sigh* It's been quite warm, a record-setting 82 degrees for Santa Barbara yesterday.

This is looking down on a little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) looking for prey on the sandstone outcrop in our canyon that still holds some moisture from December rains. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

You can see the "third eye" that these lizards have. It's that small light-brown disk in the center of my photo, towards the apex of the triangle behind the lizard's eyes. It's also called a "parietal eye" or "pineal eye". It appears to have a lens. As I understand, this third eye distinguishes light and dark, and has something to do with controlling body temperature and daily rhythms. It's associated with the pineal gland, which we have too, buried deep in our heads. It's so interesting that Descartes picked the pineal gland (our third eye, somewhat buried) as the seat of the soul.

File.Name: f107f_f106-039.jpg
Notes:
16039277060 This is a mating swarm of tiny Midge flies, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera. I slowed the photo down all I could to ISO 100 and f/22, so this photo shows the light-trails of the flies during that 1/15 second exposure. See my photo yesterday for more information. Hah, they look like little chromosomes, or maybe Bacillus bacteria! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

I was hoping for more action today, but there was less. Maybe the light was wrong or the mated flies are no longer interested?

File.Name: f108a_f108-009.jpg
Notes:
15612140044 Here is another photo of a remarkable little furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. This is just how I found it, standing almost vertically on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)

I got this photo on a pile of oak leaves below a sandstone outcrop, like many of my recent photos. The leaves look dry, but there is moisture under the surface, see my [Previous] photo. This place gets morning sun. It's my favorite place to find insects at this time of year.

Today started sunny and warm, but clouds moved in this afternoon. There's a 20% chance of rain this weekend. We need it!

File.Name: f108b_f108-006.jpg
Notes:
16046922808 This is a "butt shot" of "just a fly" taking moisture from the oak leaves in our canyon. Maybe it's a Cluster Fly (Pollenia, Calliphoridae, Diptera)? The oak leaves seem dry, but when you disturb them (by stepping on them), there is lots of moisture underneath. Unfortunately, that's where our mushrooms are - hiding under the oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)

This has been a good week for "butt shots". I got more, but enough is enough! HBBBT!

File.Name: f108c_f104-037.jpg
Notes: 16234359725 This is a "butt shot" - an "abdomen shot" anyway - of a small ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) working the deep blue nectar disk of a new flower of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure the ant is Formica moki in the subfamily Formicinae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)

File.Name: f108d_f104-010.jpg
Notes:
16234282715 Going for a ride?

This is another new caterpillar of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly inside a little boat-like seedpod of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The caterpillar is small, still less than 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f108e_f108-013.jpg
Notes:
15611753504 This is an old "rolled oak leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can look into it, and it sure looks like abnormal leaf growth inside. I'm pretty sure it's a gall. See this photo of the unopened galls. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)

This leaf is rolled onto the topside. We also have "leaf roller galls" that roll under the leaves, compare this photo.

File.Name: f108f_f107-027.jpg
Notes:
16047995639 These are galled flowers or berries of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The normal berries turned blue-black and were gobbled up by birds months ago. These galled berries are still on the shrubs long after their season. They all have that odd "crook in the neck". (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

Ron Russo doesn't describe any of these galls in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." This might be the one.

File.Name: f109a_f109-017.jpg
Notes:
16240169381 These are the surprisingly intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. These are the first open flowers I've seen this year away from the garden. There was a slight breeze today, and everything was in motion. You'll have to wait for a closer shot of these interesting flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2015)

It was cloudy this morning at home, so I drove up into the higher mountains to see what I could find. We have a chance of a bit of rain this weekend. We need it.

File.Name: f109b_f109-005.jpg
Notes: 16241102312 Here are pretty new and old flowers of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The foliage is lovely. The flowers look a lot like Ribes amarum but that plant is glandular, compare this photo. I was really looking for pollinators on the flowers, but they wouldn't pose for me. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2015)

File.Name: f109c_f109-009.jpg
Notes: 16054326328 Here are more new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These are our first native shrubs to flower, in September or October, see this photo, but they don't all flower at the same time like some other natives. New plants keep coming into flower from autumn until spring, September until March. These are growing 20 feet from the Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2015)

File.Name: f109d_f109-026.jpg
Notes: 15619412284 These are rosy flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Most of the flowers are white, but this plant is different. This is the stuff that cultivars are made of. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2015)

File.Name: f109e_f108-003.jpg
Notes: 16239940971 This is a small shrublet of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) growing in a crack in the still-moist sandstone outcrop by the creek in our canyon, with its shadow. It will be interesting to see how large it can get in this precarious place. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2015)

File.Name: f109f_f107-016.jpg
Notes: 16055865517 This is a little Fly (Diptera) grooming its wings while perched on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I don't know what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

File.Name: f110a_f110-004.jpg
Notes:
16249277682 This is a new vine of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) growing over a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree, with the dark woodland of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. I got this photo from our covered porch in the rain today. I was hoping for raindrops in the photo, but I guess that's too much to ask for. I like the depth of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2015)

This storm hasn't been a great rain maker, but we've had steady light rain and drizzle all day today, about 0.15 inch so far. The NWS says our best chance for rain is tonight. This rain is perfect to hydrate the lichens and keep the mosses and seedlings going and even bring up a few mushrooms.
CommonNames: Chaparral Clematis; Madrone; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f110b_f110-012.jpg
Notes:
16062496338 Here is a group of pretty little mushrooms with brown "umbonate" or "nippled" caps and twisted stems fruiting in the rain today. (It was raining when I got this photo.) They are growing with mostly weedy grasses along East Camino Cielo road. I can make out pink spores on the mushroom on the ground. I guess these are genus Nolanea or maybe Leptonia in the genus Entolomataceae. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2015)

David Arora (Mushrooms Demystified, 1986) describes Nolanea sericea as a mushroom that "tends to fruit during rainy spells rather than following them." Maybe this is the one?
