File.Name: f110c_f110-011.jpg
Notes: 16249928345 Here is a group of pretty little mushrooms with brown "umbonate" or "nippled" caps and twisted stems fruiting in the rain today. (It was raining when I got this photo.) They are growing with mostly weeds along East Camino Cielo road. I believe these mushrooms are pink-spored, see my [Next] photo. I guess they are genus Nolanea or maybe Leptonia in the genus Entolomataceae. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2015)

File.Name: f110d_f110-007.jpg
Notes: 16248957612 This is an oak leaf with raindrops and "Erineum Galls" caused by tiny Live Oak Erineum Mites (Eriophyes mackiei, Eriophyidae, Acarina). The mites cause pockets or pouches to form under the leaves. I found this leaf today in the rain on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I really just like photographing these leaves in the rain! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2015)
CommonNames: Erineum Galls; Live Oak Erineum Mites; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Eriophyes mackiei; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Eriophyes mackiei; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f110e_f107-030.jpg
Notes:
16249626935 These are tiny foliose lichens growing on a dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The twig is only about 1/4 inch thick which gives some scale. It's interesting that these lichens are forming odd root-like structures on their lower surface. Lichens aren't plants so they can't be real roots. Maybe they function as a holdfast? Those tiny orange lichens are pretty neat also. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2015)

I've seen the same thing on a local Pelt Lichen (genus Peltigera), maybe the Many-fruited Pelt Lichen (Peltigera polydactylon) - see this photo.
CommonNames: foliose lichens; Coast Live Oak; Pelt Lichen; Many-fruited Pelt Lichen
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peltigera; Peltigera polydactylon
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peltigera; Peltigera polydactylon

File.Name: f110f_f109-013.jpg
Notes: 16062024068 These are crustose lichens growing on a sandstone outcrop. The large lichen in the center is forming apothecia - spore-bearing structures of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. If you look close, you can see that almost the whole rock is covered with lichens. I don't know what any of them are. There's lots to learn! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2015)

File.Name: f111a_f111-038.jpg
Notes:
16259037452 This is a closer shot of the Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata in my [Previous] photo. It's our usual winter dragonfly. Th yellow "racing stripe" on the side is distinctive. This one is perched on a bare twig of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. It's almost invisible, and I was startled when I noticed it! It reckon it's grounded by our inclement weather, but it was moving when I annoyed it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)

Darned if we didn't end up with over an inch of rain yesterday and last night, more than was expected! It was a good soaking rain with no runoff at all. I did find a few new mushrooms today, but I'm sure they were already there, hiding in the oak leaves. This rain will bring out some new ones!

It's interesting that the coastal cities got more rain than we did in the mountains. It's also interesting that there was no wind to speak of after our storm moved through today. This was another storm that came from the south, with a tropical tail. Is that the pattern? We now stand at 13.92 inches of rain for the rain-year, 106% of normal, more than we had all last year.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: f111b_f111-037.jpg
Notes: 16258978842 This beautiful creature is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's our usual winter dragonfly. This one is perched on a bare twig of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. It's almost invisible, and I was startled when I noticed it! It reckon it's grounded by our inclement weather. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: f111c_f111-006.jpg
Notes: 15639839753 This is a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, with raindrops. The flowers resemble the Hillside Gooseberry (R. californicum) but the whole plant is glandular. I love that bottom raindrop on the pistil that reflects the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)

File.Name: f111d_f111-030.jpg
Notes: 16073730537 These are handsome Russula mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I'm not sure what species they are. They're not quite red enough to be R. emetica, and I didn't taste them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; R. emetica
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; R. emetica

File.Name: f111e_f111-027.jpg
Notes: 16259452045 These are the tiny fruiting bodies of Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae family of fungi. I put my pen with a 0.7mm ball in the photo for scale. The "mushrooms" are only a couple of mm across, but there are lots of them. They are rather pretty if you get close enough to notice them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)

File.Name: f111f_f111-024.jpg
Notes: 15636908664 The rain last night did bring out the lichens on the oak trees. I won't try to guess what these are , but I appreciate the diversity. There's the shaggy one in front, and the gray foliose one in the back, and several different crustose ones on the bark. All of these are on the bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. There are many more! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)

File.Name: f112a_f112-051.jpg
Notes:
16081187380 Surprise! These are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but I don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. Last year I found the first flowers on January 30, and the year before I found them on February 1, so these are early. I saw them on both East Camino Cielo and Stagecoach Road today. Yeah the white balance is a little funky, but they are flowering in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, and they should look like it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

The rain is gone. I did find quite a few mushrooms today and yesterday, but I suspect they were already there hiding in the oak leaves. But our recent rain will bring up some new ones!

File.Name: f112b_f112-015.jpg
Notes:
16242556246 These are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on fallen leaves and a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. This photo shows the plasmodia becoming sporangia. I'll go back to this place tomorrow to see what happens next. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

You have to be in the right place at the right time to notice these shy critters! They are creatures that the rain brings. They look like little fungi, but the spores will become tiny microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists that live in decaying wood and humus. See this photo for more info. They only last for a day or two. I think they might be my favorite creatures to find and photograph.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia

File.Name: f112c_f112-008.jpg
Notes: 15645963064 Slime Mold or Fungus? I suspect it's a fungus. It has none of the streaming-structure of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria), see my [Next] photo. This might be the last stage of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, see my [Previous] photo. The mushroom is completely gone, but I can see a gap in the leaves where it might have been. In short, I don't know what this is! I know where it's growing, and I'll keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

File.Name: f112d_f112-006.jpg
Notes: 16082398947 Here are a pair of small Bolete mushrooms in the woods, maybe Boletus dryophilus. They have yellow pores that stain blue from handling and a stem that is yellow on top and red lower down. But the one on the right has a "fungus infection" caused by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus. This fungus on a fungus finally becomes bright yellow and then reddish-brown. I wonder if this is the fungus in my [Next] photo? (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

File.Name: f112e_f112-023.jpg
Notes:
15648184333 This is a single Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushroom growing at the base of a dead shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's growing with mosses and a neat clump of native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. There are a few lichens in there too. It's a lot of life for one photo! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

Usually these mushrooms grow in clusters, but not this one. They are said to glow in the dark, but our west coast mushrooms don't seem to do that. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them! This is the first one I've seen this year. Compare this photo in the same place two years ago.
CommonNames: Western Jack-O-Lantern; Bigberry Manzanita; Goldback Fern
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pentagramma triangularis; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Omphalotus olivascens; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pentagramma triangularis; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: f112f_f112-026.jpg
Notes:
16266199821 This is a universe of little Cladonia lichens in the woods, growing on a burl of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in the Ericaceae plant family. These are "fruticose" lichens but they come from a "foliose" base. There appear to be at least two kinds here - the upright stalks and the little Pyxie Cups on the right in the middle. I don't know if these are the same or different. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

There's no easy way to learn lichens. They're complicated like all living things, maybe more so because they are composite organisms made of both fungus and algae. I'll get some books and try to do it right.

File.Name: f113a_f113-024.jpg
Notes:
16089770859 This is another photo of the beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata, taken today. See this photo and the one [Previous] to it. This is our usual winter dragonfly. It's perched on a bare winter twig of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek, but a different twig from my previous photos. It's almost invisible, but I was looking for it this time. I like the light. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2015)

Today's photos are this dragonfly, an interesting flower, a slime mold, a couple of mushrooms, and a lichen. The woods are coming alive again after our recent rain!
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: f113b_f112-031.jpg
Notes: 16089652459 Here is a wonderful Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora, Orobanchaceae) plant in full flower - and in the sun! These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place, though Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) is nearby. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. See this photo and read the interesting comments. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria

File.Name: f113c_f113-015.jpg
Notes:
16275658175 These are the same tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) that I showed yesterday in this photo. How quickly they mature! These are growing on fallen leaves and a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. This photo shows the spore-bearing sporangia with a lime crust. They are almost invisible now, but I marked the place. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

You have to be in the right place at the right time to notice these shy critters! They are creatures that the rain brings. They look like little fungi, but the spores will become tiny microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists that live in decaying wood and humus. See this photo for more info. They only last for a day or two. I think they might be my favorite creatures to find and photograph.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia

File.Name: f113d_f113-042.jpg
Notes:
15653149284 These are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods where the sun never shines. The velvety caps and wide-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2015)

I'm not sure that these mushrooms are red enough or frosted enough to be the real Marasmius plicatulus. They might be something else, but they are kin at least.
ScientificName: Marasmius plicatulus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius plicatulus
associatedTaxa: Marasmius plicatulus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius plicatulus

File.Name: f113e_f113-011.jpg
Notes: 16089352559 This mushroom had me confused for a while. It was quite small, only an inch or two across, and growing in the garden - but not too far from an oak tree. I dug it out for a photo and noticed the pink colors on the stem after I handled it. Aha! It's "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom. It has a neat ring, but no obvious volva. It has an unmistakable "amanita aura". These mushrooms sure are variable! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2015)

File.Name: f113f_f111-032.jpg
Notes: 15653002514 These are crustose lichens growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The lichen is forming apothecia - spore-bearing structures of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Now is that one kind of lichen or two? They are different colors, but they sure look like the same growth. This is one reason I find lichens so difficult - and interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2015)

File.Name: f114a_f114-011.jpg
Notes:
16281424811 These are first new flowers I've found this year of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family, with very hairy stems and pedicels. (We also have a variant called Hoffman's Nightshade (S. xanti var. hoffmannii) that has almost glabrous stems.) Last year I didn't find the first flowers until March 12, but the year before I found them on January 29. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2015)

Flowers this year seem on the whole a bit early. The rain may have something to do with it, but I reckon the mostly warm weather this winter is more of a factor. We did have some cold weather around New Year's, but our storms have all been warm with a tropical influence. I'm not complaining!
ScientificName: Solanum xanti; xanti; Solanaceae; S. xanti; hoffmannii
associatedTaxa: Solanum xanti; xanti; Solanaceae; S. xanti; hoffmannii

File.Name: f114b_f112-020.jpg
Notes: 16281373871 These are the first new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family that I've seen this year, growing along Stagecoach Road. Last year I found the first flowers on January 15, and the year before on January 27. I guess they are right on schedule. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

File.Name: f114c_f114-006.jpg
Notes:
15660652284 This is a closer shot of the Agaricus mushroom on the left in my [Previous] photo, in the sun. It shows the "cogwheel" pattern on its unopened partial veil, which protects and covers the young gills. It's as thin as tissue paper. In time it tears free to form a skirt-like ring on the stem. You can see some yellow stains where I rubbed the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2015)

This might well be the the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushroom. It looks delicious, but I nibbled a piece and gagged at the taste. Maybe I've just nibbled too many *bad* agaricus mushrooms? I'm wary of all our woodland species. "Once bitten, twice warned!

File.Name: f114d_f114-007.jpg
Notes:
15660560224 I found these handsome Agaricus mushrooms in the woods in the woods today. The one on the left shows a "cogwheel" pattern on its unopened partial veil. (See my [Next] photo for a closer shot.) In time it tears free to form the skirt-like ring on the right. The gills start white, but end up stained chocolate-brown from the spores. The mushroom caps stain yellow from handling, but the base of the stem does not stain yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2015)

These might well be the the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushroom. They look delicious. They are said to be "edible (for most people)" - but I nibbled a piece and gagged at the taste. Maybe I've just nibbled too many *bad* agaricus mushrooms? I'm wary of all our woodland species. "Once bitten, twice warned!
Locality: for most people)" - but I nibbled a piece and gagged at the taste.  Maybe I've just nibbled too many *bad* agaricus mushrooms?  I'm wary of all our woodland species.  "Once bitten

File.Name: f114e_f113-021.jpg
Notes: 16256881106 These are pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I actually found quite a few of them yesterday, but these were the only ones catching some sunlight in our dark winter woods. They have a chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool, so I guess they are the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin - at least that's a convenient tag for them. Not edible, and you wouldn't want to try because of the bleach-like smell. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2015)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum

File.Name: f114f_f112-011.jpg
Notes: 15662872213 Maybe these are the tiny caps of Marasmiellus candidus mushrooms on a bank of moss in the woods. They have widely spaced white gills, but I couldn't get low enough to show them any better than this. These little mushrooms seem to harm the moss. Compare this photo from 2012, the last time I found them in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2015)

File.Name: f115a_f114-008.jpg
Notes:
16288533431 These are new plants of native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in front of a plant of native Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis) in the Apiaceaeplant family. It's a delight to see them both, especially the orchids. This is the place where I found flowers last year, see this photo. The pretty leaves completely shrivel up by the time they flower. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2015)

The weather has been unremarkable - except that it is still remarkable warm for January. I've been finding lots of interesting mushrooms, more than I can show. But the woods are starting to dry out...
CommonNames: Chaparral Orchid; Denseflower Rein Orchid; Pacific Sanicle
ScientificName: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae

File.Name: f115b_f115-027.jpg
Notes: 16102717808 Here are a pair of Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushrooms, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. They are very slimy mushrooms with the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places them in the subgenus Myxacium. You can see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stems, see my [Previous] photo for a closer look at the one on the right. They are strange and beautiful, one of my favorites - partly because I know what they are! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium

File.Name: f115c_f115-030.jpg
Notes: 16102568208 This is the cortina of silky or cobwebby hairs that initially covers and protects the gills of this slimy Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. In time the cortina fibers will tear free and hang from the stem as a ring of sorts, but in the meantime they get stained rusty-red by the falling spores of the mushroom. Compare my [Previous] photo of the membranous veil of an Agaricus mushroom. See my [Next] photo for more info about this mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)
CommonNames: cortina; Banded Cortinarius; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus

File.Name: f115d_f115-022.jpg
Notes: 16264083356 This is the same handsome Agaricus mushroom on the left in my [Previous] photo. In this shot (in the sun!) the partial veil is just tearing free from the pink gills. The veil protects the immature gills and finally falls away to form a ring on the stalk. This mushroom appears to have a "double veil" attached to the edge of the cap, but also to the reddish fibers above the edge. See my [Previous] photo for some discussion about what this mushroom is. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)

File.Name: f115e_f115-019.jpg
Notes:
16104004927 This is a group of handsome Agaricus mushrooms in the woods today, growing in a thick mulch of oak leaves. I have a hard time identifying our woodland agaricus species, but I wonder if these are the poisonous Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom because of that thick flaring ring on the stem? Or maybe the edible Wine-colored Agaricus (A. subrutiIescens)? I did nibble on a bit and it tasted pleasant, not a hint of phenol, but I didn't crush the base of the stalk. I can't say if they are edible or not. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)

I'm sure this one is different from the (maybe) Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushroom I showed yesterday. It has brown fibrils on the cap, a thicker ring, and the new gills are pink not white. I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!
Locality: Agaricus silvicola) mushroom I showed yesterday. It has brown fibrils on the cap
CommonNames: Felt-Ringed Agaricus; Wine-colored Agaricus; Woodland Agaricus
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; A. subrutiIescens; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; A. subrutiIescens; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus

File.Name: f115f_f115-024.jpg
Notes: 15669915893 I found this pair of handsome Agaricus mushrooms in the woods today. They are pushing up a large "mushrump" in the deep oak mulch. I'm pretty sure these are the same mushrooms as my [Next] two photos, with unopened caps. (There were a dozen or more in this one place.) I'm not sure what they are, but they sure are beautiful. I have a hard time identifying our woodland agaricus species. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)

File.Name: f116a_f116-004.jpg
Notes:
16295633401 Here are two shots of the same tiny Fly (Diptera) on a single leaf of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. I guess it's a Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae, but I'm not sure. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

This long-legged fly is tiny. I got the photos at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The fly is about 475 pixels long. That makes it about 1.9 mm long, less than 1/10 inch. See this photo for how I figured.

I have a pleasant duty in Santa Barbara tomorrow, so I may not be able to get new photos. But I have lots of new photos waiting to be posted!
ScientificName: Diptera; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae

File.Name: f116b_f116-006.jpg
Notes: 16295544021 This is looking closer at the surprisingly intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are related to avocado and sassafras. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens, with two orange glands at the base of each, finally surrounded by an outer circle of three more fat stamens with four anthers each. The whole thing is contained within a circle of (usually) 6 sepals. They are strange and wonderful flowers for such a common tree in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

File.Name: f116c_f116-023.jpg
Notes: 16271292506 This small bolete mushroom has intensely yellow pores that don't stain blue. I guess it is Boletus flaviporus aka Aureoboletus flaviporus - except that the cap doesn't look like it was ever viscid. Darn mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

File.Name: f116d_f116-020.jpg
Notes: 16297130225 I believe these are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I sliced the gills with my knife to show the white latex "milk" which doesn't change to yellow. It has a spicy taste that is not quite pleasant. The caps are viscid and slightly zoned, and the stems are "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: f116e_f116-013.jpg
Notes: 16296153372 These are really small "mushrooms" growing from a crack in a sandstone bedrock, with moss. You could probably cover them both with a quarter. I have no idea what they are, but I guess they are tiny polypores in the fungal order Polyporaceae. They are quite smooth underneath. I've only noticed them in this one place, but I was already down on my hands and knees looking at the lichens when I saw them. They might be in the "common but rarely noticed" category. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

File.Name: f116f_f116-026.jpg
Notes: 16109582680 This is a tiny world of Cladonia lichens, mosses, liverworts, and even a few seedling plants growing on sandstone that is slowly becoming soil. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

File.Name: f117a_f117-002.jpg
Notes:
15682939534 This is a golden Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for just a moment on a narrow leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). If you know what to look for, you can the "spurious veins" on its right wing. Looking close, you can also see that this fly is missing a chunk of its abdomen and a piece (the "alula" and calypteres?) at the base of its right wing. I'm used to seeing butterflies missing chunks of their wings from bird attacks, but I can't recall ever seeing this on a fly before. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2015)

I had some time this morning before my duty in town, so I went to my favorite place by the creek to find some insects. It had some luck! The rest of my photos today are mushrooms pushing up "mushrumps".
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f117b_f117-014.jpg
Notes:
16305253395 This is a little furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) hovering in the air! It looks like genus Bombylius, see this photo and this one. You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. I see these larger ones and also smaller ones that I guess are female and male respectively, and sometimes I see them flying together. I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their meeting place. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2015)

Hah, I took 50 photos to get this one keeper. This is a tight crop, and they are fast!

File.Name: f117c_f117-005.jpg
Notes:
15682735114 Here are a pair of caterpillars of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). You can see some of the webbing that these caterpillars spin, sometimes shrouding the stems. These caterpillars are hard to focus on with those radio-tower spines on their back! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2015)

(In case you're wondering, that stem on the right belongs to a native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) that is growing up through the monkey flower.)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f117d_f116-019.jpg
Notes:
16303270611 This is a pretty red Russula mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. This one looks red enough to be in the "Russula emetica" group - maybe Russula mairei, our west coast version. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful mushrooms!
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula mairei
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula mairei

File.Name: f117e_f116-011.jpg
Notes:
16303175311 This is a nest of Russula mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, looking into the "mushrump" that the mushrooms form when they push up through the oak leaves. The green grass-like stems are our native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae). Maybe the mushrooms are Russula cremoricolor? (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2015)

The woods are starting to dry out after our good rain last weekend. There is still moisture under the oak leaves, and that's where the mushrooms are hiding. The slightest bump in the leaves might be a mushroom... or a gopher!
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Russula cremoricolor
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Russula cremoricolor

File.Name: f117f_f115-032.jpg
Notes: 16118729689 This is a Cortinarius mushroom in the woods pushing up a large "mushrump" of fallen oak leaves. The rusty-brown gills and the fibers on the stem are the marks of this genus, but I have no idea which Cortinarius mushroom it is, as they all look the same in this stage. There should be a name for those dark shadowy places under the mushrooms when they push up. Sometimes there are salamanders and other interesting creature here, but I hate to needlessly dig the mushrooms up to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2015)

File.Name: f118a_f118-039.jpg
Notes:
16128306777 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of the well-named native Padre's Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family. I noticed this one while driving down Painted Cave Road today. Darn, they seem early this year! I didn't find the first one last year until March 7, and the year before I found the first one on February 7. I guess the warm weather has something to do with it. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

It was yet another warm and beautiful day today, and I found a few more "first flowers" for the record. Warm and beautiful is pleasant, but I'd rather have cold and rainy!

File.Name: f118b_f118-031.jpg
Notes: 16312242781 These are the first flowers I've found this year of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Last year I found the first flowers on 21 January 2014 so I guess they are right in schedule. I found these in a sunny place on a south-facing slope. Our plants in the shady woods will have to wait a while. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

File.Name: f118c_f118-035.jpg
Notes:
16312949872 This is a new flowering plant of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. This plant is a monoecious vine, with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. It was growing on a south-facing slope near the Painted Cave community. Most of the flowers are staminate or pollen-bearing, but you can see a pistillate flowers with a spiny ovary in the lower-left. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

These have been flowering for a while. I got my first photo a few days ago on January 14, and they were already going strong. We also have Marah fabaceus in our mountains. It has flowers that are are smaller and flatter and a bit more yellowish than these. That one mostly grows further away from the coast.

File.Name: f118d_f118-001.jpg
Notes: 16311908761 Here are beautiful new flowers of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I got a photo of them just starting to flower a week ago on January 9, but I waited a week to get this more typical photo. The shiny leaves are as pretty as the flowers. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. I found these shrubs flowering on 31 December 2014 and 16 January 2013. I guess they're right on schedule. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

File.Name: f118e_f118-025.jpg
Notes: 16127401809 Here are female pistillate flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae with pistils ready to receive pollen. They just look sexual, and of course they are. In time, the flowers will open into proper catkins. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. Last year, the male plants never opened and the females did just fine ripening seeds without them. This year, I did find male plants in flower, but they are suffering some kind of disorder - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

File.Name: f118f_f118-005.jpg
Notes:
16287541296 Something peculiar is happening to the male staminate catkins of this native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. They are withering and turning black, and I wonder if it is a disease. They just started flowering a couple of weeks ago in this place, see this photo. I don't think they should be fading so soon, and not this way. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)

Tough luck for this shrub that never managed to flower last year. Fortunately the females (see my [Next] photo) don't seem to need the males.

File.Name: f119a_f119-004.jpg
Notes:
16134721908 Here are a pair of furry Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for just a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe they are genus Bombylius? I often see them hovering together in the air, but I think this is the first time I've ever seen them perch together. I guess they are a courting pair with the larger female in the back and the smaller male up front. But I'm confused because the "male" is larger than the usual partners I see, and isn't that female hounding the male? Maybe I have their genders reversed? Courtship is never easy! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

*Sigh* It was another warm and pleasant day. After I got this photo, I hiked up into our dark shady canyon and did manage to find a few new mushrooms.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombylius

File.Name: f119b_f119-022.jpg
Notes: 16134635368 This is a group of extraordinary tiny mushrooms growing from decomposing wood of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Darn, I didn't have my pen for scale, but these mushrooms are tiny, only a few millimeters across. There are a couple of really tiny "buttons" just above the leaf on the right side. Maybe the mushrooms are Mycena acicula (or kin), but the color looks different from this photo. Maybe that's just a white balance thing? I actually noticed these while walking along, but I was looking close! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

File.Name: f119c_f119-016.jpg
Notes: 16134491478 This is looking close at an Agaricus mushroom just shedding its partial veil. It was growing in a thick mulch of oak leaves. This one appears to have a "double ring" that connects to the edge of the cap and also to the fibrillose tissue on top of the cap. I'm really confused by our woodland agaricus species, so I wont try to guess which one this is. I nibbled on a bit and it tasted pleasant, not a hint of phenol, including the base of the stalk. I suspect they are edible, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

File.Name: f119d_f119-018.jpg
Notes: 16135767639 This is a group of handsome portobello-size Agaricus mushrooms in the woods today, growing in a thick mulch of oak leaves. I'm really confused by our woodland agaricus species, so I wont try to guess which one this is. I nibbled on a bit and it tasted pleasant, not a hint of phenol, including the base of the stalk. I suspect they are edible, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

File.Name: f119e_f119-010.jpg
Notes: 16321873605 These are pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods, growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They have a chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool, so I guess they are the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin - at least that's a convenient tag for them. Not edible, and you wouldn't want to try because of the bleach-like smell. They are handsome though! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum

File.Name: f119f_f119-020.jpg
Notes: 16320008881 These are strange antler-like fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin). I don't think the count as "mushrooms" as they are not at all fleshy, but they are fungi. I'm sure they are growing from buried wood under the oak leaves. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. The white dusting on the black stems is from asexual spores (conidia). Later on they form sexual spores from "perithecia" at the top of the fruiting body. They are not common in our mountains, though I've found them in this same place before, see this photo. I was looking for them today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

File.Name: f120a_f120-021.jpg
Notes:
16304297106 Look close! That's not a gray stick, but a moth caterpillar stretched out between new leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's quite large, maybe 3 inches long. I guess it's the caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2015)

I was sitting about five feet away, with my glasses off. (I moved a chair to the creek, and I take my glasses off to see close.) I was pretty sure it was a dead stem of wild sweetpea caught in the shrub. It kept nagging me, and I finally put my glasses on. That caterpillar has the damnedest camouflage!

It was cooler and cloudy today, at least this morning. There was no rain, but it felt like it. At least that's a step in the right direction.
Locality: I moved a chair to the creek
CommonNames: moth caterpillar; Bush Monkey Flower; Geometrid Moth
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Geometridae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Geometridae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: f120b_f120-008.jpg
Notes:
16142414418 This is another photo of the handsome portobello-size Agaricus mushrooms I've been finding in the woods lately. I now believe they are the Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) - thanks Matt! I have another photo of the same pair of mushrooms here. I've been puzzled by these mushrooms for a while, and have more photos following the last link and here. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2015)

One thing I've noticed about this mushroom is that they grow in groups of 10 or more. Our other woodland species of Agaricus are solitary or at most a few. I nibbled on a bit and it tastes pleasant, not a hint of phenol, including the base of the stalk. If this ID is right, they are regarded as "edible and choice". I'm wary of all our woodland agarics, but I'm ready to try this one.

File.Name: f120c_f120-020.jpg
Notes: 16142350938 These are Tricholoma mushrooms with "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. I regard the reddish-brown spots on the gills as distinctive. I believe they are part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, not recommended for the table. They are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2015)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f120d_f120-007.jpg
Notes: 15709901343 These are some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushrooms in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The radially fibrillose cap seems distinctive. They don't have any particular odor. You can see a bit of the brown spores on the caps. These are unremarkable mushrooms, but they do push up remarkably large "mushrumps" in the leaves for their size. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2015)

File.Name: f120e_f119-030.jpg
Notes: 16327948321 These are small mushrooms of the genus Nolanea (or maybe Leptonia) in the genus Entolomataceae. The gills don't look pink, but I skooched the mushroom on the right over a bit and you can see a pink or flesh-colored natural spore print on the mushroom in the middle. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

File.Name: f120f_f119-026.jpg
Notes:
15707217804 These are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up among the fallen oak and bay leaves in a place in the woods where the sun rarely shines. The velvety caps and wide-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2015)

I got this this photo in the sunlight - barely! You don't notice how fast the sun moves until you try to photograph mushrooms in a beam of sunlight!

File.Name: f121a_f121-005.jpg
Notes:
16311738596 I found this lovely Becker's White (Pontia beckerii) butterfly working a new/old flower of native South Coast Range Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. commutata) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. Both the butterfly and the plant are unusual, keep reading. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

I think of this butterfly as a desert species, but here it is. One of its host plants is Prince's Plume (Stanleya pinnata) in the Brassicaceae which I've found growing just up the hill - see this photo.

The South Coast Range Malacothrix is a Malacothrix saxatilis with hairy leaves. Sometimes our plants approach the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (M. saxatilis var. arachnoidea) that are downright tomentose. See this photo for a comparison. It's a summer-flowering plant that is flowering again in winter - not a lot of flowers, but a few. I've also seen this behavior in native Goldenbush (Isocoma) and Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora), both in the Asteraceae plant family. It's what they do.

Paradise Road is open again across the river, so I went over to see what I could find. The woods actually look pretty good. There are lots of growing plants and a few new flowers. There is a pretty good chance of rain next Monday-Wednesday. That will help keep it going.
CommonNames: Becker's White; South Coast Range Malacothrix; Prince's Plume; South Coast Range Malacothrix; Carmel Valley Malacothrix; Goldenbush; Telegraph Weed
ScientificName: Pontia beckerii; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata; Asteraceae; Stanleya pinnata; Brassicaceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; M. saxatilis; arachnoidea; Isocoma; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pontia beckerii; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata; Asteraceae; Stanleya pinnata; Brassicaceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; M. saxatilis; arachnoidea; Isocoma; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: f121b_f121-014.jpg
Notes:
15717642803 This unfortunate Becker's White (Pontia beckerii) butterfly has fallen into the grips of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). See my [Next] photo for more info on the interesting butterfly. You can see just one of the spider's legs touching the butterfly's abdomen - with an odd little fly sitting on it. I don't know what that means, but there it is. I found this scene on new/old flowers of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

This summer-flowering shrub seems to be flowering again in winter - not a lot of flowers, but a few. I've also seen this behavior in native Malacothrix saxatilis and Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora), both in the Asteraceae plant family. It's what they do.
CommonNames: Becker's White; Crab Spider; Goldenbush; Telegraph Weed
ScientificName: Pontia beckerii; Thomisidae; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pontia beckerii; Thomisidae; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: f121c_f121-025.jpg
Notes:
15715047804 I found this lovely Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new/old flowers of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. I've seen a few more flying around in mid-winter, but this is the first I've managed to photograph. I like the painterly background of this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

These summer-flowering shrubs seem to be flowering again in winter - not a lot of flowers, but a few. I've also seen this behavior in native Malacothrix saxatilis and Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora), both in the Asteraceae plant family. It's what they do.
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: f121d_f121-040.jpg
Notes:
16151136049 These are the first new flowers of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Maybe they are the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus), but maybe not. Some plants have woolly-white leaves, see this photo. I need to wait for seed pods and take my Jepson manual down to the river to sort this out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

These long-stemmed flower heads are tough to photograph. The flowers usually rise above the leaves, and of course you want to show both. These flowers were leaning over just right.

File.Name: f121e_f121-007.jpg
Notes: 15717358533 Here is a new flower and buds of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) in the Malvaceae plant family growing by the river. I'm not sure if these shrubs ever stopped flowering, but at least these are the first flowers I've seen this year. You can make out a few orange Thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects on the open flower. It's a lovely native shrub. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

File.Name: f121f_f121-012.jpg
Notes: 16336297982 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year on two separate plants of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. On the left are female pistillate flowers, and on the right are male staminate flowers. Yep, these are from the same species! This shrub has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. Apparently the plant gets its charming common name because it provides good forage for livestock. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

File.Name: f122a_f122-017.jpg
Notes:
16158735829 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family today. The buds are especially handsome. I didn't find the first flowers until March 10 last year. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2015)

I saw a few Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterflies in the air today, but I never saw one come down to the flowers. (See this photo from last year.)

It was mostly overcast with high clouds today, though the sun appeared for a few photos. I hiked up Aliso Canyon for the first time this year. There is lots of vegetative growth, but it's still too early for most flowers. The woods look pretty good. A bit of rain next week (as predicted) will help a lot!
Locality:
See this photo from last year.)

It was mostly overcast with high clouds today
CommonNames: Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea; Sara Orangetip
ScientificName: Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Anthocharis sara
associatedTaxa: Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Anthocharis sara

File.Name: f122b_f122-015.jpg
Notes: 15722414394 Here are the first tiny flowers and fruit of non-native Shepherd's Purse (Capsella bursa-pastoris) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a weedy mustard plant that has become part of our flora. It has neat little heart-shaped seed heads that reminded someone of little purses. Flowers and fruit are tasty if you strip them from the tough stems. I like this photo, though I have second thoughts about making the weeds look so darn pretty! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2015)

File.Name: f122c_f122-018.jpg
Notes:
16344701325 I believe this is our other Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus in the Cucurbitaceae plant family). Compare this photo of the very similar Marah macrocarpus. This one in the photo has flowers that are smaller and flatter and a bit more yellowish than the other one. The only way to know for sure is to open the spiky seed pod. M. fabaceus has 4 large seeds, while M. macrocarpus has 8 to 15 smaller seeds. Both plants are monoecious vines, with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat them! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2015)

NatureShutterbug added a couple of interesting distribution maps for these species in a comment on my previous photo. We really are at the intersection of these two different species.

File.Name: f122d_f121-035.jpg
Notes:
16342834581 This is a flower bud on California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, showing some color. It won't be long until they open. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 January 2015)

Hah - This reminds me of Rick Griffin's "flying eyeball" poster, now worth big bucks! I mostly think of Rick Griffin for his "Murphy" Surfer Magazine comics way back in the early '60s, when I was just starting high school.

File.Name: f122e_f122-001.jpg
Notes: 16157128990 I found this perfect husk of a spider above the 4 foot high retaining wall on the edge of our garden which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods. With that color and those legs, I wonder is this is the shed skin of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe the same Diaea livens spider that I show in this photo? (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2015)

File.Name: f122f_f122-013.jpg
Notes: 15721933774 These are tiny Ascomycete Cup fungi in the Pezizales or Heliotiales order. The individual caps are tiny, only a couple of millimeters across. What they are is not so interesting as where they are - on a burned out plant of native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family, see this photo. The plants were burned in the White Fire two years ago come May. Many have re-sprouted, but those that didn't have this fungus. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2015)

File.Name: f123a_f123-018.jpg
Notes:
16351695195 This is a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It may be the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes), but cp. S. diadema). It has spiny front legs, though most of the spines are hidden in my photo. This one is perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Sure, it's a predator. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2015)

I had a errands in town this morning and got home late. So I did a short hike in our canyon, and got some nice photos. I like the diversity today, though I had to borrow a couple from the recent archives - an insect, a snail, a mammal, a mushroom, liverworts, and a gall!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f123b_f123-016.jpg
Notes: 16350617112 I found this native terrestrial Shoulderband Snail (Helminthoglypta, Gastropoda) in the woods today, catching some sunlight. It's tucked in, but I can see a hint of a slime trail in the background so I'm sure it's alive. This is NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands. It's definitely a native. I was surprised to find it with the woods so dry, but I found a few mushrooms today too. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2015)

File.Name: f123c_f122-003.jpg
Notes: 15731463673 This is a California Ground Squirrel aka Beechey Ground Squirrel (Otospermophilus beecheyi) looking out of its burrow. I noticed it as I drove by, and got the photo with my camera steadied on the truck window near the river yesterday morning. They are like prairie dogs, organized and destructive - but cute if they don't live in your backyard! Fortunately, we don't have them near home. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2015)
CommonNames: California Ground Squirrel; Beechey Ground Squirrel

File.Name: f123d_f123-014.jpg
Notes: 16165460117 This is another Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. They are very slimy mushrooms with the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places them in the subgenus Myxacium. You can see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stems, and the debris stuck to what's left of the slime on the stem. They are strange and beautiful, one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2015)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium

File.Name: f123e_f123-008.jpg
Notes: 16163922770 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica)? They are producing sporangia, like little surfing palm trees. Most of our liverworts hide in the dark places, but these are out in the open. That probably explains why they are fruiting first. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2015)

File.Name: f123f_f118-018.jpg
Notes: 15731260443 I believe these bumps on the leaf are "Erineum Galls" made by Eriophyid mites in the genus Eriophyes of the Acari. I found them a few days ago on a shiny new leaf of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. What's more interesting is the tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) on the left gall. I tried to get a closer photo, and pretty much failed, but I inserted it anyway on the right. I'm not sure, but the wasp may be ovipositing. That's a tiny wasp to lay an egg on a mite! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2015)
ScientificName: Eriophyes; Acari; Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: f124a_f124-027.jpg
Notes:
16359902495 Look close! That's not a green twig, but a moth caterpillar on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's fairly large, maybe 1-1/2 inches long. It has the damnedest camouflage, right down to the bumps on its back that resemble the stipules or buds on a branch of the shrub. See my [Previous] photo. I nudged it up and plucked a leaf for this photo, but the caterpillar "shrunk" a bit in the process, contracting its segments. This is a better view, but it looks less twig-like.

I guess this is the caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). See this photo of a similar but different well-camouflaged caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2015)

The Sulphur Moth (Hesperumia sulphuraria - Hodges#6431) caterpillar at BugGuide looks similar, but slightly different. That one was found on Ceanothus megacarpus. I wonder if these caterpillars are adapted for different shrub species?

I don't know how I can notice these critters. Maybe it has something to do with hiking the same trails week after week, year after year. You notice things!

Today was lovely - too lovely for January! We have a chance of rain coming from the south on Monday or Tuesday, and another chance later in the week. I hope it happens. The woods are feeling dry.
Locality: Hesperumia sulphuraria - Hodges#6431) caterpillar at BugGuide looks similar

File.Name: f124c_f124-012.jpg
Notes:
16173301269 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on the underside of a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are stacked in three layers like hexagons, with six neighbors each. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2015)

I suspect these are the eggs of bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I know where they are. I'll try to check in every so often to see how they're doing.

File.Name: f124d_f124-018.jpg
Notes: 16359441735 As they say, "one in the oven". These are fading flowers with brilliant swelling ovaries of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These will become spiny gooseberries in time. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2015)

File.Name: f124e_f124-005.jpg
Notes: 15739338933 It's always fun to try to find the smallest proper mushroom in the woods, and this may be it. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale, so I figure the mushrooms are about 1 mm across. They seem to be growing from an undeveloped acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I found these mushrooms above the 4 foot high retaining wall on the edge of our garden which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods - and saves my aching back! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2015)

File.Name: f124f_f124-022.jpg
Notes:
16359232875 These are knobby galls of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). They are growing on stems of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see a few exit holes where the critters got away. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2015)

I didn't realize until a few minutes ago that I photographed the same galls almost exactly one year ago. See this photo. It's an interesting comparison between young and old.
CommonNames: Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak

File.Name: f125a_f125-017.jpg
Notes:
15748814023 This is a remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It looks a lot like Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. The species name refers to its short wings, from the Latin: "brevis" = short, and "penna" = a feather or wing. What were you thinking of??

These insects have thread-like rear legs, but they hold their thicker and spined front legs out in front of them. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of its head. I actually saw this one in the air like a bit of thistle-down and it landed in the dead branches of this shrub. It's not small, maybe an inch or two long. Along with the breeze, it had a rocking motion that made it hard to photograph. This is the second one I've found this year - see this photo and this one. I've had good luck finding cryptic insects lately! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)

It was warm and beautiful today - the Santa Barbara airport tied a temperature record at 79 degrees. Some clouds moved in from the south in the afternoon. We still have a 30% chance of rain tomorrow night from a storm coming from the south. I hope it happens. The woods feel dry and a bit of rain will keep the mushrooms and native plants going.
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis

File.Name: f125c_f125-024.jpg
Notes: 16366648391 Here are two photos of the beautiful native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. The flowers on the left don't look open yet, but if you look close at the photo on the right (from a different plant) you can see tiny pistils in the air waiting for pollen. These plants are mostly notable for their beautiful foliage. Later in the season they will develop tasty caraway-like seeds that are a pleasure to chew. Last year, I didn't find the first flowers until March 18 - though I didn't look this close. This is another native that is flowering way too early in this odd weather year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)

File.Name: f125d_f125-014.jpg
Notes:
16182330407 Fade to black...

These are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants going dormant on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. They are not really turning black - there's blue in there too, and they will completely revive as soon as we get some rain. They are real "resurrection plants". They are smaller and more weather sensitive than our more common California Liverwort (Asterella californica), see this photo. I'm not sure what they are, but I've had Targiona hypophylla suggested for a name. They enter dormancy very quickly when the sandstone dries out. I've never seen them produce sporangia. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; Targiona hypophylla; sporangia
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; Targiona hypophylla; sporangia

File.Name: f125e_f125-026.jpg
Notes: 15748123783 This is the "surprising guest" on the right of my [Previous] photo - a Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom, the first I've found in our mountains this season. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. It's mostly surprising because it's here at all, it feels so dry. It's also surprising because it's a spring mushroom in our area, so it's not just the plants that are coming out early. March is a more typical time to find these mushrooms in our mountains, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)

File.Name: f125f_f125-025.jpg
Notes: 16180617790 Here are a pair of Agaricus mushrooms, with a surprising guest on the right. (See my [Next] photo!) There is a small seedling of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens, Rubiaceae) coming up between the mushrooms. I'm not sure which agaricus mushrooms these are, and I find them a really confusing group. There are hints of orange-staining on the cap and stems. They smelled good, though I didn't taste them. The woods feel dry, but there a still a few mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)

File.Name: f126a_f126-035.jpg
Notes:
15757227613 Here is a caterpillar happily munching a leaf of Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum, Asteraceae), surrounded by its own frass. Maybe it will become a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly, which are said to favor thistles? The colors actually look more like a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) caterpillar, but those are said to favor mallows and nettles. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

It was a lovely overcast day today, but warm, like summer monsoon weather. We have had a splash of rain tonight, but not enough to measure yet. I hope it really rains tonight. The plants and mushrooms need it.
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Painted Lady; West Coast Lady

File.Name: f126b_f126-032.jpg
Notes: 16376233662 This is a coin-sized Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on foliage of Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum, Asteraceae). The bug may well be Largus californicus. I was surprised to find it so early in the year - and more surprised to see a mating pair running away on the ground. Darn, I missed that photo - they were too fast for me. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f126c_f126-016.jpg
Notes: 15754562354 This is a gorgeous fully-open flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers face the earth, but this plant was growing on a slight rise, which gave me a chance to get under it without using a stick to prop up the flower. It's growing as a fire-follower on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire a couple of years ago. I usually find the first flowers in mid-February, so these are early as are many other native flowers. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f126d_f126-018.jpg
Notes: 16350895016 I believe this is the Hoary Nightshade aka Blue Witch (Solanum umbelliferum var. incanum) in the Solanaceae plant family, which has whitish stems covered with short branched hairs. The Jepson manual doesn't recognize the variety any more, but I think the white stems are distinctive. I haven't seen this plant for a few years as it usually grows in the more desert-like backcountry and the upper parts of the river. It's interesting to find it here at Paradise. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f126e_f126-038.jpg
Notes: 16189102828 This must be a seedling plant of native Stinging Lupine aka Nettle Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing as a fire-follower on burned soil from the White Fire a couple of years ago. The bronze leaves are striking. The nettle-like hairs of this plant don't sting, but they are remarkable. You can see a fat un-lupine-like seed leaf at the base of the plant - it looks like a peanut. I have a photo of the flowers on a mature plant here. They are not common here. I put this photo in the back of the pile, but this is my favorite photo today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f126f_f126-007.jpg
Notes: 16190779277 These are small mushrooms of the genus Leptonia or maybe Nolanea in the family Entolomataceae. They look like little sombrero hats with those "umbiculate" caps with a knob in the center. The mushroom on the left has made a pink or flesh-colored natural spore print on the cap of the mushroom on the right. This is the hallmark of the family. You can see how they are drying out. With luck, we'll get a bit of much-needed rain tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f127a_f127-011.jpg
Notes:
16383978212 This is a small Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) perched on its web on a low shrub of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There was a slight breeze, which always makes spider photos difficult. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2015)

I agreed to go with a friend to Ventura this morning to retrieve a car, but I got a few photos both before and after our trip. Tomorrow will be a better day in the woods anyway, after our bit of rain. We ended up with 1/4 inch of real rain last night, better than I expected! I actually found several mushrooms in the woods this morning that I hadn't noticed before, though no doubt they were already there, hiding under the oak leaves.

File.Name: f127b_f127-002.jpg
Notes: 16382926431 This is a fungus on a fungus. The "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus has taken over this Bolete mushroom in the woods under oak and bay trees. The mushroom is probably Boletus dryophilus, but who knows at this point. The first stage of of an infection is white mold. The second stage is yellow, visible at the base of this mushroom. Then there is a reddish-brown final stage, see this photo. A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we find in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2015)

File.Name: f127c_f126-026.jpg
Notes: 16198385239 These are pretty new flowers on a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus), but I need to wait for seed pods and take my Jepson manual down to the river to be sure. The same plants last summer had woolly-white leaves, see this photo. (I marked this plant last summer to make sure!) This plant is growing with old stems of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). See my [Previous] photo of the fantastic foliage. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)
CommonNames: Milkvetch; Locoweed; Santa Barbara Milkvetch; Scale Broom

File.Name: f127d_f126-030.jpg
Notes: 16358537136 This is the fantastic foliage of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with flower buds. They are probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus), but I need to wait for seed pods and take my Jepson manual down to the river to be sure. The same plants last summer had woolly-white leaves, see this photo. (I marked this plant last summer to make sure!) (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f127e_f126-013.jpg
Notes: 16196764418 These are last year's seedpods on California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. They look a lot like old mustard pods, but if you look close you can see that they have four parts rather than two. I have a photo of the fresh plant here from last year. They are growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire last May, and I saw new plants yesterday. This is another local plant with desert affinities. I could probably only get this photo on an overcast day like yesterday! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 January 2015)

File.Name: f127f_f125-029.jpg
Notes:
16383389802 These are individual flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. By themselves, they are remarkably intricate, with a deep blue nectar disk in addition to the usual floral parts. Sometimes they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. This hasn't been a great year, but I can still see a white bloom on the hills when I go to town. When you think how many flowers there are in a cluster, and how many clusters there are on a shrub, and how many shrubs there are on a hillside... the numbers are beyond counting. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2015)

There's a tiny insect left of center, I believe a Barklouse in the family Psocidae of the order Psocoptera. If you hold your hand out under the flowers and whack them with a stick, you can sometimes get a handful of tiny insects! It's a good naturalist trick when you're taking kinds into the woods.

File.Name: f128a_f128-008.jpg
Notes:
16390511981 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) this morning, slowly climbing up a sandstone rock down by the creek. Maybe it's the genus Platypedia? I guess this one is freshly emerged. It was tricky getting these photos so close to the sandstone where my tripod wouldn't fit, but there's always a way. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, but we have different cicadas later in summer that do sing. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

(I have a hard time hearing cicadas. They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus, and I just can't pick them out. But it's not so bad to have a background chorus of cicadas!)

Our 1/4 inch of rain two nights ago did help the seedlings and brought up some new mushrooms, at least it brought them out of the oak leaves. We stand at 14.14 inches for the year, 83% of normal. That's actually more than we got in the last rain year, just 12.05 inches. But even if we were to get an inch every week for the next 3 months - which would be lovely! - that would only bring our total up to 27 inches, still less than our yearly average of 34.5 inches. It seems to be another dry year, but at least the rains we've had have come when we most needed them.
Locality: I have a hard time hearing cicadas.  They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus

File.Name: f128b_f128-014.jpg
Notes:
15769726214 Here are the first new female pistillate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. It seems too early to find these flowers - especially before the male staminate flowers - but here they are. Last year I found the first flowers on February 2, which isn't too far away. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

I can't explain that odd "floating" bud cover under the stem at the bottom of my photo. I guess it's caught in a spider web?

File.Name: f128c_f128-023.jpg
Notes:
16204471488 This is "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom pushing up a large "mushrump" under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. This one is still in the ground. See my [Previous] photo for the whole mushroom. The pinkish "blushing" of the cap and stem is the hallmark of this species. That's native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae) in the foreground. I've been looking for these amanitas. They are a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought, so far. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

I believe I've found this mushroom before this year. They typically fruit twice, but the early winter forms look quite different from this one. See my photos here and here. I believe these are all the same species of mushroom. That's one reason why mushrooms are so difficult to identify!
CommonNames: The Blusher; Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass
ScientificName: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae

File.Name: f128d_f128-028.jpg
Notes:
16204591150 This is "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. I put my 3-1/2 inch long pocket knife in the photo for scale. See my [Next] photo for the upper side of the cap, and compare my [Previous] photo of a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom in almost the same pose. This one has free gills, a membranous veil, and no volva at all to speak of. The pinkish "blushing" of the cap and stem is the hallmark of this species. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

I believe I've found this mushroom before this year, but the early winter forms look quite different from this one. See my photos here and here. I believe these are all the same species of mushroom. That's one reason why mushrooms are so difficult to identify!
CommonNames: The Blusher; Coast Live Oak; Panther Amanita

File.Name: f128e_f128-034.jpg
Notes:
16390151751 This is the same Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom as my [Previous] photo, out of the ground. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today, the first I've seen for almost a year. It has warts on the cap, a ring on the stem, and a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. This one may have some influence from the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata). The spots near the top of the cap are a rave of tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph!

Despite being poisonous, I've been looking for these amanitas. They are a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought, so far.
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Jonquil Amanita; Springtail
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata; Collembola; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata; Collembola; Amanita muscaria

Notes:

It has warts on the cap, a ring on the stem, and a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. This one may have some influence from the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata). It's growing in the woods with native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

File.Name: f129a_f129-003.jpg
Notes:
16399604925 This is the first flower of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family that I've found this year. The real flowers are those small greenish tubes, the rest of it is red bracts. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

It was the damnedest thing... I got this photo in the morning on the way to my photo place today. I had the magging thought that I overexposed all my photos (I did!) and thought to stop by again on my way home. Surprise, the whole hillside was weed-whacked two hours later, including these beautiful natives. There was a Santa Barbara County fire crew having lunch along the road. I considered stopping, but didn't. They are just kids doing their job, but it would be nice if they had someone on the crew who knew the natives. I guess this photo is also a memorial.

Today was another lovely day - overcast, but not too gloomy and very nice "cloudy bright" light.
Locality: I did!) and thought to stop by again on my way home.  Surprise
